prompt:
You are a mathematical research advisor with access to web search. Your job is to orchestrate solver agents to assemble the best possible proof for the problem below. You have 10 advisor calls total (including this one). Plan strategically. Use web search whenever you need to look up specific results, verify claims, or find techniques.

# Problem
Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

# Strategic Directions (from literature survey)
The problem is best viewed as a stable GL-character question: the Schur coefficient c_lambda(n) is the multiplicity of the GL_m irreducible of highest weight lambda in the multigraded Hilbert character of diagonal coinvariants. This is the framework of Bergeron's Multivariate diagonal coinvariant spaces for complex reflection groups, https://arxiv.org/abs/1105.4358, Theorem 2.1, and the GL_k x S_n bookkeeping in (GL_k × S_n)-Modules of Multivariate Diagonal Harmonics, https://arxiv.org/abs/2003.07402, Section 2. The hard part is that general multiset diagonal coinvariants lack a direct basis; many older hook results concern hooks on the S_n-Frobenius side, not hooks in the grading-variable GL side.

## Directions to Explore

### Primary route: GL(1|1) hook detector plus Fields theorem
Use the proven diagonal supersymmetry theorem to reduce ordinary GL-hook coefficients to the one-boson/one-fermion superspace coinvariant ring. For lambda=(a,1^b), a>=1, the resulting positive interpretation should be: c_(a,1^b)(n) counts triples (mu,T,U,pi), where mu is a partition of n, T and U are standard Young tableaux of shape mu, des(T)>=b+1, pi is a partition inside a b by (des(T)-1-b) rectangle, and a=maj(T)-b des(T)+binom(b,2)+|pi|. Equivalently, the refined Frobenius coefficient c_(a,1^b),mu omits the auxiliary tableau U. For b=0 this excludes the unique descent-free row tableau, accounting for the constant term.
Key ideas: Diagonal supersymmetry says the same universal coefficients c_lambda appear in Hilb(R_n^(1,1);q,u) expanded in super Schur functions s_lambda(q/u).; In one even and one odd variable, s_(a,1^b)(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^(a-1)u^(b+1), and non-hooks vanish.; Fields gives the q,z Frobenius of superspace coinvariants in fermionic degree b as C_{n,n-b}(x;q), whose Schur expansion is a standard-tableau major-index/q-binomial formula.; The q-Pascal identity [d choose b]_q=[d-1 choose b]_q+q^(d-b)[d-1 choose b-1]_q exactly inverts the triangular relation H_b=delta_{b,0}+C_b+q^(-1)C_{b-1}.
Relevant techniques: Diagonal Supersymmetry for Coinvariant Rings, https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.14885, Theorem 1.2, Corollary 1.3, and Proposition 5.1: universal super-Schur Hilbert and Frobenius coefficients are independent of the numbers of bosonic and fermionic variable sets.; Diagonal Supersymmetry for Coinvariant Rings, https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.14885, Lemma 4.4: the GL(1|1) super Schur specialization kills non-hooks and gives the two-term formula for hooks.; A Proof of the Fields Conjectures, https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.24027, Equations (2.5)-(2.10) and Theorem 5.2: grFrob(SR_n;q,z)=sum_k C_{n,k}(x;q) z^(n-k), with C_{n,k} having the standard-tableau Schur expansion.; Ordered Set Partitions, Generalized Coinvariant Algebras, and the Delta Conjecture, https://arxiv.org/abs/1609.07575, Corollary 6.13: the same kind of Schur expansion by standard tableaux and q-binomials, useful for checking indexing conventions.
Potential obstacles: The main danger is an indexing/sign convention error: Paper 14 uses super Schur s_lambda(q/u), Paper 15 uses fermionic degree n-k, and older Delta papers sometimes apply omega or q-reversal. Also, the row case b=0 has a constant term that must be removed; otherwise the formula incorrectly counts the trivial GL summand.

### Direct superspace Artin-basis model
Translate the hook coefficients into an explicit count of superspace Artin basis monomials, then construct a sign-reversing cancellation for the triangular subtraction. The superspace basis gives H_b(q)=sum_{|J|=b} product_i [st(J)_i]_q. The hook polynomial C_b(q)=sum_a c_(a,1^b)(n) q^a satisfies C_b=H_b-delta_{b,0}-q^(-1)C_{b-1}. A direct basis-level proof would identify the unpaired monomials in this recurrence.
Key ideas: Use theta-support J as fermionic degree and staircase bounds 0<=alpha_i<st(J)_i as the monomial model.; Interpret q^(-1)C_{b-1} as a degree-shifted shadow of lower fermionic degree, induced by the second term q^(a-1)u^(b+1) in s_(a,1^b)(q/u).; Seek a cancellation/injection from lower-degree shadows into the |J|=b basis elements, leaving a positive set equivalent to the tableau formula.
Relevant techniques: The Hilbert Series of the Superspace Coinvariant Ring, https://arxiv.org/abs/2301.09763, Theorem 5.3 and Lemma 2.1: Hilb(SR_n;q,z)=sum_J z^|J| product_i [st(J)_i]_q=sum_k z^(n-k)[k]!_q Stir_q(n,k).; Superspace Coinvariants and Hyperplane Arrangements, https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.17919, Theorem 7.1 and Corollary 7.2: the Sagan-Swanson Artin monomials M(J) theta_J form an actual basis, not just a Hilbert-series model.; The Hilbert Series of the Superspace Coinvariant Ring, https://arxiv.org/abs/2301.09763, Theorem 4.12: the theta_J sector is controlled by the colon quotient (I_n:f_J), explaining why the staircase product is algebraically natural.; Diagonal Supersymmetry for Coinvariant Rings, https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.14885, Corollary 5.3: the q-Stirling cancellation identity Hilb(R_n^(1,1);q,-q)=1 is the global shadow of the same cancellation mechanism.
Potential obstacles: The recurrence is positive only after cancellation; the Artin basis alone gives H_b, not C_b. A clean degree-preserving involution or injection may be nontrivial. If the goal is just a combinatorial interpretation, the tableau formula in the primary route is faster and already positive.

### Ordered-set-partition and Delta-specialization interpretation
Repackage the tableau formula in terms of ordered multiset partitions or ordered set partitions. Fields identifies the fermionic degree n-k piece of SR_n with C_{n,k}(x;q), and C_{n,k} has equivalent ordered multiset partition formulas using inv, maj, dinv, and minimaj. One can try to express c_(a,1^b)(n) as a statistic on ordered set partitions with an additional choice corresponding to the q-binomial [des(T)-1 choose b]_q.
Key ideas: Start from C_{n,k}(x;q)=sum_{OMP_{n,k}} q^stat x^content.; Use standardization and Gessel fundamental expansions to pass between ordered multiset partitions and tableau descent data.; Apply the same q-Pascal truncation that turns [des choose b]_q into [des-1 choose b]_q.
Relevant techniques: A Proof of the Fields Conjectures, https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.24027, Equations (2.5)-(2.10): C_{n,k} equals Delta'_{e_{k-1}} e_n at t=0 and has ordered multiset partition interpretations with inv, maj, dinv, and minimaj.; A Proof of the Fields Conjectures, https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.24027, Theorem 5.2 and Corollary 5.3: fermionic degree n-k of SR_n has Frobenius C_{n,k} and, ungraded, is the sign-twisted ordered-set-partition module.; Ordered Set Partitions, Generalized Coinvariant Algebras, and the Delta Conjecture, https://arxiv.org/abs/1609.07575, Equations (2.19)-(2.23) and Corollary 6.12: ordered multiset partition expansions and their Frobenius/Hilbert transformations.; Ordered Set Partitions, Generalized Coinvariant Algebras, and the Delta Conjecture, https://arxiv.org/abs/1609.07575, Gessel fundamental expansion technique: standardize reading words to connect raw statistics to Schur/tableau formulas.
Potential obstacles: The ordered-set-partition model naturally describes H_b, not the hook coefficient C_b. The q-binomial truncation has an immediate tableau meaning but may look artificial in ordered partition language unless one builds an explicit marked-descent or block-choice statistic.

### Refined Frobenius-level proof before Hilbert specialization
Prove the stronger statement for universal Frobenius coefficients c_(a,1^b),mu first, then obtain c_(a,1^b)(n) by summing over mu with weight f^mu. This keeps the S_n structure visible and avoids losing information in the Hilbert specialization.
Key ideas: For each mu, define C_{b,mu}(q)=sum_{T in SYT(mu), des(T)>=b+1} q^(maj(T)-b des(T)+binom(b,2)) [des(T)-1 choose b]_q.; Use q-Pascal to verify K_{b,mu}=delta_{b,0}delta_{mu,(n)}+C_{b,mu}+q^(-1)C_{b-1,mu}, where K_{b,mu} is the coefficient of s_mu in the fermionic-degree-b superspace Frobenius series.; Then c_(a,1^b)(n)=sum_mu f^mu [q^a] C_{b,mu}(q).
Relevant techniques: Diagonal Supersymmetry for Coinvariant Rings, https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.14885, Corollary 1.3: universal Frobenius coefficients c_{lambda,mu} exist in the super-Schur expansion.; A Proof of the Fields Conjectures, https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.24027, Lemma 2.2: parabolic antisymmetrizers compute e_mu^perp of Frobenius characteristics, useful if one wants an independent representation-theoretic verification.; A Proof of the Fields Conjectures, https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.24027, Lemma 4.19: antisymmetrized Artin-basis representatives give exact dimensions for parabolic probes, offering a proof route that does not rely only on symmetric-function manipulation.; Multivariate diagonal coinvariant spaces for complex reflection groups, https://arxiv.org/abs/1105.4358, Theorem 2.2 and small-n data: useful sanity checks for the recovered universal Frobenius coefficients.
Potential obstacles: This is slightly stronger than the asked Hilbert-series statement and therefore has more notation. However, it is often cleaner: the q-binomial identity is componentwise in mu, and the final Hilbert coefficient is just the standard dimension-weighted sum.

### Use two-variable parking and Schrodinger-path results only as checks
Do not start from the classical two-boson shuffle/Schroeder path hook formulas; they usually concern hooks in the S_n Frobenius alphabet, while the problem asks for hooks in the GL grading alphabet. They are still valuable for testing the specialization of the final c_(a,1^b)(n) formula to two grading variables.
Key ideas: Specialize the universal Schur expansion to two variables q,t and compare small n with known Hilb(DR_n;q,t).; Use parking-function and Schroeder-path scalar-product formulas to check e/h or S_n-hook projections, but keep them separate from GL-hook extraction.
Relevant techniques: Schedules and the Delta Conjecture, https://arxiv.org/abs/1908.04732, Theorem 4.13: Schroeder paths give hook Schur coefficients of nabla e_n on the Frobenius side, a useful but different hook problem.; A Combinatorial Formula for the Character of the Diagonal Coinvariants, https://arxiv.org/abs/math/0310424, Sections 4.5-4.7: parking-function and Schroeder specializations of scalar products with e_eta h_mu.; A polynomial expression for the Hilbert series of the quotient ring of diagonal coinvariants, https://www2.math.upenn.edu/~jhaglund/preprints/poly3.pdf, Corollary 1 and Section 5.3: Tesler/constant-term formulas provide independent q,t Hilbert checks.
Potential obstacles: This route can easily conflate two different Schur bases. It is best used after the supersymmetry derivation, as validation rather than as the main proof.

## Key Obstacles
The central obstacle is not existence of the hook coefficients but making the GL-hook extraction positive and conventionally correct. Older diagonal-coinvariant literature gives many hook formulas, but most are for S_n-Frobenius hooks or for two grading variables. The decisive modern input is the proven diagonal supersymmetry theorem, which turns GL hooks into GL(1|1) superspace degrees. After that, one must carefully remove the two-term overlap s_(a,1^b)(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^(a-1)u^(b+1). The q-Pascal identity gives a clean positive tableau formula; a more direct Artin-basis or ordered-set-partition interpretation would require an additional explicit cancellation or marking construction.

## Recommended Starting Point
Start with the primary route. Write H_b,mu(q)=sum_{T in SYT(mu)} q^(maj(T)-b des(T)+binom(b,2)) [des(T) choose b]_q from A Proof of the Fields Conjectures, https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.24027, Theorem 5.2. Define C_b,mu(q)=sum_{T: des(T)>=b+1} q^(maj(T)-b des(T)+binom(b,2)) [des(T)-1 choose b]_q. Check the identity H_b,mu=delta_{b,0}delta_{mu,(n)}+C_b,mu+q^(-1)C_{b-1,mu} by q-Pascal. Then invoke Diagonal Supersymmetry for Coinvariant Rings, https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.14885, Lemma 4.4 and Proposition 5.1, to identify [q^a]C_b,mu with the universal Frobenius hook coefficient, and sum over mu with weight f^mu to obtain the requested Hilbert coefficient c_(a,1^b)(n).

# Past Attempts on This Problem (from previous runs)


# Your Role
You are the sole orchestrator. You will:
1. Maintain a priority task list and a shared knowledge base.
2. Each round, assign 1–2 tasks to solver agents (parallel API calls).
3. Decide exactly what context each solver needs — you write their full prompts verbatim.
4. When you want a solver (or write-up agent) to read the **full text** of a prior    solver's output (e.g., to critique, attack, extend, or assemble it), put those task    IDs in `reference_task_ids` — the harness will auto-prepend the full output verbatim.    Do NOT try to paste prior outputs inline; just reference them by ID.
5. When significant partial results emerge, flag them for a dedicated write-up agent    (you write that prompt too, including all proof details found so far).

Note: you do NOT need to assemble a final solution yourself. When the budget runs out, a separate assembly stage will automatically synthesise the best possible solution from the shared KB. Focus every round on *exploration and proving new results*.

# Output Format
Reasoning prose first, then your plan inside an <ADVISOR_PLAN> tag. The content between the tags must be a single valid JSON object — do NOT wrap it in a markdown code block.

<ADVISOR_PLAN>
{
  "reasoning": "your strategic thinking — what to try first and why",
  "strategic_note": "2-3 sentence note for your future self: what you learned this round, what you decided, and why. This will be shown to you in every subsequent round as a memory aid.",
  "kb_updates": {
    "new_proven_results": [],
    "new_failed_attempts": [],
    "new_bottlenecks": [],
    "frontier": "the most important thing to establish next"
  },
  "task_assignments": [
    {
      "task_id": "t1",
      "description": "one-line label for logging",
      "reference_task_ids": [],
      "solver_prompt": "THE COMPLETE PROMPT to send verbatim to this solver. Include the problem statement, relevant KB results, proof technique hints, and precise instructions. Use `reference_task_ids` to pull in any prior solver outputs you want this solver to read in full — do NOT paste them inline here."
    }
  ],
  "writeup_tasks": [
    {
      "statement": "exact statement of the result being written up",
      "solves_original_problem": false,
      "reference_task_ids": [],
      "writeup_agent_prompt": "complete prompt for the write-up agent"
    }
  ],
  "action": "continue"
}
</ADVISOR_PLAN>

**reference_task_ids:** list of prior task IDs whose full outputs should be auto-attached to the agent's prompt. Strict for solver tasks (unknown IDs cause the task to fail) and lenient for writeups (unknown IDs are skipped with a warning). On round 1 there are no prior tasks, so leave this empty.

**solves_original_problem (writeup_tasks only):** set to `true` ONLY when the `statement` is the full original problem (not a relaxation, not a lemma, not a partial). When `true` and the resulting write-up passes verify+refine, the assembly stage will reuse the verified write-up verbatim instead of spending a fresh solver call. Set to `false` for any partial result, lemma, or relaxation. This flag is also used by the verifier to pick its framing: `true` ⇒ verify against the original problem verbatim; `false` ⇒ verify against the write-up's stated claim. Setting it incorrectly will give you a misleading verification verdict, so be honest about what is actually being written up.

**writeup_agent_prompt (writeup_tasks only):** the write-up agent will be evaluated by an independent referee, and the resulting artifact then flows downstream into benchmark and LaTeX-typesetting stages, none of which have access to this conversation, the shared KB, the solver outputs, or any custom notation introduced along the way. Your prompt MUST therefore instruct the agent to produce a **fully self-contained artifact**: (1) state the exact claim being proved up front, with every symbol, hypothesis, quantifier, and notational convention defined inline; (2) write the proof so a reader who has never seen the solver history, the KB, or this conversation can follow it end-to-end; (3) avoid any reference to "the problem above", "the previous result", "the construction we used", or other shorthand that depends on context the referee cannot see. If the agent needs concrete prior outputs to build on, list them in `reference_task_ids` (which auto-attaches them verbatim) — do NOT assume inherited context.

**action values:** `"continue"` — keep exploring; `"done"` — no more work needed.

Assign at most 2 solver tasks this round. Start with the highest-priority direction.

response:
Let  
\[
d(T)=\operatorname{des}(T),\qquad M(T)=\operatorname{maj}(T).
\]
Use the convention that \(\bigl[{r\atop s}\bigr]_q=0\) if \(r<0\), \(s<0\), or \(s>r\).

## Refined hook formula

For \(\mu\vdash n\), define
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1} c_{(a,1^b),\mu}\,q^a,\qquad C_{-1,\mu}(q)=0.
\]
Then the theorem to prove is equivalently
\[
\boxed{
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ d(T)\ge b+1}}
q^{M(T)-b\,d(T)+\binom b2}
\biggl[{d(T)-1\atop b}\biggr]_q .
}
\]
Taking the coefficient of \(q^a\) gives
\[
c_{(a,1^b),\mu}
=
\#\left\{(T,\pi):
\begin{array}{l}
T\in \operatorname{SYT}(\mu),\ d(T)\ge b+1,\\
\pi\subseteq b\times(d(T)-1-b),\\
a=M(T)-b\,d(T)+\binom b2+|\pi|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]

Here \(\pi\subseteq b\times r\) means \(\pi\) has at most \(b\) parts, each at most \(r\).

---

## 1. The triangular relation

Specialize the universal supersymmetric Frobenius expansion to one bosonic and one fermionic variable:
\[
\operatorname{Frob}(R_n^{(1,1)};q;u)
=
\sum_{\lambda,\mu} c_{\lambda,\mu}\,s_\lambda(q/u)s_\mu .
\]
By fact (B),
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
\]
and for hooks,
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1},
\]
while non-hooks vanish.

The empty coefficient is
\[
c_{\varnothing,\mu}=\delta_{\mu,(n)},
\]
because the degree-zero piece of the coinvariant algebra is the one-dimensional trivial \(S_n\)-module.

Now take the coefficient of \(u^b s_\mu\). The term \(u^b\) receives contributions from:

1. \(\lambda=\varnothing\), only when \(b=0\);
2. hooks \((a,1^b)\), through \(q^a u^b\);
3. hooks \((a,1^{b-1})\), through \(q^{a-1}u^b\).

Thus
\[
\boxed{
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\delta_{b,0}\delta_{\mu,(n)}
+
C_{b,\mu}(q)
+
q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q).
}
\]

---

## 2. Solving the recurrence using Fields’ formula

By fact (C),
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}
q^{M(T)-b\,d(T)+\binom b2}
\biggl[{d(T)\atop b}\biggr]_q .
\]

### Base case \(b=0\)

The triangular relation gives
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)
=
H_{0,\mu}(q)-\delta_{\mu,(n)}
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}q^{M(T)}-\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
\]
The only tableau with no descents is the unique row tableau of shape \((n)\). Hence
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ d(T)\ge 1}}
q^{M(T)}.
\]
This is the desired formula for \(b=0\), since
\[
\biggl[{d(T)-1\atop 0}\biggr]_q=1
\]
when \(d(T)\ge 1\).

### Induction step

For \(b\ge 1\), use the triangular relation:
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)=H_{b,\mu}(q)-q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q).
\]
Assuming the formula for \(C_{b-1,\mu}\),
\[
q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_T
q^{M(T)-b\,d(T)+\binom b2}
q^{d(T)-b}
\biggl[{d(T)-1\atop b-1}\biggr]_q .
\]
Therefore
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_T
q^{M(T)-b\,d(T)+\binom b2}
\left(
\biggl[{d(T)\atop b}\biggr]_q
-
q^{d(T)-b}
\biggl[{d(T)-1\atop b-1}\biggr]_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity
\[
\biggl[{d\atop b}\biggr]_q
=
\biggl[{d-1\atop b}\biggr]_q
+
q^{d-b}
\biggl[{d-1\atop b-1}\biggr]_q ,
\]
we get
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_T
q^{M(T)-b\,d(T)+\binom b2}
\biggl[{d(T)-1\atop b}\biggr]_q .
\]
Since \(\bigl[{d(T)-1\atop b}\bigr]_q=0\) unless \(d(T)\ge b+1\), this is exactly
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ d(T)\ge b+1}}
q^{M(T)-b\,d(T)+\binom b2}
\biggl[{d(T)-1\atop b}\biggr]_q .
\]

---

## 3. Translating the Gaussian binomial

The Gaussian binomial has the standard partition interpretation:
\[
\biggl[{r\atop b}\biggr]_q
=
\sum_{\pi\subseteq b\times(r-b)} q^{|\pi|}.
\]
Here \(r=d(T)-1\). Thus
\[
\biggl[{d(T)-1\atop b}\biggr]_q
=
\sum_{\pi\subseteq b\times(d(T)-1-b)} q^{|\pi|}.
\]
Hence the coefficient of \(q^a\) in \(C_{b,\mu}(q)\) counts pairs \((T,\pi)\) satisfying
\[
d(T)\ge b+1,\qquad
\pi\subseteq b\times(d(T)-1-b),
\]
and
\[
a=M(T)-b\,d(T)+\binom b2+|\pi|.
\]
This proves the refined interpretation of \(c_{(a,1^b),\mu}\).

---

## 4. Passing to the ordinary Hilbert coefficient

By fact (A),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu\,c_{(a,1^b),\mu},
\]
where \(f^\mu=\#\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\). Choosing one additional standard tableau \(U\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\) realizes the factor \(f^\mu\). Therefore \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\) is the number of quadruples
\[
(\mu,T,U,\pi)
\]
such that

\[
\mu\vdash n,\qquad T,U\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu),
\]
\[
d(T)\ge b+1,\qquad
\pi\subseteq b\times(d(T)-1-b),
\]
and
\[
a=M(T)-b\,d(T)+\binom b2+|\pi|.
\]

---

## Edge cases

- **\(b=0\).** The rectangle is \(0\times(d(T)-1)\), so \(\pi\) is forced to be empty. The formula becomes
  \[
  c_{(a),\mu}
  =
  \#\{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu): d(T)\ge 1,\ M(T)=a\}.
  \]
  The missing \(d(T)=0\) tableau is exactly the row tableau of shape \((n)\), accounted for by the empty partition coefficient.

- **Empty partition coefficient.** The coefficient for \(\lambda=\varnothing\) is
  \[
  c_{\varnothing,\mu}=\delta_{\mu,(n)},\qquad c_\varnothing(n)=1.
  \]

- **\(n=1\).** The only shape is \((1)\), and its unique tableau has \(d(T)=M(T)=0\). Thus no hook \((a,1^b)\) with \(a\ge 1\) contributes. The only nonzero coefficient is \(c_\varnothing(1)=1\).

- **Automatic vanishing for \(b\ge n-1\).** Every \(T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\) has \(d(T)\le n-1\). The formula requires \(d(T)\ge b+1\). If \(b\ge n-1\), this is impossible, so
  \[
  c_{(a,1^b),\mu}=0,\qquad c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0.
  \]

- **Positivity of \(a\).** If \(d(T)=b+r\) with \(r\ge 1\), then
  \[
  M(T)\ge 1+2+\cdots+d(T),
  \]
  so
  \[
  M(T)-b\,d(T)+\binom b2\ge \binom{r+1}{2}\ge 1.
  \]
  Thus the formula indeed only produces hooks with \(a\ge 1\).

<SOLVER_REPORT>

## Verdict

The candidate formula is **correct as stated**, with the standard Gaussian convention. The rectangle is

\[
b\times(\operatorname{des}(T)-1-b),
\]

not \(b\times(\operatorname{des}(T)-b)\), and the exponent

\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\,\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2+|\pi|
\]

does **not** need an additional shift.

---

## 1. Recurrence from the GL\((1|1)\) specialization

Let

\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1} c_{(a,1^b),\mu}q^a,\qquad C_{-1,\mu}(q)=0.
\]

Using

\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1},
\]

the coefficient of \(u^b\) receives contributions from hooks with leg length \(b\), and from hooks with leg length \(b-1\). Thus

\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)=\delta_{b,0}\delta_{\mu,(n)}+C_{b,\mu}(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q).
\]

The \(\delta_{\mu,(n)}\) term is the empty/constant term: for the row shape \((n)\), Fields’ formula gives \(H_{0,(n)}=1\), but this is not a nonempty hook coefficient.

---

## 2. Solving the recurrence

Write \(d=\operatorname{des}(T)\), \(m=\operatorname{maj}(T)\). Fields’ summand is

\[
q^{m-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q.
\]

Claim:

\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ d\ge b+1}}
q^{m-bd+\binom b2}{d-1\brack b}_q.
\]

Indeed, for one fixed tableau \(T\) with \(d\ge 1\), define

\[
c_b(T)=q^{m-bd+\binom b2}{d-1\brack b}_q.
\]

Then

\[
q^{-1}c_{b-1}(T)
=
q^{m-bd+\binom b2}q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q.
\]

Therefore

\[
c_b(T)+q^{-1}c_{b-1}(T)
=
q^{m-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d-1\brack b}_q+q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right).
\]

Using the Gaussian recurrence

\[
{d\brack b}_q
=
{d-1\brack b}_q+q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q,
\]

this equals Fields’ summand. The only missing case is \(d=0,b=0\), which occurs only for the row tableau and is exactly the \(\delta_{b,0}\delta_{\mu,(n)}\) term.

So the proposed closed form for \(C_{b,\mu}\) is proved.

---

## 3. Rectangle interpretation

Since

\[
{d-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\pi\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\pi|},
\]

the coefficient of \(q^a\) in \(C_{b,\mu}\) counts pairs \((T,\pi)\) with

\[
d=\operatorname{des}(T)\ge b+1,
\]

\[
\pi\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),
\]

and

\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\,\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2+|\pi|.
\]

Adding an independent \(U\in \operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\) gives the unrefined quadruple count. Thus the candidate’s refined/unrefined distinction is consistent.

Also, \(a\ge 1\) automatically: if the descent positions are \(i_1<\cdots<i_d\), then \(m\ge 1+\cdots+d\), and for \(b\le d-1\),

\[
m-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\frac{(d-b)(d-b+1)}2
\ge 1.
\]

So no invalid \(a=0\) hook is produced.

---

## 4. Explicit checks for \(n=1,2,3\)

Only nonzero \(H_b\) and \(C_b\) are shown.

### \(n=1\)

\[
\mu=(1)
\]

There is one tableau, with \((m,d)=(0,0)\).

\[
H_{0,(1)}=1.
\]

The recurrence gives

\[
H_0=\delta_{\mu,(1)}+C_0,
\]

so

\[
C_{0,(1)}=0.
\]

This confirms the row case: the constant \(1\) is not a hook coefficient.

---

### \(n=2\)

| \(\mu\) | Tableau stats \((m,d)\) | Fields \(H_{b,\mu}\) | Resulting \(C_{b,\mu}\) |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| \((2)\) | \((0,0)\) | \(H_0=1\) | \(C_0=0\) |
| \((1,1)\) | \((1,1)\) | \(H_0=q,\ H_1=1\) | \(C_0=q,\ C_1=0\) |

For \(\mu=(1,1)\),

\[
H_1=1=q^{-1}C_0,
\]

so the top \(H\)-degree \(b=1=d\) comes from the second term of \(s_{(1)}(q/u)=q+u\), not from \(C_1\).

---

### \(n=3\)

For \(\mu=(2,1)\), the two tableaux have descent data

\[
(m,d)=(2,1),\quad (1,1).
\]

For \(\mu=(1,1,1)\), the single tableau has

\[
(m,d)=(3,2).
\]

| \(\mu\) | Tableau stats | Fields \(H_{b,\mu}\) | Resulting \(C_{b,\mu}\) |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| \((3)\) | \((0,0)\) | \(H_0=1\) | \(C_0=0\) |
| \((2,1)\) | \((1,1),(2,1)\) | \(H_0=q+q^2,\ H_1=1+q\) | \(C_0=q+q^2,\ C_1=0\) |
| \((1,1,1)\) | \((3,2)\) | \(H_0=q^3,\ H_1=q+q^2,\ H_2=1\) | \(C_0=q^3,\ C_1=q,\ C_2=0\) |

The vertical shape \((1,1,1)\) is the key off-by-one test:

\[
C_1=q^{3-1\cdot 2}{1\brack 1}_q=q.
\]

Then

\[
H_1=C_1+q^{-1}C_0=q+q^2,
\]

and

\[
H_2=q^{-1}C_1=1.
\]

So \(C_2=0\), even though \(H_2\neq 0\). The top \(H\)-index \(b=d\) is produced by \(C_{d-1}\).

Unrefined, adding \(U\)-choices gives for \(n=3\):

\[
C_0=2q+2q^2+q^3,\qquad C_1=q,
\]

so

\[
c_{(1)}=2,\quad c_{(2)}=2,\quad c_{(3)}=1,\quad c_{(1,1)}=1.
\]

---

## 5. Off-by-one conclusion

The rectangle **must** be

\[
b\times(\operatorname{des}(T)-1-b).
\]

Using \(b\times(\operatorname{des}(T)-b)\) would give \({d\brack b}_q\), i.e. Fields’ \(H_b\)-summand itself, and would fail to subtract the \(q^{-1}C_{b-1}\) contribution. For example, for \(\mu=(1,1,1)\), \(d=2,m=3,b=1\), the wrong rectangle would give

\[
C_1=q(1+q),
\]

but the recurrence forces

\[
C_1=H_1-q^{-1}C_0=(q+q^2)-q^2=q.
\]

So the candidate’s \(b\times(\operatorname{des}(T)-1-b)\) rectangle is the correct one, and the exponent is already correctly normalized.

# Problem We Are Solving
Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

These λ_t are weakly decreasing because j_{t+1}>j_t, nonnegative because j_t≤r-h+t, and at most r-h; the construction is reversible. For a subset J ⊆ [n], define its J-staircase st(J) = (st(J)_1,...,st(J)_n) by st(J)_i = |{a ∈ [i] : a ∉ J}|; equivalently st(J)_1 is 0 if 1 ∈ J and 1 if 1 ∉ J, and st(J)_{i+1}=st(J)_i if i+1 ∈ J while st(J)_{i+1}=st(J)_i+1 if i+1 ∉ J. Let [r]_q = 1+q+...+q^{r-1} for r ≥ 1 and [0]_q=0, and let [k]!_q = [k]_q [k-1]_q ... [1]_q. Define q-Stirling numbers Stir_q(n,k) by Stir_q(0,0)=1, Stir_q(0,k)=0 for k≠0, and Stir_q(n,k) = [k]_q Stir_q(n-1,k) + Stir_q(n-1,k-1). Then the polynomial identity holds: Σ_{J⊆[n]} z^{|J|} Π_{i=1}^n [st(J)_i]_q = Σ_{k=1}^n z^{n-k} [k]!_q Stir_q(n,k). Equivalently, the coefficient of z^r on the left is the q-degree generating function of all pairs consisting of a subset J⊆[n] of size r and a monomial x_1^{a_1}...x_n^{a_n} satisfying 0 ≤ a_i < st(J)_i for every i.  (from: literature_2301.09763)
    Proof sketch: Let G(n,k) be the coefficient of z^{n-k} on the left, i.e. the sum over subsets J⊆[n] with exactly k elements outside J. The formula st(J)_i=|[i]\J| makes the recurrence transparent. Split such J according to whether n∈J. If n∈J, then J'=J\{n} has k elements outside it in [n-1], the first n-1 staircase entries are those of J', and the final entry is k; this contributes [k]_q G(n-1,k). If n∉J, then J⊆[n-1] has k-1 elements outside it in [n-1], and adjoining the nth entry again multiplies by [k]_q; this contributes [k]_q G(n-1,k-1). Thus G(n,k)=[k]_q G(n-1,k)+[k]_q G(n-1,k-1), with G(1,1)=1 and the usual boundary values. The quantity H(n,k):=[k]!_q Stir_q(n,k) satisfies the same recurrence, since multiplying Stir_q(n,k)=[k]_q Stir_q(n-1,k)+Stir_q(n-1,k-1) by [k]!_q gives H(n,k)=[k]_q H(n-1,k)+[k]_q H(n-1,k-1). Hence G(n,k)=H(n,k). The monomial interpretation is immediate from Π_i [st(J)_i]_q = Σ_{0≤a_i<st(J)_i} q^{a_1+...+a_n}.
  • [arXiv:2301.09763 Theorem 4.12] Let C[x_n]=C[x_1,...,x_n], and let I_n=(C[x_n]^{S_n}_+) be the classical coinvariant ideal, equivalently I_n=(e_1(x_n),...,e_n(x_n)). For S⊆[n], write h_d(S) for the complete homogeneous symmetric polynomial of degree d in the variables {x_s : s∈S}. For J⊆[n], define f_J = Π_{j∈J} x_j Π_{i=j+1}^n (x_j-x_i), with empty products equal to 1. Define polynomials p_{J,1},...,p_{J,n} as follows, using the convention min(∅)=∞: if i<min(J), set p_{J,i}=h_i({i,i+1,...,n}); if i≥min(J), set s=max(J∩[i]), T=J∪{i+1,i+2,...,n}, r=n-|T|+1, and p_{J,i}=∂h_r(T)/∂x_s. A permutation w∈S_n is j-resentful if w(j)=n or if w(j)+1 occurs among w(j+1),...,w(n); it is j-Nietzschean otherwise. It is J-Nietzschean if it is j-Nietzschean for every j∈J. Let N_J be the finite subset of C^n consisting of the points (w(1),...,w(n)) for J-Nietzschean w∈S_n. The quotient C[x_n]/(p_{J,*}) is a complete intersection, hence a Poincare duality algebra with socle degree Σ_i st(J)_i-n. The coinvariant algebra C[x_n]/I_n is also Poincare duality with socle degree binom(n,2), f_J∉I_n when 1∉J, and deg f_J=Σ_i(i-st(J)_i), so the socle degrees differ by deg f_J. For the equality with gr I(N_J), deform the coinvariant ideal to ~I_n=(e_d(x)-e_d(1,...,n):1≤d≤n), whose variety is the embedded set S_n⊂C^n. Give SR_n the bigrading in which q records total x-degree and z records total θ-degree. Then Hilb(SR_n;q,z)=Σ_{k=1}^n z^{n-k} [k]!_q Stir_q(n,k). For the upper bound, for every J⊆[n] the paper constructs elements q_{J,i}∈SI_n whose fermionic degree is |J| and whose Gale-leading θ_J coefficient is p_{J,i}. The polynomials p_{J,i} are the regular sequence of Theorem 4.12, with degrees st(J)_i. Choose homogeneous representatives B_n(J) for a basis of C[x_n]/(p_{J,1},...,p_{J,n}); their degree generating function is Π_i [st(J)_i]_q. A triangular straightening argument in Gale order says that any f θ_J can be reduced modulo SI_n to a linear combination of B_n(J) θ_J plus terms with strictly larger fermionic monomial in Gale order. Descending induction over Gale order shows that the union over J of B_n(J) θ_J spans SR_n, giving Hilb(SR_n;q,z) ≤ Σ_J z^{|J|}Π_i[st(J)_i]_q. For the lower bound, use differential operators D_J built from minors of the complete-homogeneous matrix H. Their key triangular property is that D_J(δ_n), where δ_n is the Vandermonde, is superharmonic and has θ_J coefficient ±f_J⊙δ_n, with only θ_K terms for K≤Gale J. The annihilator of f_J⊙δ_n in C[x_n] is (I_n:f_J). By Theorem 4.12, choose B_n(J) mapping to a basis of C[x_n]/(I_n:f_J), again with degree generating function Π_i[st(J)_i]_q. The final expression with q-Stirling numbers is exactly Lemma 2.1.

The condition λ∈P(k,j,n) is exactly the condition under which the corresponding simple gl(k|j)-character s_λ(q/u) can appear.     Proof sketch: Start from the defining expansion s_λ(q/u) = ∑_{ν⊆λ} s_ν(q) s_{λ′/ν′}(u). Since q is a single variable, s_ν(q) is nonzero only when ν is a one-row partition ν=(r), in which case it equals q^r. Since u is a single variable, the skew Schur function s_{λ′/ν′}(u) is nonzero only when the skew shape λ′/ν′ is a horizontal strip, in which case it is the corresponding power of u. For λ=(a,1^b), so λ′=(b+1,1^(a−1)), the only row partitions ν=(r) for which λ′/ν′ is a horizontal strip are r=a and r=a−1. These give the two terms q^a u^b and q^(a−1)u^(b+1), respectively; this also covers a=1, where the r=0 contribution is allowed. The empty partition gives 1. If λ_2≥2, then λ′ has at least two cells in its second column, and removing ν′=(1^r) from the first column cannot make λ′/ν′ a horizontal strip, so every summand vanishes. <full_solver_output task_id="t1">
Let  
\[
d(T)=\operatorname{des}(T),\qquad M(T)=\operatorname{maj}(T).
\]
Use the convention that \(\bigl[{r\atop s}\bigr]_q=0\) if \(r<0\), \(s<0\), or \(s>r\).

## Refined hook formula

For \(\mu\vdash n\), define
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1} c_{(a,1^b),\mu}\,q^a,\qquad C_{-1,\mu}(q)=0.
\]
Then the theorem to prove is equivalently
\[
\boxed{
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ d(T)\ge b+1}}
q^{M(T)-b\,d(T)+\binom b2}
\biggl[{d(T)-1\atop b}\biggr]_q .
}
\]
Taking the coefficient of \(q^a\) gives
\[
c_{(a,1^b),\mu}
=
\#\left\{(T,\pi):
\begin{array}{l}
T\in \operatorname{SYT}(\mu),\ d(T)\ge b+1,\\
\pi\subseteq b\times(d(T)-1-b),\\
a=M(T)-b\,d(T)+\binom b2+|\pi|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]

Here \(\pi\subseteq b\times r\) means \(\pi\) has at most \(b\) parts, each at most \(r\).

---

## 1. The triangular relation

Specialize the universal supersymmetric Frobenius expansion to one bosonic and one fermionic variable:
\[
\operatorname{Frob}(R_n^{(1,1)};q;u)
=
\sum_{\lambda,\mu} c_{\lambda,\mu}\,s_\lambda(q/u)s_\mu .
\]
By fact (B),
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
\]
and for hooks,
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1},
\]
while non-hooks vanish.

The empty coefficient is
\[
c_{\varnothing,\mu}=\delta_{\mu,(n)},
\]
because the degree-zero piece of the coinvariant algebra is the one-dimensional trivial \(S_n\)-module.

Now take the coefficient of \(u^b s_\mu\). The term \(u^b\) receives contributions from:

1. \(\lambda=\varnothing\), only when \(b=0\);
2. hooks \((a,1^b)\), through \(q^a u^b\);
3. hooks \((a,1^{b-1})\), through \(q^{a-1}u^b\).

Thus
\[
\boxed{
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\delta_{b,0}\delta_{\mu,(n)}
+
C_{b,\mu}(q)
+
q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q).
}
\]

---

## 2. Solving the recurrence using Fields’ formula

By fact (C),
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}
q^{M(T)-b\,d(T)+\binom b2}
\biggl[{d(T)\atop b}\biggr]_q .
\]

### Base case \(b=0\)

The triangular relation gives
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)
=
H_{0,\mu}(q)-\delta_{\mu,(n)}
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}q^{M(T)}-\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
\]
The only tableau with no descents is the unique row tableau of shape \((n)\). Hence
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ d(T)\ge 1}}
q^{M(T)}.
\]
This is the desired formula for \(b=0\), since
\[
\biggl[{d(T)-1\atop 0}\biggr]_q=1
\]
when \(d(T)\ge 1\).

### Induction step

For \(b\ge 1\), use the triangular relation:
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)=H_{b,\mu}(q)-q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q).
\]
Assuming the formula for \(C_{b-1,\mu}\),
\[
q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_T
q^{M(T)-b\,d(T)+\binom b2}
q^{d(T)-b}
\biggl[{d(T)-1\atop b-1}\biggr]_q .
\]
Therefore
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_T
q^{M(T)-b\,d(T)+\binom b2}
\left(
\biggl[{d(T)\atop b}\biggr]_q
-
q^{d(T)-b}
\biggl[{d(T)-1\atop b-1}\biggr]_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity
\[
\biggl[{d\atop b}\biggr]_q
=
\biggl[{d-1\atop b}\biggr]_q
+
q^{d-b}
\biggl[{d-1\atop b-1}\biggr]_q ,
\]
we get
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_T
q^{M(T)-b\,d(T)+\binom b2}
\biggl[{d(T)-1\atop b}\biggr]_q .
\]
Since \(\bigl[{d(T)-1\atop b}\bigr]_q=0\) unless \(d(T)\ge b+1\), this is exactly
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ d(T)\ge b+1}}
q^{M(T)-b\,d(T)+\binom b2}
\biggl[{d(T)-1\atop b}\biggr]_q .
\]

---

## 3. Translating the Gaussian binomial

The Gaussian binomial has the standard partition interpretation:
\[
\biggl[{r\atop b}\biggr]_q
=
\sum_{\pi\subseteq b\times(r-b)} q^{|\pi|}.
\]
Here \(r=d(T)-1\). Thus
\[
\biggl[{d(T)-1\atop b}\biggr]_q
=
\sum_{\pi\subseteq b\times(d(T)-1-b)} q^{|\pi|}.
\]
Hence the coefficient of \(q^a\) in \(C_{b,\mu}(q)\) counts pairs \((T,\pi)\) satisfying
\[
d(T)\ge b+1,\qquad
\pi\subseteq b\times(d(T)-1-b),
\]
and
\[
a=M(T)-b\,d(T)+\binom b2+|\pi|.
\]
This proves the refined interpretation of \(c_{(a,1^b),\mu}\).

---

## 4. Passing to the ordinary Hilbert coefficient

By fact (A),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu\,c_{(a,1^b),\mu},
\]
where \(f^\mu=\#\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\). Choosing one additional standard tableau \(U\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\) realizes the factor \(f^\mu\). Therefore \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\) is the number of quadruples
\[
(\mu,T,U,\pi)
\]
such that

\[
\mu\vdash n,\qquad T,U\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu),
\]
\[
d(T)\ge b+1,\qquad
\pi\subseteq b\times(d(T)-1-b),
\]
and
\[
a=M(T)-b\,d(T)+\binom b2+|\pi|.
\]

---

## Edge cases

- **\(b=0\).** The rectangle is \(0\times(d(T)-1)\), so \(\pi\) is forced to be empty. The formula becomes
  \[
  c_{(a),\mu}
  =
  \#\{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu): d(T)\ge 1,\ M(T)=a\}.
  \]
  The missing \(d(T)=0\) tableau is exactly the row tableau of shape \((n)\), accounted for by the empty partition coefficient.

- **Empty partition coefficient.** The coefficient for \(\lambda=\varnothing\) is
  \[
  c_{\varnothing,\mu}=\delta_{\mu,(n)},\qquad c_\varnothing(n)=1.
  \]

- **\(n=1\).** The only shape is \((1)\), and its unique tableau has \(d(T)=M(T)=0\). Thus no hook \((a,1^b)\) with \(a\ge 1\) contributes. The only nonzero coefficient is \(c_\varnothing(1)=1\).

- **Automatic vanishing for \(b\ge n-1\).** Every \(T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\) has \(d(T)\le n-1\). The formula requires \(d(T)\ge b+1\). If \(b\ge n-1\), this is impossible, so
  \[
  c_{(a,1^b),\mu}=0,\qquad c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0.
  \]

- **Positivity of \(a\).** If \(d(T)=b+r\) with \(r\ge 1\), then
  \[
  M(T)\ge 1+2+\cdots+d(T),
  \]
  so
  \[
  M(T)-b\,d(T)+\binom b2\ge \binom{r+1}{2}\ge 1.
  \]
  Thus the formula indeed only produces hooks with \(a\ge 1\).

## Verdict

The candidate formula is **correct as stated**, with the standard Gaussian convention. The rectangle is

\[
b\times(\operatorname{des}(T)-1-b),
\]

not \(b\times(\operatorname{des}(T)-b)\), and the exponent

\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\,\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2+|\pi|
\]

does **not** need an additional shift.

---

## 1. Recurrence from the GL\((1|1)\) specialization

Let

\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1} c_{(a,1^b),\mu}q^a,\qquad C_{-1,\mu}(q)=0.
\]

Using

\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1},
\]

the coefficient of \(u^b\) receives contributions from hooks with leg length \(b\), and from hooks with leg length \(b-1\). Thus

\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)=\delta_{b,0}\delta_{\mu,(n)}+C_{b,\mu}(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q).
\]

The \(\delta_{\mu,(n)}\) term is the empty/constant term: for the row shape \((n)\), Fields’ formula gives \(H_{0,(n)}=1\), but this is not a nonempty hook coefficient.

---

## 2. Solving the recurrence

Write \(d=\operatorname{des}(T)\), \(m=\operatorname{maj}(T)\). Fields’ summand is

\[
q^{m-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q.
\]

Claim:

\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ d\ge b+1}}
q^{m-bd+\binom b2}{d-1\brack b}_q.
\]

Indeed, for one fixed tableau \(T\) with \(d\ge 1\), define

\[
c_b(T)=q^{m-bd+\binom b2}{d-1\brack b}_q.
\]

Then

\[
q^{-1}c_{b-1}(T)
=
q^{m-bd+\binom b2}q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q.
\]

Therefore

\[
c_b(T)+q^{-1}c_{b-1}(T)
=
q^{m-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d-1\brack b}_q+q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right).
\]

Using the Gaussian recurrence

\[
{d\brack b}_q
=
{d-1\brack b}_q+q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q,
\]

this equals Fields’ summand. The only missing case is \(d=0,b=0\), which occurs only for the row tableau and is exactly the \(\delta_{b,0}\delta_{\mu,(n)}\) term.

So the proposed closed form for \(C_{b,\mu}\) is proved.

---

## 3. Rectangle interpretation

Since

\[
{d-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\pi\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\pi|},
\]

the coefficient of \(q^a\) in \(C_{b,\mu}\) counts pairs \((T,\pi)\) with

\[
d=\operatorname{des}(T)\ge b+1,
\]

\[
\pi\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),
\]

and

\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\,\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2+|\pi|.
\]

Adding an independent \(U\in \operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\) gives the unrefined quadruple count. Thus the candidate’s refined/unrefined distinction is consistent.

Also, \(a\ge 1\) automatically: if the descent positions are \(i_1<\cdots<i_d\), then \(m\ge 1+\cdots+d\), and for \(b\le d-1\),

\[
m-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\frac{(d-b)(d-b+1)}2
\ge 1.
\]

So no invalid \(a=0\) hook is produced.

---

## 4. Explicit checks for \(n=1,2,3\)

Only nonzero \(H_b\) and \(C_b\) are shown.

### \(n=1\)

\[
\mu=(1)
\]

There is one tableau, with \((m,d)=(0,0)\).

\[
H_{0,(1)}=1.
\]

The recurrence gives

\[
H_0=\delta_{\mu,(1)}+C_0,
\]

so

\[
C_{0,(1)}=0.
\]

This confirms the row case: the constant \(1\) is not a hook coefficient.

---

### \(n=2\)

| \(\mu\) | Tableau stats \((m,d)\) | Fields \(H_{b,\mu}\) | Resulting \(C_{b,\mu}\) |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| \((2)\) | \((0,0)\) | \(H_0=1\) | \(C_0=0\) |
| \((1,1)\) | \((1,1)\) | \(H_0=q,\ H_1=1\) | \(C_0=q,\ C_1=0\) |

For \(\mu=(1,1)\),

\[
H_1=1=q^{-1}C_0,
\]

so the top \(H\)-degree \(b=1=d\) comes from the second term of \(s_{(1)}(q/u)=q+u\), not from \(C_1\).

---

### \(n=3\)

For \(\mu=(2,1)\), the two tableaux have descent data

\[
(m,d)=(2,1),\quad (1,1).
\]

For \(\mu=(1,1,1)\), the single tableau has

\[
(m,d)=(3,2).
\]

| \(\mu\) | Tableau stats | Fields \(H_{b,\mu}\) | Resulting \(C_{b,\mu}\) |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| \((3)\) | \((0,0)\) | \(H_0=1\) | \(C_0=0\) |
| \((2,1)\) | \((1,1),(2,1)\) | \(H_0=q+q^2,\ H_1=1+q\) | \(C_0=q+q^2,\ C_1=0\) |
| \((1,1,1)\) | \((3,2)\) | \(H_0=q^3,\ H_1=q+q^2,\ H_2=1\) | \(C_0=q^3,\ C_1=q,\ C_2=0\) |

The vertical shape \((1,1,1)\) is the key off-by-one test:

\[
C_1=q^{3-1\cdot 2}{1\brack 1}_q=q.
\]

Then

\[
H_1=C_1+q^{-1}C_0=q+q^2,
\]

and

\[
H_2=q^{-1}C_1=1.
\]

So \(C_2=0\), even though \(H_2\neq 0\). The top \(H\)-index \(b=d\) is produced by \(C_{d-1}\).

Unrefined, adding \(U\)-choices gives for \(n=3\):

\[
C_0=2q+2q^2+q^3,\qquad C_1=q,
\]

so

\[
c_{(1)}=2,\quad c_{(2)}=2,\quad c_{(3)}=1,\quad c_{(1,1)}=1.
\]

---

## 5. Off-by-one conclusion

The rectangle **must** be

\[
b\times(\operatorname{des}(T)-1-b).
\]

Using \(b\times(\operatorname{des}(T)-b)\) would give \({d\brack b}_q\), i.e. Fields’ \(H_b\)-summand itself, and would fail to subtract the \(q^{-1}C_{b-1}\) contribution. For example, for \(\mu=(1,1,1)\), \(d=2,m=3,b=1\), the wrong rectangle would give

\[
C_1=q(1+q),
\]

but the recurrence forces

\[
C_1=H_1-q^{-1}C_0=(q+q^2)-q^2=q.
\]

So the candidate’s \(b\times(\operatorname{des}(T)-1-b)\) rectangle is the correct one, and the exponent is already correctly normalized.

Let  
\[
d(T)=\operatorname{des}(T),\qquad M(T)=\operatorname{maj}(T).
\]
Use the convention that \(\bigl[{r\atop s}\bigr]_q=0\) if \(r<0\), \(s<0\), or \(s>r\).

## Refined hook formula

For \(\mu\vdash n\), define
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1} c_{(a,1^b),\mu}\,q^a,\qquad C_{-1,\mu}(q)=0.
\]
Then the theorem to prove is equivalently
\[
\boxed{
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ d(T)\ge b+1}}
q^{M(T)-b\,d(T)+\binom b2}
\biggl[{d(T)-1\atop b}\biggr]_q .
}
\]
Taking the coefficient of \(q^a\) gives
\[
c_{(a,1^b),\mu}
=
\#\left\{(T,\pi):
\begin{array}{l}
T\in \operatorname{SYT}(\mu),\ d(T)\ge b+1,\\
\pi\subseteq b\times(d(T)-1-b),\\
a=M(T)-b\,d(T)+\binom b2+|\pi|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]

Here \(\pi\subseteq b\times r\) means \(\pi\) has at most \(b\) parts, each at most \(r\).

---

## 1. The triangular relation

Specialize the universal supersymmetric Frobenius expansion to one bosonic and one fermionic variable:
\[
\operatorname{Frob}(R_n^{(1,1)};q;u)
=
\sum_{\lambda,\mu} c_{\lambda,\mu}\,s_\lambda(q/u)s_\mu .
\]
By fact (B),
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
\]
and for hooks,
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1},
\]
while non-hooks vanish.

The empty coefficient is
\[
c_{\varnothing,\mu}=\delta_{\mu,(n)},
\]
because the degree-zero piece of the coinvariant algebra is the one-dimensional trivial \(S_n\)-module.

Now take the coefficient of \(u^b s_\mu\). The term \(u^b\) receives contributions from:

1. \(\lambda=\varnothing\), only when \(b=0\);
2. hooks \((a,1^b)\), through \(q^a u^b\);
3. hooks \((a,1^{b-1})\), through \(q^{a-1}u^b\).

Thus
\[
\boxed{
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\delta_{b,0}\delta_{\mu,(n)}
+
C_{b,\mu}(q)
+
q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q).
}
\]

---

## 2. Solving the recurrence using Fields’ formula

By fact (C),
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}
q^{M(T)-b\,d(T)+\binom b2}
\biggl[{d(T)\atop b}\biggr]_q .
\]

### Base case \(b=0\)

The triangular relation gives
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)
=
H_{0,\mu}(q)-\delta_{\mu,(n)}
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}q^{M(T)}-\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
\]
The only tableau with no descents is the unique row tableau of shape \((n)\). Hence
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ d(T)\ge 1}}
q^{M(T)}.
\]
This is the desired formula for \(b=0\), since
\[
\biggl[{d(T)-1\atop 0}\biggr]_q=1
\]
when \(d(T)\ge 1\).

### Induction step

For \(b\ge 1\), use the triangular relation:
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)=H_{b,\mu}(q)-q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q).
\]
Assuming the formula for \(C_{b-1,\mu}\),
\[
q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_T
q^{M(T)-b\,d(T)+\binom b2}
q^{d(T)-b}
\biggl[{d(T)-1\atop b-1}\biggr]_q .
\]
Therefore
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_T
q^{M(T)-b\,d(T)+\binom b2}
\left(
\biggl[{d(T)\atop b}\biggr]_q
-
q^{d(T)-b}
\biggl[{d(T)-1\atop b-1}\biggr]_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity
\[
\biggl[{d\atop b}\biggr]_q
=
\biggl[{d-1\atop b}\biggr]_q
+
q^{d-b}
\biggl[{d-1\atop b-1}\biggr]_q ,
\]
we get
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_T
q^{M(T)-b\,d(T)+\binom b2}
\biggl[{d(T)-1\atop b}\biggr]_q .
\]
Since \(\bigl[{d(T)-1\atop b}\bigr]_q=0\) unless \(d(T)\ge b+1\), this is exactly
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ d(T)\ge b+1}}
q^{M(T)-b\,d(T)+\binom b2}
\biggl[{d(T)-1\atop b}\biggr]_q .
\]

---

## 3. Translating the Gaussian binomial

The Gaussian binomial has the standard partition interpretation:
\[
\biggl[{r\atop b}\biggr]_q
=
\sum_{\pi\subseteq b\times(r-b)} q^{|\pi|}.
\]
Here \(r=d(T)-1\). Thus
\[
\biggl[{d(T)-1\atop b}\biggr]_q
=
\sum_{\pi\subseteq b\times(d(T)-1-b)} q^{|\pi|}.
\]
Hence the coefficient of \(q^a\) in \(C_{b,\mu}(q)\) counts pairs \((T,\pi)\) satisfying
\[
d(T)\ge b+1,\qquad
\pi\subseteq b\times(d(T)-1-b),
\]
and
\[
a=M(T)-b\,d(T)+\binom b2+|\pi|.
\]
This proves the refined interpretation of \(c_{(a,1^b),\mu}\).

---

## 4. Passing to the ordinary Hilbert coefficient

By fact (A),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu\,c_{(a,1^b),\mu},
\]
where \(f^\mu=\#\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\). Choosing one additional standard tableau \(U\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\) realizes the factor \(f^\mu\). Therefore \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\) is the number of quadruples
\[
(\mu,T,U,\pi)
\]
such that

\[
\mu\vdash n,\qquad T,U\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu),
\]
\[
d(T)\ge b+1,\qquad
\pi\subseteq b\times(d(T)-1-b),
\]
and
\[
a=M(T)-b\,d(T)+\binom b2+|\pi|.
\]

---

## Edge cases

- **\(b=0\).** The rectangle is \(0\times(d(T)-1)\), so \(\pi\) is forced to be empty. The formula becomes
  \[
  c_{(a),\mu}
  =
  \#\{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu): d(T)\ge 1,\ M(T)=a\}.
  \]
  The missing \(d(T)=0\) tableau is exactly the row tableau of shape \((n)\), accounted for by the empty partition coefficient.

- **Empty partition coefficient.** The coefficient for \(\lambda=\varnothing\) is
  \[
  c_{\varnothing,\mu}=\delta_{\mu,(n)},\qquad c_\varnothing(n)=1.
  \]

- **\(n=1\).** The only shape is \((1)\), and its unique tableau has \(d(T)=M(T)=0\). Thus no hook \((a,1^b)\) with \(a\ge 1\) contributes. The only nonzero coefficient is \(c_\varnothing(1)=1\).

- **Automatic vanishing for \(b\ge n-1\).** Every \(T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\) has \(d(T)\le n-1\). The formula requires \(d(T)\ge b+1\). If \(b\ge n-1\), this is impossible, so
  \[
  c_{(a,1^b),\mu}=0,\qquad c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0.
  \]

- **Positivity of \(a\).** If \(d(T)=b+r\) with \(r\ge 1\), then
  \[
  M(T)\ge 1+2+\cdots+d(T),
  \]
  so
  \[
  M(T)-b\,d(T)+\binom b2\ge \binom{r+1}{2}\ge 1.
  \]
  Thus the formula indeed only produces hooks with \(a\ge 1\).

## Verdict

The candidate formula is **correct as stated**, with the standard Gaussian convention. The rectangle is

\[
b\times(\operatorname{des}(T)-1-b),
\]

not \(b\times(\operatorname{des}(T)-b)\), and the exponent

\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\,\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2+|\pi|
\]

does **not** need an additional shift.

---

## 1. Recurrence from the GL\((1|1)\) specialization

Let

\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1} c_{(a,1^b),\mu}q^a,\qquad C_{-1,\mu}(q)=0.
\]

Using

\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1},
\]

the coefficient of \(u^b\) receives contributions from hooks with leg length \(b\), and from hooks with leg length \(b-1\). Thus

\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)=\delta_{b,0}\delta_{\mu,(n)}+C_{b,\mu}(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q).
\]

The \(\delta_{\mu,(n)}\) term is the empty/constant term: for the row shape \((n)\), Fields’ formula gives \(H_{0,(n)}=1\), but this is not a nonempty hook coefficient.

---

## 2. Solving the recurrence

Write \(d=\operatorname{des}(T)\), \(m=\operatorname{maj}(T)\). Fields’ summand is

\[
q^{m-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q.
\]

Claim:

\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ d\ge b+1}}
q^{m-bd+\binom b2}{d-1\brack b}_q.
\]

Indeed, for one fixed tableau \(T\) with \(d\ge 1\), define

\[
c_b(T)=q^{m-bd+\binom b2}{d-1\brack b}_q.
\]

Then

\[
q^{-1}c_{b-1}(T)
=
q^{m-bd+\binom b2}q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q.
\]

Therefore

\[
c_b(T)+q^{-1}c_{b-1}(T)
=
q^{m-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d-1\brack b}_q+q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right).
\]

Using the Gaussian recurrence

\[
{d\brack b}_q
=
{d-1\brack b}_q+q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q,
\]

this equals Fields’ summand. The only missing case is \(d=0,b=0\), which occurs only for the row tableau and is exactly the \(\delta_{b,0}\delta_{\mu,(n)}\) term.

So the proposed closed form for \(C_{b,\mu}\) is proved.

---

## 3. Rectangle interpretation

Since

\[
{d-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\pi\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\pi|},
\]

the coefficient of \(q^a\) in \(C_{b,\mu}\) counts pairs \((T,\pi)\) with

\[
d=\operatorname{des}(T)\ge b+1,
\]

\[
\pi\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),
\]

and

\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\,\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2+|\pi|.
\]

Adding an independent \(U\in \operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\) gives the unrefined quadruple count. Thus the candidate’s refined/unrefined distinction is consistent.

Also, \(a\ge 1\) automatically: if the descent positions are \(i_1<\cdots<i_d\), then \(m\ge 1+\cdots+d\), and for \(b\le d-1\),

\[
m-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\frac{(d-b)(d-b+1)}2
\ge 1.
\]

So no invalid \(a=0\) hook is produced.

---

## 4. Explicit checks for \(n=1,2,3\)

Only nonzero \(H_b\) and \(C_b\) are shown.

### \(n=1\)

\[
\mu=(1)
\]

There is one tableau, with \((m,d)=(0,0)\).

\[
H_{0,(1)}=1.
\]

The recurrence gives

\[
H_0=\delta_{\mu,(1)}+C_0,
\]

so

\[
C_{0,(1)}=0.
\]

This confirms the row case: the constant \(1\) is not a hook coefficient.

---

### \(n=2\)

| \(\mu\) | Tableau stats \((m,d)\) | Fields \(H_{b,\mu}\) | Resulting \(C_{b,\mu}\) |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| \((2)\) | \((0,0)\) | \(H_0=1\) | \(C_0=0\) |
| \((1,1)\) | \((1,1)\) | \(H_0=q,\ H_1=1\) | \(C_0=q,\ C_1=0\) |

For \(\mu=(1,1)\),

\[
H_1=1=q^{-1}C_0,
\]

so the top \(H\)-degree \(b=1=d\) comes from the second term of \(s_{(1)}(q/u)=q+u\), not from \(C_1\).

---

### \(n=3\)

For \(\mu=(2,1)\), the two tableaux have descent data

\[
(m,d)=(2,1),\quad (1,1).
\]

For \(\mu=(1,1,1)\), the single tableau has

\[
(m,d)=(3,2).
\]

| \(\mu\) | Tableau stats | Fields \(H_{b,\mu}\) | Resulting \(C_{b,\mu}\) |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| \((3)\) | \((0,0)\) | \(H_0=1\) | \(C_0=0\) |
| \((2,1)\) | \((1,1),(2,1)\) | \(H_0=q+q^2,\ H_1=1+q\) | \(C_0=q+q^2,\ C_1=0\) |
| \((1,1,1)\) | \((3,2)\) | \(H_0=q^3,\ H_1=q+q^2,\ H_2=1\) | \(C_0=q^3,\ C_1=q,\ C_2=0\) |

The vertical shape \((1,1,1)\) is the key off-by-one test:

\[
C_1=q^{3-1\cdot 2}{1\brack 1}_q=q.
\]

Then

\[
H_1=C_1+q^{-1}C_0=q+q^2,
\]

and

\[
H_2=q^{-1}C_1=1.
\]

So \(C_2=0\), even though \(H_2\neq 0\). The top \(H\)-index \(b=d\) is produced by \(C_{d-1}\).

Unrefined, adding \(U\)-choices gives for \(n=3\):

\[
C_0=2q+2q^2+q^3,\qquad C_1=q,
\]

so

\[
c_{(1)}=2,\quad c_{(2)}=2,\quad c_{(3)}=1,\quad c_{(1,1)}=1.
\]

---

## 5. Off-by-one conclusion

The rectangle **must** be

\[
b\times(\operatorname{des}(T)-1-b).
\]

Using \(b\times(\operatorname{des}(T)-b)\) would give \({d\brack b}_q\), i.e. Fields’ \(H_b\)-summand itself, and would fail to subtract the \(q^{-1}C_{b-1}\) contribution. For example, for \(\mu=(1,1,1)\), \(d=2,m=3,b=1\), the wrong rectangle would give

\[
C_1=q(1+q),
\]

but the recurrence forces

\[
C_1=H_1-q^{-1}C_0=(q+q^2)-q^2=q.
\]

So the candidate’s \(b\times(\operatorname{des}(T)-1-b)\) rectangle is the correct one, and the exponent is already correctly normalized.

Let  
\[
d(T)=\operatorname{des}(T),\qquad M(T)=\operatorname{maj}(T).
\]
Use the convention that \(\bigl[{r\atop s}\bigr]_q=0\) if \(r<0\), \(s<0\), or \(s>r\).

## Refined hook formula

For \(\mu\vdash n\), define
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1} c_{(a,1^b),\mu}\,q^a,\qquad C_{-1,\mu}(q)=0.
\]
Then the theorem to prove is equivalently
\[
\boxed{
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ d(T)\ge b+1}}
q^{M(T)-b\,d(T)+\binom b2}
\biggl[{d(T)-1\atop b}\biggr]_q .
}
\]
Taking the coefficient of \(q^a\) gives
\[
c_{(a,1^b),\mu}
=
\#\left\{(T,\pi):
\begin{array}{l}
T\in \operatorname{SYT}(\mu),\ d(T)\ge b+1,\\
\pi\subseteq b\times(d(T)-1-b),\\
a=M(T)-b\,d(T)+\binom b2+|\pi|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]

Here \(\pi\subseteq b\times r\) means \(\pi\) has at most \(b\) parts, each at most \(r\).

---

## 1. The triangular relation

Specialize the universal supersymmetric Frobenius expansion to one bosonic and one fermionic variable:
\[
\operatorname{Frob}(R_n^{(1,1)};q;u)
=
\sum_{\lambda,\mu} c_{\lambda,\mu}\,s_\lambda(q/u)s_\mu .
\]
By fact (B),
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
\]
and for hooks,
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1},
\]
while non-hooks vanish.

The empty coefficient is
\[
c_{\varnothing,\mu}=\delta_{\mu,(n)},
\]
because the degree-zero piece of the coinvariant algebra is the one-dimensional trivial \(S_n\)-module.

Now take the coefficient of \(u^b s_\mu\). The term \(u^b\) receives contributions from:

1. \(\lambda=\varnothing\), only when \(b=0\);
2. hooks \((a,1^b)\), through \(q^a u^b\);
3. hooks \((a,1^{b-1})\), through \(q^{a-1}u^b\).

Thus
\[
\boxed{
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\delta_{b,0}\delta_{\mu,(n)}
+
C_{b,\mu}(q)
+
q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q).
}
\]

---

## 2. Solving the recurrence using Fields’ formula

By fact (C),
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}
q^{M(T)-b\,d(T)+\binom b2}
\biggl[{d(T)\atop b}\biggr]_q .
\]

### Base case \(b=0\)

The triangular relation gives
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)
=
H_{0,\mu}(q)-\delta_{\mu,(n)}
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}q^{M(T)}-\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
\]
The only tableau with no descents is the unique row tableau of shape \((n)\). Hence
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ d(T)\ge 1}}
q^{M(T)}.
\]
This is the desired formula for \(b=0\), since
\[
\biggl[{d(T)-1\atop 0}\biggr]_q=1
\]
when \(d(T)\ge 1\).

### Induction step

For \(b\ge 1\), use the triangular relation:
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)=H_{b,\mu}(q)-q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q).
\]
Assuming the formula for \(C_{b-1,\mu}\),
\[
q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_T
q^{M(T)-b\,d(T)+\binom b2}
q^{d(T)-b}
\biggl[{d(T)-1\atop b-1}\biggr]_q .
\]
Therefore
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_T
q^{M(T)-b\,d(T)+\binom b2}
\left(
\biggl[{d(T)\atop b}\biggr]_q
-
q^{d(T)-b}
\biggl[{d(T)-1\atop b-1}\biggr]_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity
\[
\biggl[{d\atop b}\biggr]_q
=
\biggl[{d-1\atop b}\biggr]_q
+
q^{d-b}
\biggl[{d-1\atop b-1}\biggr]_q ,
\]
we get
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_T
q^{M(T)-b\,d(T)+\binom b2}
\biggl[{d(T)-1\atop b}\biggr]_q .
\]
Since \(\bigl[{d(T)-1\atop b}\bigr]_q=0\) unless \(d(T)\ge b+1\), this is exactly
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ d(T)\ge b+1}}
q^{M(T)-b\,d(T)+\binom b2}
\biggl[{d(T)-1\atop b}\biggr]_q .
\]

---

## 3. Translating the Gaussian binomial

The Gaussian binomial has the standard partition interpretation:
\[
\biggl[{r\atop b}\biggr]_q
=
\sum_{\pi\subseteq b\times(r-b)} q^{|\pi|}.
\]
Here \(r=d(T)-1\). Thus
\[
\biggl[{d(T)-1\atop b}\biggr]_q
=
\sum_{\pi\subseteq b\times(d(T)-1-b)} q^{|\pi|}.
\]
Hence the coefficient of \(q^a\) in \(C_{b,\mu}(q)\) counts pairs \((T,\pi)\) satisfying
\[
d(T)\ge b+1,\qquad
\pi\subseteq b\times(d(T)-1-b),
\]
and
\[
a=M(T)-b\,d(T)+\binom b2+|\pi|.
\]
This proves the refined interpretation of \(c_{(a,1^b),\mu}\).

---

## 4. Passing to the ordinary Hilbert coefficient

By fact (A),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu\,c_{(a,1^b),\mu},
\]
where \(f^\mu=\#\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\). Choosing one additional standard tableau \(U\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\) realizes the factor \(f^\mu\). Therefore \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\) is the number of quadruples
\[
(\mu,T,U,\pi)
\]
such that

\[
\mu\vdash n,\qquad T,U\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu),
\]
\[
d(T)\ge b+1,\qquad
\pi\subseteq b\times(d(T)-1-b),
\]
and
\[
a=M(T)-b\,d(T)+\binom b2+|\pi|.
\]

---

## Edge cases

- **\(b=0\).** The rectangle is \(0\times(d(T)-1)\), so \(\pi\) is forced to be empty. The formula becomes
  \[
  c_{(a),\mu}
  =
  \#\{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu): d(T)\ge 1,\ M(T)=a\}.
  \]
  The missing \(d(T)=0\) tableau is exactly the row tableau of shape \((n)\), accounted for by the empty partition coefficient.

- **Empty partition coefficient.** The coefficient for \(\lambda=\varnothing\) is
  \[
  c_{\varnothing,\mu}=\delta_{\mu,(n)},\qquad c_\varnothing(n)=1.
  \]

- **\(n=1\).** The only shape is \((1)\), and its unique tableau has \(d(T)=M(T)=0\). Thus no hook \((a,1^b)\) with \(a\ge 1\) contributes. The only nonzero coefficient is \(c_\varnothing(1)=1\).

- **Automatic vanishing for \(b\ge n-1\).** Every \(T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\) has \(d(T)\le n-1\). The formula requires \(d(T)\ge b+1\). If \(b\ge n-1\), this is impossible, so
  \[
  c_{(a,1^b),\mu}=0,\qquad c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0.
  \]

- **Positivity of \(a\).** If \(d(T)=b+r\) with \(r\ge 1\), then
  \[
  M(T)\ge 1+2+\cdots+d(T),
  \]
  so
  \[
  M(T)-b\,d(T)+\binom b2\ge \binom{r+1}{2}\ge 1.
  \]
  Thus the formula indeed only produces hooks with \(a\ge 1\).

## Verdict

The candidate formula is **correct as stated**, with the standard Gaussian convention. The rectangle is

\[
b\times(\operatorname{des}(T)-1-b),
\]

not \(b\times(\operatorname{des}(T)-b)\), and the exponent

\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\,\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2+|\pi|
\]

does **not** need an additional shift.

## 1. Recurrence from the GL\((1|1)\) specialization

Let

\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1} c_{(a,1^b),\mu}q^a,\qquad C_{-1,\mu}(q)=0.
\]

Using

\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1},
\]

the coefficient of \(u^b\) receives contributions from hooks with leg length \(b\), and from hooks with leg length \(b-1\). Thus

\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)=\delta_{b,0}\delta_{\mu,(n)}+C_{b,\mu}(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q).
\]

The \(\delta_{\mu,(n)}\) term is the empty/constant term: for the row shape \((n)\), Fields’ formula gives \(H_{0,(n)}=1\), but this is not a nonempty hook coefficient.

---

## 2. Solving the recurrence

Write \(d=\operatorname{des}(T)\), \(m=\operatorname{maj}(T)\). Fields’ summand is

\[
q^{m-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q.
\]

Claim:

\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ d\ge b+1}}
q^{m-bd+\binom b2}{d-1\brack b}_q.
\]

Indeed, for one fixed tableau \(T\) with \(d\ge 1\), define

\[
c_b(T)=q^{m-bd+\binom b2}{d-1\brack b}_q.
\]

Then

\[
q^{-1}c_{b-1}(T)
=
q^{m-bd+\binom b2}q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q.
\]

Therefore

\[
c_b(T)+q^{-1}c_{b-1}(T)
=
q^{m-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d-1\brack b}_q+q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right).
\]

Using the Gaussian recurrence

\[
{d\brack b}_q
=
{d-1\brack b}_q+q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q,
\]

this equals Fields’ summand. The only missing case is \(d=0,b=0\), which occurs only for the row tableau and is exactly the \(\delta_{b,0}\delta_{\mu,(n)}\) term.

So the proposed closed form for \(C_{b,\mu}\) is proved.

---

## 3. Rectangle interpretation

Since

\[
{d-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\pi\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\pi|},
\]

the coefficient of \(q^a\) in \(C_{b,\mu}\) counts pairs \((T,\pi)\) with

\[
d=\operatorname{des}(T)\ge b+1,
\]

\[
\pi\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),
\]

and

\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\,\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2+|\pi|.
\]

Adding an independent \(U\in \operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\) gives the unrefined quadruple count. Thus the candidate’s refined/unrefined distinction is consistent.

Also, \(a\ge 1\) automatically: if the descent positions are \(i_1<\cdots<i_d\), then \(m\ge 1+\cdots+d\), and for \(b\le d-1\),

\[
m-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\frac{(d-b)(d-b+1)}2
\ge 1.
\]

So no invalid \(a=0\) hook is produced.

---

## 4. Explicit checks for \(n=1,2,3\)

Only nonzero \(H_b\) and \(C_b\) are shown.

\]

\[
H_{0,(1)}=1.

\[
H_0=\delta_{\mu,(1)}+C_0,

so

\[
C_{0,(1)}=0.

This confirms the row case: the constant \(1\) is not a hook coefficient.

| \((2)\) | \((0,0)\) | \(H_0=1\) | \(C_0=0\) |
| \((1,1)\) | \((1,1)\) | \(H_0=q,\ H_1=1\) | \(C_0=q,\ C_1=0\) |

For \(\mu=(1,1)\),

\[
H_1=1=q^{-1}C_0,
\]

so the top \(H\)-degree \(b=1=d\) comes from the second term of \(s_{(1)}(q/u)=q+u\), not from \(C_1\).

---

### \(n=3\)

For \(\mu=(2,1)\), the two tableaux have descent data

\[
(m,d)=(2,1),\quad (1,1).
\]

For \(\mu=(1,1,1)\), the single tableau has

\[
(m,d)=(3,2).
\]

| \(\mu\) | Tableau stats | Fields \(H_{b,\mu}\) | Resulting \(C_{b,\mu}\) |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| \((3)\) | \((0,0)\) | \(H_0=1\) | \(C_0=0\) |
| \((2,1)\) | \((1,1),(2,1)\) | \(H_0=q+q^2,\ H_1=1+q\) | \(C_0=q+q^2,\ C_1=0\) |
| \((1,1,1)\) | \((3,2)\) | \(H_0=q^3,\ H_1=q+q^2,\ H_2=1\) | \(C_0=q^3,\ C_1=q,\ C_2=0\) |

The vertical shape \((1,1,1)\) is the key off-by-one test:

\[
C_1=q^{3-1\cdot 2}{1\brack 1}_q=q.
\]

Then

\[
H_1=C_1+q^{-1}C_0=q+q^2,
\]

and

\[
H_2=q^{-1}C_1=1.
\]

So \(C_2=0\), even though \(H_2\neq 0\). The top \(H\)-index \(b=d\) is produced by \(C_{d-1}\).

Unrefined, adding \(U\)-choices gives for \(n=3\):

\[
C_0=2q+2q^2+q^3,\qquad C_1=q,
\]

so

\[
c_{(1)}=2,\quad c_{(2)}=2,\quad c_{(3)}=1,\quad c_{(1,1)}=1.
\]

---

## 5. Off-by-one conclusion

The rectangle **must** be

\[
b\times(\operatorname{des}(T)-1-b).
\]

Using \(b\times(\operatorname{des}(T)-b)\) would give \({d\brack b}_q\), i.e. Fields’ \(H_b\)-summand itself, and would fail to subtract the \(q^{-1}C_{b-1}\) contribution. For example, for \(\mu=(1,1,1)\), \(d=2,m=3,b=1\), the wrong rectangle would give

\[
C_1=q(1+q),
\]

but the recurrence forces

\[
C_1=H_1-q^{-1}C_0=(q+q^2)-q^2=q.
\]

So the candidate’s \(b\times(\operatorname{des}(T)-1-b)\) rectangle is the correct one, and the exponent is already correctly normalized.

response:
Let

\[
C_b^{(n)}(q):=\sum_{a\ge 1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a .
\]

From t1/t2, we have the tableau-pair count

\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(\mu,T,U,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
\mu\vdash n,\quad T,U\in \operatorname{SYT}(\mu),\\
\operatorname{des}(T)\ge b+1,\quad
\rho\subseteq b\times(\operatorname{des}(T)-1-b),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\,\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]

## RSK convention

Use ordinary **row-insertion RSK** on the one-line word \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\):

- \(P(w)\) is the insertion tableau;
- \(Q(w)\) is the recording tableau, with entry \(i\) placed in the new cell created when \(w_i\) is inserted.

For tableaux, take \(i\in \operatorname{Des}(T)\) iff \(i+1\) lies in a strictly lower row than \(i\). With this convention,

\[
\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w),
\qquad
\operatorname{Des}(P(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w^{-1}).
\]

Thus in the tableau-pair formula we identify

\[
T=Q(w),\qquad U=P(w).
\]

RSK is a bijection

\[
S_n \longleftrightarrow \bigsqcup_{\mu\vdash n}
\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\times \operatorname{SYT}(\mu),
\]

so the quadruple count becomes the following permutation count.

## Final hook formula

For \(a\ge 1\), \(b\ge 0\),

\[
\boxed{
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\quad d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
}
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\boxed{
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ \operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
{\operatorname{des}(w)-1\brack b}_q .
}
\]

### Special cases

- Empty coefficient:

\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1.
\]

- For \(b=0\), the rectangle has height \(0\), so \(\rho=\varnothing\). Hence

\[
c_{(a)}(n)
=
\#\{w\in S_n:\operatorname{des}(w)\ge 1,\ \operatorname{maj}(w)=a\}.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
C_0^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ w\ne 12\cdots n}}q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)}
=[n]_q!-1.
\]

- If \(b\ge n-1\), then \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\), since \(\operatorname{des}(w)\le n-1\) but the formula requires \(\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1\).

## Decorated-permutation version

Let

\[
\operatorname{Des}(w)=\{i_1<i_2<\cdots<i_d\}.
\]

Instead of choosing \(\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)\), equivalently choose \(b\) marked descents among the first \(d-1\) descent ranks:

\[
J=\{j_1<\cdots<j_b\}\subseteq \{1,\ldots,d-1\}.
\]

Mark the actual descents \(i_{j_1},\ldots,i_{j_b}\). Then

\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,J):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
J\subseteq\{1,\ldots,d-1\},\ |J|=b,\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-\sum_{j\in J}(d+1-j)
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]

This follows from the standard bijection

\[
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)
\quad\longleftrightarrow\quad
J=\{j_1<\cdots<j_b\}\subseteq [d-1],
\]

with

\[
|\rho|=\sum_{s=1}^b j_s-\binom{b+1}{2}.
\]

The rectangle-partition version is slightly cleaner because it keeps the add-on statistic as the manifestly nonnegative quantity \(|\rho|\).

## Checks

For \(n=3\),

\[
C_0^{(3)}(q)=2q+2q^2+q^3,\qquad
C_1^{(3)}(q)=q,\qquad
C_b^{(3)}(q)=0\ \text{for }b\ge 2,
\]

and \(c_\varnothing(3)=1\). Thus

\[
c_{(1)}=2,\quad c_{(2)}=2,\quad c_{(3)}=1,\quad c_{(1,1)}=1.
\]

For \(n=4\), the descent-major distributions are

\[
\sum_{\operatorname{des}=1}q^{\operatorname{maj}}=3q+5q^2+3q^3,
\]

\[
\sum_{\operatorname{des}=2}q^{\operatorname{maj}}=3q^3+5q^4+3q^5,
\]

\[
\sum_{\operatorname{des}=3}q^{\operatorname{maj}}=q^6.
\]

Therefore

\[
C_0^{(4)}(q)=3q+5q^2+6q^3+5q^4+3q^5+q^6,
\]

\[
C_1^{(4)}(q)=3q+5q^2+4q^3+q^4,
\]

\[
C_2^{(4)}(q)=q,
\qquad
C_b^{(4)}(q)=0\ \text{for }b\ge 3.
\]

No corrections to t1/t2 are needed. The only convention-sensitive point is that under standard row-insertion RSK the descent-preserving tableau is \(Q(w)\), the recording tableau, not \(P(w)\).

## Original Problem

Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

## Problem Solved

For every integer \(n\ge 1\), let \(c_\lambda(n)\) denote the universal Schur coefficient in the multigraded Hilbert-series expansion of \(R_n^{(m)}\). If \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\) is a hook partition, where \(a\ge 1\) and \(b\ge 0\), then
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Here \(S_n\) is the symmetric group, a descent of \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\) is an index \(i\in\{1,\dots,n-1\}\) with \(w_i>w_{i+1}\),
\[
\operatorname{des}(w)=|\{i:w_i>w_{i+1}\}|,\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{w_i>w_{i+1}} i.
\]
A partition \(\rho\subseteq b\times r\) means that \(\rho\) has at most \(b\) parts and every part is at most \(r\); the empty partition is allowed, and \(|\rho|\) denotes the sum of its parts. For a standard Young tableau \(T\), a descent is an index \(i\) such that \(i+1\) appears in a strictly lower row than \(i\), and \(\operatorname{des}(T)\), \(\operatorname{maj}(T)\) are defined analogously.

The empty coefficient is
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]
Consequently,
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\qquad\text{whenever }b\ge n-1.
\]

Gaussian binomial coefficients are used with the convention
\[
\biggl[{r\atop s}\biggr]_q
=
\begin{cases}
\displaystyle\prod_{i=1}^{s}\frac{1-q^{r-s+i}}{1-q^i},&0\le s\le r,\\[1em]
0,&r<0,\ s<0,\text{ or }s>r.
\end{cases}
\]
Equivalently, for \(0\le s\le r\),
\[
\biggl[{r\atop s}\biggr]_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq s\times(r-s)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]

This proves the original hook-coefficient problem as stated; it is not a relaxation.

## Proof

Fix \(n\ge 1\). For a partition \(\mu\vdash n\), let \(\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\) be the set of standard Young tableaux of shape \(\mu\), and let \(f^\mu=|\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)|\).

We use the following standard inputs.

1. There are refined universal Frobenius coefficients \(c_{\lambda,\mu}\) such that, after passing to the one-bosonic-one-fermionic specialization,
   \[
   \operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)
   =
   \sum_{\lambda,\,\mu\vdash n}
   c_{\lambda,\mu}\,s_\lambda(q/u)\,s_\mu.
   \]
   The original Hilbert coefficient is obtained by forgetting the \(S_n\)-module structure:
   \[
   c_\lambda(n)=\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu c_{\lambda,\mu}.
   \]
   Also, the degree-zero piece is the constant polynomial carrying the trivial representation, so
   \[
   c_{\varnothing,\mu}=\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
   \]

2. In one bosonic variable \(q\) and one fermionic variable \(u\),
   \[
   s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
   \]
   and for hooks,
   \[
   s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
   \]
   All nonempty non-hook Schur functions vanish under this specialization.

3. Fields’ formula gives
   \[
   \operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)
   =
   \sum_T
   q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(T)+1}{2}}
   \prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(T)}(q^i+u)\,
   s_{\operatorname{shape}(T)},
   \]
   where the sum is over all standard Young tableaux \(T\) of size \(n\).

### 1. Extracting \(u^b s_\mu\) from Fields’ formula

Let \(H_{b,\mu}(q)\) be the coefficient of \(u^b s_\mu\) in \(\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)\).

Fix \(T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\), and write
\[
d=\operatorname{des}(T),\qquad M=\operatorname{maj}(T).
\]
The coefficient of \(u^b\) in
\[
\prod_{i=1}^{d}(q^i+u)
\]
is obtained by choosing \(b\) factors contributing \(u\), or equivalently \(d-b\) factors contributing \(q^i\). Thus, for \(0\le b\le d\),
\[
[u^b]\prod_{i=1}^{d}(q^i+u)
=
q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}
\biggl[{d\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]
Multiplying by Fields’ prefactor gives
\[
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}}
q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}
\biggl[{d\atop b}\biggr]_q
=
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\biggl[{d\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]
Therefore
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
\biggl[{\operatorname{des}(T)\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]

### 2. The triangular relation for hook coefficients

For \(b\ge 0\), define
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1} c_{(a,1^b),\mu}q^a,
\qquad
C_{-1,\mu}(q)=0.
\]
Compare the coefficient of \(u^b s_\mu\) in
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{\lambda,\mu}
c_{\lambda,\mu}s_\lambda(q/u)s_\mu.
\]
The empty partition contributes only when \(b=0\), giving
\[
\delta_{b,0}\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
\]
A hook \((a,1^b)\) contributes \(q^a u^b\), giving \(C_{b,\mu}(q)\). A hook \((a,1^{b-1})\) contributes \(q^{a-1}u^b\), giving \(q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q)\). Hence
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\delta_{b,0}\delta_{\mu,(n)}
+
C_{b,\mu}(q)
+
q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q).
\]

### 3. Solving the recurrence

We claim that
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ \operatorname{des}(T)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
\biggl[{\operatorname{des}(T)-1\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]

For \(b=0\), the triangular relation gives
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)=H_{0,\mu}(q)-\delta_{\mu,(n)}
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)}
-\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
\]
A standard tableau has no descents iff it is the unique row tableau of shape \((n)\). Therefore
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ \operatorname{des}(T)\ge 1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)},
\]
which is the claimed formula for \(b=0\), since
\[
\biggl[{\operatorname{des}(T)-1\atop 0}\biggr]_q=1
\]
when \(\operatorname{des}(T)\ge 1\).

Now assume \(b\ge 1\) and the formula is known for \(b-1\). From the triangular relation,
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)=H_{b,\mu}(q)-q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q).
\]
For a fixed tableau \(T\), with \(d=\operatorname{des}(T)\) and \(M=\operatorname{maj}(T)\), the contribution from \(q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q)\) is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
q^{d-b}
\biggl[{d-1\atop b-1}\biggr]_q.
\]
Thus
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_T
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\biggl[{d\atop b}\biggr]_q
-
q^{d-b}
\biggl[{d-1\atop b-1}\biggr]_q
\right).
\]
Using the \(q\)-Pascal identity
\[
\biggl[{d\atop b}\biggr]_q
=
\biggl[{d-1\atop b}\biggr]_q
+
q^{d-b}
\biggl[{d-1\atop b-1}\biggr]_q,
\]
we get
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_T
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\biggl[{d-1\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]
By the Gaussian convention, this summand is zero unless \(d-1\ge b\), i.e. unless \(d\ge b+1\). Hence
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ d\ge b+1}}
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\biggl[{d-1\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]

### 4. Translating the Gaussian binomial

For \(d\ge b+1\),
\[
\biggl[{d-1\atop b}\biggr]_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Therefore the coefficient of \(q^a\) in \(C_{b,\mu}(q)\) counts pairs \((T,\rho)\) such that
\[
T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu),\qquad d=\operatorname{des}(T)\ge b+1,
\]
\[
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(T)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

The exponent is always positive in this situation. Indeed, if \(d\ge b+1\), then the \(d\) descent positions have sum at least \(1+2+\cdots+d\), so
\[
\operatorname{maj}(T)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}
\ge 1.
\]
Thus the formula produces only hook arms \(a\ge 1\), as required.

### 5. Passing from tableaux to permutations by RSK

Now use
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu c_{(a,1^b),\mu}.
\]
Multiplying by \(f^\mu\) means choosing an additional standard tableau \(U\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\). Hence \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\) counts quadruples
\[
(\mu,T,U,\rho)
\]
such that
\[
\mu\vdash n,\qquad T,U\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu),
\]
\[
d=\operatorname{des}(T)\ge b+1,\qquad
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(T)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

By the Robinson-Schensted-Knuth correspondence, permutations \(w\in S_n\) are in bijection with pairs \((P(w),Q(w))\) of standard Young tableaux of the same shape. Moreover,
\[
\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w),
\]
and therefore
\[
\operatorname{des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{des}(w),\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(Q(w))=\operatorname{maj}(w).
\]
Identify \(U=P(w)\) and \(T=Q(w)\). Then the above quadruples are in bijection with pairs \((w,\rho)\) satisfying
\[
w\in S_n,\qquad d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,
\]
\[
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]
This proves the claimed permutation interpretation.

### 6. Edge cases

For the empty partition,
\[
c_\varnothing(n)
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu c_{\varnothing,\mu}
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu \delta_{\mu,(n)}
=
f^{(n)}
=
1.
\]

When \(b=0\), the rectangle is \(0\times(d-1)\), so \(\rho\) must be empty. The formula becomes
\[
c_{(a)}(n)
=
\#\{w\in S_n:\operatorname{des}(w)\ge 1,\ \operatorname{maj}(w)=a\}.
\]
Since \(a\ge 1\), the condition \(\operatorname{maj}(w)=a\) already forces at least one descent.

Finally, every permutation in \(S_n\) has at most \(n-1\) descents. If \(b\ge n-1\), then the condition
\[
\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1
\]
is impossible. Hence
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0
\qquad\text{for all }b\ge n-1.
\]

## Task t1 — Main supersymmetry-plus-Fields proof of hook formula
