## Original Problem

Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

## Problem Solved

Fix \(n\ge 1\). Work over \(\mathbb C\). For \(m\ge 1\), let
\[
P_n^{(m)}=\mathbb C[x_i^{(r)}:1\le i\le n,\ 1\le r\le m],
\]
with \(S_n\) acting diagonally by permuting the index \(i\). Let \(I_n^{(m)}\) be the ideal generated by all \(S_n\)-invariant polynomials in \(P_n^{(m)}\) with zero constant term, and define the diagonal coinvariant algebra
\[
R_n^{(m)}=P_n^{(m)}/I_n^{(m)}.
\]
Give \(R_n^{(m)}\) the \(\mathbb Z_{\ge0}^m\)-grading in which \(x_i^{(r)}\) has degree \(1\) in the \(r\)-th component and degree \(0\) in the others. The universal Hilbert coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) are the coefficients in the stable Schur expansion
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots,q_m)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots,q_m),
\]
where \(s_\lambda\) is the Schur polynomial and \(s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots,q_m)=0\) if \(\ell(\lambda)>m\).

A hook partition is written
\[
\lambda=(a,1^b)
\]
with \(a\ge 1\) and \(b\ge 0\). For a permutation \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\in S_n\), define
\[
{\rm Des}(w)=\{i\in\{1,\ldots,n-1\}: w_i>w_{i+1}\},
\]
\[
{\rm des}(w)=|{\rm Des}(w)|,\qquad
{\rm maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in{\rm Des}(w)} i.
\]
For integers \(h,r\ge0\), a partition \(\rho\subseteq h\times r\) means a weakly decreasing sequence
\[
\rho=(\rho_1,\ldots,\rho_h)
\]
with \(r\ge \rho_1\ge\cdots\ge\rho_h\ge0\), and \(|\rho|=\rho_1+\cdots+\rho_h\). If \(h=0\), only the empty partition is allowed.

The theorem proved here is:
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1,
\]
and for every hook \((a,1^b)\) with \(a\ge1\), \(b\ge0\),
\[
\boxed{
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\\
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
}
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ {\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

Here the Gaussian binomial coefficient is
\[
\begin{bmatrix} r\\ s\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{cases}
\displaystyle\prod_{i=1}^{s}\frac{1-q^{r-s+i}}{1-q^i},&0\le s\le r,\\[1em]
0,&\text{otherwise},
\end{cases}
\]
and, for \(0\le s\le r\),
\[
\begin{bmatrix}r\\ s\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq s\times(r-s)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]

In particular, when \(b=0\),
\[
c_{(a)}(n)=\#\{w\in S_n:{\rm des}(w)\ge1,\ {\rm maj}(w)=a\},
\]
and if \(b\ge n-1\), then
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0.
\]
This is exactly the requested hook-coefficient interpretation, not a relaxation.

## Proof

First note that the degree-zero component of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is spanned by the image of \(1\). Since every nonempty Schur polynomial is homogeneous of positive total degree and \(s_{\varnothing}=1\), the constant term of the Hilbert series gives
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1.
\]

We now use three standard facts, stated in the precise forms needed.

### Known input 1: the \(GL(1|1)\) super Hilbert specialization

Let
\[
SR_n=\mathbb C[x_1,\ldots,x_n]\otimes \Lambda(\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_n)\big/\langle \text{positive-degree }S_n\text{-invariants}\rangle
\]
be the superspace coinvariant algebra with one even set \(x_1,\ldots,x_n\) and one odd set \(\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_n\), where \(S_n\) permutes the indices simultaneously. Let \(q\) record \(x\)-degree and \(u\) record \(\theta\)-degree, using the ordinary unsigned Hilbert series
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)=\sum_{i,j\ge0}\dim(SR_n)_{i,j}q^iu^j.
\]
The \(GL(1|1)\) specialization theorem says that the same universal coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) satisfy
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)=\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u),
\]
where \(s_\lambda(q/u)\) is the one-even-one-odd super Schur character.

### Known input 2: one-even-one-odd super Schur characters

In one even variable \(q\) and one odd variable \(u\),
\[
s_{\varnothing}(q/u)=1.
\]
For a hook \((a,1^b)\), \(a\ge1\), \(b\ge0\),
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
All nonempty non-hook Schur characters vanish under this \(GL(1|1)\) specialization.

### Known input 3: ordinary superspace coinvariant Hilbert product formula

The ordinary bigraded Hilbert series of \(SR_n\) is
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-\binom{{\rm des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{{\rm des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\]

### Step 1: compute the coefficient of \(u^b\) from the product formula

Let
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)=[u^b]\,{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u).
\]
Fix \(w\in S_n\), and write
\[
d={\rm des}(w),\qquad M={\rm maj}(w).
\]
The coefficient of \(u^b\) in \(\prod_{i=1}^d(q^i+u)\) is obtained by choosing \(b\) factors to contribute \(u\), equivalently choosing \(d-b\) factors to contribute powers of \(q\). Thus
\[
[u^b]\prod_{i=1}^{d}(q^i+u)
=
q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Therefore
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}}
q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Since
\[
\binom{d+1}{2}-\binom{d-b+1}{2}
=
bd-\binom b2,
\]
we get
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

### Step 2: compare with the \(GL(1|1)\) Schur expansion

For \(b\ge0\), define
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
and set
\[
C_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0.
\]
Using the \(GL(1|1)\) specialization and the hook character formula,
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
1+
\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\left(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\right).
\]
Taking the coefficient of \(u^b\) gives the triangular relation
\[
\boxed{
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\delta_{b,0}
+
C_b^{(n)}(q)
+
q^{-1}C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
}
\]

### Step 3: solve the triangular recurrence

We claim that
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ {\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

For \(b=0\), the triangular relation gives
\[
C_0^{(n)}(q)=H_0^{(n)}(q)-1.
\]
But
\[
H_0^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{w\in S_n}q^{{\rm maj}(w)}.
\]
The unique permutation with no descents is \(12\cdots n\), and it contributes \(1\). Hence
\[
C_0^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ {\rm des}(w)\ge1}}q^{{\rm maj}(w)},
\]
which is the desired formula when \(b=0\).

Now let \(b\ge1\). From the triangular relation,
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=H_b^{(n)}(q)-q^{-1}C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
\]
Using the formula for \(H_b^{(n)}(q)\) and the induction hypothesis for \(C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q)\), the contribution of a fixed \(w\), with \(d={\rm des}(w)\) and \(M={\rm maj}(w)\), is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
-
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
this equals
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Thus
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Since
\[
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q=0
\]
unless \({\rm des}(w)-1\ge b\), this is equivalently
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ {\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

### Step 4: interpret the Gaussian binomial coefficient

If \(d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1\), then
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Therefore
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Since
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
the coefficient of \(q^a\) is exactly the number of pairs \((w,\rho)\) satisfying
\[
w\in S_n,\qquad d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,
\]
\[
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

Finally, the exponent is always positive in the nonzero case. Indeed, if \(d\ge b+1\), then the \(d\) descent positions have sum at least \(1+\cdots+d\), so
\[
{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}
\ge1.
\]
Thus the formula produces only powers \(q^a\) with \(a\ge1\), as required for hooks \((a,1^b)\).

When \(b=0\), the rectangle \(0\times(d-1)\) contains only the empty partition, so the formula reduces to
\[
c_{(a)}(n)=\#\{w\in S_n:{\rm des}(w)\ge1,\ {\rm maj}(w)=a\}.
\]
When \(b\ge n-1\), every permutation has \({\rm des}(w)\le n-1\), so the condition \({\rm des}(w)\ge b+1\) is impossible. Hence
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0
\]
for \(b\ge n-1\). This completes the proof.

Let

\[
C_b^{(n)}(q):=\sum_{a\ge 1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a .
\]

From t1/t2, we have the tableau-pair count

\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(\mu,T,U,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
\mu\vdash n,\quad T,U\in \operatorname{SYT}(\mu),\\
\operatorname{des}(T)\ge b+1,\quad
\rho\subseteq b\times(\operatorname{des}(T)-1-b),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\,\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]

## RSK convention

Use ordinary **row-insertion RSK** on the one-line word \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\):

- \(P(w)\) is the insertion tableau;
- \(Q(w)\) is the recording tableau, with entry \(i\) placed in the new cell created when \(w_i\) is inserted.

For tableaux, take \(i\in \operatorname{Des}(T)\) iff \(i+1\) lies in a strictly lower row than \(i\). With this convention,

\[
\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w),
\qquad
\operatorname{Des}(P(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w^{-1}).
\]

Thus in the tableau-pair formula we identify

\[
T=Q(w),\qquad U=P(w).
\]

RSK is a bijection

\[
S_n \longleftrightarrow \bigsqcup_{\mu\vdash n}
\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\times \operatorname{SYT}(\mu),
\]

so the quadruple count becomes the following permutation count.

## Final hook formula

For \(a\ge 1\), \(b\ge 0\),

\[
\boxed{
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\quad d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
}
\]

Equivalently,

\[
\boxed{
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ \operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
{\operatorname{des}(w)-1\brack b}_q .
}
\]

### Special cases

- Empty coefficient:

\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1.
\]

- For \(b=0\), the rectangle has height \(0\), so \(\rho=\varnothing\). Hence

\[
c_{(a)}(n)
=
\#\{w\in S_n:\operatorname{des}(w)\ge 1,\ \operatorname{maj}(w)=a\}.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
C_0^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ w\ne 12\cdots n}}q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)}
=[n]_q!-1.
\]

- If \(b\ge n-1\), then \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\), since \(\operatorname{des}(w)\le n-1\) but the formula requires \(\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1\).

## Decorated-permutation version

Let

\[
\operatorname{Des}(w)=\{i_1<i_2<\cdots<i_d\}.
\]

Instead of choosing \(\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)\), equivalently choose \(b\) marked descents among the first \(d-1\) descent ranks:

\[
J=\{j_1<\cdots<j_b\}\subseteq \{1,\ldots,d-1\}.
\]

Mark the actual descents \(i_{j_1},\ldots,i_{j_b}\). Then

\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,J):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
J\subseteq\{1,\ldots,d-1\},\ |J|=b,\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-\sum_{j\in J}(d+1-j)
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]

This follows from the standard bijection

\[
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)
\quad\longleftrightarrow\quad
J=\{j_1<\cdots<j_b\}\subseteq [d-1],
\]

with

\[
|\rho|=\sum_{s=1}^b j_s-\binom{b+1}{2}.
\]

The rectangle-partition version is slightly cleaner because it keeps the add-on statistic as the manifestly nonnegative quantity \(|\rho|\).

## Checks

For \(n=3\),

For \(n=4\), the descent-major distributions are

\[

\]

Therefore

\[
C_0^{(4)}(q)=3q+5q^2+6q^3+5q^4+3q^5+q^6,
\]

\[
C_1^{(4)}(q)=3q+5q^2+4q^3+q^4,
\]

\[
C_2^{(4)}(q)=q,
\qquad
C_b^{(4)}(q)=0\ \text{for }b\ge 3.
\]

No corrections to t1/t2 are needed. The only convention-sensitive point is that under standard row-insertion RSK the descent-preserving tableau is \(Q(w)\), the recording tableau, not \(P(w)\).

## Bottom line

I found **no indexing error** in t1/t2. The hook formula, the \(b\times(\operatorname{des}(T)-1-b)\) rectangle, the \(q^{-1}C_{b-1}\) term, and the \(b=0\) row-tableau exception are all consistent.

The one real red-team caveat is logical, not combinatorial: the proof is not self-contained from the scalar Hilbert-series statement alone. It must explicitly cite the **Frobenius refinement/superspace universality theorem** and **Fields’ \(GL(1|1)\) formula**. Once those are allowed, the proof is sound.

---

## Correct hook interpretation

For \(a\ge 1\), \(b\ge 0\), and hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), the ordinary Hilbert coefficient is

\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(\mu,T,U,\pi):
\begin{array}{l}
\mu\vdash n,\quad T,U\in \operatorname{SYT}(\mu),\\[2pt]
\operatorname{des}(T)\ge b+1,\\[2pt]
\pi\subseteq b\times(\operatorname{des}(T)-1-b),\\[2pt]
a=\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\,\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2+|\pi|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]

Here \(U\) is the extra standard tableau accounting for the factor \(f^\mu=\#\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\) when passing from refined Frobenius coefficients to ordinary Hilbert coefficients.

Also,

\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1,
\]

and for nonempty hooks,

\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\qquad\text{if }b\ge n-1.
\]

---

## Audit of the requested points

### 1. Refined Frobenius coefficients to ordinary Hilbert coefficients

This step is correct, provided one explicitly introduces the refined Frobenius expansion

\[
\operatorname{Frob}(R_n;\mathbf q;\mathbf x)
=
\sum_{\lambda,\mu} c_{\lambda,\mu}(n)\,
s_\lambda(\mathbf q)s_\mu(\mathbf x).
\]

If the \(\lambda\)-isotypic \(GL\)-multiplicity space decomposes as

\[
M_\lambda \cong \bigoplus_{\mu\vdash n} c_{\lambda,\mu}(n) S^\mu,
\]

then its ordinary dimension is

\[
\dim M_\lambda
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} c_{\lambda,\mu}(n) f^\mu.
\]

Therefore

\[
c_\lambda(n)=\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu c_{\lambda,\mu}(n).
\]

So the \(U\in \operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\) factor in the final quadruple count is exactly right. The hidden requirement is that the refined Frobenius expansion must be available; it is not implied by the bare scalar Hilbert-series expansion alone.

---

### 2. \(GL(1|1)\) super specialization

The specialization is correct under the ordinary, unsigned fermionic Hilbert convention. In one even variable \(q\) and one odd variable \(u\),

\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
\]

and

\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]

All non-hook shapes vanish because only shapes fitting the \((1|1)\)-hook survive. This is exactly the specialization needed to isolate hook coefficients through a triangular system.

Important normalization caveat: if one used a signed supertrace convention, signs would appear. The proof uses the positive Hilbert-series convention, which matches Fields’ formula.

---

### 3. Triangular relation

With

\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)=\sum_{a\ge1} c_{(a,1^b),\mu}(n)q^a,
\qquad
C_{-1,\mu}(q)=0,
\]

the coefficient of \(u^b s_\mu\) in the \(GL(1|1)\) specialization is

\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\delta_{b,0}\delta_{\mu,(n)}
+
C_{b,\mu}(q)
+
q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q).
\]

This is correct:

- \(C_{b,\mu}\) comes from \(q^a u^b\) in \(s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)\);
- \(q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}\) comes from \(q^{a-1}u^b\) in \(s_{(a,1^{b-1})}(q/u)\);
- the \(\delta\)-term is the empty partition coefficient, i.e. the degree-zero trivial representation.

No hook is missing, and no extra hook is included.

---

### 4. \(q\)-Pascal solution and \(b=0\) exception

Fields’ formula gives

\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}
\operatorname{des}(T)\\ b
\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

The proposed solution is

\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ \operatorname{des}(T)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}
\operatorname{des}(T)-1\\ b
\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

Indeed, for one tableau with \(d=\operatorname{des}(T)\), \(M=\operatorname{maj}(T)\),

\[
q^{-1}C_{b-1}\text{ contributes }
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}
d-1\\ b-1
\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

Adding the \(C_b\) contribution gives

\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}
d-1\\ b
\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}
d-1\\ b-1
\end{bmatrix}_q
\right)
=
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}
d\\ b
\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

That is exactly Fields’ summand.

The only missing case is \(b=0,d=0\), where

\[
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\0\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}-1\\0\end{bmatrix}_q
\]

is declared zero. The unique \(d=0\) tableau is the row tableau of shape \((n)\), and it is precisely accounted for by

\[
\delta_{b,0}\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
\]

So the row-tableau exception is correct.

---

## Small-\(n\) checks through \(n=4\)

Define the unrefined hook generating polynomial

\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge1} c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a.
\]

The formula gives:

\[
\begin{array}{c|l}
n & \text{nonzero } C_b^{(n)}(q)\\
\hline
1 & \text{none; only } c_\varnothing(1)=1\\[2pt]
2 & C_0=q\\[2pt]
3 & C_0=2q+2q^2+q^3,\qquad C_1=q\\[2pt]
4 &
\begin{aligned}
C_0&=3q+5q^2+6q^3+5q^4+3q^5+q^6,\\
C_1&=3q+5q^2+4q^3+q^4,\\
C_2&=q.
\end{aligned}
\end{array}
\]

For \(n=4\), the refined data are:

\[
\begin{array}{c|c|c|c|c}
\mu & (M,d)\text{ over }T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)
& C_{0,\mu} & C_{1,\mu} & C_{2,\mu}\\
\hline
(4) & (0,0) & 0 & 0 & 0\\
(3,1) & (1,1),(2,1),(3,1)
& q+q^2+q^3 & 0 & 0\\
(2,2) & (2,1),(4,2)
& q^2+q^4 & q^2 & 0\\
(2,1,1) & (3,2),(4,2),(5,2)
& q^3+q^4+q^5 & q+q^2+q^3 & 0\\
(1^4) & (6,3)
& q^6 & q^3+q^4 & q
\end{array}
\]

Multiplying these rows by

\[
f^{(4)},f^{(3,1)},f^{(2,2)},f^{(2,1,1)},f^{(1^4)}
=
1,3,2,3,1
\]

gives the unrefined \(C_b^{(4)}\) above.

Two sanity checks:

- For \(m=1\), only row Schur functions survive, so

  \[
  1+C_0^{(4)}(q)
  =
  1+3q+5q^2+6q^3+5q^4+3q^5+q^6
  =
  [4]_q!,
  \]

  as required for the ordinary coinvariant algebra.

- For \(m=2\), evaluating at \(q_1=q_2=1\) gives dimension

  \[
  1+\sum_a c_{(a)}(4)(a+1)+\sum_a c_{(a,1)}(4)a
  =
  1+95+29
  =
  125,
  \]

  matching the known \(n=4\) diagonal coinvariant dimension.

The vertical shape also confirms the top-degree off-by-one behavior: for \(\mu=(1^4)\),

\[
C_{2,\mu}=q,\qquad C_{3,\mu}=0,
\]

but Fields’ top exterior-degree term satisfies

\[
H_{3,\mu}=1=q^{-1}C_{2,\mu}.
\]

Thus \(H_b\) is not \(C_b\); the triangular subtraction is essential.

---

## Theorem-ready proof outline

1. Introduce the refined Frobenius expansion

   \[
   \operatorname{Frob}(R_n;\mathbf q;\mathbf x)
   =
   \sum_{\lambda,\mu} c_{\lambda,\mu}(n)
   s_\lambda(\mathbf q)s_\mu(\mathbf x).
   \]

   Then

   \[
   c_\lambda(n)=\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu c_{\lambda,\mu}(n).
   \]

2. Use superspace universality to specialize the same coefficients to one bosonic and one fermionic variable:

   \[
   \operatorname{Frob}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u;\mathbf x)
   =
   \sum_{\lambda,\mu}
   c_{\lambda,\mu}(n)s_\lambda(q/u)s_\mu(\mathbf x).
   \]

3. Since

   \[
   s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1},
   \]

   comparing coefficients of \(u^b s_\mu\) gives

   \[
   H_{b,\mu}(q)
   =
   \delta_{b,0}\delta_{\mu,(n)}
   +
   C_{b,\mu}(q)
   +
   q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q).
   \]

4. Insert Fields’ formula for \(H_{b,\mu}\) and solve the recurrence using

   \[
   \begin{bmatrix} d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
   =
   \begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
   +
   q^{d-b}
   \begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q.
   \]

   This yields

   \[
   C_{b,\mu}(q)
   =
   \sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ d(T)\ge b+1}}
   q^{M(T)-b d(T)+\binom b2}
   \begin{bmatrix}
   d(T)-1\\ b
   \end{bmatrix}_q.
   \]

5. Interpret

   \[
   \begin{bmatrix}
   d(T)-1\\ b
   \end{bmatrix}_q
   =
   \sum_{\pi\subseteq b\times(d(T)-1-b)} q^{|\pi|}.
   \]

   Taking coefficients of \(q^a\) gives the refined pair count \((T,\pi)\). Multiplying by \(f^\mu\), realized by an independent \(U\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\), gives the ordinary Hilbert coefficient count.

So the proof is theorem-ready after explicitly naming the Frobenius/superspace universality and Fields formula inputs.

## Original Problem

Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

## Problem Solved

For every integer \(n\ge 1\), let \(c_\lambda(n)\) denote the universal Schur coefficient in the multigraded Hilbert-series expansion of \(R_n^{(m)}\). If \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\) is a hook partition, where \(a\ge 1\) and \(b\ge 0\), then
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Here \(S_n\) is the symmetric group, a descent of \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\) is an index \(i\in\{1,\dots,n-1\}\) with \(w_i>w_{i+1}\),
\[
\operatorname{des}(w)=|\{i:w_i>w_{i+1}\}|,\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{w_i>w_{i+1}} i.
\]
A partition \(\rho\subseteq b\times r\) means that \(\rho\) has at most \(b\) parts and every part is at most \(r\); the empty partition is allowed, and \(|\rho|\) denotes the sum of its parts. For a standard Young tableau \(T\), a descent is an index \(i\) such that \(i+1\) appears in a strictly lower row than \(i\), and \(\operatorname{des}(T)\), \(\operatorname{maj}(T)\) are defined analogously.

The empty coefficient is
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]
Consequently,
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\qquad\text{whenever }b\ge n-1.
\]

Gaussian binomial coefficients are used with the convention
\[
\biggl[{r\atop s}\biggr]_q
=
\begin{cases}
\displaystyle\prod_{i=1}^{s}\frac{1-q^{r-s+i}}{1-q^i},&0\le s\le r,\\[1em]
0,&r<0,\ s<0,\text{ or }s>r.
\end{cases}
\]
Equivalently, for \(0\le s\le r\),
\[
\biggl[{r\atop s}\biggr]_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq s\times(r-s)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]

This proves the original hook-coefficient problem as stated; it is not a relaxation.

## Proof

Fix \(n\ge 1\). For a partition \(\mu\vdash n\), let \(\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\) be the set of standard Young tableaux of shape \(\mu\), and let \(f^\mu=|\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)|\).

We use the following standard inputs.

1. There are refined universal Frobenius coefficients \(c_{\lambda,\mu}\) such that, after passing to the one-bosonic-one-fermionic specialization,
   \[
   \operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)
   =
   \sum_{\lambda,\,\mu\vdash n}
   c_{\lambda,\mu}\,s_\lambda(q/u)\,s_\mu.
   \]
   The original Hilbert coefficient is obtained by forgetting the \(S_n\)-module structure:
   \[
   c_\lambda(n)=\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu c_{\lambda,\mu}.
   \]
   Also, the degree-zero piece is the constant polynomial carrying the trivial representation, so
   \[
   c_{\varnothing,\mu}=\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
   \]

2. In one bosonic variable \(q\) and one fermionic variable \(u\),
   \[
   s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
   \]
   and for hooks,
   \[
   s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
   \]
   All nonempty non-hook Schur functions vanish under this specialization.

3. Fields’ formula gives
   \[
   \operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)
   =
   \sum_T
   q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(T)+1}{2}}
   \prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(T)}(q^i+u)\,
   s_{\operatorname{shape}(T)},
   \]
   where the sum is over all standard Young tableaux \(T\) of size \(n\).

### 1. Extracting \(u^b s_\mu\) from Fields’ formula

Let \(H_{b,\mu}(q)\) be the coefficient of \(u^b s_\mu\) in \(\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)\).

Fix \(T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\), and write
\[
d=\operatorname{des}(T),\qquad M=\operatorname{maj}(T).
\]
The coefficient of \(u^b\) in
\[
\prod_{i=1}^{d}(q^i+u)
\]
is obtained by choosing \(b\) factors contributing \(u\), or equivalently \(d-b\) factors contributing \(q^i\). Thus, for \(0\le b\le d\),
\[
[u^b]\prod_{i=1}^{d}(q^i+u)
=
q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}
\biggl[{d\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]
Multiplying by Fields’ prefactor gives
\[
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}}
q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}
\biggl[{d\atop b}\biggr]_q
=
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\biggl[{d\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]
Therefore
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
\biggl[{\operatorname{des}(T)\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]

### 2. The triangular relation for hook coefficients

For \(b\ge 0\), define
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1} c_{(a,1^b),\mu}q^a,
\qquad
C_{-1,\mu}(q)=0.
\]
Compare the coefficient of \(u^b s_\mu\) in
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{\lambda,\mu}
c_{\lambda,\mu}s_\lambda(q/u)s_\mu.
\]
The empty partition contributes only when \(b=0\), giving
\[
\delta_{b,0}\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
\]
A hook \((a,1^b)\) contributes \(q^a u^b\), giving \(C_{b,\mu}(q)\). A hook \((a,1^{b-1})\) contributes \(q^{a-1}u^b\), giving \(q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q)\). Hence
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\delta_{b,0}\delta_{\mu,(n)}
+
C_{b,\mu}(q)
+
q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q).
\]

### 3. Solving the recurrence

We claim that
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ \operatorname{des}(T)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
\biggl[{\operatorname{des}(T)-1\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]

For \(b=0\), the triangular relation gives
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)=H_{0,\mu}(q)-\delta_{\mu,(n)}
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)}
-\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
\]
A standard tableau has no descents iff it is the unique row tableau of shape \((n)\). Therefore
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ \operatorname{des}(T)\ge 1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)},
\]
which is the claimed formula for \(b=0\), since
\[
\biggl[{\operatorname{des}(T)-1\atop 0}\biggr]_q=1
\]
when \(\operatorname{des}(T)\ge 1\).

Now assume \(b\ge 1\) and the formula is known for \(b-1\). From the triangular relation,
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)=H_{b,\mu}(q)-q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q).
\]
For a fixed tableau \(T\), with \(d=\operatorname{des}(T)\) and \(M=\operatorname{maj}(T)\), the contribution from \(q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q)\) is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
q^{d-b}
\biggl[{d-1\atop b-1}\biggr]_q.
\]
Thus
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_T
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\biggl[{d\atop b}\biggr]_q
-
q^{d-b}
\biggl[{d-1\atop b-1}\biggr]_q
\right).
\]
Using the \(q\)-Pascal identity
\[
\biggl[{d\atop b}\biggr]_q
=
\biggl[{d-1\atop b}\biggr]_q
+
q^{d-b}
\biggl[{d-1\atop b-1}\biggr]_q,
\]
we get
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_T
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\biggl[{d-1\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]
By the Gaussian convention, this summand is zero unless \(d-1\ge b\), i.e. unless \(d\ge b+1\). Hence
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ d\ge b+1}}
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\biggl[{d-1\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]

### 4. Translating the Gaussian binomial

For \(d\ge b+1\),
\[
\biggl[{d-1\atop b}\biggr]_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Therefore the coefficient of \(q^a\) in \(C_{b,\mu}(q)\) counts pairs \((T,\rho)\) such that
\[
T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu),\qquad d=\operatorname{des}(T)\ge b+1,
\]
\[
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(T)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

The exponent is always positive in this situation. Indeed, if \(d\ge b+1\), then the \(d\) descent positions have sum at least \(1+2+\cdots+d\), so
\[
\operatorname{maj}(T)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}
\ge 1.
\]
Thus the formula produces only hook arms \(a\ge 1\), as required.

### 5. Passing from tableaux to permutations by RSK

Now use
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu c_{(a,1^b),\mu}.
\]
Multiplying by \(f^\mu\) means choosing an additional standard tableau \(U\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\). Hence \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\) counts quadruples
\[
(\mu,T,U,\rho)
\]
such that
\[
\mu\vdash n,\qquad T,U\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu),
\]
\[
d=\operatorname{des}(T)\ge b+1,\qquad
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(T)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

By the Robinson-Schensted-Knuth correspondence, permutations \(w\in S_n\) are in bijection with pairs \((P(w),Q(w))\) of standard Young tableaux of the same shape. Moreover,
\[
\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w),
\]
and therefore
\[
\operatorname{des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{des}(w),\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(Q(w))=\operatorname{maj}(w).
\]
Identify \(U=P(w)\) and \(T=Q(w)\). Then the above quadruples are in bijection with pairs \((w,\rho)\) satisfying
\[
w\in S_n,\qquad d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,
\]
\[
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]
This proves the claimed permutation interpretation.

### 6. Edge cases

For the empty partition,
\[
c_\varnothing(n)
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu c_{\varnothing,\mu}
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu \delta_{\mu,(n)}
=
f^{(n)}
=
1.
\]

When \(b=0\), the rectangle is \(0\times(d-1)\), so \(\rho\) must be empty. The formula becomes
\[
c_{(a)}(n)
=
\#\{w\in S_n:\operatorname{des}(w)\ge 1,\ \operatorname{maj}(w)=a\}.
\]
Since \(a\ge 1\), the condition \(\operatorname{maj}(w)=a\) already forces at least one descent.

Finally, every permutation in \(S_n\) has at most \(n-1\) descents. If \(b\ge n-1\), then the condition
\[
\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1
\]
is impossible. Hence
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0
\qquad\text{for all }b\ge n-1.
\]

## Verdict

I find **no indexing or combinatorial error** in the claimed hook formula. The theorem is correct **provided** the proof explicitly cites the two substantial external inputs:

1. **Diagonal supersymmetry / universal super-Schur expansion**: the same universal coefficients occur after passing from \(m\) bosonic variable sets to the \((1|1)\) boson–fermion specialization. Lentfer’s diagonal supersymmetry theorem gives this universal super-Schur expansion. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.14885))  
2. **The Fields \(SR_n\) Frobenius formula**, now proved by Murai–Rhoades–Wilson: it gives the bigraded \(S_n\)-isomorphism type of the superspace coinvariant ring \(SR_n\), proving the Fields conjectures. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.24027))  

Without these inputs, the proof is not self-contained. In particular, older Hilbert-series-only results were insufficient: Rhoades–Wilson proved the bigraded Hilbert series but stated at that time that the \(S_n\)-module structure remained open. ([cambridge.org](https://www.cambridge.org/core/product/D7F1C91BE28068FC384AFE85B6AED8B8))

---

## Checks of the requested points

### 1. \(GL(1|1)\) character formula

Using the positive supersymmetric Schur convention
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)\,s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u),
\]
one has
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
Indeed, in one even variable only row shapes \(\nu=(r)\) survive, and in one odd variable the skew shape \(\lambda'/\nu'\) must be a horizontal strip. For \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), only \(\nu=(a)\) and \(\nu=(a-1)\) contribute. Non-hook shapes vanish. This is correct under the ordinary unsigned Hilbert convention; a signed supertrace convention would change signs.

### 2. Triangular relation

Refined by \(S_n\)-type, define
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b),\mu}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1,\mu}=0.
\]
Comparing coefficients of \(u^b s_\mu\) in the \((1|1)\) super-Schur expansion gives
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\delta_{b,0}\delta_{\mu,(n)}
+
C_{b,\mu}(q)
+
q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q).
\]
After forgetting \(S_n\)-structure, this becomes
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
\]
So the triangular relation is correct; the \(\delta\)-term is exactly the empty partition / degree-zero trivial representation.

### 3. Fields formula and \(q\)-Pascal step

The Fields formula gives
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Solving
\[
C_{b,\mu}=H_{b,\mu}-q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}
\]
uses
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
For a tableau with \(d=\operatorname{des}(T)\), the \(q^{-1}C_{b-1}\) contribution is exactly
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
so subtraction leaves
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Thus
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ \operatorname{des}(T)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

### 4. \(b=0,\ d=0\) exception

For \(b=0\),
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)=H_{0,\mu}(q)-\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
\]
The only standard tableau with no descents is the row tableau of shape \((n)\), so subtracting \(\delta_{\mu,(n)}\) removes exactly that case. Hence the formula correctly becomes
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ \operatorname{des}(T)\ge1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)}.
\]
No off-by-one error occurs.

### 5. Gaussian binomial to rectangles

Since
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|},
\]
the coefficient of \(q^a\) counts \((T,\rho)\) with
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(T)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

### 6. RSK convention

With ordinary row-insertion RSK,
\[
w\longleftrightarrow (P(w),Q(w)),
\]
and the standard descent convention “\(i\) is a descent if \(i+1\) lies in a lower row than \(i\),” one has
\[
\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w),\qquad
\operatorname{Des}(P(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w^{-1}).
\]
Thus the tableau \(T\) carrying the descent and major-index statistics must be identified with \(Q(w)\), not \(P(w)\). The claimed permutation formula uses the correct convention.

---

## Final validated statement

For \(n\ge1\), \(a\ge1\), and \(b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Also
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1.
\]
Consequently \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\) for \(b\ge n-1\). No smallest counterexample exists under the cited external theorems.

# Problem We Are Solving
Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

    Proof sketch: Choose distinct rationals α_1,...,α_k and let Y_{n,k} ⊂ Q^n be the points whose coordinate set is exactly {α_1,...,α_k}. The point (y_1,...,y_n) corresponds equivariantly to the ordered set partition whose i-th block is {j : y_j=α_i}. Let I(Y) be the vanishing ideal and T(Y) the ideal generated by top homogeneous parts of elements of I(Y). Then Q[x_n]/T(Y) has dimension |Y|=|OP_{n,k}|. Show I_{n,k} ⊂ T(Y): the top part of ∏_{r=1}^k (x_i-α_r) gives x_i^k, and Lemma 3.1 gives, for n-k+1 ≤ r ≤ n, a vanishing linear combination ∑_{j=0}^r (-1)^j e_{r-j}(x_n) h_j(α_1,...,α_k), whose top part is e_r(x_n). To bound dim R_{n,k} from above, use lexicographic Gröbner theory. For S={s_1<...<s_m} define the skip monomial x(S)=x_{s_1}^{s_1} x_{s_2}^{s_2-1} ... x_{s_m}^{s_m-m+1}. Lemmas 3.4 and 3.5 show that for every |S|=n-k+1 a reverse Demazure character in I_{n,k} has leading monomial x(S); also x_i^k ∈ in(I_{n,k}). Hence every standard monomial is in M_{n,k}, the set of monomials divisible by no x_i^k and no such x(S). Theorem 4.9 constructs a degree-preserving bijection Ψ: OP_{n,k} → M_{n,k}: recursively remove n; if n is inserted into an existing block in position i+1, multiply by x_n^i; if n is inserted as a singleton block in position i+1, use Lemma 4.7 to choose the unique size n-k set S for which x(S) divides m(S) times the previous monomial, and multiply by m(S)x_n^i. Define q-Stirling numbers Stir_q(n,k) by Stir_q(0,0)=1, Stir_q(0,k)=0 for k≠0, and Stir_q(n,k) = [k]_q Stir_q(n-1,k) + Stir_q(n-1,k-1). Equivalently, the coefficient of z^r on the left is the q-degree generating function of all pairs consisting of a subset J⊆[n] of size r and a monomial x_1^{a_1}...x_n^{a_n} satisfying 0 ≤ a_i < st(J)_i for every i.     Proof sketch: Let G(n,k) be the coefficient of z^{n-k} on the left, i.e. the sum over subsets J⊆[n] with exactly k elements outside J. The formula st(J)_i=|[i]\J| makes the recurrence transparent. Split such J according to whether n∈J. If n∈J, then J'=J\{n} has k elements outside it in [n-1], the first n-1 staircase entries are those of J', and the final entry is k; this contributes [k]_q G(n-1,k). If n∉J, then J⊆[n-1] has k-1 elements outside it in [n-1], and adjoining the nth entry again multiplies by [k]_q; this contributes [k]_q G(n-1,k-1). Thus G(n,k)=[k]_q G(n-1,k)+[k]_q G(n-1,k-1), with G(1,1)=1 and the usual boundary values. The quantity H(n,k):=[k]!_q Stir_q(n,k) satisfies the same recurrence, since multiplying Stir_q(n,k)=[k]_q Stir_q(n-1,k)+Stir_q(n-1,k-1) by [k]!_q gives H(n,k)=[k]_q H(n-1,k)+[k]_q H(n-1,k-1). The coinvariant algebra C[x_n]/I_n is also Poincare duality with socle degree binom(n,2), f_J∉I_n when 1∉J, and deg f_J=Σ_i(i-st(J)_i), so the socle degrees differ by deg f_J. Since u is a single variable, the skew Schur function s_{λ′/ν′}(u) is nonzero only when the skew shape λ′/ν′ is a horizontal strip, in which case it is the corresponding power of u. For λ=(a,1^b), so λ′=(b+1,1^(a−1)), the only row partitions ν=(r) for which λ′/ν′ is a horizontal strip are r=a and r=a−1. These give the two terms q^a u^b and q^(a−1)u^(b+1), respectively; this also covers a=1, where the r=0 contribution is allowed. The empty partition gives 1. If λ_2≥2, then λ′ has at least two cells in its second column, and removing ν′=(1^r) from the first column cannot make λ′/ν′ a horizontal strip, so every summand vanishes. ## Theorem-ready Hilbert-level proof

Write

\[
d(w)=\operatorname{des}(w),\qquad M(w)=\operatorname{maj}(w),
\]

and define

\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1} c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0.
\]

Let

\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]

From the assumed \(GL(1|1)\) hook specialization,

\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
1+\sum_{a\ge1,b\ge0} c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
\left(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\right),
\]

so comparing coefficients of \(u^b\) gives the triangular relation

\[
\boxed{
H_b^{(n)}(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b^{(n)}(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
}
\tag{1}
\]

Now use the Fields/Rhoades-Wilson product formula

\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{M(w)-\binom{d(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{d(w)}(q^i+u).
\]

For fixed \(w\), the coefficient of \(u^b\) is

\[
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}}
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq [d]\\ |J|=b}}
q^{\sum_{i\notin J} i}
=
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq [d]\\ |J|=b}}
q^{M-\sum_{j\in J}j}.
\]

Using

\[
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq[d]\\ |J|=b}}q^{-\sum_{j\in J}j}
=
q^{-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix} d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]

we get

\[
\boxed{
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d(w)\ge b}}
q^{M(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix} d(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
}
\tag{2}
\]

Now define

\[
D_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{M(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix} d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

I claim \(C_b^{(n)}(q)=D_b^{(n)}(q)\). Indeed, for fixed \(w\), the contribution of \(D_b+q^{-1}D_{b-1}\) is

\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right),
\]

because

\[
q^{-1}q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}
=
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}q^{d-b}.
\]

By the \(q\)-Pascal identity,

\[
\begin{bmatrix} d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]

this equals the \(w\)-summand in \(H_b\). The only exceptional case is \(b=0,d=0\), i.e. the identity permutation, whose contribution is exactly the \(\delta_{b,0}\) term in (1). Therefore \(D_b\) satisfies the same triangular recurrence as \(C_b\), so

\[
\boxed{
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{M(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix} d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
}
\tag{3}
\]

Since

\[
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)} q^{|\rho|},
\]

we obtain the desired coefficient interpretation:

\[
\boxed{
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\quad d(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b),\\
a=M(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
}
\]

Also \(c_{\varnothing}(n)=1\). The exponent is always positive: if \(d\ge b+1\), then \(M\ge 1+\cdots+d\), so

\[
M-bd+\binom b2\ge \binom{d-b+1}{2}\ge 1.
\]

## Check: \(n=3\)

The descent-major data are:

- \(d=0,M=0\): one permutation;
- \(d=1,M=1\): two permutations;
- \(d=1,M=2\): two permutations;
- \(d=2,M=3\): one permutation.

Thus

\[
H_0=1+2q+2q^2+q^3,
\]

\[
H_1=2+3q+q^2,
\]

\[
H_2=1.
\]

The recurrence gives

\[
C_0=H_0-1=2q+2q^2+q^3,
\]

\[
C_1=H_1-q^{-1}C_0=q,
\]

\[
C_2=H_2-q^{-1}C_1=0.
\]

This matches the rectangle formula: for \(b=1\), only \(321\) has \(d\ge2\), and its contribution is

\[
q^{3-1\cdot2}=q.
\]

For \(n=4\), the same formula gives

\[
C_0=3q+5q^2+6q^3+5q^4+3q^5+q^6,
\]

\[
C_1=3q+5q^2+4q^3+q^4,
\]

\[
C_2=q,
\]

again confirming the exponent \(M-bd+\binom b2\) and the off-by-one rectangle \(b\times(d-1-b)\).

## Sufficiency and hidden assumptions

This is a fully Hilbert-level proof: no refined Frobenius coefficients \(c_{\lambda,\mu}\) are needed.

However, it is sufficient for the original hook-coefficient problem only after accepting the hidden universality input that the \(GL(1|1)\) superspace Hilbert expansion uses the same universal coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) as the ordinary multigraded Hilbert expansion. One also needs the positive, unsigned fermionic Hilbert convention, so that hooks specialize as

\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]

With those assumptions, the proof above directly recovers the original hook coefficients.

Lentfer’s diagonal supersymmetry theorem gives this universal super-Schur expansion. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.14885))  
2. **The Fields \(SR_n\) Frobenius formula**, now proved by Murai–Rhoades–Wilson: it gives the bigraded \(S_n\)-isomorphism type of the superspace coinvariant ring \(SR_n\), proving the Fields conjectures. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.24027))  

Without these inputs, the proof is not self-contained. In particular, older Hilbert-series-only results were insufficient: Rhoades–Wilson proved the bigraded Hilbert series but stated at that time that the \(S_n\)-module structure remained open. ## Checks of the requested points

### 1. \(GL(1|1)\) character formula

Using the positive supersymmetric Schur convention
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)\,s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u),
\]
one has
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
Indeed, in one even variable only row shapes \(\nu=(r)\) survive, and in one odd variable the skew shape \(\lambda'/\nu'\) must be a horizontal strip. For \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), only \(\nu=(a)\) and \(\nu=(a-1)\) contribute. Non-hook shapes vanish. This is correct under the ordinary unsigned Hilbert convention; a signed supertrace convention would change signs.

### 2. Triangular relation

Refined by \(S_n\)-type, define
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b),\mu}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1,\mu}=0.
\]
Comparing coefficients of \(u^b s_\mu\) in the \((1|1)\) super-Schur expansion gives
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\delta_{b,0}\delta_{\mu,(n)}
+
C_{b,\mu}(q)
+
q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q).
\]
After forgetting \(S_n\)-structure, this becomes
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
\]
So the triangular relation is correct; the \(\delta\)-term is exactly the empty partition / degree-zero trivial representation.

### 3. Fields formula and \(q\)-Pascal step

The Fields formula gives
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Solving
\[
C_{b,\mu}=H_{b,\mu}-q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}
\]
uses
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
For a tableau with \(d=\operatorname{des}(T)\), the \(q^{-1}C_{b-1}\) contribution is exactly
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
so subtraction leaves
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Thus
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ \operatorname{des}(T)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

### 4. \(b=0,\ d=0\) exception

For \(b=0\),
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)=H_{0,\mu}(q)-\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
\]
The only standard tableau with no descents is the row tableau of shape \((n)\), so subtracting \(\delta_{\mu,(n)}\) removes exactly that case. Hence the formula correctly becomes
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ \operatorname{des}(T)\ge1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)}.
\]
No off-by-one error occurs.

### 5. Gaussian binomial to rectangles

Since
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|},
\]
the coefficient of \(q^a\) counts \((T,\rho)\) with
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(T)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

### 6. RSK convention

With ordinary row-insertion RSK,
\[
w\longleftrightarrow (P(w),Q(w)),
\]
and the standard descent convention “\(i\) is a descent if \(i+1\) lies in a lower row than \(i\),” one has
\[
\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w),\qquad
\operatorname{Des}(P(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w^{-1}).
\]
Thus the tableau \(T\) carrying the descent and major-index statistics must be identified with \(Q(w)\), not \(P(w)\). The claimed permutation formula uses the correct convention.

---

## Final validated statement

For \(n\ge1\), \(a\ge1\), and \(b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Also
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1.
\]
Consequently \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\) for \(b\ge n-1\). No smallest counterexample exists under the cited external theorems.

I find **no indexing or combinatorial error** in the claimed hook formula. The theorem is correct **provided** the proof explicitly cites the two substantial external inputs:

## Checks of the requested points

### 1. \(GL(1|1)\) character formula

Using the positive supersymmetric Schur convention
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)\,s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u),
\]
one has
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
Indeed, in one even variable only row shapes \(\nu=(r)\) survive, and in one odd variable the skew shape \(\lambda'/\nu'\) must be a horizontal strip. For \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), only \(\nu=(a)\) and \(\nu=(a-1)\) contribute. Non-hook shapes vanish. This is correct under the ordinary unsigned Hilbert convention; a signed supertrace convention would change signs.

### 2. Triangular relation

Refined by \(S_n\)-type, define
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b),\mu}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1,\mu}=0.
\]
Comparing coefficients of \(u^b s_\mu\) in the \((1|1)\) super-Schur expansion gives
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\delta_{b,0}\delta_{\mu,(n)}
+
C_{b,\mu}(q)
+
q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q).
\]
After forgetting \(S_n\)-structure, this becomes
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
\]
So the triangular relation is correct; the \(\delta\)-term is exactly the empty partition / degree-zero trivial representation.

### 3. Fields formula and \(q\)-Pascal step

The Fields formula gives
\[

=
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}

\]

\[

\]

\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
For a tableau with \(d=\operatorname{des}(T)\), the \(q^{-1}C_{b-1}\) contribution is exactly
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
so subtraction leaves
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Thus
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ \operatorname{des}(T)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

### 4. \(b=0,\ d=0\) exception

For \(b=0\),
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)=H_{0,\mu}(q)-\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
\]
The only standard tableau with no descents is the row tableau of shape \((n)\), so subtracting \(\delta_{\mu,(n)}\) removes exactly that case. Hence the formula correctly becomes
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ \operatorname{des}(T)\ge1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)}.
\]
No off-by-one error occurs.

### 5. Gaussian binomial to rectangles

Since
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|},
\]
the coefficient of \(q^a\) counts \((T,\rho)\) with
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(T)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

### 6. RSK convention

With ordinary row-insertion RSK,
\[
w\longleftrightarrow (P(w),Q(w)),
\]
and the standard descent convention “\(i\) is a descent if \(i+1\) lies in a lower row than \(i\),” one has

\operatorname{Des}(P(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w^{-1}).
\]
Thus the tableau \(T\) carrying the descent and major-index statistics must be identified with \(Q(w)\), not \(P(w)\). The claimed permutation formula uses the correct convention.

## Theorem-ready Hilbert-level proof

Write

\[
d(w)=\operatorname{des}(w),\qquad M(w)=\operatorname{maj}(w),
\]

and define

\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1} c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0.
\]

Let

\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]

From the assumed \(GL(1|1)\) hook specialization,

\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
1+\sum_{a\ge1,b\ge0} c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
\left(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\right),
\]

so comparing coefficients of \(u^b\) gives the triangular relation

\[
\boxed{
H_b^{(n)}(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b^{(n)}(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
}
\tag{1}
\]

Now use the Fields/Rhoades-Wilson product formula

\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{M(w)-\binom{d(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{d(w)}(q^i+u).
\]

For fixed \(w\), the coefficient of \(u^b\) is

\[
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}}
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq [d]\\ |J|=b}}
q^{\sum_{i\notin J} i}
=
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq [d]\\ |J|=b}}
q^{M-\sum_{j\in J}j}.
\]

Using

\[
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq[d]\\ |J|=b}}q^{-\sum_{j\in J}j}
=
q^{-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix} d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]

we get

\[
\boxed{
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d(w)\ge b}}
q^{M(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix} d(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
}
\tag{2}
\]

Now define

\[
D_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{M(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix} d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

I claim \(C_b^{(n)}(q)=D_b^{(n)}(q)\). Indeed, for fixed \(w\), the contribution of \(D_b+q^{-1}D_{b-1}\) is

\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right),
\]

because

\[
q^{-1}q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}
=
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}q^{d-b}.
\]

By the \(q\)-Pascal identity,

\[
\begin{bmatrix} d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]

this equals the \(w\)-summand in \(H_b\). The only exceptional case is \(b=0,d=0\), i.e. the identity permutation, whose contribution is exactly the \(\delta_{b,0}\) term in (1). Therefore \(D_b\) satisfies the same triangular recurrence as \(C_b\), so

\[
\boxed{
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{M(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix} d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
}
\tag{3}
\]

Since

\[
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)} q^{|\rho|},
\]

we obtain the desired coefficient interpretation:

\[
\boxed{
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\quad d(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b),\\
a=M(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
}
\]

Also \(c_{\varnothing}(n)=1\). The exponent is always positive: if \(d\ge b+1\), then \(M\ge 1+\cdots+d\), so

\[
M-bd+\binom b2\ge \binom{d-b+1}{2}\ge 1.
\]

## Check: \(n=3\)

The descent-major data are:

- \(d=0,M=0\): one permutation;
- \(d=1,M=1\): two permutations;
- \(d=1,M=2\): two permutations;
- \(d=2,M=3\): one permutation.

Thus

\[
H_0=1+2q+2q^2+q^3,
\]

\[
H_1=2+3q+q^2,
\]

\[
H_2=1.
\]

The recurrence gives

\[
C_0=H_0-1=2q+2q^2+q^3,
\]

\[
C_1=H_1-q^{-1}C_0=q,
\]

\[
C_2=H_2-q^{-1}C_1=0.
\]

This matches the rectangle formula: for \(b=1\), only \(321\) has \(d\ge2\), and its contribution is

\[
q^{3-1\cdot2}=q.
\]

For \(n=4\), the same formula gives

\[
C_0=3q+5q^2+6q^3+5q^4+3q^5+q^6,
\]

\[
C_1=3q+5q^2+4q^3+q^4,
\]

\[
C_2=q,
\]

again confirming the exponent \(M-bd+\binom b2\) and the off-by-one rectangle \(b\times(d-1-b)\).

## Sufficiency and hidden assumptions

This is a fully Hilbert-level proof: no refined Frobenius coefficients \(c_{\lambda,\mu}\) are needed.

However, it is sufficient for the original hook-coefficient problem only after accepting the hidden universality input that the \(GL(1|1)\) superspace Hilbert expansion uses the same universal coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) as the ordinary multigraded Hilbert expansion. One also needs the positive, unsigned fermionic Hilbert convention, so that hooks specialize as

\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]

With those assumptions, the proof above directly recovers the original hook coefficients.

response:
## Audit verdict

I found **no normalization discrepancy** in the hook-coefficient proof, provided the final writeup explicitly uses the **ordinary unsigned Hilbert-series convention** for fermions and either proves or cites the permutation product form. The only source-accuracy caveat is that the product formula is best presented as a **derived equivalent form** of the Fields/Rhoades–Wilson/Murai–Rhoades–Wilson formulas, not as a formula visibly stated in exactly that form.

---

## Theorem-ready external inputs

### 1. Universal super-Schur Hilbert expansion

Use Lentfer’s bosonic–fermionic coinvariant ring
\[
R_{n}^{(k,j)}
=
\mathbb C[x^{(1)},\dots,x^{(k)},\theta^{(1)},\dots,\theta^{(j)}]\Big/
\left\langle
\mathbb C[x^{(1)},\dots,x^{(k)},\theta^{(1)},\dots,\theta^{(j)}]^{\mathfrak S_n}_{+}
\right\rangle,
\]
where the \(x\)’s commute, the \(\theta\)’s anticommute, and \(\mathfrak S_n\) acts diagonally by permuting indices. Lentfer denotes the symmetric-group case by \(R_n^{(k,j)}\), so the ordinary \(m\)-set diagonal coinvariant algebra is \(R_n^{(m,0)}\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

The exact universality input is:

\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\lambda\in P(k,j,n)}
c_\lambda(n)\,s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u),
\qquad
P(k,j,n)=\{\lambda:\ell(\lambda)\le n,\ \lambda_{k+1}\le j\},
\]
with **nonnegative integers \(c_\lambda(n)\) independent of \((k,j)\)**. For \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), so these are exactly the ordinary Schur-Hilbert coefficients in the user’s \(m\)-boson expansion. For \(k=j=1\), the same \(c_\lambda(n)\) occur in the one-boson/one-fermion Hilbert series. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

**Caveat.** In the \((1,1)\) specialization, only \(\lambda_2\le1\) can contribute, i.e. \(\lambda=\varnothing\) or a hook \((a,1^b)\). Thus this specialization is sufficient for hook coefficients but does not determine nonhook coefficients.

---

### 2. Super-Schur normalization and signs

Use Lentfer’s positive super-Schur convention:
\[
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}
s_\nu(\mathbf q)\,s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(\mathbf u).
\]
This is also written plethystically as \(s_\lambda[\mathbf q-\epsilon\mathbf u]\), and **it is not the signed supertrace convention**. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

For one even variable \(q\) and one odd Hilbert variable \(u\),
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
\]
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\qquad(a\ge1,\ b\ge0),
\]
and
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=0\quad\text{if }\lambda\text{ is not contained in a }(1,1)\text{-hook}.
\]
This is exactly Lentfer’s Lemma 4.4. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

So, with
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u),
\]
the required triangular relation is
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
\]

**Sign caveat.** If someone uses a signed supertrace variable, then \(u\) is replaced by \(-u\), and the hook specialization would acquire alternating signs. That convention would break the proof. For example, at \(n=2\), the unsigned Hilbert series is \(1+q+u\), whereas a signed product would give \(1+q-u\).

---

### 3. Matching \(R_n^{(1,1)}\) with the superspace coinvariant ring \(SR_n\)

Murai–Rhoades–Wilson define
\[
\Omega_n=\mathbb F[x_1,\dots,x_n]\otimes \wedge\{\theta_1,\dots,\theta_n\},
\]
with diagonal action
\[
w\cdot x_i=x_{w(i)},\qquad w\cdot\theta_i=\theta_{w(i)}.
\]
They define
\[
SI_n=\big((\Omega_n)^{\mathfrak S_n}_{+}\big)
\]
and
\[
SR_n=\Omega_n/SI_n.
\]
They also record Solomon’s generating-set normalization
\[
SI_n=(e_1,\dots,e_n,de_1,\dots,de_n).
\]
Thus \(SR_n\) is exactly Lentfer’s \(R_n^{(1,1)}\), after identifying \(x_i=x_i^{(1)}\) and \(\theta_i=\theta_i^{(1)}\). Their variable \(z\) for fermionic degree should be renamed \(u\) in the hook proof. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

---

### 4. Superspace Hilbert/product formula

A source-clean input is Murai–Rhoades–Wilson’s Fields Frobenius formula:
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,z)
=
\sum_{k=1}^n C_{n,k}(\mathbf x;q)\,z^{n-k},
\]
where \(q\) tracks bosonic degree and \(z\) tracks fermionic degree. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

Their tableau expansion gives
\[
C_{n,k}(\mathbf x;q)
=
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(n)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)+\binom{n-k}{2}-(n-k)\operatorname{des}(T)}
\begin{bmatrix}
\operatorname{des}(T)\\ n-k
\end{bmatrix}_q
s_{\lambda(T)}(\mathbf x).
\]
Setting \(b=n-k\), pairing the Frobenius characteristic with \(h_{(1^n)}=h_1^n\) to forget the \(\mathfrak S_n\)-structure, and applying ordinary row-insertion RSK with \(\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w)\), gives
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}
\operatorname{des}(w)\\ b
\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
The Hilbert-series-from-Frobenius pairing convention is also explicitly stated by Lentfer. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

Equivalently,
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\]
Indeed, the coefficient of \(u^b\) in the \(w\)-summand is
\[
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{d+1}{2}}
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq[d]\\ |J|=b}}
q^{\sum_{i\notin J}i}
=
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
where \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\).

**Source caveat.** The sources state the \(q\)-Stirling Hilbert formula and the Frobenius formula; the above permutation product form should be presented as a short derivation, not as a verbatim quoted theorem unless a writeup independently cites a source where it is printed in that form. Rhoades–Wilson proved the Hilbert series, while Murai–Rhoades–Wilson later proved the full Fields Frobenius structure. ([cambridge.org](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/forum-of-mathematics-pi/article/hilbert-series-of-the-superspace-coinvariant-ring/D7F1C91BE28068FC384AFE85B6AED8B8))

---

## Final normalization checklist

- Use \(u\) as an **ordinary positive fermionic Hilbert variable**, not a supertrace variable.
- Identify \(R_n^{(1,1)}=SR_n\).
- Rename Murai–Rhoades–Wilson’s fermionic variable \(z\) to \(u\).
- Use \([r]_q=1+q+\cdots+q^{r-1}\) and the standard Gaussian binomial.
- In RSK, the statistic-bearing tableau is the **recording tableau** \(Q(w)\); with row insertion, \(\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w)\). If a different RSK convention is used, the inverse permutation may appear.

Under these normalizations, the hook formula in t5/t6 is source-consistent and has no smallest counterexample.

# Problem We Are Solving
Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

# Budget
Advisor calls used so far: 4 of 10. **Remaining (including this call): 6.**

For T ∈ SYT(n), let λ(T) be its shape, let Des(T) = {i ∈ {1,...,n−1}: i appears in a strictly higher row than i+1 in T}, let des(T) = |Des(T)|, and let maj(T) = ∑_{i ∈ Des(T)} i. With [a choose b]_q denoting the usual q-binomial coefficient, interpreted as 0 if b < 0 or b > a, the symmetric function C_{n,k}(x;q) = Δ′_{e_{k−1}} e_n evaluated at t = 0 has the Schur expansion C_{n,k}(x;q) = ∑_{T ∈ SYT(n)} q^{maj(T) + binom(n−k,2) − (n−k) des(T)} [des(T) choose n−k]_q s_{λ(T)}(x).  (from: literature_2505.24027)
    Proof sketch: The paper records this formula as a known description of C_{n,k}(x;q), citing the t = 0 Delta-conjecture literature. These give the two terms q^a u^b and q^(a−1)u^(b+1), respectively; this also covers a=1, where the r=0 contribution is allowed. The empty partition gives 1. If λ_2≥2, then λ′ has at least two cells in its second column, and removing ν′=(1^r) from the first column cannot make λ′/ν′ a horizontal strip, so every summand vanishes. Useful consequence for hook coefficients: combining this lemma with Proposition 5.1 at k=j=1 gives Hilb(R_n^(1,1);q;u)=c_∅+∑_{a≥1,b≥0} c_(a,1^b)(q^a u^b+q^(a−1)u^(b+1)). Thus, if h_{r,s}=[q^r u^s]Hilb(R_n^(1,1);q;u), then for a≥1 and b≥0, c_(a,1^b)=∑_{i=0}^b (−1)^i h_{a+i,b−i}; equivalently h_{a,b}=c_(a,1^b)+c_(a+1,1^(b−1)) with the second term omitted when b=0.

## Theorem-ready external inputs

### 1. Universal super-Schur Hilbert expansion

Use Lentfer’s bosonic–fermionic coinvariant ring
\[
R_{n}^{(k,j)}
=
\mathbb C[x^{(1)},\dots,x^{(k)},\theta^{(1)},\dots,\theta^{(j)}]\Big/
\left\langle
\mathbb C[x^{(1)},\dots,x^{(k)},\theta^{(1)},\dots,\theta^{(j)}]^{\mathfrak S_n}_{+}
\right\rangle,
\]
where the \(x\)’s commute, the \(\theta\)’s anticommute, and \(\mathfrak S_n\) acts diagonally by permuting indices. Lentfer denotes the symmetric-group case by \(R_n^{(k,j)}\), so the ordinary \(m\)-set diagonal coinvariant algebra is \(R_n^{(m,0)}\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

The exact universality input is:

\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\lambda\in P(k,j,n)}
c_\lambda(n)\,s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u),
\qquad
P(k,j,n)=\{\lambda:\ell(\lambda)\le n,\ \lambda_{k+1}\le j\},
\]
with **nonnegative integers \(c_\lambda(n)\) independent of \((k,j)\)**. For \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), so these are exactly the ordinary Schur-Hilbert coefficients in the user’s \(m\)-boson expansion. For \(k=j=1\), the same \(c_\lambda(n)\) occur in the one-boson/one-fermion Hilbert series. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

**Caveat.** In the \((1,1)\) specialization, only \(\lambda_2\le1\) can contribute, i.e. \(\lambda=\varnothing\) or a hook \((a,1^b)\). Thus this specialization is sufficient for hook coefficients but does not determine nonhook coefficients.

---

### 2. Super-Schur normalization and signs

Use Lentfer’s positive super-Schur convention:
\[
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}
s_\nu(\mathbf q)\,s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(\mathbf u).
\]
This is also written plethystically as \(s_\lambda[\mathbf q-\epsilon\mathbf u]\), and **it is not the signed supertrace convention**. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

For one even variable \(q\) and one odd Hilbert variable \(u\),
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
\]
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\qquad(a\ge1,\ b\ge0),
\]
and
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=0\quad\text{if }\lambda\text{ is not contained in a }(1,1)\text{-hook}.
\]
This is exactly Lentfer’s Lemma 4.4. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

So, with
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u),
\]
the required triangular relation is
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
\]

**Sign caveat.** If someone uses a signed supertrace variable, then \(u\) is replaced by \(-u\), and the hook specialization would acquire alternating signs. That convention would break the proof. For example, at \(n=2\), the unsigned Hilbert series is \(1+q+u\), whereas a signed product would give \(1+q-u\).

---

### 3. Matching \(R_n^{(1,1)}\) with the superspace coinvariant ring \(SR_n\)

Murai–Rhoades–Wilson define
\[
\Omega_n=\mathbb F[x_1,\dots,x_n]\otimes \wedge\{\theta_1,\dots,\theta_n\},
\]
with diagonal action
\[
w\cdot x_i=x_{w(i)},\qquad w\cdot\theta_i=\theta_{w(i)}.
\]
They define
\[
SI_n=\big((\Omega_n)^{\mathfrak S_n}_{+}\big)
\]
and
\[
SR_n=\Omega_n/SI_n.
\]
They also record Solomon’s generating-set normalization
\[
SI_n=(e_1,\dots,e_n,de_1,\dots,de_n).
\]
Thus \(SR_n\) is exactly Lentfer’s \(R_n^{(1,1)}\), after identifying \(x_i=x_i^{(1)}\) and \(\theta_i=\theta_i^{(1)}\). Their variable \(z\) for fermionic degree should be renamed \(u\) in the hook proof. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

---

### 4. Superspace Hilbert/product formula

A source-clean input is Murai–Rhoades–Wilson’s Fields Frobenius formula:
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,z)
=
\sum_{k=1}^n C_{n,k}(\mathbf x;q)\,z^{n-k},
\]
where \(q\) tracks bosonic degree and \(z\) tracks fermionic degree. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

Their tableau expansion gives
\[
C_{n,k}(\mathbf x;q)
=
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(n)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)+\binom{n-k}{2}-(n-k)\operatorname{des}(T)}
\begin{bmatrix}
\operatorname{des}(T)\\ n-k
\end{bmatrix}_q
s_{\lambda(T)}(\mathbf x).
\]
Setting \(b=n-k\), pairing the Frobenius characteristic with \(h_{(1^n)}=h_1^n\) to forget the \(\mathfrak S_n\)-structure, and applying ordinary row-insertion RSK with \(\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w)\), gives
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}
\operatorname{des}(w)\\ b
\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
The Hilbert-series-from-Frobenius pairing convention is also explicitly stated by Lentfer. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

Equivalently,
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\]
Indeed, the coefficient of \(u^b\) in the \(w\)-summand is
\[
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{d+1}{2}}
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq[d]\\ |J|=b}}
q^{\sum_{i\notin J}i}
=
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
where \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\).

**Source caveat.** The sources state the \(q\)-Stirling Hilbert formula and the Frobenius formula; the above permutation product form should be presented as a short derivation, not as a verbatim quoted theorem unless a writeup independently cites a source where it is printed in that form. Rhoades–Wilson proved the Hilbert series, while Murai–Rhoades–Wilson later proved the full Fields Frobenius structure. ([cambridge.org](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/forum-of-mathematics-pi/article/hilbert-series-of-the-superspace-coinvariant-ring/D7F1C91BE28068FC384AFE85B6AED8B8))

## Final normalization checklist

- Use \(u\) as an **ordinary positive fermionic Hilbert variable**, not a supertrace variable.
- Identify \(R_n^{(1,1)}=SR_n\).
- Rename Murai–Rhoades–Wilson’s fermionic variable \(z\) to \(u\).
- Use \([r]_q=1+q+\cdots+q^{r-1}\) and the standard Gaussian binomial.
- In RSK, the statistic-bearing tableau is the **recording tableau** \(Q(w)\); with row insertion, \(\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w)\). If a different RSK convention is used, the inverse permutation may appear.

Under these normalizations, the hook formula in t5/t6 is source-consistent and has no smallest counterexample.

## Theorem-ready Hilbert-level proof

Write

\[
d(w)=\operatorname{des}(w),\qquad M(w)=\operatorname{maj}(w),
\]

and define

\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1} c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0.
\]

Let

\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]

From the assumed \(GL(1|1)\) hook specialization,

\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
1+\sum_{a\ge1,b\ge0} c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
\left(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\right),
\]

so comparing coefficients of \(u^b\) gives the triangular relation

\[
\boxed{
H_b^{(n)}(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b^{(n)}(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
}
\tag{1}
\]

Now use the Fields/Rhoades-Wilson product formula

\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{M(w)-\binom{d(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{d(w)}(q^i+u).
\]

For fixed \(w\), the coefficient of \(u^b\) is

\[
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}}
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq [d]\\ |J|=b}}
q^{\sum_{i\notin J} i}
=
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq [d]\\ |J|=b}}
q^{M-\sum_{j\in J}j}.
\]

Using

\[
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq[d]\\ |J|=b}}q^{-\sum_{j\in J}j}
=
q^{-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix} d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]

we get

\[
\boxed{
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d(w)\ge b}}
q^{M(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix} d(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
}
\tag{2}
\]

Now define

\[
D_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{M(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix} d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

I claim \(C_b^{(n)}(q)=D_b^{(n)}(q)\). Indeed, for fixed \(w\), the contribution of \(D_b+q^{-1}D_{b-1}\) is

\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right),
\]

because

\[
q^{-1}q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}
=
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}q^{d-b}.
\]

By the \(q\)-Pascal identity,

\[
\begin{bmatrix} d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]

this equals the \(w\)-summand in \(H_b\). The only exceptional case is \(b=0,d=0\), i.e. the identity permutation, whose contribution is exactly the \(\delta_{b,0}\) term in (1). Therefore \(D_b\) satisfies the same triangular recurrence as \(C_b\), so

\[
\boxed{
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{M(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix} d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
}
\tag{3}
\]

Since

\[
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)} q^{|\rho|},
\]

we obtain the desired coefficient interpretation:

\[
\boxed{
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\quad d(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b),\\
a=M(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
}
\]

Also \(c_{\varnothing}(n)=1\). The exponent is always positive: if \(d\ge b+1\), then \(M\ge 1+\cdots+d\), so

\[
M-bd+\binom b2\ge \binom{d-b+1}{2}\ge 1.
\]

## Check: \(n=3\)

The descent-major data are:

- \(d=0,M=0\): one permutation;
- \(d=1,M=1\): two permutations;
- \(d=1,M=2\): two permutations;
- \(d=2,M=3\): one permutation.

Thus

\[
H_0=1+2q+2q^2+q^3,
\]

\[
H_1=2+3q+q^2,
\]

\[
H_2=1.
\]

The recurrence gives

\[
C_0=H_0-1=2q+2q^2+q^3,
\]

\[
C_1=H_1-q^{-1}C_0=q,
\]

\[
C_2=H_2-q^{-1}C_1=0.
\]

This matches the rectangle formula: for \(b=1\), only \(321\) has \(d\ge2\), and its contribution is

\[
q^{3-1\cdot2}=q.
\]

For \(n=4\), the same formula gives

\[
C_0=3q+5q^2+6q^3+5q^4+3q^5+q^6,
\]

\[
C_1=3q+5q^2+4q^3+q^4,
\]

\[
C_2=q,
\]

again confirming the exponent \(M-bd+\binom b2\) and the off-by-one rectangle \(b\times(d-1-b)\).

## Sufficiency and hidden assumptions

This is a fully Hilbert-level proof: no refined Frobenius coefficients \(c_{\lambda,\mu}\) are needed.

However, it is sufficient for the original hook-coefficient problem only after accepting the hidden universality input that the \(GL(1|1)\) superspace Hilbert expansion uses the same universal coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) as the ordinary multigraded Hilbert expansion. One also needs the positive, unsigned fermionic Hilbert convention, so that hooks specialize as

\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]

With those assumptions, the proof above directly recovers the original hook coefficients.
