## Theorem-ready external inputs

### 1. Universal super-Schur Hilbert expansion

Use Lentfer’s bosonic–fermionic coinvariant ring
\[
R_{n}^{(k,j)}
=
\mathbb C[x^{(1)},\dots,x^{(k)},\theta^{(1)},\dots,\theta^{(j)}]\Big/
\left\langle
\mathbb C[x^{(1)},\dots,x^{(k)},\theta^{(1)},\dots,\theta^{(j)}]^{\mathfrak S_n}_{+}
\right\rangle,
\]
where the \(x\)’s commute, the \(\theta\)’s anticommute, and \(\mathfrak S_n\) acts diagonally by permuting indices. Lentfer denotes the symmetric-group case by \(R_n^{(k,j)}\), so the ordinary \(m\)-set diagonal coinvariant algebra is \(R_n^{(m,0)}\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

The exact universality input is:

\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\lambda\in P(k,j,n)}
c_\lambda(n)\,s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u),
\qquad
P(k,j,n)=\{\lambda:\ell(\lambda)\le n,\ \lambda_{k+1}\le j\},
\]
with **nonnegative integers \(c_\lambda(n)\) independent of \((k,j)\)**. For \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), so these are exactly the ordinary Schur-Hilbert coefficients in the user’s \(m\)-boson expansion. For \(k=j=1\), the same \(c_\lambda(n)\) occur in the one-boson/one-fermion Hilbert series. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

**Caveat.** In the \((1,1)\) specialization, only \(\lambda_2\le1\) can contribute, i.e. \(\lambda=\varnothing\) or a hook \((a,1^b)\). Thus this specialization is sufficient for hook coefficients but does not determine nonhook coefficients.

---

### 2. Super-Schur normalization and signs

Use Lentfer’s positive super-Schur convention:
\[
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}
s_\nu(\mathbf q)\,s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(\mathbf u).
\]
This is also written plethystically as \(s_\lambda[\mathbf q-\epsilon\mathbf u]\), and **it is not the signed supertrace convention**. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

For one even variable \(q\) and one odd Hilbert variable \(u\),
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
\]
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\qquad(a\ge1,\ b\ge0),
\]
and
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=0\quad\text{if }\lambda\text{ is not contained in a }(1,1)\text{-hook}.
\]
This is exactly Lentfer’s Lemma 4.4. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

So, with
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u),
\]
the required triangular relation is
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
\]

**Sign caveat.** If someone uses a signed supertrace variable, then \(u\) is replaced by \(-u\), and the hook specialization would acquire alternating signs. That convention would break the proof. For example, at \(n=2\), the unsigned Hilbert series is \(1+q+u\), whereas a signed product would give \(1+q-u\).

---

### 3. Matching \(R_n^{(1,1)}\) with the superspace coinvariant ring \(SR_n\)

Murai–Rhoades–Wilson define
\[
\Omega_n=\mathbb F[x_1,\dots,x_n]\otimes \wedge\{\theta_1,\dots,\theta_n\},
\]
with diagonal action
\[
w\cdot x_i=x_{w(i)},\qquad w\cdot\theta_i=\theta_{w(i)}.
\]
They define
\[
SI_n=\big((\Omega_n)^{\mathfrak S_n}_{+}\big)
\]
and
\[
SR_n=\Omega_n/SI_n.
\]
They also record Solomon’s generating-set normalization
\[
SI_n=(e_1,\dots,e_n,de_1,\dots,de_n).
\]
Thus \(SR_n\) is exactly Lentfer’s \(R_n^{(1,1)}\), after identifying \(x_i=x_i^{(1)}\) and \(\theta_i=\theta_i^{(1)}\). Their variable \(z\) for fermionic degree should be renamed \(u\) in the hook proof. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

---

### 4. Superspace Hilbert/product formula

A source-clean input is Murai–Rhoades–Wilson’s Fields Frobenius formula:
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,z)
=
\sum_{k=1}^n C_{n,k}(\mathbf x;q)\,z^{n-k},
\]
where \(q\) tracks bosonic degree and \(z\) tracks fermionic degree. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

Their tableau expansion gives
\[
C_{n,k}(\mathbf x;q)
=
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(n)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)+\binom{n-k}{2}-(n-k)\operatorname{des}(T)}
\begin{bmatrix}
\operatorname{des}(T)\\ n-k
\end{bmatrix}_q
s_{\lambda(T)}(\mathbf x).
\]
Setting \(b=n-k\), pairing the Frobenius characteristic with \(h_{(1^n)}=h_1^n\) to forget the \(\mathfrak S_n\)-structure, and applying ordinary row-insertion RSK with \(\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w)\), gives
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}
\operatorname{des}(w)\\ b
\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
The Hilbert-series-from-Frobenius pairing convention is also explicitly stated by Lentfer. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

Equivalently,
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\]
Indeed, the coefficient of \(u^b\) in the \(w\)-summand is
\[
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{d+1}{2}}
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq[d]\\ |J|=b}}
q^{\sum_{i\notin J}i}
=
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
where \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\).

**Source caveat.** The sources state the \(q\)-Stirling Hilbert formula and the Frobenius formula; the above permutation product form should be presented as a short derivation, not as a verbatim quoted theorem unless a writeup independently cites a source where it is printed in that form. Rhoades–Wilson proved the Hilbert series, while Murai–Rhoades–Wilson later proved the full Fields Frobenius structure. ([cambridge.org](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/forum-of-mathematics-pi/article/hilbert-series-of-the-superspace-coinvariant-ring/D7F1C91BE28068FC384AFE85B6AED8B8))

- Use \(u\) as an **ordinary positive fermionic Hilbert variable**, not a supertrace variable.
- Identify \(R_n^{(1,1)}=SR_n\).
- Rename Murai–Rhoades–Wilson’s fermionic variable \(z\) to \(u\).
- Use \([r]_q=1+q+\cdots+q^{r-1}\) and the standard Gaussian binomial.
- In RSK, the statistic-bearing tableau is the **recording tableau** \(Q(w)\); with row insertion, \(\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w)\). If a different RSK convention is used, the inverse permutation may appear.

Under these normalizations, the hook formula in t5/t6 is source-consistent and has no smallest counterexample.

## Original Problem

Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

## Problem Solved

Fix \(n\ge 1\). Work over \(\mathbb C\). For \(m\ge 1\), let
\[
P_n^{(m)}=\mathbb C[x_i^{(r)}:1\le i\le n,\ 1\le r\le m],
\]
with \(S_n\) acting diagonally by permuting the index \(i\). Let \(I_n^{(m)}\) be the ideal generated by all \(S_n\)-invariant polynomials in \(P_n^{(m)}\) with zero constant term, and define the diagonal coinvariant algebra
\[
R_n^{(m)}=P_n^{(m)}/I_n^{(m)}.
\]
Give \(R_n^{(m)}\) the \(\mathbb Z_{\ge0}^m\)-grading in which \(x_i^{(r)}\) has degree \(1\) in the \(r\)-th component and degree \(0\) in the others. The universal Hilbert coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) are the coefficients in the stable Schur expansion
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots,q_m)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots,q_m),
\]
where \(s_\lambda\) is the Schur polynomial and \(s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots,q_m)=0\) if \(\ell(\lambda)>m\).

A hook partition is written
\[
\lambda=(a,1^b)
\]
with \(a\ge 1\) and \(b\ge 0\). For a permutation \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\in S_n\), define
\[
{\rm Des}(w)=\{i\in\{1,\ldots,n-1\}: w_i>w_{i+1}\},
\]
\[
{\rm des}(w)=|{\rm Des}(w)|,\qquad
{\rm maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in{\rm Des}(w)} i.
\]
For integers \(h,r\ge0\), a partition \(\rho\subseteq h\times r\) means a weakly decreasing sequence
\[
\rho=(\rho_1,\ldots,\rho_h)
\]
with \(r\ge \rho_1\ge\cdots\ge\rho_h\ge0\), and \(|\rho|=\rho_1+\cdots+\rho_h\). If \(h=0\), only the empty partition is allowed.

The theorem proved here is:
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1,
\]
and for every hook \((a,1^b)\) with \(a\ge1\), \(b\ge0\),
\[
\boxed{
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\\
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
}
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ {\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

Here the Gaussian binomial coefficient is
\[
\begin{bmatrix} r\\ s\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{cases}
\displaystyle\prod_{i=1}^{s}\frac{1-q^{r-s+i}}{1-q^i},&0\le s\le r,\\[1em]
0,&\text{otherwise},
\end{cases}
\]
and, for \(0\le s\le r\),
\[
\begin{bmatrix}r\\ s\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq s\times(r-s)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]

In particular, when \(b=0\),
\[
c_{(a)}(n)=\#\{w\in S_n:{\rm des}(w)\ge1,\ {\rm maj}(w)=a\},
\]
and if \(b\ge n-1\), then
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0.
\]
This is exactly the requested hook-coefficient interpretation, not a relaxation.

## Proof

First note that the degree-zero component of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is spanned by the image of \(1\). Since every nonempty Schur polynomial is homogeneous of positive total degree and \(s_{\varnothing}=1\), the constant term of the Hilbert series gives
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1.
\]

We now use three standard facts, stated in the precise forms needed.

### Known input 1: the \(GL(1|1)\) super Hilbert specialization

Let
\[
SR_n=\mathbb C[x_1,\ldots,x_n]\otimes \Lambda(\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_n)\big/\langle \text{positive-degree }S_n\text{-invariants}\rangle
\]
be the superspace coinvariant algebra with one even set \(x_1,\ldots,x_n\) and one odd set \(\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_n\), where \(S_n\) permutes the indices simultaneously. Let \(q\) record \(x\)-degree and \(u\) record \(\theta\)-degree, using the ordinary unsigned Hilbert series
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)=\sum_{i,j\ge0}\dim(SR_n)_{i,j}q^iu^j.
\]
The \(GL(1|1)\) specialization theorem says that the same universal coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) satisfy
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)=\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u),
\]
where \(s_\lambda(q/u)\) is the one-even-one-odd super Schur character.

### Known input 2: one-even-one-odd super Schur characters

In one even variable \(q\) and one odd variable \(u\),
\[
s_{\varnothing}(q/u)=1.
\]
For a hook \((a,1^b)\), \(a\ge1\), \(b\ge0\),
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
All nonempty non-hook Schur characters vanish under this \(GL(1|1)\) specialization.

### Known input 3: ordinary superspace coinvariant Hilbert product formula

The ordinary bigraded Hilbert series of \(SR_n\) is
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-\binom{{\rm des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{{\rm des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\]

### Step 1: compute the coefficient of \(u^b\) from the product formula

Let
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)=[u^b]\,{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u).
\]
Fix \(w\in S_n\), and write
\[
d={\rm des}(w),\qquad M={\rm maj}(w).
\]
The coefficient of \(u^b\) in \(\prod_{i=1}^d(q^i+u)\) is obtained by choosing \(b\) factors to contribute \(u\), equivalently choosing \(d-b\) factors to contribute powers of \(q\). Thus
\[
[u^b]\prod_{i=1}^{d}(q^i+u)
=
q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Therefore
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}}
q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Since
\[
\binom{d+1}{2}-\binom{d-b+1}{2}
=
bd-\binom b2,
\]
we get
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

### Step 2: compare with the \(GL(1|1)\) Schur expansion

For \(b\ge0\), define
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
and set
\[
C_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0.
\]
Using the \(GL(1|1)\) specialization and the hook character formula,
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
1+
\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\left(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\right).
\]
Taking the coefficient of \(u^b\) gives the triangular relation
\[
\boxed{
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\delta_{b,0}
+
C_b^{(n)}(q)
+
q^{-1}C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
}
\]

### Step 3: solve the triangular recurrence

We claim that
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ {\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

For \(b=0\), the triangular relation gives
\[
C_0^{(n)}(q)=H_0^{(n)}(q)-1.
\]
But
\[
H_0^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{w\in S_n}q^{{\rm maj}(w)}.
\]
The unique permutation with no descents is \(12\cdots n\), and it contributes \(1\). Hence
\[
C_0^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ {\rm des}(w)\ge1}}q^{{\rm maj}(w)},
\]
which is the desired formula when \(b=0\).

Now let \(b\ge1\). From the triangular relation,
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=H_b^{(n)}(q)-q^{-1}C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
\]
Using the formula for \(H_b^{(n)}(q)\) and the induction hypothesis for \(C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q)\), the contribution of a fixed \(w\), with \(d={\rm des}(w)\) and \(M={\rm maj}(w)\), is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
-
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
this equals
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Thus
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Since
\[
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q=0
\]
unless \({\rm des}(w)-1\ge b\), this is equivalently
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ {\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

### Step 4: interpret the Gaussian binomial coefficient

If \(d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1\), then
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Therefore
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Since
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
the coefficient of \(q^a\) is exactly the number of pairs \((w,\rho)\) satisfying
\[
w\in S_n,\qquad d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,
\]
\[
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

Finally, the exponent is always positive in the nonzero case. Indeed, if \(d\ge b+1\), then the \(d\) descent positions have sum at least \(1+\cdots+d\), so
\[
{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}
\ge1.
\]
Thus the formula produces only powers \(q^a\) with \(a\ge1\), as required for hooks \((a,1^b)\).

When \(b=0\), the rectangle \(0\times(d-1)\) contains only the empty partition, so the formula reduces to
\[
c_{(a)}(n)=\#\{w\in S_n:{\rm des}(w)\ge1,\ {\rm maj}(w)=a\}.
\]
When \(b\ge n-1\), every permutation has \({\rm des}(w)\le n-1\), so the condition \({\rm des}(w)\ge b+1\) is impossible. Hence
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0
\]
for \(b\ge n-1\). This completes the proof.

## Audit verdict

I find **no broken step and no counterexample**. The proposed hook formula is correct, provided the final write-up states the external inputs and conventions precisely.

### Key checks

1. **Universality really gives the original bosonic \(c_\lambda(n)\).**  
   Yes. Lentfer’s diagonal supersymmetry theorem gives universal Frobenius coefficients \(c_{\lambda\mu}\), and his Hilbert-series proposition defines
   \[
   c_\lambda=\sum_{\mu\vdash n}c_{\lambda\mu}\langle s_\mu,h_{(1^n)}\rangle
   \]
   independent of \((k,j)\). For \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), so these are exactly the original bosonic Schur-Hilbert coefficients. For \((k,j)=(1,1)\), the same \(c_\lambda\) occur. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

2. **Triangular relation.**  
   Correct with the **ordinary unsigned** Hilbert variable \(u\). Lentfer’s one-even/one-odd specialization is
   \[
   s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1},\qquad s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
   \]
   with no alternating sign. Hence
   \[
   H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
   \]
   The \(\delta_{b,0}\) is exactly the empty partition contribution. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

3. **\(q\)-Pascal step.**  
   Correct. If \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\), then the subtraction uses
   \[
   q^{-1}q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}
   =
   q^{M-bd+\binom b2}q^{d-b},
   \]
   and
   \[
   \begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
   =
   \begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
   +q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q.
   \]
   This gives exactly \(\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q\), with no shift error.

4. **Fields + RSK.**  
   Correct, but the write-up should explicitly say **row-insertion RSK and recording tableau \(Q(w)\)**. Murai–Rhoades–Wilson’s \(C_{n,k}\) tableau formula has exponent
   \[
   \operatorname{maj}(T)+\binom{n-k}{2}-(n-k)\operatorname{des}(T)
   \]
   and Gaussian factor \(\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)\\ n-k\end{bmatrix}_q\); their descent convention is \(i\) appears in a higher row than \(i+1\). Pairing with \(h_{(1^n)}\) and using RSK with \(T=Q(w)\) gives \(\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w)\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

5. **Edge cases.**  
   All check out:
   - \(n=1\): only the identity has \(d=0\), so all nonempty hook coefficients vanish and \(c_\varnothing(1)=1\).
   - \(b=0\): rectangle height \(0\), so only \(\rho=\varnothing\); \(c_{(a)}(n)\) counts permutations with at least one descent and \(\operatorname{maj}(w)=a\).
   - \(b\ge n-1\): condition \(\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1\) is impossible, so coefficient is \(0\).
   - Vertical hooks \((1,1^b)\) are included; for \(b=n-2\) only the reverse permutation contributes, giving \(c_{(1,1^{n-2})}(n)=1\), while \(b=n-1\) gives \(0\).

## Theorem-ready proof skeleton

Let
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]

By the \(GL(1|1)\) specialization,
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
1+\sum_{a\ge1,b\ge0}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
\left(q^au^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\right),
\]
so
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
\]

From Fields’ Frobenius formula, pairing with \(h_{(1^n)}\), and row-insertion RSK using \(Q(w)\),
\[
H_b(q)=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

Now set
\[
D_b(q)=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
For each fixed \(w\), \(D_b+q^{-1}D_{b-1}\) contributes
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right)
=
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
The only missing case is \(b=0,d=0\), exactly supplied by \(\delta_{b,0}\). Thus \(D_b=C_b\).

Finally,
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|},
\]
so
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Also \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\) from the degree-zero component.

---

- Say \(c_\lambda(n)\) are the **Hilbert** coefficients \(c_\lambda\), not the refined Frobenius coefficients \(c_{\lambda\mu}\).
- Specify the **ordinary unsigned fermionic Hilbert variable** \(u\); do not use supertrace signs.

  \]
  present it as derived from Fields + RSK, not necessarily as a verbatim cited theorem.
- State the Gaussian convention and rectangle interpretation clearly.

# Problem We Are Solving
Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

On the algebra side, antisymmetrizing in the last j variables gives e_j^⊥ grFrob(R_{n,k})=grFrob(ε_j R_{n,k}); Lemma 6.8 identifies ε_j R_{n,k} with R_{n-j,k,k-j} tensored with the alternant space in j variables, contributing q^{binom(j,2)} [k choose j]_q. Lemma 6.10 then expands grFrob(R_{n-j,k,k-j}) via short exact sequences for the auxiliary quotients R_{n,k,s}. On the combinatorial side, Lemma 3.7 gives exactly the same e_j^⊥ recursion for D_{n,k}. Since constant terms agree, Lemma 3.6 forces equality. For the displayed Schur expansion, use Wilson's theorem on the t=0 Delta expression. Taking the coefficient corresponding to n-k rises gives ω C_{n,k}(x;q)=∑_{T∈SYT(n)} q^{maj(T)+binom(n-k,2)-(n-k)des(T)} [des(T) choose n-k]_q s_{shape(T)'}(x), before q-reversal. The maximal q-degree of grFrob(R_{n,k};q) is (n-k)(k-1)+binom(k,2), known from Theorem 4.10. Applying rev_q converts the exponent to binom(n,2)-maj(T) and leaves the q-binomial in the form [des(T) choose n-k]_q. Finally transpose tableaux: shape(T')=shape(T)', maj(T')=binom(n,2)-maj(T), and des(T')=n-1-des(T). Reindexing by T' gives the stated formula.
  • [arXiv:2301.09763 Lemma 2.1 (Sagan-Swanson staircase identity)] Fix n ≥ 1. For a subset J ⊆ [n], define its J-staircase st(J) = (st(J)_1,...,st(J)_n) by st(J)_i = |{a ∈ [i] : a ∉ J}|; equivalently st(J)_1 is 0 if 1 ∈ J and 1 if 1 ∉ J, and st(J)_{i+1}=st(J)_i if i+1 ∈ J while st(J)_{i+1}=st(J)_i+1 if i+1 ∉ J. Let [r]_q = 1+q+...+q^{r-1} for r ≥ 1 and [0]_q=0, and let [k]!_q = [k]_q [k-1]_q ... [1]_q. Define q-Stirling numbers Stir_q(n,k) by Stir_q(0,0)=1, Stir_q(0,k)=0 for k≠0, and Stir_q(n,k) = [k]_q Stir_q(n-1,k) + Stir_q(n-1,k-1). Then the polynomial identity holds: Σ_{J⊆[n]} z^{|J|} Π_{i=1}^n [st(J)_i]_q = Σ_{k=1}^n z^{n-k} [k]!_q Stir_q(n,k). Equivalently, the coefficient of z^r on the left is the q-degree generating function of all pairs consisting of a subset J⊆[n] of size r and a monomial x_1^{a_1}...x_n^{a_n} satisfying 0 ≤ a_i < st(J)_i for every i.  (from: literature_2301.09763)
    Proof sketch: Let G(n,k) be the coefficient of z^{n-k} on the left, i.e. the sum over subsets J⊆[n] with exactly k elements outside J. The formula st(J)_i=|[i]\J| makes the recurrence transparent. Split such J according to whether n∈J. If n∈J, then J'=J\{n} has k elements outside it in [n-1], the first n-1 staircase entries are those of J', and the final entry is k; this contributes [k]_q G(n-1,k). If n∉J, then J⊆[n-1] has k-1 elements outside it in [n-1], and adjoining the nth entry again multiplies by [k]_q; this contributes [k]_q G(n-1,k-1). Thus G(n,k)=[k]_q G(n-1,k)+[k]_q G(n-1,k-1), with G(1,1)=1 and the usual boundary values. The quantity H(n,k):=[k]!_q Stir_q(n,k) satisfies the same recurrence, since multiplying Stir_q(n,k)=[k]_q Stir_q(n-1,k)+Stir_q(n-1,k-1) by [k]!_q gives H(n,k)=[k]_q H(n-1,k)+[k]_q H(n-1,k-1). Hence G(n,k)=H(n,k). The monomial interpretation is immediate from Π_i [st(J)_i]_q = Σ_{0≤a_i<st(J)_i} q^{a_1+...+a_n}.
  • [arXiv:2301.09763 Theorem 4.12] Let C[x_n]=C[x_1,...,x_n], and let I_n=(C[x_n]^{S_n}_+) be the classical coinvariant ideal, equivalently I_n=(e_1(x_n),...,e_n(x_n)). For S⊆[n], write h_d(S) for the complete homogeneous symmetric polynomial of degree d in the variables {x_s : s∈S}. For J⊆[n], define f_J = Π_{j∈J} x_j Π_{i=j+1}^n (x_j-x_i), with empty products equal to 1. Define polynomials p_{J,1},...,p_{J,n} as follows, using the convention min(∅)=∞: if i<min(J), set p_{J,i}=h_i({i,i+1,...,n}); if i≥min(J), set s=max(J∩[i]), T=J∪{i+1,i+2,...,n}, r=n-|T|+1, and p_{J,i}=∂h_r(T)/∂x_s. A permutation w∈S_n is j-resentful if w(j)=n or if w(j)+1 occurs among w(j+1),...,w(n); it is j-Nietzschean otherwise. It is J-Nietzschean if it is j-Nietzschean for every j∈J. Let N_J be the finite subset of C^n consisting of the points (w(1),...,w(n)) for J-Nietzschean w∈S_n. For a finite set X⊂C^n, let I(X) be its vanishing ideal and gr I(X) the homogeneous ideal generated by the top-degree homogeneous parts of nonzero elements of I(X). Then, for every J⊆[n], the following three homogeneous ideals in C[x_n] are equal: (1) the colon ideal (I_n : f_J)={g∈C[x_n] : g f_J∈I_n}; (2) the ideal (p_{J,1},...,p_{J,n}); (3) the orbit-harmonic ideal gr I(N_J). If I_J denotes this common ideal, then Hilb(C[x_n]/I_J;q)=Π_{i=1}^n [st(J)_i]_q, where st(J)_i=|[i]\J| and [0]_q=0.  (from: literature_2301.09763)
    Proof sketch: If 1∈J, all three ideals are the unit ideal: f_J∈I_n, p_{J,1}=∂h_1([n])/∂x_1=1, and no permutation is 1-Nietzschean. Assume 1∉J. A major input is that p_{J,1},...,p_{J,n} is a regular sequence of degrees st(J)_1,...,st(J)_n. The regularity proof uses the identity ∂_a h_r(S)=∂_b h_r(S)+(x_c-x_b)∂_b h_{r-1}(S∪{c})-(x_c-x_a)∂_a h_{r-1}(S∪{c}) for a,b∈S, c∉S. Iterating it puts all derivatives ∂_j h_{n-|J|+1}(J), j∈J, in (p_{J,*}); these derivatives have no common zero except 0 in the J-variables, and after setting those variables to 0 the remaining p-generators are triangular, so the zero locus is {0}. Next the paper proves (p_{J,*})⊆(I_n:f_J). This uses superspace elements q_{J,i} in the supercoinvariant ideal whose Gale-leading θ_J coefficient is p_{J,i}, and differential operators D_J whose θ_J-leading coefficient on the Vandermonde δ_n is ±f_J⊙δ_n; since q_{J,i} annihilates the superharmonic D_J(δ_n), triangularity forces (p_{J,i} f_J)⊙δ_n=0, hence p_{J,i} f_J∈I_n. The quotient C[x_n]/(p_{J,*}) is a complete intersection, hence a Poincare duality algebra with socle degree Σ_i st(J)_i-n. The coinvariant algebra C[x_n]/I_n is also Poincare duality with socle degree binom(n,2), f_J∉I_n when 1∉J, and deg f_J=Σ_i(i-st(J)_i), so the socle degrees differ by deg f_J. The colon-ideal lemma of Abe-Horiguchi-Masuda-Murai-Sato then upgrades the containment to (p_{J,*})=(I_n:f_J). For the equality with gr I(N_J), deform the coinvariant ideal to ~I_n=(e_d(x)-e_d(1,...,n):1≤d≤n), whose variety is the embedded set S_n⊂C^n. The inhomogeneous polynomial ~f_J=Π_{j∈J}(x_j-n)Π_{i>j}(x_j-x_i+1) vanishes exactly on the J-resentful points of S_n, so N_J=V(~I_n)-V(~f_J), and radicality gives I(N_J)=(~I_n:~f_J). Passing to top homogeneous parts gives gr I(N_J)⊆(I_n:f_J). Finally |N_J|=Π_i st(J)_i by an insertion/deletion recurrence for J-Nietzschean permutations, while dim C[x_n]/(I_n:f_J)=Π_i st(J)_i from the complete intersection Hilbert series, forcing equality of ideals and yielding the stated Hilbert series.
  • [arXiv:2301.09763 Theorem 5.3] Let Ω_n=C[x_1,...,x_n]⊗Λ(θ_1,...,θ_n), where the x_i commute and the θ_i anticommute, and let S_n act diagonally by permuting x_i and θ_i simultaneously. Let SI_n be the ideal of Ω_n generated by all S_n-invariant elements with vanishing constant term, and set SR_n=Ω_n/SI_n. Give SR_n the bigrading in which q records total x-degree and z records total θ-degree. Then Hilb(SR_n;q,z)=Σ_{k=1}^n z^{n-k} [k]!_q Stir_q(n,k). Equivalently, using the J-staircase st(J)_i=|[i]\J|, Hilb(SR_n;q,z)=Σ_{J⊆[n]} z^{|J|} Π_{i=1}^n [st(J)_i]_q.  (from: literature_2301.09763)
    Proof sketch: The proof has matching upper and lower bounds. For the upper bound, for every J⊆[n] the paper constructs elements q_{J,i}∈SI_n whose fermionic degree is |J| and whose Gale-leading θ_J coefficient is p_{J,i}. The polynomials p_{J,i} are the regular sequence of Theorem 4.12, with degrees st(J)_i. Choose homogeneous representatives B_n(J) for a basis of C[x_n]/(p_{J,1},...,p_{J,n}); their degree generating function is Π_i [st(J)_i]_q. A triangular straightening argument in Gale order says that any f θ_J can be reduced modulo SI_n to a linear combination of B_n(J) θ_J plus terms with strictly larger fermionic monomial in Gale order. Descending induction over Gale order shows that the union over J of B_n(J) θ_J spans SR_n, giving Hilb(SR_n;q,z) ≤ Σ_J z^{|J|}Π_i[st(J)_i]_q. For the lower bound, use differential operators D_J built from minors of the complete-homogeneous matrix H. Their key triangular property is that D_J(δ_n), where δ_n is the Vandermonde, is superharmonic and has θ_J coefficient ±f_J⊙δ_n, with only θ_K terms for K≤Gale J. The annihilator of f_J⊙δ_n in C[x_n] is (I_n:f_J). By Theorem 4.12, choose B_n(J) mapping to a basis of C[x_n]/(I_n:f_J), again with degree generating function Π_i[st(J)_i]_q. If a nontrivial linear relation among the images of all B_n(J)θ_J held in SR_n, then, after taking all terms of one fermionic degree and choosing a Gale-minimal J_0 among those with nonzero coefficient, pairing/acting on D_{J_0}(δ_n) would kill all higher Gale terms and produce a nontrivial relation among {b⊙(f_{J_0}⊙δ_n): b∈B_n(J_0)}. This contradicts the basis property modulo (I_n:f_{J_0}). Hence the same set is linearly independent in SR_n, so the upper bound is sharp. The final expression with q-Stirling numbers is exactly Lemma 2.1.
  • [arXiv:2505.14885 Proposition 5.1] Fix a positive integer n. For k,j ≥ 0 let R_n^(k,j) be the symmetric-group bosonic-fermionic diagonal coinvariant ring with k sets of n commuting variables and j sets of n anticommuting variables, i.e. R_n^(k,j) = Sym(C^(k|j) ⊗ C^n)/⟨Sym(C^(k|j) ⊗ C^n)_+^(S_n)⟩, with S_n acting diagonally by permuting the n indices. Let P(k,j,n) be the set of partitions λ with length ℓ(λ) ≤ n and λ_(k+1) ≤ j, with missing parts interpreted as 0. For alphabets q = q_1,...,q_k and u = u_1,...,u_j, define the super Schur function by s_λ(q/u) = ∑_{ν⊆λ} s_ν(q) s_{λ′/ν′}(u). Then there exist nonnegative integers c_λ, depending only on n and λ and independent of k and j, such that for every k,j one has Hilb(R_n^(k,j); q; u) = ∑_{λ∈P(k,j,n)} c_λ s_λ(q/u). More explicitly, if c_{λμ} are the universal Frobenius coefficients from Corollary 1.3, then c_λ = ∑_{μ⊢n} c_{λμ} ⟨s_μ(z), h_(1^n)(z)⟩. In particular, when j = 0 this specializes to the ordinary bosonic Schur expansion Hilb(R_n^(k,0); q_1,...,q_k) = ∑_{ℓ(λ)≤min(k,n)} c_λ s_λ(q_1,...,q_k).  (from: literature_2505.14885)
    Proof sketch: Use Corollary 1.3, which gives the multigraded Frobenius series in the form Frob(R_n^(k,j); q; u) = ∑_{λ∈P(k,j,n)} ∑_{μ⊢n} c_{λμ} s_λ(q/u) s_μ(z), where c_{λμ} are nonnegative and independent of k,j. The Hilbert series is recovered from the Frobenius series by taking the Hall inner product in the z-variables with h_(1^n)(z): Hilb(R_n^(k,j); q; u) = ⟨Frob(R_n^(k,j); q; u), h_(1^n)(z)⟩. Pull the inner product through the finite sum over μ for each λ and set c_λ = ∑_{μ⊢n} c_{λμ}⟨s_μ,h_(1^n)⟩. The factors ⟨s_μ,h_(1^n)⟩ are nonnegative integers, so c_λ is nonnegative; independence of k,j is inherited from Corollary 1.3. The condition λ∈P(k,j,n) is exactly the condition under which the corresponding simple gl(k|j)-character s_λ(q/u) can appear. For the purely bosonic case j=0, the super Schur function reduces to the usual Schur function s_λ(q), and P(k,0,n) imposes ℓ(λ)≤min(k,n), giving the ordinary multigraded coinvariant Hilbert coefficients.
  • [arXiv:2505.14885 Lemma 4.4] For the super Schur functions in one bosonic variable q and one fermionic variable u, one has s_∅(q/u) = 1. If λ is a hook partition λ = (a,1^b) with a ≥ 1 and b ≥ 0, then s_λ(q/u) = q^a u^b + q^(a−1) u^(b+1). If λ is neither empty nor of the form (a,1^b), equivalently if λ_2 ≥ 2, then s_λ(q/u) = 0.  (from: literature_2505.14885)
    Proof sketch: Start from the defining expansion s_λ(q/u) = ∑_{ν⊆λ} s_ν(q) s_{λ′/ν′}(u). Since q is a single variable, s_ν(q) is nonzero only when ν is a one-row partition ν=(r), in which case it equals q^r. Since u is a single variable, the skew Schur function s_{λ′/ν′}(u) is nonzero only when the skew shape λ′/ν′ is a horizontal strip, in which case it is the corresponding power of u. For λ=(a,1^b), so λ′=(b+1,1^(a−1)), the only row partitions ν=(r) for which λ′/ν′ is a horizontal strip are r=a and r=a−1. These give the two terms q^a u^b and q^(a−1)u^(b+1), respectively; this also covers a=1, where the r=0 contribution is allowed. The empty partition gives 1. If λ_2≥2, then λ′ has at least two cells in its second column, and removing ν′=(1^r) from the first column cannot make λ′/ν′ a horizontal strip, so every summand vanishes. Useful consequence for hook coefficients: combining this lemma with Proposition 5.1 at k=j=1 gives Hilb(R_n^(1,1);q;u)=c_∅+∑_{a≥1,b≥0} c_(a,1^b)(q^a u^b+q^(a−1)u^(b+1)). Thus, if h_{r,s}=[q^r u^s]Hilb(R_n^(1,1);q;u), then for a≥1 and b≥0, c_(a,1^b)=∑_{i=0}^b (−1)^i h_{a+i,b−i}; equivalently h_{a,b}=c_(a,1^b)+c_(a+1,1^(b−1)) with the second term omitted when b=0.
  • [arXiv:2505.14885 Equation (50), quoted theorem of Rhoades--Wilson] For the coinvariant ring R_n^(1,1) with one set of n commuting variables and one set of n anticommuting variables, the bigraded Hilbert series is Hilb(R_n^(1,1); q; u) = ∑_{d=0}^n [d]_q! Stir_q(n,d) u^(n−d). Here [d]_q = 1+q+⋯+q^(d−1), [d]_q! = [d]_q [d−1]_q ⋯ [1]_q with [0]_q! = 1, and the q-Stirling numbers are defined by Stir_q(n,d) = [d]_q Stir_q(n−1,d) + Stir_q(n−1,d−1), with initial conditions Stir_q(0,d)=δ_{d,0}.  (from: literature_2505.14885)
    Proof sketch: Lentfer records this formula as a theorem proved by Rhoades--Wilson, originally conjectured by Sagan--Swanson; the present paper does not reprove it, but uses it as an input in Section 5. In the paper, substituting this formula into the cancellation identity of Proposition 5.2 yields the Sagan--Swanson identity ∑_{d=0}^n [d]_q! Stir_q(n,d)(−q)^(n−d)=1. For extracting hook Schur coefficients, combine this quoted Hilbert formula with Lemma 4.4 and Proposition 5.1. If h_{r,s}=[q^r u^s]Hilb(R_n^(1,1);q;u), then the formula gives h_{r,s}=[q^r]([n−s]_q! Stir_q(n,n−s)) for 0≤s≤n. Therefore the hook coefficient in the universal Hilbert expansion satisfies c_(a,1^b)=∑_{i=0}^b (−1)^i [q^(a+i)]([n−b+i]_q! Stir_q(n,n−b+i)) for a≥1,b≥0, with c_∅=1. This is the GL(1|1) extraction mechanism: the two monomials in the character of a hook simple module make the hook multiplicity an alternating finite difference of the q-Stirling Hilbert coefficients.

## Audit verdict

I find **no broken step and no counterexample**. The proposed hook formula is correct, provided the final write-up states the external inputs and conventions precisely.

### Key checks

1. **Universality really gives the original bosonic \(c_\lambda(n)\).**  
   Yes. Lentfer’s diagonal supersymmetry theorem gives universal Frobenius coefficients \(c_{\lambda\mu}\), and his Hilbert-series proposition defines
   \[
   c_\lambda=\sum_{\mu\vdash n}c_{\lambda\mu}\langle s_\mu,h_{(1^n)}\rangle
   \]
   independent of \((k,j)\). For \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), so these are exactly the original bosonic Schur-Hilbert coefficients. For \((k,j)=(1,1)\), the same \(c_\lambda\) occur. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

2. **Triangular relation.**  
   Correct with the **ordinary unsigned** Hilbert variable \(u\). Lentfer’s one-even/one-odd specialization is
   \[
   s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1},\qquad s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
   \]
   with no alternating sign. Hence
   \[
   H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
   \]
   The \(\delta_{b,0}\) is exactly the empty partition contribution. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

3. **\(q\)-Pascal step.**  
   Correct. If \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\), then the subtraction uses
   \[
   q^{-1}q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}
   =
   q^{M-bd+\binom b2}q^{d-b},
   \]
   and
   \[
   \begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
   =
   \begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
   +q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q.
   \]
   This gives exactly \(\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q\), with no shift error.

4. **Fields + RSK.**  
   Correct, but the write-up should explicitly say **row-insertion RSK and recording tableau \(Q(w)\)**. Murai–Rhoades–Wilson’s \(C_{n,k}\) tableau formula has exponent
   \[
   \operatorname{maj}(T)+\binom{n-k}{2}-(n-k)\operatorname{des}(T)
   \]
   and Gaussian factor \(\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)\\ n-k\end{bmatrix}_q\); their descent convention is \(i\) appears in a higher row than \(i+1\). Pairing with \(h_{(1^n)}\) and using RSK with \(T=Q(w)\) gives \(\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w)\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

5. **Edge cases.**  
   All check out:
   - \(n=1\): only the identity has \(d=0\), so all nonempty hook coefficients vanish and \(c_\varnothing(1)=1\).
   - \(b=0\): rectangle height \(0\), so only \(\rho=\varnothing\); \(c_{(a)}(n)\) counts permutations with at least one descent and \(\operatorname{maj}(w)=a\).
   - \(b\ge n-1\): condition \(\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1\) is impossible, so coefficient is \(0\).
   - Vertical hooks \((1,1^b)\) are included; for \(b=n-2\) only the reverse permutation contributes, giving \(c_{(1,1^{n-2})}(n)=1\), while \(b=n-1\) gives \(0\).

---

## Theorem-ready proof skeleton

Let
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]

By the \(GL(1|1)\) specialization,
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
1+\sum_{a\ge1,b\ge0}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
\left(q^au^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\right),
\]
so
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
\]

From Fields’ Frobenius formula, pairing with \(h_{(1^n)}\), and row-insertion RSK using \(Q(w)\),
\[
H_b(q)=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

Now set
\[
D_b(q)=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
For each fixed \(w\), \(D_b+q^{-1}D_{b-1}\) contributes
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right)
=
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
The only missing case is \(b=0,d=0\), exactly supplied by \(\delta_{b,0}\). Thus \(D_b=C_b\).

Finally,
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|},
\]
so
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Also \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\) from the degree-zero component.

---

  \]

- State the Gaussian convention and rectangle interpretation clearly.

## Theorem-ready Hilbert-level proof

Write

\[
d(w)=\operatorname{des}(w),\qquad M(w)=\operatorname{maj}(w),
\]

and define

\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1} c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0.
\]

Let

\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]

From the assumed \(GL(1|1)\) hook specialization,

\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
1+\sum_{a\ge1,b\ge0} c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
\left(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\right),
\]

so comparing coefficients of \(u^b\) gives the triangular relation

\[
\boxed{
H_b^{(n)}(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b^{(n)}(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
}
\tag{1}
\]

Now use the Fields/Rhoades-Wilson product formula

\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{M(w)-\binom{d(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{d(w)}(q^i+u).
\]

For fixed \(w\), the coefficient of \(u^b\) is

\[
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}}
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq [d]\\ |J|=b}}
q^{\sum_{i\notin J} i}
=
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq [d]\\ |J|=b}}
q^{M-\sum_{j\in J}j}.
\]

Using

\[
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq[d]\\ |J|=b}}q^{-\sum_{j\in J}j}
=
q^{-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix} d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]

we get

\[
\boxed{
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d(w)\ge b}}
q^{M(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix} d(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
}
\tag{2}
\]

Now define

\[
D_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{M(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix} d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

I claim \(C_b^{(n)}(q)=D_b^{(n)}(q)\). Indeed, for fixed \(w\), the contribution of \(D_b+q^{-1}D_{b-1}\) is

\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right),
\]

because

\[
q^{-1}q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}
=
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}q^{d-b}.
\]

By the \(q\)-Pascal identity,

\[
\begin{bmatrix} d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]

this equals the \(w\)-summand in \(H_b\). The only exceptional case is \(b=0,d=0\), i.e. the identity permutation, whose contribution is exactly the \(\delta_{b,0}\) term in (1). Therefore \(D_b\) satisfies the same triangular recurrence as \(C_b\), so

\[
\boxed{
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{M(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix} d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
}
\tag{3}
\]

Since

\[
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)} q^{|\rho|},
\]

we obtain the desired coefficient interpretation:

\[
\boxed{
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\quad d(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b),\\
a=M(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
}
\]

Also \(c_{\varnothing}(n)=1\). The exponent is always positive: if \(d\ge b+1\), then \(M\ge 1+\cdots+d\), so

\[
M-bd+\binom b2\ge \binom{d-b+1}{2}\ge 1.
\]

## Check: \(n=3\)

The descent-major data are:

- \(d=0,M=0\): one permutation;
- \(d=1,M=1\): two permutations;
- \(d=1,M=2\): two permutations;
- \(d=2,M=3\): one permutation.

Thus

\[
H_0=1+2q+2q^2+q^3,
\]

\[
H_1=2+3q+q^2,
\]

\[
H_2=1.
\]

The recurrence gives

\[
C_0=H_0-1=2q+2q^2+q^3,
\]

\[
C_1=H_1-q^{-1}C_0=q,
\]

\[
C_2=H_2-q^{-1}C_1=0.
\]

This matches the rectangle formula: for \(b=1\), only \(321\) has \(d\ge2\), and its contribution is

\[
q^{3-1\cdot2}=q.
\]

For \(n=4\), the same formula gives

\[
C_0=3q+5q^2+6q^3+5q^4+3q^5+q^6,
\]

\[
C_1=3q+5q^2+4q^3+q^4,
\]

\[
C_2=q,
\]

again confirming the exponent \(M-bd+\binom b2\) and the off-by-one rectangle \(b\times(d-1-b)\).

## Sufficiency and hidden assumptions

This is a fully Hilbert-level proof: no refined Frobenius coefficients \(c_{\lambda,\mu}\) are needed.

However, it is sufficient for the original hook-coefficient problem only after accepting the hidden universality input that the \(GL(1|1)\) superspace Hilbert expansion uses the same universal coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) as the ordinary multigraded Hilbert expansion. One also needs the positive, unsigned fermionic Hilbert convention, so that hooks specialize as

\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]

With those assumptions, the proof above directly recovers the original hook coefficients.

## Audit verdict

I found **no normalization discrepancy** in the hook-coefficient proof, provided the final writeup explicitly uses the **ordinary unsigned Hilbert-series convention** for fermions and either proves or cites the permutation product form. The only source-accuracy caveat is that the product formula is best presented as a **derived equivalent form** of the Fields/Rhoades–Wilson/Murai–Rhoades–Wilson formulas, not as a formula visibly stated in exactly that form.

---

## Theorem-ready external inputs

### 1. Universal super-Schur Hilbert expansion

Use Lentfer’s bosonic–fermionic coinvariant ring
\[
R_{n}^{(k,j)}
=
\mathbb C[x^{(1)},\dots,x^{(k)},\theta^{(1)},\dots,\theta^{(j)}]\Big/
\left\langle
\mathbb C[x^{(1)},\dots,x^{(k)},\theta^{(1)},\dots,\theta^{(j)}]^{\mathfrak S_n}_{+}
\right\rangle,
\]
where the \(x\)’s commute, the \(\theta\)’s anticommute, and \(\mathfrak S_n\) acts diagonally by permuting indices. Lentfer denotes the symmetric-group case by \(R_n^{(k,j)}\), so the ordinary \(m\)-set diagonal coinvariant algebra is \(R_n^{(m,0)}\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

The exact universality input is:

\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\lambda\in P(k,j,n)}
c_\lambda(n)\,s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u),
\qquad
P(k,j,n)=\{\lambda:\ell(\lambda)\le n,\ \lambda_{k+1}\le j\},
\]
with **nonnegative integers \(c_\lambda(n)\) independent of \((k,j)\)**. For \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), so these are exactly the ordinary Schur-Hilbert coefficients in the user’s \(m\)-boson expansion. For \(k=j=1\), the same \(c_\lambda(n)\) occur in the one-boson/one-fermion Hilbert series. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

**Caveat.** In the \((1,1)\) specialization, only \(\lambda_2\le1\) can contribute, i.e. \(\lambda=\varnothing\) or a hook \((a,1^b)\). Thus this specialization is sufficient for hook coefficients but does not determine nonhook coefficients.

---

### 2. Super-Schur normalization and signs

Use Lentfer’s positive super-Schur convention:
\[
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}
s_\nu(\mathbf q)\,s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(\mathbf u).
\]
This is also written plethystically as \(s_\lambda[\mathbf q-\epsilon\mathbf u]\), and **it is not the signed supertrace convention**. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

For one even variable \(q\) and one odd Hilbert variable \(u\),
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
\]
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\qquad(a\ge1,\ b\ge0),
\]
and
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=0\quad\text{if }\lambda\text{ is not contained in a }(1,1)\text{-hook}.
\]
This is exactly Lentfer’s Lemma 4.4. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

So, with
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u),
\]
the required triangular relation is
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
\]

**Sign caveat.** If someone uses a signed supertrace variable, then \(u\) is replaced by \(-u\), and the hook specialization would acquire alternating signs. That convention would break the proof. For example, at \(n=2\), the unsigned Hilbert series is \(1+q+u\), whereas a signed product would give \(1+q-u\).

---

### 3. Matching \(R_n^{(1,1)}\) with the superspace coinvariant ring \(SR_n\)

Murai–Rhoades–Wilson define
\[
\Omega_n=\mathbb F[x_1,\dots,x_n]\otimes \wedge\{\theta_1,\dots,\theta_n\},
\]
with diagonal action
\[
w\cdot x_i=x_{w(i)},\qquad w\cdot\theta_i=\theta_{w(i)}.
\]
They define
\[
SI_n=\big((\Omega_n)^{\mathfrak S_n}_{+}\big)
\]
and
\[
SR_n=\Omega_n/SI_n.
\]
They also record Solomon’s generating-set normalization
\[
SI_n=(e_1,\dots,e_n,de_1,\dots,de_n).
\]
Thus \(SR_n\) is exactly Lentfer’s \(R_n^{(1,1)}\), after identifying \(x_i=x_i^{(1)}\) and \(\theta_i=\theta_i^{(1)}\). Their variable \(z\) for fermionic degree should be renamed \(u\) in the hook proof. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

---

### 4. Superspace Hilbert/product formula

A source-clean input is Murai–Rhoades–Wilson’s Fields Frobenius formula:
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,z)
=
\sum_{k=1}^n C_{n,k}(\mathbf x;q)\,z^{n-k},
\]
where \(q\) tracks bosonic degree and \(z\) tracks fermionic degree. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

Their tableau expansion gives
\[
C_{n,k}(\mathbf x;q)
=
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(n)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)+\binom{n-k}{2}-(n-k)\operatorname{des}(T)}
\begin{bmatrix}
\operatorname{des}(T)\\ n-k
\end{bmatrix}_q
s_{\lambda(T)}(\mathbf x).
\]
Setting \(b=n-k\), pairing the Frobenius characteristic with \(h_{(1^n)}=h_1^n\) to forget the \(\mathfrak S_n\)-structure, and applying ordinary row-insertion RSK with \(\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w)\), gives
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}
\operatorname{des}(w)\\ b
\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
The Hilbert-series-from-Frobenius pairing convention is also explicitly stated by Lentfer. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

Equivalently,
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\]
Indeed, the coefficient of \(u^b\) in the \(w\)-summand is
\[
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{d+1}{2}}
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq[d]\\ |J|=b}}
q^{\sum_{i\notin J}i}
=
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
where \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\).

**Source caveat.** The sources state the \(q\)-Stirling Hilbert formula and the Frobenius formula; the above permutation product form should be presented as a short derivation, not as a verbatim quoted theorem unless a writeup independently cites a source where it is printed in that form. Rhoades–Wilson proved the Hilbert series, while Murai–Rhoades–Wilson later proved the full Fields Frobenius structure. ([cambridge.org](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/forum-of-mathematics-pi/article/hilbert-series-of-the-superspace-coinvariant-ring/D7F1C91BE28068FC384AFE85B6AED8B8))

---

## Final normalization checklist

- Use \(u\) as an **ordinary positive fermionic Hilbert variable**, not a supertrace variable.
- Identify \(R_n^{(1,1)}=SR_n\).
- Rename Murai–Rhoades–Wilson’s fermionic variable \(z\) to \(u\).
- Use \([r]_q=1+q+\cdots+q^{r-1}\) and the standard Gaussian binomial.
- In RSK, the statistic-bearing tableau is the **recording tableau** \(Q(w)\); with row insertion, \(\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w)\). If a different RSK convention is used, the inverse permutation may appear.

Under these normalizations, the hook formula in t5/t6 is source-consistent and has no smallest counterexample.

## Audit verdict

I find **no broken step and no counterexample**. The proposed hook formula is correct, provided the final write-up states the external inputs and conventions precisely.

### Key checks

1. **Universality really gives the original bosonic \(c_\lambda(n)\).**  
   Yes. Lentfer’s diagonal supersymmetry theorem gives universal Frobenius coefficients \(c_{\lambda\mu}\), and his Hilbert-series proposition defines
   \[
   c_\lambda=\sum_{\mu\vdash n}c_{\lambda\mu}\langle s_\mu,h_{(1^n)}\rangle
   \]
   independent of \((k,j)\). For \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), so these are exactly the original bosonic Schur-Hilbert coefficients. For \((k,j)=(1,1)\), the same \(c_\lambda\) occur. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

2. **Triangular relation.**  
   Correct with the **ordinary unsigned** Hilbert variable \(u\). Lentfer’s one-even/one-odd specialization is
   \[
   s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1},\qquad s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
   \]
   with no alternating sign. Hence
   \[
   H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
   \]
   The \(\delta_{b,0}\) is exactly the empty partition contribution. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

3. **\(q\)-Pascal step.**  
   Correct. If \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\), then the subtraction uses
   \[
   q^{-1}q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}
   =
   q^{M-bd+\binom b2}q^{d-b},
   \]
   and
   \[
   \begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
   =
   \begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
   +q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q.
   \]
   This gives exactly \(\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q\), with no shift error.

4. **Fields + RSK.**  
   Correct, but the write-up should explicitly say **row-insertion RSK and recording tableau \(Q(w)\)**. Murai–Rhoades–Wilson’s \(C_{n,k}\) tableau formula has exponent
   \[
   \operatorname{maj}(T)+\binom{n-k}{2}-(n-k)\operatorname{des}(T)
   \]
   and Gaussian factor \(\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)\\ n-k\end{bmatrix}_q\); their descent convention is \(i\) appears in a higher row than \(i+1\). Pairing with \(h_{(1^n)}\) and using RSK with \(T=Q(w)\) gives \(\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w)\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

5. **Edge cases.**  
   All check out:
   - \(n=1\): only the identity has \(d=0\), so all nonempty hook coefficients vanish and \(c_\varnothing(1)=1\).
   - \(b=0\): rectangle height \(0\), so only \(\rho=\varnothing\); \(c_{(a)}(n)\) counts permutations with at least one descent and \(\operatorname{maj}(w)=a\).
   - \(b\ge n-1\): condition \(\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1\) is impossible, so coefficient is \(0\).
   - Vertical hooks \((1,1^b)\) are included; for \(b=n-2\) only the reverse permutation contributes, giving \(c_{(1,1^{n-2})}(n)=1\), while \(b=n-1\) gives \(0\).

## Theorem-ready proof skeleton

Let

# Problem We Are Solving
Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

These λ_t are weakly decreasing because j_{t+1}>j_t, nonnegative because j_t≤r-h+t, and at most r-h; the construction is reversible. For a subset J ⊆ [n], define its J-staircase st(J) = (st(J)_1,...,st(J)_n) by st(J)_i = |{a ∈ [i] : a ∉ J}|; equivalently st(J)_1 is 0 if 1 ∈ J and 1 if 1 ∉ J, and st(J)_{i+1}=st(J)_i if i+1 ∈ J while st(J)_{i+1}=st(J)_i+1 if i+1 ∉ J. Let [r]_q = 1+q+...+q^{r-1} for r ≥ 1 and [0]_q=0, and let [k]!_q = [k]_q [k-1]_q ... [1]_q. Define q-Stirling numbers Stir_q(n,k) by Stir_q(0,0)=1, Stir_q(0,k)=0 for k≠0, and Stir_q(n,k) = [k]_q Stir_q(n-1,k) + Stir_q(n-1,k-1). Then the polynomial identity holds: Σ_{J⊆[n]} z^{|J|} Π_{i=1}^n [st(J)_i]_q = Σ_{k=1}^n z^{n-k} [k]!_q Stir_q(n,k). Equivalently, the coefficient of z^r on the left is the q-degree generating function of all pairs consisting of a subset J⊆[n] of size r and a monomial x_1^{a_1}...x_n^{a_n} satisfying 0 ≤ a_i < st(J)_i for every i.  (from: literature_2301.09763)
    Proof sketch: Let G(n,k) be the coefficient of z^{n-k} on the left, i.e. the sum over subsets J⊆[n] with exactly k elements outside J. The formula st(J)_i=|[i]\J| makes the recurrence transparent. Split such J according to whether n∈J. If n∈J, then J'=J\{n} has k elements outside it in [n-1], the first n-1 staircase entries are those of J', and the final entry is k; this contributes [k]_q G(n-1,k). If n∉J, then J⊆[n-1] has k-1 elements outside it in [n-1], and adjoining the nth entry again multiplies by [k]_q; this contributes [k]_q G(n-1,k-1). Thus G(n,k)=[k]_q G(n-1,k)+[k]_q G(n-1,k-1), with G(1,1)=1 and the usual boundary values. The quantity H(n,k):=[k]!_q Stir_q(n,k) satisfies the same recurrence, since multiplying Stir_q(n,k)=[k]_q Stir_q(n-1,k)+Stir_q(n-1,k-1) by [k]!_q gives H(n,k)=[k]_q H(n-1,k)+[k]_q H(n-1,k-1). Hence G(n,k)=H(n,k). The monomial interpretation is immediate from Π_i [st(J)_i]_q = Σ_{0≤a_i<st(J)_i} q^{a_1+...+a_n}.
  • [arXiv:2301.09763 Theorem 4.12] Let C[x_n]=C[x_1,...,x_n], and let I_n=(C[x_n]^{S_n}_+) be the classical coinvariant ideal, equivalently I_n=(e_1(x_n),...,e_n(x_n)). For S⊆[n], write h_d(S) for the complete homogeneous symmetric polynomial of degree d in the variables {x_s : s∈S}. For J⊆[n], define f_J = Π_{j∈J} x_j Π_{i=j+1}^n (x_j-x_i), with empty products equal to 1. Define polynomials p_{J,1},...,p_{J,n} as follows, using the convention min(∅)=∞: if i<min(J), set p_{J,i}=h_i({i,i+1,...,n}); if i≥min(J), set s=max(J∩[i]), T=J∪{i+1,i+2,...,n}, r=n-|T|+1, and p_{J,i}=∂h_r(T)/∂x_s. A permutation w∈S_n is j-resentful if w(j)=n or if w(j)+1 occurs among w(j+1),...,w(n); it is j-Nietzschean otherwise. It is J-Nietzschean if it is j-Nietzschean for every j∈J. Let N_J be the finite subset of C^n consisting of the points (w(1),...,w(n)) for J-Nietzschean w∈S_n. The quotient C[x_n]/(p_{J,*}) is a complete intersection, hence a Poincare duality algebra with socle degree Σ_i st(J)_i-n. The coinvariant algebra C[x_n]/I_n is also Poincare duality with socle degree binom(n,2), f_J∉I_n when 1∉J, and deg f_J=Σ_i(i-st(J)_i), so the socle degrees differ by deg f_J. For the equality with gr I(N_J), deform the coinvariant ideal to ~I_n=(e_d(x)-e_d(1,...,n):1≤d≤n), whose variety is the embedded set S_n⊂C^n. Give SR_n the bigrading in which q records total x-degree and z records total θ-degree. Then Hilb(SR_n;q,z)=Σ_{k=1}^n z^{n-k} [k]!_q Stir_q(n,k). For the upper bound, for every J⊆[n] the paper constructs elements q_{J,i}∈SI_n whose fermionic degree is |J| and whose Gale-leading θ_J coefficient is p_{J,i}. The polynomials p_{J,i} are the regular sequence of Theorem 4.12, with degrees st(J)_i. Choose homogeneous representatives B_n(J) for a basis of C[x_n]/(p_{J,1},...,p_{J,n}); their degree generating function is Π_i [st(J)_i]_q. A triangular straightening argument in Gale order says that any f θ_J can be reduced modulo SI_n to a linear combination of B_n(J) θ_J plus terms with strictly larger fermionic monomial in Gale order. Descending induction over Gale order shows that the union over J of B_n(J) θ_J spans SR_n, giving Hilb(SR_n;q,z) ≤ Σ_J z^{|J|}Π_i[st(J)_i]_q. For the lower bound, use differential operators D_J built from minors of the complete-homogeneous matrix H. Their key triangular property is that D_J(δ_n), where δ_n is the Vandermonde, is superharmonic and has θ_J coefficient ±f_J⊙δ_n, with only θ_K terms for K≤Gale J. The annihilator of f_J⊙δ_n in C[x_n] is (I_n:f_J). By Theorem 4.12, choose B_n(J) mapping to a basis of C[x_n]/(I_n:f_J), again with degree generating function Π_i[st(J)_i]_q. The final expression with q-Stirling numbers is exactly Lemma 2.1.

The condition λ∈P(k,j,n) is exactly the condition under which the corresponding simple gl(k|j)-character s_λ(q/u) can appear.     Proof sketch: Start from the defining expansion s_λ(q/u) = ∑_{ν⊆λ} s_ν(q) s_{λ′/ν′}(u). Since q is a single variable, s_ν(q) is nonzero only when ν is a one-row partition ν=(r), in which case it equals q^r. Since u is a single variable, the skew Schur function s_{λ′/ν′}(u) is nonzero only when the skew shape λ′/ν′ is a horizontal strip, in which case it is the corresponding power of u. For λ=(a,1^b), so λ′=(b+1,1^(a−1)), the only row partitions ν=(r) for which λ′/ν′ is a horizontal strip are r=a and r=a−1. These give the two terms q^a u^b and q^(a−1)u^(b+1), respectively; this also covers a=1, where the r=0 contribution is allowed. The empty partition gives 1. If λ_2≥2, then λ′ has at least two cells in its second column, and removing ν′=(1^r) from the first column cannot make λ′/ν′ a horizontal strip, so every summand vanishes. **Verdict:** no mathematical correction is needed. The proof is ready. The only wording caveat is bibliographic: present the permutation product formula as **derived from** the Fields/Murai–Rhoades–Wilson Frobenius formula plus RSK, not necessarily as a verbatim theorem statement.

I checked the normalizations. Lentfer’s superspace Hilbert expansion uses the same universal Hilbert coefficients \(c_\lambda\) for all \((k,j)\); when \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), so these are the original bosonic coefficients. The \((1,1)\) superspace ring matches the usual \(SR_n\), and the Hilbert variables count dimensions positively, not by supertrace. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885)) Lentfer’s one-even/one-odd specialization is exactly
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1},\qquad s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
\]
with no sign. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885)) The Fields Frobenius formula and the \(C_{n,k}\) tableau expansion give the stated \(H_b\) formula after pairing with \(h_{(1^n)}\); the descent convention matches row-insertion RSK with the recording tableau \(Q(w)\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

**Theorem-ready proof summary.** Let
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and \(H_b(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)\). The hook specialization gives
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
\]
Fields/MRW plus row-insertion RSK gives
\[
H_b(q)=\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q .
\]
Equivalently, this is the coefficient extraction from
\[
\sum_{w\in S_n}q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\]
Define
\[
D_b(q)=\sum_{\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q .
\]
For each fixed \(w\), the contribution to \(D_b+q^{-1}D_{b-1}\) is converted by
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q+
q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\]
into the \(w\)-summand of \(H_b\), with the sole missing case \(b=d=0\) supplied by \(\delta_{b,0}\). Hence \(C_b=D_b\). Finally
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|},
\]
so
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(\operatorname{des}(w)-1-b),\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\right\}.
\]
Also \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\). The edge cases check: \(b=0\) counts permutations with at least one descent and \(\operatorname{maj}(w)=a\); \(b\ge n-1\) gives zero; \(n=1\) has only \(c_\varnothing(1)=1\).

## Audit verdict

---

## Theorem-ready external inputs

### 1. Universal super-Schur Hilbert expansion

Use Lentfer’s bosonic–fermionic coinvariant ring
\[
R_{n}^{(k,j)}
=
\mathbb C[x^{(1)},\dots,x^{(k)},\theta^{(1)},\dots,\theta^{(j)}]\Big/
\left\langle
\mathbb C[x^{(1)},\dots,x^{(k)},\theta^{(1)},\dots,\theta^{(j)}]^{\mathfrak S_n}_{+}
\right\rangle,
\]
where the \(x\)’s commute, the \(\theta\)’s anticommute, and \(\mathfrak S_n\) acts diagonally by permuting indices. Lentfer denotes the symmetric-group case by \(R_n^{(k,j)}\), so the ordinary \(m\)-set diagonal coinvariant algebra is \(R_n^{(m,0)}\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

The exact universality input is:

\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\lambda\in P(k,j,n)}
c_\lambda(n)\,s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u),
\qquad
P(k,j,n)=\{\lambda:\ell(\lambda)\le n,\ \lambda_{k+1}\le j\},
\]
with **nonnegative integers \(c_\lambda(n)\) independent of \((k,j)\)**. For \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), so these are exactly the ordinary Schur-Hilbert coefficients in the user’s \(m\)-boson expansion. For \(k=j=1\), the same \(c_\lambda(n)\) occur in the one-boson/one-fermion Hilbert series. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

**Caveat.** In the \((1,1)\) specialization, only \(\lambda_2\le1\) can contribute, i.e. \(\lambda=\varnothing\) or a hook \((a,1^b)\). Thus this specialization is sufficient for hook coefficients but does not determine nonhook coefficients.

---

### 2. Super-Schur normalization and signs

Use Lentfer’s positive super-Schur convention:
\[
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}
s_\nu(\mathbf q)\,s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(\mathbf u).
\]
This is also written plethystically as \(s_\lambda[\mathbf q-\epsilon\mathbf u]\), and **it is not the signed supertrace convention**. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

For one even variable \(q\) and one odd Hilbert variable \(u\),
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
\]
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\qquad(a\ge1,\ b\ge0),
\]
and
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=0\quad\text{if }\lambda\text{ is not contained in a }(1,1)\text{-hook}.
\]
This is exactly Lentfer’s Lemma 4.4. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

So, with
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u),
\]
the required triangular relation is
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
\]

**Sign caveat.** If someone uses a signed supertrace variable, then \(u\) is replaced by \(-u\), and the hook specialization would acquire alternating signs. That convention would break the proof. For example, at \(n=2\), the unsigned Hilbert series is \(1+q+u\), whereas a signed product would give \(1+q-u\).

---

### 3. Matching \(R_n^{(1,1)}\) with the superspace coinvariant ring \(SR_n\)

Murai–Rhoades–Wilson define
\[
\Omega_n=\mathbb F[x_1,\dots,x_n]\otimes \wedge\{\theta_1,\dots,\theta_n\},
\]
with diagonal action
\[
w\cdot x_i=x_{w(i)},\qquad w\cdot\theta_i=\theta_{w(i)}.
\]
They define
\[
SI_n=\big((\Omega_n)^{\mathfrak S_n}_{+}\big)
\]
and
\[
SR_n=\Omega_n/SI_n.
\]
They also record Solomon’s generating-set normalization
\[
SI_n=(e_1,\dots,e_n,de_1,\dots,de_n).
\]
Thus \(SR_n\) is exactly Lentfer’s \(R_n^{(1,1)}\), after identifying \(x_i=x_i^{(1)}\) and \(\theta_i=\theta_i^{(1)}\). Their variable \(z\) for fermionic degree should be renamed \(u\) in the hook proof. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

---

### 4. Superspace Hilbert/product formula

A source-clean input is Murai–Rhoades–Wilson’s Fields Frobenius formula:
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,z)
=
\sum_{k=1}^n C_{n,k}(\mathbf x;q)\,z^{n-k},
\]
where \(q\) tracks bosonic degree and \(z\) tracks fermionic degree. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

Their tableau expansion gives
\[
C_{n,k}(\mathbf x;q)
=
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(n)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)+\binom{n-k}{2}-(n-k)\operatorname{des}(T)}
\begin{bmatrix}
\operatorname{des}(T)\\ n-k
\end{bmatrix}_q
s_{\lambda(T)}(\mathbf x).
\]
Setting \(b=n-k\), pairing the Frobenius characteristic with \(h_{(1^n)}=h_1^n\) to forget the \(\mathfrak S_n\)-structure, and applying ordinary row-insertion RSK with \(\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w)\), gives
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}
\operatorname{des}(w)\\ b
\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
The Hilbert-series-from-Frobenius pairing convention is also explicitly stated by Lentfer. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

Equivalently,
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\]
Indeed, the coefficient of \(u^b\) in the \(w\)-summand is
\[
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{d+1}{2}}
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq[d]\\ |J|=b}}
q^{\sum_{i\notin J}i}
=
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
where \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\).

**Source caveat.** The sources state the \(q\)-Stirling Hilbert formula and the Frobenius formula; the above permutation product form should be presented as a short derivation, not as a verbatim quoted theorem unless a writeup independently cites a source where it is printed in that form. Rhoades–Wilson proved the Hilbert series, while Murai–Rhoades–Wilson later proved the full Fields Frobenius structure. ([cambridge.org](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/forum-of-mathematics-pi/article/hilbert-series-of-the-superspace-coinvariant-ring/D7F1C91BE28068FC384AFE85B6AED8B8))

---

## Final normalization checklist

- Use \(u\) as an **ordinary positive fermionic Hilbert variable**, not a supertrace variable.
- Identify \(R_n^{(1,1)}=SR_n\).
- Rename Murai–Rhoades–Wilson’s fermionic variable \(z\) to \(u\).
- Use \([r]_q=1+q+\cdots+q^{r-1}\) and the standard Gaussian binomial.
- In RSK, the statistic-bearing tableau is the **recording tableau** \(Q(w)\); with row insertion, \(\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w)\). If a different RSK convention is used, the inverse permutation may appear.

Under these normalizations, the hook formula in t5/t6 is source-consistent and has no smallest counterexample.

I checked the normalizations. Lentfer’s superspace Hilbert expansion uses the same universal Hilbert coefficients \(c_\lambda\) for all \((k,j)\); when \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), so these are the original bosonic coefficients. The \((1,1)\) superspace ring matches the usual \(SR_n\), and the Hilbert variables count dimensions positively, not by supertrace. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885)) Lentfer’s one-even/one-odd specialization is exactly
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1},\qquad s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
\]
with no sign. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885)) The Fields Frobenius formula and the \(C_{n,k}\) tableau expansion give the stated \(H_b\) formula after pairing with \(h_{(1^n)}\); the descent convention matches row-insertion RSK with the recording tableau \(Q(w)\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

**Theorem-ready proof summary.** Let
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and \(H_b(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)\). The hook specialization gives
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
\]
Fields/MRW plus row-insertion RSK gives
\[
H_b(q)=\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q .
\]
Equivalently, this is the coefficient extraction from
\[
\sum_{w\in S_n}q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\]
Define
\[
D_b(q)=\sum_{\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q .
\]
For each fixed \(w\), the contribution to \(D_b+q^{-1}D_{b-1}\) is converted by
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q+
q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\]
into the \(w\)-summand of \(H_b\), with the sole missing case \(b=d=0\) supplied by \(\delta_{b,0}\). Hence \(C_b=D_b\). Finally
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|},
\]
so
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(\operatorname{des}(w)-1-b),\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\right\}.
\]
Also \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\). The edge cases check: \(b=0\) counts permutations with at least one descent and \(\operatorname{maj}(w)=a\); \(b\ge n-1\) gives zero; \(n=1\) has only \(c_\varnothing(1)=1\).

## Theorem-ready Hilbert-level proof

Write

\[
d(w)=\operatorname{des}(w),\qquad M(w)=\operatorname{maj}(w),
\]

and define

\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1} c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0.
\]

Let

\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]

From the assumed \(GL(1|1)\) hook specialization,

\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
1+\sum_{a\ge1,b\ge0} c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
\left(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\right),
\]

so comparing coefficients of \(u^b\) gives the triangular relation

\[
\boxed{
H_b^{(n)}(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b^{(n)}(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
}
\tag{1}
\]

Now use the Fields/Rhoades-Wilson product formula

\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{M(w)-\binom{d(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{d(w)}(q^i+u).
\]

For fixed \(w\), the coefficient of \(u^b\) is

\[
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}}
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq [d]\\ |J|=b}}
q^{\sum_{i\notin J} i}
=
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq [d]\\ |J|=b}}
q^{M-\sum_{j\in J}j}.
\]

Using

\[
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq[d]\\ |J|=b}}q^{-\sum_{j\in J}j}
=
q^{-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix} d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]

we get

\[
\boxed{
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d(w)\ge b}}
q^{M(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix} d(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
}
\tag{2}
\]

Now define

\[
D_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{M(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix} d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

I claim \(C_b^{(n)}(q)=D_b^{(n)}(q)\). Indeed, for fixed \(w\), the contribution of \(D_b+q^{-1}D_{b-1}\) is

\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right),
\]

because

\[
q^{-1}q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}
=
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}q^{d-b}.
\]

By the \(q\)-Pascal identity,

\[
\begin{bmatrix} d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]

this equals the \(w\)-summand in \(H_b\). The only exceptional case is \(b=0,d=0\), i.e. the identity permutation, whose contribution is exactly the \(\delta_{b,0}\) term in (1). Therefore \(D_b\) satisfies the same triangular recurrence as \(C_b\), so

\[
\boxed{
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{M(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix} d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
}
\tag{3}
\]

Since

\[
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)} q^{|\rho|},
\]

we obtain the desired coefficient interpretation:

\[
\boxed{
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\quad d(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b),\\
a=M(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
}
\]

Also \(c_{\varnothing}(n)=1\). The exponent is always positive: if \(d\ge b+1\), then \(M\ge 1+\cdots+d\), so

\[
M-bd+\binom b2\ge \binom{d-b+1}{2}\ge 1.
\]

## Check: \(n=3\)

The descent-major data are:

- \(d=0,M=0\): one permutation;
- \(d=1,M=1\): two permutations;
- \(d=1,M=2\): two permutations;
- \(d=2,M=3\): one permutation.

Thus

\[
H_0=1+2q+2q^2+q^3,
\]

\[
H_1=2+3q+q^2,
\]

\[
H_2=1.
\]

The recurrence gives

\[
C_0=H_0-1=2q+2q^2+q^3,
\]

\[
C_1=H_1-q^{-1}C_0=q,
\]

\[
C_2=H_2-q^{-1}C_1=0.
\]

This matches the rectangle formula: for \(b=1\), only \(321\) has \(d\ge2\), and its contribution is

\[
q^{3-1\cdot2}=q.
\]

For \(n=4\), the same formula gives

\[
C_0=3q+5q^2+6q^3+5q^4+3q^5+q^6,
\]

\[
C_1=3q+5q^2+4q^3+q^4,
\]

\[
C_2=q,
\]

again confirming the exponent \(M-bd+\binom b2\) and the off-by-one rectangle \(b\times(d-1-b)\).

## Sufficiency and hidden assumptions

This is a fully Hilbert-level proof: no refined Frobenius coefficients \(c_{\lambda,\mu}\) are needed.

However, it is sufficient for the original hook-coefficient problem only after accepting the hidden universality input that the \(GL(1|1)\) superspace Hilbert expansion uses the same universal coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) as the ordinary multigraded Hilbert expansion. One also needs the positive, unsigned fermionic Hilbert convention, so that hooks specialize as

\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]

With those assumptions, the proof above directly recovers the original hook coefficients.
