## (1) Exact known inputs to cite/state

**Supersymmetric universality.** Use Lentfer’s bosonic–fermionic coinvariant ring
\[
R_n^{(k,j)}=\mathbb C[x^{(1)},\ldots,x^{(k)},\theta^{(1)},\ldots,\theta^{(j)}]/
\langle \mathbb C[x^{(1)},\ldots,x^{(k)},\theta^{(1)},\ldots,\theta^{(j)}]^{S_n}_{+}\rangle .
\]
For fixed \(n\), there are nonnegative integers \(c_\lambda(n)\), independent of \((k,j)\), such that
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=\sum_{\lambda\in P(k,j,n)} c_\lambda(n)\,s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u),
\qquad
P(k,j,n)=\{\lambda:\ell(\lambda)\le n,\ \lambda_{k+1}\le j\}.
\]
For \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), giving the original bosonic coefficients. For \((k,j)=(1,1)\), only \(\varnothing\) and hooks \((a,1^b)\) can contribute. Lentfer’s Hilbert series uses ordinary positive dimension-counting variables. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

**GL\((1|1)\) hook specialization.** Use Lentfer’s positive super-Schur convention
\[
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(\mathbf q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(\mathbf u),
\]
not a signed supertrace convention. In one even variable \(q\) and one odd Hilbert variable \(u\),
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,\qquad
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\quad(a\ge1,b\ge0),
\]
and non-\((1,1)\)-hook shapes specialize to \(0\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

**Superspace formula leading to the permutation product.** Identify \(R_n^{(1,1)}\) with the superspace coinvariant ring
\[
SR_n=\Omega_n/SI_n,\qquad
\Omega_n=\mathbb C[x_1,\ldots,x_n]\otimes\wedge\{\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_n\},
\]
with diagonal \(S_n\)-action and \(SI_n=((\Omega_n)^{S_n}_+)=(e_1,\ldots,e_n,de_1,\ldots,de_n)\). Murai–Rhoades–Wilson prove
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,z)=\sum_{k=1}^n C_{n,k}(\mathbf x;q)\,z^{n-k},
\]
where \(q\) is bosonic degree and \(z\) is fermionic degree, and
\[
C_{n,k}(\mathbf x;q)=
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(n)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)+\binom{n-k}{2}-(n-k)\operatorname{des}(T)}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)\\ n-k\end{bmatrix}_q
s_{\lambda(T)}(\mathbf x).
\]
Pairing Frobenius with \(h_{(1^n)}=h_1^n\), then applying row-insertion RSK with the recording tableau carrying descents, gives, after renaming \(z=u\),
\[
H_b(q):=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q .
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\]
This product form should be presented as a derivation from the Frobenius formula plus RSK, not as a verbatim cited theorem. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

---

## (2) Term-by-term triangular recurrence check

Let
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0,
\qquad
H_b(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]
The GL\((1|1)\) expansion is
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
c_\varnothing(n)+
\sum_{a\ge1,b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\bigl(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\bigr).
\]
Since the degree-zero component is one-dimensional, \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\). For fixed \(b\), the coefficient of \(u^b\) receives: \(1\) only if \(b=0\); \(C_b(q)\) from \(q^a u^b\); and \(q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q)\) from the second term attached to hooks with leg \(b-1\). Thus
\[
\boxed{H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).}
\]

The \(d=0\) case is also consistent with the permutation product. The only permutation with \(d(w)=0\) is the identity, with \(\operatorname{maj}(w)=0\), and its product contribution is the empty product \(1\), contributing only to \(H_0\). In the solved form
\[
D_b(q)=\sum_{\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
the identity contributes to no \(D_b\); it is exactly the missing \(\delta_{b,0}\). For every \(w\) with \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge1\), the contribution of \(D_b+q^{-1}D_{b-1}\) is converted by
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\]
into the \(w\)-summand of \(H_b\). No off-by-one issue appears.

---

## (3) Edge-case audit

For \(n=1\), \(S_1\) has only the identity, so \(H_0=1\), \(H_b=0\) for \(b>0\), \(c_\varnothing(1)=1\), and all nonempty hook coefficients vanish. For \(b=0\), the rectangle in the final formula is \(0\times(\operatorname{des}(w)-1)\), so only the empty partition \(\rho\) occurs; hence \(c_{(a)}(n)\) counts permutations with at least one descent and \(\operatorname{maj}(w)=a\), equivalently all permutations of major index \(a\ge1\). For \(b\ge n-1\), the final hook coefficient is zero because it requires \(\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1\), impossible since \(\operatorname{des}(w)\le n-1\). This is compatible with \(H_{n-1}\neq0\): the top fermionic term is supplied by \(q^{-1}C_{n-2}\), not by \(C_{n-1}\). All Gaussian binomials should use the convention \(\bigl[\begin{smallmatrix}r\\s\end{smallmatrix}\bigr]_q=0\) unless \(0\le s\le r\); equivalently, the final rectangle \(b\times(\operatorname{des}(w)-1-b)\) is used only when its width is nonnegative.

---

## (4) Last warnings for the final write-up

- Use \(u\) as an **ordinary positive fermionic Hilbert variable**, not a signed supertrace variable.
- Explicitly rename Murai–Rhoades–Wilson’s fermionic variable \(z\) to \(u\).
- State \(R_n^{(1,1)}=SR_n\) before using the superspace formula.
- Do not cite the permutation product as a printed theorem unless you have a source for exactly that form; derive it from the Fields/Murai–Rhoades–Wilson Frobenius formula plus RSK.
- State the RSK convention: row insertion, recording tableau \(Q(w)\), with \(\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w)\).
- Keep \(c_\lambda(n)\) for Hilbert coefficients separate from Lentfer’s finer Frobenius coefficients \(c_{\lambda\mu}\).
- Treat \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\) separately; nonempty hooks are \((a,1^b)\) with \(a\ge1\).
- Use the \(q\)-Pascal orientation
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b-1\end{bmatrix}_q .
\]

**No issue remains.**

# Problem We Are Solving
Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

  • [arXiv:2404.17919 Theorem 1.2 (Rhoades–Wilson transfer principle)] Let K be a field of characteristic 0, let S=K[x_1,...,x_n], and let Ω=S⊗∧{θ_1,...,θ_n}, with S_n acting by simultaneous permutation of the x_i and θ_i. (from: literature_2404.17919)
    Proof sketch: The paper uses this as an input from Rhoades–Wilson. The underlying mechanism is a filtration of Ω, hence of SR, by an order on fermionic supports J. For a fixed J, examine the associated-graded quotient whose leading exterior term is h θ_J. Rhoades–Wilson compute the ideal of leading coefficients h which become zero at this stage: it is exactly a:f_J, i.e. h θ_J is killed modulo lower fermionic-support terms precisely when h f_J lies in the ordinary coinvariant ideal a. The factor f_J is the triangular/Vandermonde-type factor that appears when isolating the θ_J-coefficient of elements in the differential closure of a. Thus gr(SR) is, as a graded vector space, the direct sum over J of S/(a:f_J) θ_J. If B(J) is a homogeneous basis for each summand, the elements b θ_J have distinct leading terms and are linearly independent in the associated graded; conversely they span each associated-graded subquotient, so a standard filtered-vector-space induction gives spanning and independence in SR itself.
  • [arXiv:2404.17919 Theorem 7.1] For J⊆[n], define the J-staircase st(J)=(st(J)_1,...,st(J)_n) recursively by st(J)_1=0 if 1∈J and st(J)_1=1 if 1∉J; for i>1, st(J)_i=st(J)_{i-1} if i∈J and st(J)_i=st(J)_{i-1}+1 if i∉J. Define the modified polynomial f~_J=∏_{j∈J}∏_{i=j+1}^n(x_j-x_i), obtained from f_J by deleting the coordinate factors x_j. Let A be the subarrangement of the augmented braid arrangement consisting of all coordinate hyperplanes H_{x_1},...,H_{x_n} together with all hyperplanes H_{x_j-x_i} for j∉J and j<i≤n. This A is a southwest arrangement, hence free. Its complementary product in the augmented braid arrangement is f~_J, so Proposition 5.2 identifies S/(a:f~_J) with the Solomon–Terao algebra ST(A,i), where i sends every partial derivative ∂_k to 1. The h-sequence of A is h_k=st(J)_k for k∉J and h_k=st(J)_k+1 for k∈J. Theorem 6.2 gives a monomial basis of ST(A,i), namely all x^b with b_k<h_k. The inequalities a_k<st(J)_k imply b_k<h_k, so these images are distinct members of the monomial basis of S/(a:f~_J). Let [r]_q = 1 + q + ... + q^{r-1}, [k]!_q = [k]_q [k-1]_q ... [1]_q, and let the q-Stirling numbers be defined by Stir_q(1,1)=1, Stir_q(1,k)=0 for k>1, and Stir_q(n,k)=Stir_q(n-1,k-1)+[k]_q Stir_q(n-1,k).     Proof sketch: Choose distinct rationals α_1,...,α_k and let Y_{n,k} ⊂ Q^n be the points whose coordinate set is exactly {α_1,...,α_k}. The point (y_1,...,y_n) corresponds equivariantly to the ordered set partition whose i-th block is {j : y_j=α_i}. Let I(Y) be the vanishing ideal and T(Y) the ideal generated by top homogeneous parts of elements of I(Y). Then Q[x_n]/T(Y) has dimension |Y|=|OP_{n,k}|. Hence every standard monomial is in M_{n,k}, the set of monomials divisible by no x_i^k and no such x(S). Theorem 4.9 constructs a degree-preserving bijection Ψ: OP_{n,k} → M_{n,k}: recursively remove n; if n is inserted into an existing block in position i+1, multiply by x_n^i; if n is inserted as a singleton block in position i+1, use Lemma 4.7 to choose the unique size n-k set S for which x(S) divides m(S) times the previous monomial, and multiply by m(S)x_n^i. Conversely every pair (π,S) with S⊂Asc(π), |S|=n-k, gives an element of OP_{n,k}. For a permutation π, let maj(π)=∑_{i: π_i>π_{i+1}} i and asc(π)=|Asc(π)|.     Proof sketch: Fix π and write h=n-k and r=asc(π). If h>r there are no star sets, matching the zero q-binomial. Otherwise list Asc(π)={a_1<...<a_r}. A star set S={a_{j_1}<...<a_{j_h}} is equivalent to a partition λ inside an h by r-h rectangle by setting λ_t = r-h-j_t+t for t=1,...,h. These λ_t are weakly decreasing because j_{t+1}>j_t, nonnegative because j_t≤r-h+t, and at most r-h; the construction is reversible. Its size is |λ|=∑_{t=1}^h (r-j_t+1)-binom(h+1,2), since r-j_t+1 is the number of ascents weakly to the right of a_{j_t}. Now maj(π^c)=∑_{a∈Asc(π)} a and maj(π)+maj(π^c)=binom(n,2). Also (n-k)(k-1)+binom(k,2)=binom(n,2)-binom(h+1,2). Substituting the definition of maj(σ) gives comaj(π,S)=maj(π)+|λ|. Thus the contribution of all S for this fixed π is q^{maj(π)} times the generating function of partitions in the h by r-h rectangle, namely [r choose h]_q=[asc(π) choose n-k]_q. For a subset J ⊆ [n], define its J-staircase st(J) = (st(J)_1,...,st(J)_n) by st(J)_i = |{a ∈ [i] : a ∉ J}|; equivalently st(J)_1 is 0 if 1 ∈ J and 1 if 1 ∉ J, and st(J)_{i+1}=st(J)_i if i+1 ∈ J while st(J)_{i+1}=st(J)_i+1 if i+1 ∉ J. Let [r]_q = 1+q+...+q^{r-1} for r ≥ 1 and [0]_q=0, and let [k]!_q = [k]_q [k-1]_q ... [1]_q. Define q-Stirling numbers Stir_q(n,k) by Stir_q(0,0)=1, Stir_q(0,k)=0 for k≠0, and Stir_q(n,k) = [k]_q Stir_q(n-1,k) + Stir_q(n-1,k-1). Then the polynomial identity holds: Σ_{J⊆[n]} z^{|J|} Π_{i=1}^n [st(J)_i]_q = Σ_{k=1}^n z^{n-k} [k]!_q Stir_q(n,k).     Proof sketch: Let G(n,k) be the coefficient of z^{n-k} on the left, i.e. the sum over subsets J⊆[n] with exactly k elements outside J. The formula st(J)_i=|[i]\J| makes the recurrence transparent. Split such J according to whether n∈J. If n∈J, then J'=J\{n} has k elements outside it in [n-1], the first n-1 staircase entries are those of J', and the final entry is k; this contributes [k]_q G(n-1,k). If n∉J, then J⊆[n-1] has k-1 elements outside it in [n-1], and adjoining the nth entry again multiplies by [k]_q; this contributes [k]_q G(n-1,k-1). Thus G(n,k)=[k]_q G(n-1,k)+[k]_q G(n-1,k-1), with G(1,1)=1 and the usual boundary values. The quantity H(n,k):=[k]!_q Stir_q(n,k) satisfies the same recurrence, since multiplying Stir_q(n,k)=[k]_q Stir_q(n-1,k)+Stir_q(n-1,k-1) by [k]!_q gives H(n,k)=[k]_q H(n-1,k)+[k]_q H(n-1,k-1). Hence G(n,k)=H(n,k). The monomial interpretation is immediate from Π_i [st(J)_i]_q = Σ_{0≤a_i<st(J)_i} q^{a_1+...+a_n}.

    Proof sketch: The proof has matching upper and lower bounds. For the upper bound, for every J⊆[n] the paper constructs elements q_{J,i}∈SI_n whose fermionic degree is |J| and whose Gale-leading θ_J coefficient is p_{J,i}. The polynomials p_{J,i} are the regular sequence of Theorem 4.12, with degrees st(J)_i. A triangular straightening argument in Gale order says that any f θ_J can be reduced modulo SI_n to a linear combination of B_n(J) θ_J plus terms with strictly larger fermionic monomial in Gale order. Their key triangular property is that D_J(δ_n), where δ_n is the Vandermonde, is superharmonic and has θ_J coefficient ±f_J⊙δ_n, with only θ_K terms for K≤Gale J. The final expression with q-Stirling numbers is exactly Lemma 2.1.

For k,j ≥ 0 let R_n^(k,j) be the symmetric-group bosonic-fermionic diagonal coinvariant ring with k sets of n commuting variables and j sets of n anticommuting variables, i.e. R_n^(k,j) = Sym(C^(k|j) ⊗ C^n)/⟨Sym(C^(k|j) ⊗ C^n)_+^(S_n)⟩, with S_n acting diagonally by permuting the n indices. Let P(k,j,n) be the set of partitions λ with length ℓ(λ) ≤ n and λ_(k+1) ≤ j, with missing parts interpreted as 0. For alphabets q = q_1,...,q_k and u = u_1,...,u_j, define the super Schur function by s_λ(q/u) = ∑_{ν⊆λ} s_ν(q) s_{λ′/ν′}(u). Then there exist nonnegative integers c_λ, depending only on n and λ and independent of k and j, such that for every k,j one has Hilb(R_n^(k,j); q; u) = ∑_{λ∈P(k,j,n)} c_λ s_λ(q/u). More explicitly, if c_{λμ} are the universal Frobenius coefficients from Corollary 1.3, then c_λ = ∑_{μ⊢n} c_{λμ} ⟨s_μ(z), h_(1^n)(z)⟩. In particular, when j = 0 this specializes to the ordinary bosonic Schur expansion Hilb(R_n^(k,0); q_1,...,q_k) = ∑_{ℓ(λ)≤min(k,n)} c_λ s_λ(q_1,...,q_k).  (from: literature_2505.14885)
    Proof sketch: Use Corollary 1.3, which gives the multigraded Frobenius series in the form Frob(R_n^(k,j); q; u) = ∑_{λ∈P(k,j,n)} ∑_{μ⊢n} c_{λμ} s_λ(q/u) s_μ(z), where c_{λμ} are nonnegative and independent of k,j. The Hilbert series is recovered from the Frobenius series by taking the Hall inner product in the z-variables with h_(1^n)(z): Hilb(R_n^(k,j); q; u) = ⟨Frob(R_n^(k,j); q; u), h_(1^n)(z)⟩. Pull the inner product through the finite sum over μ for each λ and set c_λ = ∑_{μ⊢n} c_{λμ}⟨s_μ,h_(1^n)⟩. The factors ⟨s_μ,h_(1^n)⟩ are nonnegative integers, so c_λ is nonnegative; independence of k,j is inherited from Corollary 1.3. The condition λ∈P(k,j,n) is exactly the condition under which the corresponding simple gl(k|j)-character s_λ(q/u) can appear. For the purely bosonic case j=0, the super Schur function reduces to the usual Schur function s_λ(q), and P(k,0,n) imposes ℓ(λ)≤min(k,n), giving the ordinary multigraded coinvariant Hilbert coefficients.
  • [arXiv:2505.14885 Lemma 4.4] For the super Schur functions in one bosonic variable q and one fermionic variable u, one has s_∅(q/u) = 1. If λ is a hook partition λ = (a,1^b) with a ≥ 1 and b ≥ 0, then s_λ(q/u) = q^a u^b + q^(a−1) u^(b+1). If λ is neither empty nor of the form (a,1^b), equivalently if λ_2 ≥ 2, then s_λ(q/u) = 0.  (from: literature_2505.14885)
    Proof sketch: Start from the defining expansion s_λ(q/u) = ∑_{ν⊆λ} s_ν(q) s_{λ′/ν′}(u). Since q is a single variable, s_ν(q) is nonzero only when ν is a one-row partition ν=(r), in which case it equals q^r. Since u is a single variable, the skew Schur function s_{λ′/ν′}(u) is nonzero only when the skew shape λ′/ν′ is a horizontal strip, in which case it is the corresponding power of u. For λ=(a,1^b), so λ′=(b+1,1^(a−1)), the only row partitions ν=(r) for which λ′/ν′ is a horizontal strip are r=a and r=a−1. These give the two terms q^a u^b and q^(a−1)u^(b+1), respectively; this also covers a=1, where the r=0 contribution is allowed. The empty partition gives 1. If λ_2≥2, then λ′ has at least two cells in its second column, and removing ν′=(1^r) from the first column cannot make λ′/ν′ a horizontal strip, so every summand vanishes. Useful consequence for hook coefficients: combining this lemma with Proposition 5.1 at k=j=1 gives Hilb(R_n^(1,1);q;u)=c_∅+∑_{a≥1,b≥0} c_(a,1^b)(q^a u^b+q^(a−1)u^(b+1)). Thus, if h_{r,s}=[q^r u^s]Hilb(R_n^(1,1);q;u), then for a≥1 and b≥0, c_(a,1^b)=∑_{i=0}^b (−1)^i h_{a+i,b−i}; equivalently h_{a,b}=c_(a,1^b)+c_(a+1,1^(b−1)) with the second term omitted when b=0.
  • [arXiv:2505.14885 Equation (50), quoted theorem of Rhoades--Wilson] For the coinvariant ring R_n^(1,1) with one set of n commuting variables and one set of n anticommuting variables, the bigraded Hilbert series is Hilb(R_n^(1,1); q; u) = ∑_{d=0}^n [d]_q! Stir_q(n,d) u^(n−d). Here [d]_q = 1+q+⋯+q^(d−1), [d]_q! = [d]_q [d−1]_q ⋯ [1]_q with [0]_q! = 1, and the q-Stirling numbers are defined by Stir_q(n,d) = [d]_q Stir_q(n−1,d) + Stir_q(n−1,d−1), with initial conditions Stir_q(0,d)=δ_{d,0}.  (from: literature_2505.14885)
    Proof sketch: Lentfer records this formula as a theorem proved by Rhoades--Wilson, originally conjectured by Sagan--Swanson; the present paper does not reprove it, but uses it as an input in Section 5. In the paper, substituting this formula into the cancellation identity of Proposition 5.2 yields the Sagan--Swanson identity ∑_{d=0}^n [d]_q! Stir_q(n,d)(−q)^(n−d)=1. For extracting hook Schur coefficients, combine this quoted Hilbert formula with Lemma 4.4 and Proposition 5.1. If h_{r,s}=[q^r u^s]Hilb(R_n^(1,1);q;u), then the formula gives h_{r,s}=[q^r]([n−s]_q! Stir_q(n,n−s)) for 0≤s≤n. Therefore the hook coefficient in the universal Hilbert expansion satisfies c_(a,1^b)=∑_{i=0}^b (−1)^i [q^(a+i)]([n−b+i]_q! Stir_q(n,n−b+i)) for a≥1,b≥0, with c_∅=1. This is the GL(1|1) extraction mechanism: the two monomials in the character of a hook simple module make the hook multiplicity an alternating finite difference of the q-Stirling Hilbert coefficients.

## Failed / Ruled-out Approaches
  • Use rectangle b by (des(T)-b) directly for C_b: It gives Fields' H_b summand rather than the hook coefficient C_b and double-counts the q^{-1}C_{b-1} contribution; for mu=(1,1,1), b=1 it gives C_1=q+q^2 instead of q.
  • Use a b by des(T)-b rectangle for hook coefficients.: That gives the Fields H_b summand before subtracting the q^{-1}C_{b-1} contribution; the correct hook coefficient uses [des(T)-1 choose b]_q and hence a b by des(T)-1-b rectangle.
  • Derive the result from the scalar Hilbert expansion alone: The scalar expansion does not by itself define the refined coefficients c_{lambda,mu} or justify the GL(1|1) superspace specialization; the stronger Frobenius/superspace universality theorem is needed.
  • Use the rectangle b by (des(T)-b): That gives Fields' H_b summand [des(T) choose b]_q rather than the hook coefficient C_b and overcounts, as shown by vertical-shape checks for n=3 and n=4.
  • Use a b by des(T)-b rectangle for the hook coefficient.: That rectangle gives the superspace fermionic-degree coefficient H_b before subtracting the q^{-1}C_{b-1} contribution; the hook coefficient requires [des(T)-1 choose b]_q.
  • Derive the hook formula from the original bosonic scalar Hilbert expansion alone, without superspace or GL(1|1) input.: The scalar expansion alone does not isolate hook Schur coefficients; one needs the superspace specialization or an equivalent triangular extraction mechanism.
  • Derive the hook coefficients from the scalar m-variable Hilbert expansion alone: The scalar expansion alone does not justify that the same c_lambda(n) appear in the GL(1|1) superspace Hilbert expansion; that universality/specialization statement is an external assumption.
  • Derive the theorem from the scalar Hilbert Schur expansion alone: The scalar expansion alone does not justify the (1|1) superspace specialization or the refined Fields Frobenius formula; those must be cited as external theorems.

## (1) Exact known inputs to cite/state

**Supersymmetric universality.** Use Lentfer’s bosonic–fermionic coinvariant ring
\[
R_n^{(k,j)}=\mathbb C[x^{(1)},\ldots,x^{(k)},\theta^{(1)},\ldots,\theta^{(j)}]/
\langle \mathbb C[x^{(1)},\ldots,x^{(k)},\theta^{(1)},\ldots,\theta^{(j)}]^{S_n}_{+}\rangle .
\]
For fixed \(n\), there are nonnegative integers \(c_\lambda(n)\), independent of \((k,j)\), such that
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=\sum_{\lambda\in P(k,j,n)} c_\lambda(n)\,s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u),
\qquad
P(k,j,n)=\{\lambda:\ell(\lambda)\le n,\ \lambda_{k+1}\le j\}.
\]
For \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), giving the original bosonic coefficients. For \((k,j)=(1,1)\), only \(\varnothing\) and hooks \((a,1^b)\) can contribute. Lentfer’s Hilbert series uses ordinary positive dimension-counting variables. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

**GL\((1|1)\) hook specialization.** Use Lentfer’s positive super-Schur convention
\[
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(\mathbf q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(\mathbf u),
\]
not a signed supertrace convention. In one even variable \(q\) and one odd Hilbert variable \(u\),
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,\qquad
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\quad(a\ge1,b\ge0),
\]
and non-\((1,1)\)-hook shapes specialize to \(0\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

**Superspace formula leading to the permutation product.** Identify \(R_n^{(1,1)}\) with the superspace coinvariant ring
\[
SR_n=\Omega_n/SI_n,\qquad
\Omega_n=\mathbb C[x_1,\ldots,x_n]\otimes\wedge\{\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_n\},
\]
with diagonal \(S_n\)-action and \(SI_n=((\Omega_n)^{S_n}_+)=(e_1,\ldots,e_n,de_1,\ldots,de_n)\). Murai–Rhoades–Wilson prove
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,z)=\sum_{k=1}^n C_{n,k}(\mathbf x;q)\,z^{n-k},
\]
where \(q\) is bosonic degree and \(z\) is fermionic degree, and
\[
C_{n,k}(\mathbf x;q)=
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(n)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)+\binom{n-k}{2}-(n-k)\operatorname{des}(T)}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)\\ n-k\end{bmatrix}_q
s_{\lambda(T)}(\mathbf x).
\]
Pairing Frobenius with \(h_{(1^n)}=h_1^n\), then applying row-insertion RSK with the recording tableau carrying descents, gives, after renaming \(z=u\),
\[
H_b(q):=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q .
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\]
This product form should be presented as a derivation from the Frobenius formula plus RSK, not as a verbatim cited theorem. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

---

## (2) Term-by-term triangular recurrence check

Let
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0,
\qquad
H_b(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]
The GL\((1|1)\) expansion is
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
c_\varnothing(n)+
\sum_{a\ge1,b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\bigl(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\bigr).
\]
Since the degree-zero component is one-dimensional, \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\). For fixed \(b\), the coefficient of \(u^b\) receives: \(1\) only if \(b=0\); \(C_b(q)\) from \(q^a u^b\); and \(q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q)\) from the second term attached to hooks with leg \(b-1\). Thus
\[
\boxed{H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).}
\]

The \(d=0\) case is also consistent with the permutation product. The only permutation with \(d(w)=0\) is the identity, with \(\operatorname{maj}(w)=0\), and its product contribution is the empty product \(1\), contributing only to \(H_0\). In the solved form
\[
D_b(q)=\sum_{\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
the identity contributes to no \(D_b\); it is exactly the missing \(\delta_{b,0}\). For every \(w\) with \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge1\), the contribution of \(D_b+q^{-1}D_{b-1}\) is converted by
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\]
into the \(w\)-summand of \(H_b\). No off-by-one issue appears.

---

## (3) Edge-case audit

For \(n=1\), \(S_1\) has only the identity, so \(H_0=1\), \(H_b=0\) for \(b>0\), \(c_\varnothing(1)=1\), and all nonempty hook coefficients vanish. For \(b=0\), the rectangle in the final formula is \(0\times(\operatorname{des}(w)-1)\), so only the empty partition \(\rho\) occurs; hence \(c_{(a)}(n)\) counts permutations with at least one descent and \(\operatorname{maj}(w)=a\), equivalently all permutations of major index \(a\ge1\). For \(b\ge n-1\), the final hook coefficient is zero because it requires \(\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1\), impossible since \(\operatorname{des}(w)\le n-1\). This is compatible with \(H_{n-1}\neq0\): the top fermionic term is supplied by \(q^{-1}C_{n-2}\), not by \(C_{n-1}\). All Gaussian binomials should use the convention \(\bigl[\begin{smallmatrix}r\\s\end{smallmatrix}\bigr]_q=0\) unless \(0\le s\le r\); equivalently, the final rectangle \(b\times(\operatorname{des}(w)-1-b)\) is used only when its width is nonnegative.

---

## (4) Last warnings for the final write-up

- Use \(u\) as an **ordinary positive fermionic Hilbert variable**, not a signed supertrace variable.
- Explicitly rename Murai–Rhoades–Wilson’s fermionic variable \(z\) to \(u\).
- State \(R_n^{(1,1)}=SR_n\) before using the superspace formula.
- Do not cite the permutation product as a printed theorem unless you have a source for exactly that form; derive it from the Fields/Murai–Rhoades–Wilson Frobenius formula plus RSK.
- State the RSK convention: row insertion, recording tableau \(Q(w)\), with \(\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w)\).
- Keep \(c_\lambda(n)\) for Hilbert coefficients separate from Lentfer’s finer Frobenius coefficients \(c_{\lambda\mu}\).
- Treat \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\) separately; nonempty hooks are \((a,1^b)\) with \(a\ge1\).
- Use the \(q\)-Pascal orientation
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b-1\end{bmatrix}_q .
\]

**No issue remains.**

\]

\[

\]

The \(d=0\) case is also consistent with the permutation product. The only permutation with \(d(w)=0\) is the identity, with \(\operatorname{maj}(w)=0\), and its product contribution is the empty product \(1\), contributing only to \(H_0\). In the solved form

+
q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\]
into the \(w\)-summand of \(H_b\). No off-by-one issue appears.

---

## (3) Edge-case audit

For \(n=1\), \(S_1\) has only the identity, so \(H_0=1\), \(H_b=0\) for \(b>0\), \(c_\varnothing(1)=1\), and all nonempty hook coefficients vanish. For \(b=0\), the rectangle in the final formula is \(0\times(\operatorname{des}(w)-1)\), so only the empty partition \(\rho\) occurs; hence \(c_{(a)}(n)\) counts permutations with at least one descent and \(\operatorname{maj}(w)=a\), equivalently all permutations of major index \(a\ge1\). For \(b\ge n-1\), the final hook coefficient is zero because it requires \(\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1\), impossible since \(\operatorname{des}(w)\le n-1\). This is compatible with \(H_{n-1}\neq0\): the top fermionic term is supplied by \(q^{-1}C_{n-2}\), not by \(C_{n-1}\). All Gaussian binomials should use the convention \(\bigl[\begin{smallmatrix}r\\s\end{smallmatrix}\bigr]_q=0\) unless \(0\le s\le r\); equivalently, the final rectangle \(b\times(\operatorname{des}(w)-1-b)\) is used only when its width is nonnegative.

---

=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b-1\end{bmatrix}_q .
\]

**No issue remains.**

I rechecked the recurrence, the \(q\)-Pascal step, and small cases. **No mathematical correction appears needed.** The only caveats are proof-wording/normalization caveats listed below.

## 1. Triangular recurrence

Use the positive Hilbert-series convention: the fermionic variable \(u\) counts dimension, not supertrace. Lentfer’s universal Hilbert expansion gives the same \(c_\lambda(n)\) after passing to the \((1,1)\) bosonic–fermionic specialization, and in one even/one odd variable the hook specialization is
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,\qquad
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
Non-hooks vanish in \(GL(1|1)\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

Let
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1} c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]
Then
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
c_\varnothing(n)+\sum_{a\ge1,b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}).
\]
The constant term forces \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\). Taking the coefficient of \(u^b\) gives
\[
\boxed{H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).}
\]
For \(b=0\), this is \(H_0=1+C_0\). For \(b>0\), the \(\delta\)-term vanishes.

The identity permutation has \(d=\operatorname{des}=0\), \(\operatorname{maj}=0\), and contributes exactly \(1\) to \(H_0\) and nothing to \(H_b\) for \(b>0\). That is precisely the \(\delta_{b,0}\) term.

## 2. \(q\)-Pascal check

Murai–Rhoades–Wilson identify \(SR_n\) as the one-boson/one-fermion superspace coinvariant ring and prove
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,z)=\sum_{k=1}^n C_{n,k}(\mathbf x;q)z^{n-k},
\]
with the standard-tableau expansion of \(C_{n,k}\). Pairing Frobenius with \(h_1^n\), then using row-insertion RSK with \(\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w)\), gives
\[
H_b(q)=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q .
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_b H_b(q)u^b
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\]
([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

Define the claimed solution
\[
D_b(q)=
\sum_{\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q .
\]
For a fixed permutation with \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge1\) and \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\), the contribution to
\[
D_b+q^{-1}D_{b-1}
\]
is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right),
\]
because
\[
-1+M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}
=
M-bd+\binom b2+(d-b).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
this equals the \(w\)-summand of \(H_b\). The sole \(d=0\) case is the identity permutation, already accounted for by \(\delta_{b,0}\). Hence \(C_b=D_b\).

## 3. Small cases

### \(n=1\)
Only \(w=1\), with \((d,M)=(0,0)\).
\[
H_0=1,\qquad H_b=0\ (b>0).
\]
Thus
\[
C_b=0\quad\text{for all }b\ge0,\qquad c_\varnothing(1)=1.
\]

### \(n=2\)
Descent-major inventory:
\[
(0,0)^1,\quad (1,1)^1.
\]
So
\[
H_0=1+q,\qquad H_1=1.
\]
The recurrence gives
\[
C_0=q,\qquad C_1=H_1-q^{-1}C_0=1-1=0.
\]
Matches the claimed formula.

### \(n=3\)
Descent-major inventory:
\[
(0,0)^1,\quad (1,1)^2,\quad (1,2)^2,\quad (2,3)^1.
\]
Hence
\[
H_0=1+2q+2q^2+q^3,
\]
\[
H_1=2+3q+q^2,
\]
\[
H_2=1.
\]
The recurrence gives
\[
C_0=H_0-1=2q+2q^2+q^3,
\]
\[
C_1=H_1-q^{-1}C_0=q,
\]
\[
C_2=H_2-q^{-1}C_1=0.
\]
So the only nonzero nonempty hook coefficients are
\[
c_{(1)}=2,\quad c_{(2)}=2,\quad c_{(3)}=1,\quad c_{(1,1)}=1.
\]

### \(n=4\)
Descent-major inventory:
\[
(0,0)^1,\ (1,1)^3,\ (1,2)^5,\ (1,3)^3,
\]
\[
(2,3)^3,\ (2,4)^5,\ (2,5)^3,\ (3,6)^1.
\]
Thus
\[
H_0=1+3q+5q^2+6q^3+5q^4+3q^5+q^6,
\]
\[
H_1=3+8q+11q^2+9q^3+4q^4+q^5,
\]
\[
H_2=3+6q+4q^2+q^3,
\]
\[
H_3=1.
\]
The recurrence gives
\[
C_0=3q+5q^2+6q^3+5q^4+3q^5+q^6,
\]
\[
C_1=3q+5q^2+4q^3+q^4,
\]
\[
C_2=q,
\]
\[
C_3=0.
\]
These also agree directly with the claimed \(D_b\)-formula. For example, for \(b=1\), the \(d=2\) permutations contribute \(3q+5q^2+3q^3\), and the decreasing permutation with \(d=3,M=6\) contributes \(q^3+q^4\), giving \(C_1=3q+5q^2+4q^3+q^4\).

## 4. Final theorem statement

Let
\[
\operatorname{Des}(w)=\{i:w_i>w_{i+1}\},\quad
d(w)=|\operatorname{Des}(w)|,\quad
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in\operatorname{Des}(w)}i.
\]
Then
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]
For \(a\ge1\) and \(b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

No correction to the formula is needed. The proof should explicitly state: \(u\) is a positive fermionic Hilbert variable, \(R_n^{(1,1)}=SR_n\), the MRW permutation formula is derived via Frobenius plus row-insertion RSK, and Gaussian binomials are zero outside their natural range.

# Problem We Are Solving
Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

  • [arXiv:2505.24027 Equation (2.5) (standard-tableau Schur expansion of C_{n,k})] Fix integers n ≥ 1 and 1 ≤ k ≤ n. Let SYT(n) denote the set of standard Young tableaux with entries 1,2,...,n. For T ∈ SYT(n), let λ(T) be its shape, let Des(T) = {i ∈ {1,...,n−1}: i appears in a strictly higher row than i+1 in T}, let des(T) = |Des(T)|, and let maj(T) = ∑_{i ∈ Des(T)} i. With [a choose b]_q denoting the usual q-binomial coefficient, interpreted as 0 if b < 0 or b > a, the symmetric function C_{n,k}(x;q) = Δ′_{e_{k−1}} e_n evaluated at t = 0 has the Schur expansion C_{n,k}(x;q) = ∑_{T ∈ SYT(n)} q^{maj(T) + binom(n−k,2) − (n−k) des(T)} [des(T) choose n−k]_q s_{λ(T)}(x).  (from: literature_2505.24027)
    Proof sketch: The paper records this formula as a known description of C_{n,k}(x;q), citing the t = 0 Delta-conjecture literature. The substantive ingredients in the cited derivations are: (1) identify C_{n,k} with Δ′_{e_{k−1}} e_n at t=0, equivalently with a Hall–Littlewood or ordered-multiset-partition generating function; (2) pass from the combinatorial/quasisymmetric expansion to the Schur basis using Gessel’s standard-tableau expansion of Schur functions into fundamental quasisymmetric functions, so standard tableaux index the Schur summands; (3) for a tableau T, the choice of n−k selected descents contributes the q-binomial factor [des(T) choose n−k]_q; and (4) the normalization of the t=0 Delta operator gives the exponent maj(T)+binom(n−k,2)−(n−k)des(T). The vanishing convention for q-binomial coefficients enforces n−k ≤ des(T).
  • [arXiv:2505.24027 Theorem 5.2 (Fields Conjecture 3)] Let F be a field of characteristic 0 and let Ω_n = F[x_1,...,x_n] ⊗ exterior(θ_1,...,θ_n), bigraded by polynomial x-degree and exterior θ-degree. The symmetric group S_n acts diagonally by permuting the indices of both x_i and θ_i. Let e_1,...,e_n be the elementary symmetric polynomials in x_1,...,x_n, and let d e_j = ∑_{i=1}^n (∂e_j/∂x_i) θ_i. Define the superspace coinvariant ring SR_n = Ω_n / (e_1,...,e_n,d e_1,...,d e_n). Then its bigraded Frobenius characteristic is grFrob(SR_n;q,z) = ∑_{k=1}^n C_{n,k}(x;q) z^{n−k}, where q records x-degree, z records θ-degree, and C_{n,k}(x;q) is the symmetric function in Equation (2.5). Equivalently, combining with Equation (2.5) and the q-binomial theorem, if d = des(T), then grFrob(SR_n;q,z) = ∑_{T ∈ SYT(n)} q^{maj(T) − binom(d+1,2)} ∏_{i=1}^d (q^i + z) s_{λ(T)}(x), with the empty product equal to 1. Consequently, the ordinary bigraded Hilbert series is obtained from this last formula by replacing each s_{λ(T)} by f^{λ(T)}, the number of standard Young tableaux of shape λ(T).  (from: literature_2505.24027)
    Proof sketch: Fix a fermionic degree r and put k = n−r. The comparison object is the superspace Vandermonde quotient W_{n,k} = Ω_n / ann_{Ω_n}(δ_{n,k}), where δ_{n,k} is the antisymmetrization of x_1^0 x_2^1 ... x_k^{k−1} x_{n−k+1}^{k−1} ... x_n^{k−1} θ_{n−k+1}...θ_n. Earlier work gives: the top fermionic degree of W_{n,k} has Frobenius characteristic C_{n,k}(x;q), and a monomial basis indexed by the same Artin-type monomials A_n(J) θ_J with |J|=r. The paper proves a parabolic antisymmetrized basis theorem, Lemma 4.19: for every partition μ of n, ε_μ·SR_n has a basis obtained by applying the Young-subgroup antisymmetrizer ε_μ to explicit monomials A_n(μ,γ) θ_{J(μ,γ)}, summed over 0≤γ≤μ. Restricting to ∑ γ_i = r gives exactly the same q-graded counting as the corresponding top-degree basis of ε_μ·W_{n,k}. Thus Hilb(ε_μ·(SR_n)_{*,r};q) = Hilb(ε_μ·(W_{n,k})_{*,r};q) for every μ. Lemma 2.2 identifies dim ε_μ·V with e_μ^⊥Frob(V); since the e_μ form a basis and the Hall inner product is nondegenerate, equality of all these parabolic antisymmetrized Hilbert series forces equality of graded Frobenius characteristics. Varying r proves grFrob(SR_n) = ∑ C_{n,k} z^{n−k}. The displayed product form follows by summing Equation (2.5) over r=n−k and using ∑_{r=0}^d q^{binom(r,2)} [d choose r]_q u^r = ∏_{i=0}^{d−1}(1+u q^i) with u=z q^{−d}.
  • [arXiv:2505.24027 Theorem A / Fields Conjecture 1 (Hilbert series of SR_n)] For the superspace coinvariant ring SR_n defined above, the ordinary bigraded Hilbert series is Hilb(SR_n;q,z) = ∑_{k=1}^n z^{n−k} [k]!_q Stir_q(n,k), where [k]!_q = ∏_{i=1}^k (1+q+...+q^{i−1}) and the q-Stirling numbers Stir_q(n,k) are defined recursively by Stir_q(0,0)=1, Stir_q(0,k)=0 for k>0, and Stir_q(n,k)=Stir_q(n−1,k−1)+[k]_q Stir_q(n−1,k) for n>0. In particular, dim_F SR_n = ∑_{k=1}^n k! Stir(n,k), the number of ordered set partitions of {1,...,n}.  (from: literature_2505.24027)
    Proof sketch: The paper’s Theorem A states all three Fields conjectures, including this Hilbert-series formula; the formula was previously proved by Rhoades–Wilson and is also compatible with the paper’s new basis methods. One route is through the Artin-type monomial basis of Theorem 3.1: for each J⊆[n], define the J-staircase st(J) by st(J)_1=0 if 1∈J and 1 otherwise, and st(J)_i=st(J)_{i−1} if i∈J and st(J)_{i−1}+1 otherwise; then A_n(J) consists of monomials x_1^{a_1}...x_n^{a_n} with a_i < st(J)_i, and the union of A_n(J) θ_J over all J descends to a basis of SR_n. Therefore Hilb(SR_n;q,z) is the generating function of these monomials by x-degree and |J|. Grouping terms with |J|=n−k gives the recurrence for [k]!_q Stir_q(n,k): when inserting the element n into an ordered set partition, either it creates a new block, giving the Stir_q(n−1,k−1) term, or it is inserted into one of k existing ordered positions with q-weight [k]_q, giving the [k]_q Stir_q(n−1,k) term; ordering the blocks accounts for [k]!_q. Equivalently, the same Hilbert series is obtained by taking dimensions in Theorem 5.2.
  • [arXiv:2404.17919 Theorem 1.2 (Rhoades–Wilson transfer principle)] Let K be a field of characteristic 0, let S=K[x_1,...,x_n], and let Ω=S⊗∧{θ_1,...,θ_n}, with S_n acting by simultaneous permutation of the x_i and θ_i. Let a=(S^{S_n}_+) be the ideal of S generated by symmetric polynomials with vanishing constant term, let I⊂Ω be the differential closure of a, equivalently the ideal generated by S_n-invariants in Ω with vanishing constant term, and let SR=Ω/I. For J⊆[n], let θ_J be the product of the θ_j with j∈J in increasing order, and define f_J=∏_{j∈J} x_j · ∏_{j∈J} ∏_{i=j+1}^n (x_j-x_i) ∈ S. For an ideal b⊂S and f∈S, write b:f={g∈S: gf∈b}. Suppose that for each J⊆[n], B(J)⊂S is a set of homogeneous polynomials whose images form a K-basis of S/(a:f_J). Then the disjoint union ⋃_{J⊆[n]} B(J)·θ_J has images forming a K-basis of SR.  (from: literature_2404.17919)
    Proof sketch: The paper uses this as an input from Rhoades–Wilson. The underlying mechanism is a filtration of Ω, hence of SR, by an order on fermionic supports J. For a fixed J, examine the associated-graded quotient whose leading exterior term is h θ_J. Rhoades–Wilson compute the ideal of leading coefficients h which become zero at this stage: it is exactly a:f_J, i.e. h θ_J is killed modulo lower fermionic-support terms precisely when h f_J lies in the ordinary coinvariant ideal a. The factor f_J is the triangular/Vandermonde-type factor that appears when isolating the θ_J-coefficient of elements in the differential closure of a. Thus gr(SR) is, as a graded vector space, the direct sum over J of S/(a:f_J) θ_J. If B(J) is a homogeneous basis for each summand, the elements b θ_J have distinct leading terms and are linearly independent in the associated graded; conversely they span each associated-graded subquotient, so a standard filtered-vector-space induction gives spanning and independence in SR itself.
  • [arXiv:2404.17919 Theorem 7.1] For J⊆[n], define the J-staircase st(J)=(st(J)_1,...,st(J)_n) recursively by st(J)_1=0 if 1∈J and st(J)_1=1 if 1∉J; for i>1, st(J)_i=st(J)_{i-1} if i∈J and st(J)_i=st(J)_{i-1}+1 if i∉J. Let M(J)={x_1^{a_1}⋯x_n^{a_n}: a_i are nonnegative integers and a_i<st(J)_i for all i}. Let a=(S^{S_n}_+)⊂S=K[x_1,...,x_n] be the ordinary type A coinvariant ideal, and let f_J=∏_{j∈J} x_j · ∏_{j∈J} ∏_{i=j+1}^n (x_j-x_i). Then, for every J⊆[n], the images of the monomials in M(J) form a K-basis of S/(a:f_J). In particular, if 1∈J then M(J)=∅ and a:f_J=S.  (from: literature_2404.17919)
    Proof sketch: First one only needs linear independence: Rhoades–Wilson had already shown |M(J)|=dim_K S/(a:f_J); equivalently, this dimension follows from the regular-sequence description of a:f_J in Corollary 5.6 when 1∉J, and from a:f_J=S when 1∈J. Define the modified polynomial f~_J=∏_{j∈J}∏_{i=j+1}^n(x_j-x_i), obtained from f_J by deleting the coordinate factors x_j. Let A be the subarrangement of the augmented braid arrangement consisting of all coordinate hyperplanes H_{x_1},...,H_{x_n} together with all hyperplanes H_{x_j-x_i} for j∉J and j<i≤n. This A is a southwest arrangement, hence free. Its complementary product in the augmented braid arrangement is f~_J, so Proposition 5.2 identifies S/(a:f~_J) with the Solomon–Terao algebra ST(A,i), where i sends every partial derivative ∂_k to 1. The h-sequence of A is h_k=st(J)_k for k∉J and h_k=st(J)_k+1 for k∈J. Theorem 6.2 gives a monomial basis of ST(A,i), namely all x^b with b_k<h_k. The colon identity a:(f~_J∏_{j∈J}x_j)=(a:f~_J):∏_{j∈J}x_j yields an injection S/(a:f_J) --×∏_{j∈J}x_j--> S/(a:f~_J). Under this injection, x^a∈M(J) maps to x^b with b_k=a_k+1 for k∈J and b_k=a_k otherwise. The inequalities a_k<st(J)_k imply b_k<h_k, so these images are distinct members of the monomial basis of S/(a:f~_J). Hence M(J) is linearly independent in S/(a:f_J), and the dimension count gives that it is a basis.
  • [arXiv:2404.17919 Corollary 7.2] Let SR=Ω/I be the superspace coinvariant ring defined above. For each J⊆[n], let M(J) be the set of monomials under the J-staircase as in Theorem 7.1, and let θ_J be the increasing product of the θ_j for j∈J. Define the set of superspace Artin monomials M=⋃_{J⊆[n]} M(J)·θ_J. Then the images of the elements of M form a K-basis of SR. Consequently, counting bosonic degree by q and fermionic degree by z, Hilb(SR;q,z)=∑_{J⊆[n]} z^{|J|} ∑_{x^a∈M(J)} q^{a_1+...+a_n}=∑_{J⊆[n]} z^{|J|} ∏_{i=1}^n (1-q^{st(J)_i})/(1-q), with the convention that a factor with st(J)_i=0 is zero.  (from: literature_2404.17919)
    Proof sketch: Take B(J)=M(J) in the Rhoades–Wilson transfer theorem, Theorem 1.2. Theorem 7.1 verifies the required hypothesis for every J, namely that M(J) is a homogeneous K-basis of S/(a:f_J). Therefore the transferred set ⋃_J M(J)θ_J is a basis of SR. The displayed Hilbert series is obtained by direct enumeration of this basis: θ_J contributes fermionic degree |J|, and for fixed J the allowed exponent range 0≤a_i<st(J)_i contributes the finite geometric factor 1+q+...+q^{st(J)_i-1}=(1-q^{st(J)_i})/(1-q).
  • [arXiv:1609.07575 Theorem 4.10] Let 1 ≤ k ≤ n. Put Q[x_n] = Q[x_1,...,x_n] and I_{n,k} = ⟨x_1^k, x_2^k, ..., x_n^k, e_n(x_1,...,x_n), e_{n-1}(x_1,...,x_n), ..., e_{n-k+1}(x_1,...,x_n)⟩, where e_r is the elementary symmetric polynomial of degree r. Let R_{n,k} = Q[x_n]/I_{n,k}, graded by total degree. Let OP_{n,k} be the set of ordered set partitions σ = (B_1 | ... | B_k) of [n] into k nonempty ordered blocks. For σ, define inv(σ) to be the number of pairs i < j such that i is the minimum element of some block B_m, j lies in a block B_l, and l < m. Define coinv(σ) = (n-k)(k-1) + binom(k,2) - inv(σ). Let [r]_q = 1 + q + ... + q^{r-1}, [k]!_q = [k]_q [k-1]_q ... [1]_q, and let the q-Stirling numbers be defined by Stir_q(1,1)=1, Stir_q(1,k)=0 for k>1, and Stir_q(n,k)=Stir_q(n-1,k-1)+[k]_q Stir_q(n-1,k). If rev_q reverses a polynomial in q, then Hilb(R_{n,k};q) = ∑_{σ in OP_{n,k}} q^{coinv(σ)} = rev_q([k]!_q Stir_q(n,k)). In particular dim R_{n,k} = |OP_{n,k}| = k! Stir(n,k).  (from: literature_1609.07575)
    Proof sketch: Choose distinct rationals α_1,...,α_k and let Y_{n,k} ⊂ Q^n be the points whose coordinate set is exactly {α_1,...,α_k}. The point (y_1,...,y_n) corresponds equivariantly to the ordered set partition whose i-th block is {j : y_j=α_i}. Let I(Y) be the vanishing ideal and T(Y) the ideal generated by top homogeneous parts of elements of I(Y). Then Q[x_n]/T(Y) has dimension |Y|=|OP_{n,k}|. Show I_{n,k} ⊂ T(Y): the top part of ∏_{r=1}^k (x_i-α_r) gives x_i^k, and Lemma 3.1 gives, for n-k+1 ≤ r ≤ n, a vanishing linear combination ∑_{j=0}^r (-1)^j e_{r-j}(x_n) h_j(α_1,...,α_k), whose top part is e_r(x_n). To bound dim R_{n,k} from above, use lexicographic Gröbner theory. For S={s_1<...<s_m} define the skip monomial x(S)=x_{s_1}^{s_1} x_{s_2}^{s_2-1} ... x_{s_m}^{s_m-m+1}. Lemmas 3.4 and 3.5 show that for every |S|=n-k+1 a reverse Demazure character in I_{n,k} has leading monomial x(S); also x_i^k ∈ in(I_{n,k}). Hence every standard monomial is in M_{n,k}, the set of monomials divisible by no x_i^k and no such x(S). Theorem 4.9 constructs a degree-preserving bijection Ψ: OP_{n,k} → M_{n,k}: recursively remove n; if n is inserted into an existing block in position i+1, multiply by x_n^i; if n is inserted as a singleton block in position i+1, use Lemma 4.7 to choose the unique size n-k set S for which x(S) divides m(S) times the previous monomial, and multiply by m(S)x_n^i. The degree increments are exactly the coinv increments. Thus |M_{n,k}|=|OP_{n,k}| and ∑_{m∈M_{n,k}} q^{deg m}=∑_{σ}q^{coinv σ}. Since I_{n,k}⊂T(Y) gives dim R_{n,k}≥|OP_{n,k}| while the standard monomial bound gives dim R_{n,k}≤|M_{n,k}|=|OP_{n,k}|, all inequalities are equalities. The final expression with rev_q([k]!_q Stir_q(n,k)) uses the known Mahonian distribution of inv on OP_{n,k} and the definition of coinv as the complementary statistic.
  • [arXiv:1609.07575 Lemma 5.1] Let OP_{n,k} be identified with ascent-starred permutations as follows: write an ordered set partition in increasing order within each block, remove the bars to get a permutation π=π_1...π_n, and put a star between adjacent letters that came from the same block; then the star set S is a subset of Asc(π)={i : π_i<π_{i+1}} with |S|=n-k. Conversely every pair (π,S) with S⊂Asc(π), |S|=n-k, gives an element of OP_{n,k}. For a permutation π, let maj(π)=∑_{i: π_i>π_{i+1}} i and asc(π)=|Asc(π)|. For σ=(π,S), let π^c be the value-complement permutation π^c_i=n-π_i+1 and define maj(σ)=maj(π^c)-∑_{i∈S} |Asc(π)∩{i,i+1,...,n-1}|. Define comaj(σ)=(n-k)(k-1)+binom(k,2)-maj(σ). Then ∑_{σ∈OP_{n,k}} q^{comaj(σ)} = ∑_{π∈S_n} q^{maj(π)} [asc(π) choose n-k]_q, where the q-binomial coefficient is interpreted as 0 if n-k>asc(π).  (from: literature_1609.07575)
    Proof sketch: Fix π and write h=n-k and r=asc(π). If h>r there are no star sets, matching the zero q-binomial. Otherwise list Asc(π)={a_1<...<a_r}. A star set S={a_{j_1}<...<a_{j_h}} is equivalent to a partition λ inside an h by r-h rectangle by setting λ_t = r-h-j_t+t for t=1,...,h. These λ_t are weakly decreasing because j_{t+1}>j_t, nonnegative because j_t≤r-h+t, and at most r-h; the construction is reversible. Its size is |λ|=∑_{t=1}^h (r-j_t+1)-binom(h+1,2), since r-j_t+1 is the number of ascents weakly to the right of a_{j_t}. Now maj(π^c)=∑_{a∈Asc(π)} a and maj(π)+maj(π^c)=binom(n,2). Also (n-k)(k-1)+binom(k,2)=binom(n,2)-binom(h+1,2). Substituting the definition of maj(σ) gives comaj(π,S)=maj(π)+|λ|. Thus the contribution of all S for this fixed π is q^{maj(π)} times the generating function of partitions in the h by r-h rectangle, namely [r choose h]_q=[asc(π) choose n-k]_q. Summing over π proves the identity.
  • [arXiv:1609.07575 Corollary 6.13] For 1 ≤ k ≤ n, let R_{n,k}=Q[x_1,...,x_n]/I_{n,k} with I_{n,k}=⟨x_1^k,...,x_n^k,e_n(x_1,...,x_n),e_{n-1}(x_1,...,x_n),...,e_{n-k+1}(x_1,...,x_n)⟩, carrying the natural graded S_n-action. Let grFrob(R_{n,k};q) be its graded Frobenius characteristic. For a standard Young tableau T on n boxes, let shape(T) be its partition shape, let Des(T) be the set of entries i such that i appears in a higher row than i+1, let des(T)=|Des(T)|, and let maj(T)=∑_{i∈Des(T)} i. Then grFrob(R_{n,k};q) = ∑_{T∈SYT(n)} q^{maj(T)} [n - des(T) - 1 choose n-k]_q s_{shape(T)}(x), where s_{shape(T)} is the Schur function in the Frobenius variables x and the q-binomial is zero when its lower index is outside the allowed range.  (from: literature_1609.07575)
    Proof sketch: The paper first proves Theorem 6.11: grFrob(R_{n,k};q)=D_{n,k}(x;q), where D_{n,k}=rev_q ω C_{n,k} and C_{n,k} is the common t=0 specialization of the Delta-conjecture combinatorial functions. The proof of Theorem 6.11 uses the operator e_j(x)^⊥. On the algebra side, antisymmetrizing in the last j variables gives e_j^⊥ grFrob(R_{n,k})=grFrob(ε_j R_{n,k}); Lemma 6.8 identifies ε_j R_{n,k} with R_{n-j,k,k-j} tensored with the alternant space in j variables, contributing q^{binom(j,2)} [k choose j]_q. Lemma 6.10 then expands grFrob(R_{n-j,k,k-j}) via short exact sequences for the auxiliary quotients R_{n,k,s}. On the combinatorial side, Lemma 3.7 gives exactly the same e_j^⊥ recursion for D_{n,k}. Since constant terms agree, Lemma 3.6 forces equality. For the displayed Schur expansion, use Wilson's theorem on the t=0 Delta expression. Taking the coefficient corresponding to n-k rises gives ω C_{n,k}(x;q)=∑_{T∈SYT(n)} q^{maj(T)+binom(n-k,2)-(n-k)des(T)} [des(T) choose n-k]_q s_{shape(T)'}(x), before q-reversal. The maximal q-degree of grFrob(R_{n,k};q) is (n-k)(k-1)+binom(k,2), known from Theorem 4.10. Applying rev_q converts the exponent to binom(n,2)-maj(T) and leaves the q-binomial in the form [des(T) choose n-k]_q. Finally transpose tableaux: shape(T')=shape(T)', maj(T')=binom(n,2)-maj(T), and des(T')=n-1-des(T). Reindexing by T' gives the stated formula.
  • [arXiv:2301.09763 Lemma 2.1 (Sagan-Swanson staircase identity)] Fix n ≥ 1. For a subset J ⊆ [n], define its J-staircase st(J) = (st(J)_1,...,st(J)_n) by st(J)_i = |{a ∈ [i] : a ∉ J}|; equivalently st(J)_1 is 0 if 1 ∈ J and 1 if 1 ∉ J, and st(J)_{i+1}=st(J)_i if i+1 ∈ J while st(J)_{i+1}=st(J)_i+1 if i+1 ∉ J. Let [r]_q = 1+q+...+q^{r-1} for r ≥ 1 and [0]_q=0, and let [k]!_q = [k]_q [k-1]_q ... [1]_q. Define q-Stirling numbers Stir_q(n,k) by Stir_q(0,0)=1, Stir_q(0,k)=0 for k≠0, and Stir_q(n,k) = [k]_q Stir_q(n-1,k) + Stir_q(n-1,k-1). Then the polynomial identity holds: Σ_{J⊆[n]} z^{|J|} Π_{i=1}^n [st(J)_i]_q = Σ_{k=1}^n z^{n-k} [k]!_q Stir_q(n,k). Equivalently, the coefficient of z^r on the left is the q-degree generating function of all pairs consisting of a subset J⊆[n] of size r and a monomial x_1^{a_1}...x_n^{a_n} satisfying 0 ≤ a_i < st(J)_i for every i.  (from: literature_2301.09763)
    Proof sketch: Let G(n,k) be the coefficient of z^{n-k} on the left, i.e. the sum over subsets J⊆[n] with exactly k elements outside J. The formula st(J)_i=|[i]\J| makes the recurrence transparent. Split such J according to whether n∈J. If n∈J, then J'=J\{n} has k elements outside it in [n-1], the first n-1 staircase entries are those of J', and the final entry is k; this contributes [k]_q G(n-1,k). If n∉J, then J⊆[n-1] has k-1 elements outside it in [n-1], and adjoining the nth entry again multiplies by [k]_q; this contributes [k]_q G(n-1,k-1). Thus G(n,k)=[k]_q G(n-1,k)+[k]_q G(n-1,k-1), with G(1,1)=1 and the usual boundary values. The quantity H(n,k):=[k]!_q Stir_q(n,k) satisfies the same recurrence, since multiplying Stir_q(n,k)=[k]_q Stir_q(n-1,k)+Stir_q(n-1,k-1) by [k]!_q gives H(n,k)=[k]_q H(n-1,k)+[k]_q H(n-1,k-1). Hence G(n,k)=H(n,k). The monomial interpretation is immediate from Π_i [st(J)_i]_q = Σ_{0≤a_i<st(J)_i} q^{a_1+...+a_n}.
  • [arXiv:2301.09763 Theorem 4.12] Let C[x_n]=C[x_1,...,x_n], and let I_n=(C[x_n]^{S_n}_+) be the classical coinvariant ideal, equivalently I_n=(e_1(x_n),...,e_n(x_n)). For S⊆[n], write h_d(S) for the complete homogeneous symmetric polynomial of degree d in the variables {x_s : s∈S}. For J⊆[n], define f_J = Π_{j∈J} x_j Π_{i=j+1}^n (x_j-x_i), with empty products equal to 1. Define polynomials p_{J,1},...,p_{J,n} as follows, using the convention min(∅)=∞: if i<min(J), set p_{J,i}=h_i({i,i+1,...,n}); if i≥min(J), set s=max(J∩[i]), T=J∪{i+1,i+2,...,n}, r=n-|T|+1, and p_{J,i}=∂h_r(T)/∂x_s. A permutation w∈S_n is j-resentful if w(j)=n or if w(j)+1 occurs among w(j+1),...,w(n); it is j-Nietzschean otherwise. It is J-Nietzschean if it is j-Nietzschean for every j∈J. Let N_J be the finite subset of C^n consisting of the points (w(1),...,w(n)) for J-Nietzschean w∈S_n. For a finite set X⊂C^n, let I(X) be its vanishing ideal and gr I(X) the homogeneous ideal generated by the top-degree homogeneous parts of nonzero elements of I(X). Then, for every J⊆[n], the following three homogeneous ideals in C[x_n] are equal: (1) the colon ideal (I_n : f_J)={g∈C[x_n] : g f_J∈I_n}; (2) the ideal (p_{J,1},...,p_{J,n}); (3) the orbit-harmonic ideal gr I(N_J). If I_J denotes this common ideal, then Hilb(C[x_n]/I_J;q)=Π_{i=1}^n [st(J)_i]_q, where st(J)_i=|[i]\J| and [0]_q=0.  (from: literature_2301.09763)
    Proof sketch: If 1∈J, all three ideals are the unit ideal: f_J∈I_n, p_{J,1}=∂h_1([n])/∂x_1=1, and no permutation is 1-Nietzschean. Assume 1∉J. A major input is that p_{J,1},...,p_{J,n} is a regular sequence of degrees st(J)_1,...,st(J)_n. The regularity proof uses the identity ∂_a h_r(S)=∂_b h_r(S)+(x_c-x_b)∂_b h_{r-1}(S∪{c})-(x_c-x_a)∂_a h_{r-1}(S∪{c}) for a,b∈S, c∉S. Iterating it puts all derivatives ∂_j h_{n-|J|+1}(J), j∈J, in (p_{J,*}); these derivatives have no common zero except 0 in the J-variables, and after setting those variables to 0 the remaining p-generators are triangular, so the zero locus is {0}. Next the paper proves (p_{J,*})⊆(I_n:f_J). This uses superspace elements q_{J,i} in the supercoinvariant ideal whose Gale-leading θ_J coefficient is p_{J,i}, and differential operators D_J whose θ_J-leading coefficient on the Vandermonde δ_n is ±f_J⊙δ_n; since q_{J,i} annihilates the superharmonic D_J(δ_n), triangularity forces (p_{J,i} f_J)⊙δ_n=0, hence p_{J,i} f_J∈I_n. The quotient C[x_n]/(p_{J,*}) is a complete intersection, hence a Poincare duality algebra with socle degree Σ_i st(J)_i-n. The coinvariant algebra C[x_n]/I_n is also Poincare duality with socle degree binom(n,2), f_J∉I_n when 1∉J, and deg f_J=Σ_i(i-st(J)_i), so the socle degrees differ by deg f_J. The colon-ideal lemma of Abe-Horiguchi-Masuda-Murai-Sato then upgrades the containment to (p_{J,*})=(I_n:f_J). For the equality with gr I(N_J), deform the coinvariant ideal to ~I_n=(e_d(x)-e_d(1,...,n):1≤d≤n), whose variety is the embedded set S_n⊂C^n. The inhomogeneous polynomial ~f_J=Π_{j∈J}(x_j-n)Π_{i>j}(x_j-x_i+1) vanishes exactly on the J-resentful points of S_n, so N_J=V(~I_n)-V(~f_J), and radicality gives I(N_J)=(~I_n:~f_J). Passing to top homogeneous parts gives gr I(N_J)⊆(I_n:f_J). Finally |N_J|=Π_i st(J)_i by an insertion/deletion recurrence for J-Nietzschean permutations, while dim C[x_n]/(I_n:f_J)=Π_i st(J)_i from the complete intersection Hilbert series, forcing equality of ideals and yielding the stated Hilbert series.
  • [arXiv:2301.09763 Theorem 5.3] Let Ω_n=C[x_1,...,x_n]⊗Λ(θ_1,...,θ_n), where the x_i commute and the θ_i anticommute, and let S_n act diagonally by permuting x_i and θ_i simultaneously. Let SI_n be the ideal of Ω_n generated by all S_n-invariant elements with vanishing constant term, and set SR_n=Ω_n/SI_n. Give SR_n the bigrading in which q records total x-degree and z records total θ-degree. Then Hilb(SR_n;q,z)=Σ_{k=1}^n z^{n-k} [k]!_q Stir_q(n,k). Equivalently, using the J-staircase st(J)_i=|[i]\J|, Hilb(SR_n;q,z)=Σ_{J⊆[n]} z^{|J|} Π_{i=1}^n [st(J)_i]_q.  (from: literature_2301.09763)
    Proof sketch: The proof has matching upper and lower bounds. For the upper bound, for every J⊆[n] the paper constructs elements q_{J,i}∈SI_n whose fermionic degree is |J| and whose Gale-leading θ_J coefficient is p_{J,i}. The polynomials p_{J,i} are the regular sequence of Theorem 4.12, with degrees st(J)_i. Choose homogeneous representatives B_n(J) for a basis of C[x_n]/(p_{J,1},...,p_{J,n}); their degree generating function is Π_i [st(J)_i]_q. A triangular straightening argument in Gale order says that any f θ_J can be reduced modulo SI_n to a linear combination of B_n(J) θ_J plus terms with strictly larger fermionic monomial in Gale order. Descending induction over Gale order shows that the union over J of B_n(J) θ_J spans SR_n, giving Hilb(SR_n;q,z) ≤ Σ_J z^{|J|}Π_i[st(J)_i]_q. For the lower bound, use differential operators D_J built from minors of the complete-homogeneous matrix H. Their key triangular property is that D_J(δ_n), where δ_n is the Vandermonde, is superharmonic and has θ_J coefficient ±f_J⊙δ_n, with only θ_K terms for K≤Gale J. The annihilator of f_J⊙δ_n in C[x_n] is (I_n:f_J). By Theorem 4.12, choose B_n(J) mapping to a basis of C[x_n]/(I_n:f_J), again with degree generating function Π_i[st(J)_i]_q. If a nontrivial linear relation among the images of all B_n(J)θ_J held in SR_n, then, after taking all terms of one fermionic degree and choosing a Gale-minimal J_0 among those with nonzero coefficient, pairing/acting on D_{J_0}(δ_n) would kill all higher Gale terms and produce a nontrivial relation among {b⊙(f_{J_0}⊙δ_n): b∈B_n(J_0)}. This contradicts the basis property modulo (I_n:f_{J_0}). Hence the same set is linearly independent in SR_n, so the upper bound is sharp. The final expression with q-Stirling numbers is exactly Lemma 2.1.
  • [arXiv:2505.14885 Proposition 5.1] Fix a positive integer n. For k,j ≥ 0 let R_n^(k,j) be the symmetric-group bosonic-fermionic diagonal coinvariant ring with k sets of n commuting variables and j sets of n anticommuting variables, i.e. R_n^(k,j) = Sym(C^(k|j) ⊗ C^n)/⟨Sym(C^(k|j) ⊗ C^n)_+^(S_n)⟩, with S_n acting diagonally by permuting the n indices. Let P(k,j,n) be the set of partitions λ with length ℓ(λ) ≤ n and λ_(k+1) ≤ j, with missing parts interpreted as 0. For alphabets q = q_1,...,q_k and u = u_1,...,u_j, define the super Schur function by s_λ(q/u) = ∑_{ν⊆λ} s_ν(q) s_{λ′/ν′}(u). Then there exist nonnegative integers c_λ, depending only on n and λ and independent of k and j, such that for every k,j one has Hilb(R_n^(k,j); q; u) = ∑_{λ∈P(k,j,n)} c_λ s_λ(q/u). More explicitly, if c_{λμ} are the universal Frobenius coefficients from Corollary 1.3, then c_λ = ∑_{μ⊢n} c_{λμ} ⟨s_μ(z), h_(1^n)(z)⟩. In particular, when j = 0 this specializes to the ordinary bosonic Schur expansion Hilb(R_n^(k,0); q_1,...,q_k) = ∑_{ℓ(λ)≤min(k,n)} c_λ s_λ(q_1,...,q_k).  (from: literature_2505.14885)
    Proof sketch: Use Corollary 1.3, which gives the multigraded Frobenius series in the form Frob(R_n^(k,j); q; u) = ∑_{λ∈P(k,j,n)} ∑_{μ⊢n} c_{λμ} s_λ(q/u) s_μ(z), where c_{λμ} are nonnegative and independent of k,j. The Hilbert series is recovered from the Frobenius series by taking the Hall inner product in the z-variables with h_(1^n)(z): Hilb(R_n^(k,j); q; u) = ⟨Frob(R_n^(k,j); q; u), h_(1^n)(z)⟩. Pull the inner product through the finite sum over μ for each λ and set c_λ = ∑_{μ⊢n} c_{λμ}⟨s_μ,h_(1^n)⟩. The factors ⟨s_μ,h_(1^n)⟩ are nonnegative integers, so c_λ is nonnegative; independence of k,j is inherited from Corollary 1.3. The condition λ∈P(k,j,n) is exactly the condition under which the corresponding simple gl(k|j)-character s_λ(q/u) can appear. For the purely bosonic case j=0, the super Schur function reduces to the usual Schur function s_λ(q), and P(k,0,n) imposes ℓ(λ)≤min(k,n), giving the ordinary multigraded coinvariant Hilbert coefficients.
  • [arXiv:2505.14885 Lemma 4.4] For the super Schur functions in one bosonic variable q and one fermionic variable u, one has s_∅(q/u) = 1. If λ is a hook partition λ = (a,1^b) with a ≥ 1 and b ≥ 0, then s_λ(q/u) = q^a u^b + q^(a−1) u^(b+1). If λ is neither empty nor of the form (a,1^b), equivalently if λ_2 ≥ 2, then s_λ(q/u) = 0.  (from: literature_2505.14885)
    Proof sketch: Start from the defining expansion s_λ(q/u) = ∑_{ν⊆λ} s_ν(q) s_{λ′/ν′}(u). Since q is a single variable, s_ν(q) is nonzero only when ν is a one-row partition ν=(r), in which case it equals q^r. Since u is a single variable, the skew Schur function s_{λ′/ν′}(u) is nonzero only when the skew shape λ′/ν′ is a horizontal strip, in which case it is the corresponding power of u. For λ=(a,1^b), so λ′=(b+1,1^(a−1)), the only row partitions ν=(r) for which λ′/ν′ is a horizontal strip are r=a and r=a−1. These give the two terms q^a u^b and q^(a−1)u^(b+1), respectively; this also covers a=1, where the r=0 contribution is allowed. The empty partition gives 1. If λ_2≥2, then λ′ has at least two cells in its second column, and removing ν′=(1^r) from the first column cannot make λ′/ν′ a horizontal strip, so every summand vanishes. Useful consequence for hook coefficients: combining this lemma with Proposition 5.1 at k=j=1 gives Hilb(R_n^(1,1);q;u)=c_∅+∑_{a≥1,b≥0} c_(a,1^b)(q^a u^b+q^(a−1)u^(b+1)). Thus, if h_{r,s}=[q^r u^s]Hilb(R_n^(1,1);q;u), then for a≥1 and b≥0, c_(a,1^b)=∑_{i=0}^b (−1)^i h_{a+i,b−i}; equivalently h_{a,b}=c_(a,1^b)+c_(a+1,1^(b−1)) with the second term omitted when b=0.
  • [arXiv:2505.14885 Equation (50), quoted theorem of Rhoades--Wilson] For the coinvariant ring R_n^(1,1) with one set of n commuting variables and one set of n anticommuting variables, the bigraded Hilbert series is Hilb(R_n^(1,1); q; u) = ∑_{d=0}^n [d]_q! Stir_q(n,d) u^(n−d). Here [d]_q = 1+q+⋯+q^(d−1), [d]_q! = [d]_q [d−1]_q ⋯ [1]_q with [0]_q! = 1, and the q-Stirling numbers are defined by Stir_q(n,d) = [d]_q Stir_q(n−1,d) + Stir_q(n−1,d−1), with initial conditions Stir_q(0,d)=δ_{d,0}. In the paper, substituting this formula into the cancellation identity of Proposition 5.2 yields the Sagan--Swanson identity ∑_{d=0}^n [d]_q! Stir_q(n,d)(−q)^(n−d)=1. For extracting hook Schur coefficients, combine this quoted Hilbert formula with Lemma 4.4 and Proposition 5.1. If h_{r,s}=[q^r u^s]Hilb(R_n^(1,1);q;u), then the formula gives h_{r,s}=[q^r]([n−s]_q! Stir_q(n,n−s)) for 0≤s≤n. Therefore the hook coefficient in the universal Hilbert expansion satisfies c_(a,1^b)=∑_{i=0}^b (−1)^i [q^(a+i)]([n−b+i]_q! Stir_q(n,n−b+i)) for a≥1,b≥0, with c_∅=1. I rechecked the recurrence, the \(q\)-Pascal step, and small cases. **No mathematical correction appears needed.** The only caveats are proof-wording/normalization caveats listed below.

## 1. Triangular recurrence

Use the positive Hilbert-series convention: the fermionic variable \(u\) counts dimension, not supertrace. Lentfer’s universal Hilbert expansion gives the same \(c_\lambda(n)\) after passing to the \((1,1)\) bosonic–fermionic specialization, and in one even/one odd variable the hook specialization is
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,\qquad
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
Non-hooks vanish in \(GL(1|1)\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

Let
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1} c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]
Then
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
c_\varnothing(n)+\sum_{a\ge1,b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}).
\]
The constant term forces \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\). Taking the coefficient of \(u^b\) gives
\[
\boxed{H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).}
\]
For \(b=0\), this is \(H_0=1+C_0\). For \(b>0\), the \(\delta\)-term vanishes.

The identity permutation has \(d=\operatorname{des}=0\), \(\operatorname{maj}=0\), and contributes exactly \(1\) to \(H_0\) and nothing to \(H_b\) for \(b>0\). That is precisely the \(\delta_{b,0}\) term.

## 2. \(q\)-Pascal check

Murai–Rhoades–Wilson identify \(SR_n\) as the one-boson/one-fermion superspace coinvariant ring and prove
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,z)=\sum_{k=1}^n C_{n,k}(\mathbf x;q)z^{n-k},
\]
with the standard-tableau expansion of \(C_{n,k}\). Pairing Frobenius with \(h_1^n\), then using row-insertion RSK with \(\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w)\), gives
\[
H_b(q)=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q .
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_b H_b(q)u^b
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\]
([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

Define the claimed solution
\[
D_b(q)=
\sum_{\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q .
\]
For a fixed permutation with \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge1\) and \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\), the contribution to
\[
D_b+q^{-1}D_{b-1}
\]
is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right),
\]
because
\[
-1+M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}
=
M-bd+\binom b2+(d-b).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
this equals the \(w\)-summand of \(H_b\). The sole \(d=0\) case is the identity permutation, already accounted for by \(\delta_{b,0}\). Hence \(C_b=D_b\).

## 3. Small cases

### \(n=1\)
Only \(w=1\), with \((d,M)=(0,0)\).
\[
H_0=1,\qquad H_b=0\ (b>0).
\]
Thus
\[
C_b=0\quad\text{for all }b\ge0,\qquad c_\varnothing(1)=1.
\]

### \(n=2\)
Descent-major inventory:
\[
(0,0)^1,\quad (1,1)^1.
\]
So
\[
H_0=1+q,\qquad H_1=1.
\]
The recurrence gives
\[
C_0=q,\qquad C_1=H_1-q^{-1}C_0=1-1=0.
\]
Matches the claimed formula.

### \(n=3\)
Descent-major inventory:
\[
(0,0)^1,\quad (1,1)^2,\quad (1,2)^2,\quad (2,3)^1.
\]
Hence
\[
H_0=1+2q+2q^2+q^3,
\]
\[
H_1=2+3q+q^2,
\]
\[
H_2=1.
\]
The recurrence gives
\[
C_0=H_0-1=2q+2q^2+q^3,
\]
\[
C_1=H_1-q^{-1}C_0=q,
\]
\[
C_2=H_2-q^{-1}C_1=0.
\]
So the only nonzero nonempty hook coefficients are
\[
c_{(1)}=2,\quad c_{(2)}=2,\quad c_{(3)}=1,\quad c_{(1,1)}=1.
\]

### \(n=4\)
Descent-major inventory:
\[
(0,0)^1,\ (1,1)^3,\ (1,2)^5,\ (1,3)^3,
\]
\[
(2,3)^3,\ (2,4)^5,\ (2,5)^3,\ (3,6)^1.
\]
Thus
\[
H_0=1+3q+5q^2+6q^3+5q^4+3q^5+q^6,
\]
\[
H_1=3+8q+11q^2+9q^3+4q^4+q^5,
\]
\[
H_2=3+6q+4q^2+q^3,
\]
\[
H_3=1.
\]
The recurrence gives
\[
C_0=3q+5q^2+6q^3+5q^4+3q^5+q^6,
\]
\[
C_1=3q+5q^2+4q^3+q^4,
\]
\[
C_2=q,
\]
\[
C_3=0.
\]
These also agree directly with the claimed \(D_b\)-formula. For example, for \(b=1\), the \(d=2\) permutations contribute \(3q+5q^2+3q^3\), and the decreasing permutation with \(d=3,M=6\) contributes \(q^3+q^4\), giving \(C_1=3q+5q^2+4q^3+q^4\).

## 4. Final theorem statement

Let
\[
\operatorname{Des}(w)=\{i:w_i>w_{i+1}\},\quad
d(w)=|\operatorname{Des}(w)|,\quad
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in\operatorname{Des}(w)}i.
\]
Then
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]
For \(a\ge1\) and \(b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

No correction to the formula is needed. The proof should explicitly state: \(u\) is a positive fermionic Hilbert variable, \(R_n^{(1,1)}=SR_n\), the MRW permutation formula is derived via Frobenius plus row-insertion RSK, and Gaussian binomials are zero outside their natural range.

# Original Problem
Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.
