# Solution
## Original Problem

Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

## Problem Solved

Fix \(n\ge 1\). Work over \(\mathbb C\). For \(m\ge 1\), let
\[
P_n^{(m)}=\mathbb C[x_i^{(r)}:1\le i\le n,\ 1\le r\le m],
\]
with \(S_n\) acting diagonally by permuting the index \(i\). Let \(I_n^{(m)}\) be the ideal generated by all \(S_n\)-invariant polynomials in \(P_n^{(m)}\) with zero constant term, and define the diagonal coinvariant algebra
\[
R_n^{(m)}=P_n^{(m)}/I_n^{(m)}.
\]
Give \(R_n^{(m)}\) the \(\mathbb Z_{\ge0}^m\)-grading in which \(x_i^{(r)}\) has degree \(1\) in the \(r\)-th component and degree \(0\) in the others. The universal Hilbert coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) are the coefficients in the stable Schur expansion
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots,q_m)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots,q_m),
\]
where \(s_\lambda\) is the Schur polynomial and \(s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots,q_m)=0\) if \(\ell(\lambda)>m\).

A hook partition is written
\[
\lambda=(a,1^b)
\]
with \(a\ge 1\) and \(b\ge 0\). For a permutation \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\in S_n\), define
\[
{\rm Des}(w)=\{i\in\{1,\ldots,n-1\}: w_i>w_{i+1}\},
\]
\[
{\rm des}(w)=|{\rm Des}(w)|,\qquad
{\rm maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in{\rm Des}(w)} i.
\]
For integers \(h,r\ge0\), a partition \(\rho\subseteq h\times r\) means a weakly decreasing sequence
\[
\rho=(\rho_1,\ldots,\rho_h)
\]
with \(r\ge \rho_1\ge\cdots\ge\rho_h\ge0\), and \(|\rho|=\rho_1+\cdots+\rho_h\). If \(h=0\), only the empty partition is allowed.

The theorem proved here is:
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1,
\]
and for every hook \((a,1^b)\) with \(a\ge1\), \(b\ge0\),
\[
\boxed{
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\\
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
}
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ {\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

Here the Gaussian binomial coefficient is
\[
\begin{bmatrix} r\\ s\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{cases}
\displaystyle\prod_{i=1}^{s}\frac{1-q^{r-s+i}}{1-q^i},&0\le s\le r,\\[1em]
0,&\text{otherwise},
\end{cases}
\]
and, for \(0\le s\le r\),
\[
\begin{bmatrix}r\\ s\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq s\times(r-s)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]

In particular, when \(b=0\),
\[
c_{(a)}(n)=\#\{w\in S_n:{\rm des}(w)\ge1,\ {\rm maj}(w)=a\},
\]
and if \(b\ge n-1\), then
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0.
\]
This is exactly the requested hook-coefficient interpretation, not a relaxation.

## Proof

First note that the degree-zero component of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is spanned by the image of \(1\). Since every nonempty Schur polynomial is homogeneous of positive total degree and \(s_{\varnothing}=1\), the constant term of the Hilbert series gives
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1.
\]

We now use three standard facts, stated in the precise forms needed.

### Known input 1: the \(GL(1|1)\) super Hilbert specialization

Let
\[
SR_n=\mathbb C[x_1,\ldots,x_n]\otimes \Lambda(\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_n)\big/\langle \text{positive-degree }S_n\text{-invariants}\rangle
\]
be the superspace coinvariant algebra with one even set \(x_1,\ldots,x_n\) and one odd set \(\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_n\), where \(S_n\) permutes the indices simultaneously. Let \(q\) record \(x\)-degree and \(u\) record \(\theta\)-degree, using the ordinary unsigned Hilbert series
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)=\sum_{i,j\ge0}\dim(SR_n)_{i,j}q^iu^j.
\]
The \(GL(1|1)\) specialization theorem says that the same universal coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) satisfy
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)=\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u),
\]
where \(s_\lambda(q/u)\) is the one-even-one-odd super Schur character.

### Known input 2: one-even-one-odd super Schur characters

In one even variable \(q\) and one odd variable \(u\),
\[
s_{\varnothing}(q/u)=1.
\]
For a hook \((a,1^b)\), \(a\ge1\), \(b\ge0\),
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
All nonempty non-hook Schur characters vanish under this \(GL(1|1)\) specialization.

### Known input 3: ordinary superspace coinvariant Hilbert product formula

The ordinary bigraded Hilbert series of \(SR_n\) is
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-\binom{{\rm des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{{\rm des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\]

### Step 1: compute the coefficient of \(u^b\) from the product formula

Let
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)=[u^b]\,{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u).
\]
Fix \(w\in S_n\), and write
\[
d={\rm des}(w),\qquad M={\rm maj}(w).
\]
The coefficient of \(u^b\) in \(\prod_{i=1}^d(q^i+u)\) is obtained by choosing \(b\) factors to contribute \(u\), equivalently choosing \(d-b\) factors to contribute powers of \(q\). Thus
\[
[u^b]\prod_{i=1}^{d}(q^i+u)
=
q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Therefore
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}}
q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Since
\[
\binom{d+1}{2}-\binom{d-b+1}{2}
=
bd-\binom b2,
\]
we get
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

### Step 2: compare with the \(GL(1|1)\) Schur expansion

For \(b\ge0\), define
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
and set
\[
C_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0.
\]
Using the \(GL(1|1)\) specialization and the hook character formula,
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
1+
\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\left(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\right).
\]
Taking the coefficient of \(u^b\) gives the triangular relation
\[
\boxed{
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\delta_{b,0}
+
C_b^{(n)}(q)
+
q^{-1}C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
}
\]

### Step 3: solve the triangular recurrence

We claim that
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ {\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

For \(b=0\), the triangular relation gives
\[
C_0^{(n)}(q)=H_0^{(n)}(q)-1.
\]
But
\[
H_0^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{w\in S_n}q^{{\rm maj}(w)}.
\]
The unique permutation with no descents is \(12\cdots n\), and it contributes \(1\). Hence
\[
C_0^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ {\rm des}(w)\ge1}}q^{{\rm maj}(w)},
\]
which is the desired formula when \(b=0\).

Now let \(b\ge1\). From the triangular relation,
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=H_b^{(n)}(q)-q^{-1}C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
\]
Using the formula for \(H_b^{(n)}(q)\) and the induction hypothesis for \(C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q)\), the contribution of a fixed \(w\), with \(d={\rm des}(w)\) and \(M={\rm maj}(w)\), is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
-
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
this equals
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Thus
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Since
\[
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q=0
\]
unless \({\rm des}(w)-1\ge b\), this is equivalently
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ {\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

### Step 4: interpret the Gaussian binomial coefficient

If \(d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1\), then
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Therefore
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Since
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
the coefficient of \(q^a\) is exactly the number of pairs \((w,\rho)\) satisfying
\[
w\in S_n,\qquad d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,
\]
\[
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

Finally, the exponent is always positive in the nonzero case. Indeed, if \(d\ge b+1\), then the \(d\) descent positions have sum at least \(1+\cdots+d\), so
\[
{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}
\ge1.
\]
Thus the formula produces only powers \(q^a\) with \(a\ge1\), as required for hooks \((a,1^b)\).

When \(b=0\), the rectangle \(0\times(d-1)\) contains only the empty partition, so the formula reduces to
\[
c_{(a)}(n)=\#\{w\in S_n:{\rm des}(w)\ge1,\ {\rm maj}(w)=a\}.
\]
When \(b\ge n-1\), every permutation has \({\rm des}(w)\le n-1\), so the condition \({\rm des}(w)\ge b+1\) is impossible. Hence
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0
\]
for \(b\ge n-1\). This completes the proof.

1. **Patch for the \(GL(1|1)\) specialization.** Insert the following after “Known input 1.” Let \(R_n^{(k,j)}\) denote the coinvariant ring with \(k\) bosonic and \(j\) fermionic sets. Diagonal supersymmetry gives
\[
{\rm Frob}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\lambda,\mu} c_{\lambda,\mu}\,s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)s_\mu(\mathbf z),
\]
with \(c_{\lambda,\mu}\) independent of \(k,j\). Applying the dimension map \(s_\mu(\mathbf z)\mapsto f^\mu=\dim S^\mu\) gives
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=
\sum_\lambda \Big(\sum_\mu c_{\lambda,\mu}f^\mu\Big)s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u).
\]
For \(j=0\), this is the original bosonic Schur expansion, so by uniqueness
\[
c_\lambda(n)=\sum_\mu c_{\lambda,\mu}f^\mu.
\]
Taking \((k,j)=(1,1)\) gives exactly
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)=\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u).
\]

2. **Patch for the superspace Hilbert product formula.** Rhoades–Wilson prove
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,z)=\sum_{k=1}^n z^{\,n-k}[k]!_q\,{\rm Stir}_q(n,k),
\]
with \({\rm Stir}_q(n,k)\) satisfying the usual Carlitz recurrence. Remmel–Wilson’s identity gives
\[
\sum_{w\in S_n}q^{{\rm maj}(w)}
\prod_{j=1}^{{\rm des}(w)}(1+z/q^j)
=
\sum_{b=0}^{n-1} z^b [n-b]!_q{\rm Stir}_q(n,n-b).
\]
Since
\[
q^{{\rm maj}(w)}\prod_{j=1}^{d}(1+z/q^j)
=
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-\binom{d+1}{2}}\prod_{j=1}^{d}(q^j+z),
\]
this is precisely the product formula used in the proof, with \(z=u\).

3. **Optional derivation of the \(1|1\) super Schur formula.** From
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u),
\]

## Audit verdict

I found **no normalization discrepancy** in the hook-coefficient proof, provided the final writeup explicitly uses the **ordinary unsigned Hilbert-series convention** for fermions and either proves or cites the permutation product form. The only source-accuracy caveat is that the product formula is best presented as a **derived equivalent form** of the Fields/Rhoades–Wilson/Murai–Rhoades–Wilson formulas, not as a formula visibly stated in exactly that form.

---

## Theorem-ready external inputs

### 1. Universal super-Schur Hilbert expansion

Use Lentfer’s bosonic–fermionic coinvariant ring
\[
R_{n}^{(k,j)}
=
\mathbb C[x^{(1)},\dots,x^{(k)},\theta^{(1)},\dots,\theta^{(j)}]\Big/
\left\langle
\mathbb C[x^{(1)},\dots,x^{(k)},\theta^{(1)},\dots,\theta^{(j)}]^{\mathfrak S_n}_{+}
\right\rangle,
\]
where the \(x\)’s commute, the \(\theta\)’s anticommute, and \(\mathfrak S_n\) acts diagonally by permuting indices. Lentfer denotes the symmetric-group case by \(R_n^{(k,j)}\), so the ordinary \(m\)-set diagonal coinvariant algebra is \(R_n^{(m,0)}\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

The exact universality input is:

\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\lambda\in P(k,j,n)}
c_\lambda(n)\,s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u),
\qquad
P(k,j,n)=\{\lambda:\ell(\lambda)\le n,\ \lambda_{k+1}\le j\},
\]
with **nonnegative integers \(c_\lambda(n)\) independent of \((k,j)\)**. For \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), so these are exactly the ordinary Schur-Hilbert coefficients in the user’s \(m\)-boson expansion. For \(k=j=1\), the same \(c_\lambda(n)\) occur in the one-boson/one-fermion Hilbert series. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

**Caveat.** In the \((1,1)\) specialization, only \(\lambda_2\le1\) can contribute, i.e. \(\lambda=\varnothing\) or a hook \((a,1^b)\). Thus this specialization is sufficient for hook coefficients but does not determine nonhook coefficients.

---

### 2. Super-Schur normalization and signs

Use Lentfer’s positive super-Schur convention:
\[
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}
s_\nu(\mathbf q)\,s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(\mathbf u).
\]
This is also written plethystically as \(s_\lambda[\mathbf q-\epsilon\mathbf u]\), and **it is not the signed supertrace convention**. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

For one even variable \(q\) and one odd Hilbert variable \(u\),
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
\]
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\qquad(a\ge1,\ b\ge0),
\]
and
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=0\quad\text{if }\lambda\text{ is not contained in a }(1,1)\text{-hook}.
\]
This is exactly Lentfer’s Lemma 4.4. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

So, with
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u),
\]
the required triangular relation is
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
\]

**Sign caveat.** If someone uses a signed supertrace variable, then \(u\) is replaced by \(-u\), and the hook specialization would acquire alternating signs. That convention would break the proof. For example, at \(n=2\), the unsigned Hilbert series is \(1+q+u\), whereas a signed product would give \(1+q-u\).

---

### 3. Matching \(R_n^{(1,1)}\) with the superspace coinvariant ring \(SR_n\)

Murai–Rhoades–Wilson define
\[
\Omega_n=\mathbb F[x_1,\dots,x_n]\otimes \wedge\{\theta_1,\dots,\theta_n\},
\]
with diagonal action
\[
w\cdot x_i=x_{w(i)},\qquad w\cdot\theta_i=\theta_{w(i)}.
\]
They define
\[
SI_n=\big((\Omega_n)^{\mathfrak S_n}_{+}\big)
\]
and
\[
SR_n=\Omega_n/SI_n.
\]
They also record Solomon’s generating-set normalization
\[
SI_n=(e_1,\dots,e_n,de_1,\dots,de_n).
\]
Thus \(SR_n\) is exactly Lentfer’s \(R_n^{(1,1)}\), after identifying \(x_i=x_i^{(1)}\) and \(\theta_i=\theta_i^{(1)}\). Their variable \(z\) for fermionic degree should be renamed \(u\) in the hook proof. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

---

### 4. Superspace Hilbert/product formula

A source-clean input is Murai–Rhoades–Wilson’s Fields Frobenius formula:
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,z)
=
\sum_{k=1}^n C_{n,k}(\mathbf x;q)\,z^{n-k},
\]
where \(q\) tracks bosonic degree and \(z\) tracks fermionic degree. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

Their tableau expansion gives
\[
C_{n,k}(\mathbf x;q)
=
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(n)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)+\binom{n-k}{2}-(n-k)\operatorname{des}(T)}
\begin{bmatrix}
\operatorname{des}(T)\\ n-k
\end{bmatrix}_q
s_{\lambda(T)}(\mathbf x).
\]
Setting \(b=n-k\), pairing the Frobenius characteristic with \(h_{(1^n)}=h_1^n\) to forget the \(\mathfrak S_n\)-structure, and applying ordinary row-insertion RSK with \(\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w)\), gives
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}
\operatorname{des}(w)\\ b
\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
The Hilbert-series-from-Frobenius pairing convention is also explicitly stated by Lentfer. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

Equivalently,
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\]
Indeed, the coefficient of \(u^b\) in the \(w\)-summand is
\[
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{d+1}{2}}
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq[d]\\ |J|=b}}
q^{\sum_{i\notin J}i}
=
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
where \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\).

**Source caveat.** The sources state the \(q\)-Stirling Hilbert formula and the Frobenius formula; the above permutation product form should be presented as a short derivation, not as a verbatim quoted theorem unless a writeup independently cites a source where it is printed in that form. Rhoades–Wilson proved the Hilbert series, while Murai–Rhoades–Wilson later proved the full Fields Frobenius structure. ([cambridge.org](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/forum-of-mathematics-pi/article/hilbert-series-of-the-superspace-coinvariant-ring/D7F1C91BE28068FC384AFE85B6AED8B8))

---

## Final normalization checklist

- Use \(u\) as an **ordinary positive fermionic Hilbert variable**, not a supertrace variable.
- Identify \(R_n^{(1,1)}=SR_n\).
- Rename Murai–Rhoades–Wilson’s fermionic variable \(z\) to \(u\).
- Use \([r]_q=1+q+\cdots+q^{r-1}\) and the standard Gaussian binomial.
- In RSK, the statistic-bearing tableau is the **recording tableau** \(Q(w)\); with row insertion, \(\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w)\). If a different RSK convention is used, the inverse permutation may appear.

Under these normalizations, the hook formula in t5/t6 is source-consistent and has no smallest counterexample.

## (1) Exact known inputs to cite/state

**Supersymmetric universality.** Use Lentfer’s bosonic–fermionic coinvariant ring
\[

\]
For fixed \(n\), there are nonnegative integers \(c_\lambda(n)\), independent of \((k,j)\), such that
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=\sum_{\lambda\in P(k,j,n)} c_\lambda(n)\,s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u),
\qquad
P(k,j,n)=\{\lambda:\ell(\lambda)\le n,\ \lambda_{k+1}\le j\}.
\]
For \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), giving the original bosonic coefficients. For \((k,j)=(1,1)\), only \(\varnothing\) and hooks \((a,1^b)\) can contribute. Lentfer’s Hilbert series uses ordinary positive dimension-counting variables. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

**GL\((1|1)\) hook specialization.** Use Lentfer’s positive super-Schur convention
\[
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(\mathbf q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(\mathbf u),
\]
not a signed supertrace convention. In one even variable \(q\) and one odd Hilbert variable \(u\),
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,\qquad
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\quad(a\ge1,b\ge0),
\]
and non-\((1,1)\)-hook shapes specialize to \(0\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

**Superspace formula leading to the permutation product.** Identify \(R_n^{(1,1)}\) with the superspace coinvariant ring
\[
SR_n=\Omega_n/SI_n,\qquad
\Omega_n=\mathbb C[x_1,\ldots,x_n]\otimes\wedge\{\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_n\},
\]
with diagonal \(S_n\)-action and \(SI_n=((\Omega_n)^{S_n}_+)=(e_1,\ldots,e_n,de_1,\ldots,de_n)\). Murai–Rhoades–Wilson prove
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,z)=\sum_{k=1}^n C_{n,k}(\mathbf x;q)\,z^{n-k},
\]
where \(q\) is bosonic degree and \(z\) is fermionic degree, and
\[
C_{n,k}(\mathbf x;q)=
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(n)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)+\binom{n-k}{2}-(n-k)\operatorname{des}(T)}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)\\ n-k\end{bmatrix}_q
s_{\lambda(T)}(\mathbf x).
\]
Pairing Frobenius with \(h_{(1^n)}=h_1^n\), then applying row-insertion RSK with the recording tableau carrying descents, gives, after renaming \(z=u\),
\[
H_b(q):=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q .
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\]
This product form should be presented as a derivation from the Frobenius formula plus RSK, not as a verbatim cited theorem. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

---

## (2) Term-by-term triangular recurrence check

Let
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0,
\qquad
H_b(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]
The GL\((1|1)\) expansion is
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
c_\varnothing(n)+
\sum_{a\ge1,b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\bigl(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\bigr).
\]
Since the degree-zero component is one-dimensional, \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\). For fixed \(b\), the coefficient of \(u^b\) receives: \(1\) only if \(b=0\); \(C_b(q)\) from \(q^a u^b\); and \(q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q)\) from the second term attached to hooks with leg \(b-1\). Thus
\[
\boxed{H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).}
\]

The \(d=0\) case is also consistent with the permutation product. The only permutation with \(d(w)=0\) is the identity, with \(\operatorname{maj}(w)=0\), and its product contribution is the empty product \(1\), contributing only to \(H_0\). In the solved form
\[
D_b(q)=\sum_{\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,

\[

=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\]
into the \(w\)-summand of \(H_b\). No off-by-one issue appears.

---

## (3) Edge-case audit

For \(n=1\), \(S_1\) has only the identity, so \(H_0=1\), \(H_b=0\) for \(b>0\), \(c_\varnothing(1)=1\), and all nonempty hook coefficients vanish. For \(b=0\), the rectangle in the final formula is \(0\times(\operatorname{des}(w)-1)\), so only the empty partition \(\rho\) occurs; hence \(c_{(a)}(n)\) counts permutations with at least one descent and \(\operatorname{maj}(w)=a\), equivalently all permutations of major index \(a\ge1\). For \(b\ge n-1\), the final hook coefficient is zero because it requires \(\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1\), impossible since \(\operatorname{des}(w)\le n-1\). This is compatible with \(H_{n-1}\neq0\): the top fermionic term is supplied by \(q^{-1}C_{n-2}\), not by \(C_{n-1}\). All Gaussian binomials should use the convention \(\bigl[\begin{smallmatrix}r\\s\end{smallmatrix}\bigr]_q=0\) unless \(0\le s\le r\); equivalently, the final rectangle \(b\times(\operatorname{des}(w)-1-b)\) is used only when its width is nonnegative.

\]
For \(b=0\), this is \(H_0=1+C_0\). For \(b>0\), the \(\delta\)-term vanishes.

The identity permutation has \(d=\operatorname{des}=0\), \(\operatorname{maj}=0\), and contributes exactly \(1\) to \(H_0\) and nothing to \(H_b\) for \(b>0\). That is precisely the \(\delta_{b,0}\) term.

## 2. \(q\)-Pascal check

Murai–Rhoades–Wilson identify \(SR_n\) as the one-boson/one-fermion superspace coinvariant ring and prove
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,z)=\sum_{k=1}^n C_{n,k}(\mathbf x;q)z^{n-k},
\]
with the standard-tableau expansion of \(C_{n,k}\). Pairing Frobenius with \(h_1^n\), then using row-insertion RSK with \(\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w)\), gives
\[
H_b(q)=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q .
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_b H_b(q)u^b
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\]
([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

Define the claimed solution
\[
D_b(q)=
\sum_{\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q .
\]
For a fixed permutation with \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge1\) and \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\), the contribution to
\[
D_b+q^{-1}D_{b-1}
\]
is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right),
\]
because
\[
-1+M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}
=
M-bd+\binom b2+(d-b).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
this equals the \(w\)-summand of \(H_b\). The sole \(d=0\) case is the identity permutation, already accounted for by \(\delta_{b,0}\). Hence \(C_b=D_b\).

## 3. Small cases

### \(n=1\)
Only \(w=1\), with \((d,M)=(0,0)\).
\[
H_0=1,\qquad H_b=0\ (b>0).
\]
Thus
\[
C_b=0\quad\text{for all }b\ge0,\qquad c_\varnothing(1)=1.
\]

### \(n=2\)
Descent-major inventory:
\[
(0,0)^1,\quad (1,1)^1.
\]
So
\[
H_0=1+q,\qquad H_1=1.
\]
The recurrence gives
\[
C_0=q,\qquad C_1=H_1-q^{-1}C_0=1-1=0.
\]
Matches the claimed formula.

### \(n=3\)
Descent-major inventory:
\[
(0,0)^1,\quad (1,1)^2,\quad (1,2)^2,\quad (2,3)^1.
\]
Hence
\[
H_0=1+2q+2q^2+q^3,
\]
\[
H_1=2+3q+q^2,
\]
\[
H_2=1.
\]
The recurrence gives
\[
C_0=H_0-1=2q+2q^2+q^3,
\]
\[
C_1=H_1-q^{-1}C_0=q,
\]
\[
C_2=H_2-q^{-1}C_1=0.
\]
So the only nonzero nonempty hook coefficients are
\[
c_{(1)}=2,\quad c_{(2)}=2,\quad c_{(3)}=1,\quad c_{(1,1)}=1.
\]

### \(n=4\)
Descent-major inventory:
\[
(0,0)^1,\ (1,1)^3,\ (1,2)^5,\ (1,3)^3,
\]
\[
(2,3)^3,\ (2,4)^5,\ (2,5)^3,\ (3,6)^1.
\]
Thus
\[
H_0=1+3q+5q^2+6q^3+5q^4+3q^5+q^6,
\]
\[
H_1=3+8q+11q^2+9q^3+4q^4+q^5,
\]
\[
H_2=3+6q+4q^2+q^3,
\]
\[
H_3=1.
\]
The recurrence gives
\[
C_0=3q+5q^2+6q^3+5q^4+3q^5+q^6,
\]
\[
C_1=3q+5q^2+4q^3+q^4,
\]
\[
C_2=q,
\]
\[
C_3=0.
\]
These also agree directly with the claimed \(D_b\)-formula. For example, for \(b=1\), the \(d=2\) permutations contribute \(3q+5q^2+3q^3\), and the decreasing permutation with \(d=3,M=6\) contributes \(q^3+q^4\), giving \(C_1=3q+5q^2+4q^3+q^4\).

## 4. Final theorem statement

Let
\[
\operatorname{Des}(w)=\{i:w_i>w_{i+1}\},\quad
d(w)=|\operatorname{Des}(w)|,\quad
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in\operatorname{Des}(w)}i.
\]
Then
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]
For \(a\ge1\) and \(b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

No correction to the formula is needed. The proof should explicitly state: \(u\) is a positive fermionic Hilbert variable, \(R_n^{(1,1)}=SR_n\), the MRW permutation formula is derived via Frobenius plus row-insertion RSK, and Gaussian binomials are zero outside their natural range.

## Referee verdict: **no flaw found**

The proposed solution really answers the original problem, under the stated ordinary Hilbert-series normalization. The key source-dependent points check out: Lentfer’s bosonic–fermionic Hilbert expansion gives the same universal Hilbert coefficients after setting \((k,j)=(m,0)\), and his Hilbert variables count dimensions positively, not supertraces. His convention for super Schur functions is the positive one, and in \((1,1)\) variables Lemma 4.4 gives exactly
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,\qquad
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1},
\]
with non-\((1,1)\)-hooks vanishing. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

The use of superspace is also correct: Murai–Rhoades–Wilson’s \(SR_n=\Omega_n/SI_n\) is exactly the \((1,1)\) bosonic–fermionic coinvariant ring, with \(q\) tracking bosonic degree and \(z\) tracking fermionic degree; their Fields Frobenius formula and tableau expansion of \(C_{n,k}\) give the asserted \(H_b(q)\) after pairing with \(h_1^n\) and applying row-insertion RSK. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

The triangular relation
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q)
\]
is correct. The \(q\)-Pascal subtraction is also correct: for a permutation with \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge1\), the contribution of \(C_b+q^{-1}C_{b-1}\) becomes
\[
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right)
=
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q .
\]
The identity permutation is exactly the missing \(\delta_{b,0}\) term.

## Final theorem

Let
\[
\operatorname{Des}(w)=\{i:w_i>w_{i+1}\},\qquad
d(w)=|\operatorname{Des}(w)|,\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in\operatorname{Des}(w)} i .
\]
Then
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]
For a nonempty hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), with \(a\ge1\), \(b\ge0\),
\[
\boxed{
\sum_{a\ge1} c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q .
}
\]
Equivalently,
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]

Here \(\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b)\) means that \(\rho\) is a partition whose Ferrers diagram fits in a rectangle of height \(b\) and width \(d(w)-1-b\). The Gaussian binomial is interpreted as zero outside its natural range.

Edge cases also check:
- \(b=0\): only the empty \(\rho\) occurs, and the identity permutation is excluded, giving \(c_{(a)}(n)\) as the number of nonidentity permutations with major index \(a\).
- \(b\ge n-1\): no permutation has \(d(w)\ge b+1\), so \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\).
- \(n=1\): only \(c_\varnothing(1)=1\), all nonempty hook coefficients vanish.

No corrected formula or counterexample is needed.

# Problem We Are Solving
Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

# Budget
Advisor calls used so far: 9 of 10. **Remaining (including this call): 1.**

# Shared Knowledge Base (full view of progress so far)
# Shared Knowledge Base (full view — all agents, current run)

## Solver Notes (exploration log — not yet verified unless tagged)

For T ∈ SYT(n), let λ(T) be its shape, let Des(T) = {i ∈ {1,...,n−1}: i appears in a strictly higher row than i+1 in T}, let des(T) = |Des(T)|, and let maj(T) = ∑_{i ∈ Des(T)} i. With [a choose b]_q denoting the usual q-binomial coefficient, interpreted as 0 if b < 0 or b > a, the symmetric function C_{n,k}(x;q) = Δ′_{e_{k−1}} e_n evaluated at t = 0 has the Schur expansion C_{n,k}(x;q) = ∑_{T ∈ SYT(n)} q^{maj(T) + binom(n−k,2) − (n−k) des(T)} [des(T) choose n−k]_q s_{λ(T)}(x).  (from: literature_2505.24027)
    Proof sketch: The paper records this formula as a known description of C_{n,k}(x;q), citing the t = 0 Delta-conjecture literature. The substantive ingredients in the cited derivations are: (1) identify C_{n,k} with Δ′_{e_{k−1}} e_n at t=0, equivalently with a Hall–Littlewood or ordered-multiset-partition generating function; (2) pass from the combinatorial/quasisymmetric expansion to the Schur basis using Gessel’s standard-tableau expansion of Schur functions into fundamental quasisymmetric functions, so standard tableaux index the Schur summands; (3) for a tableau T, the choice of n−k selected descents contributes the q-binomial factor [des(T) choose n−k]_q; and (4) the normalization of the t=0 Delta operator gives the exponent maj(T)+binom(n−k,2)−(n−k)des(T). The vanishing convention for q-binomial coefficients enforces n−k ≤ des(T).

The symmetric group S_n acts diagonally by permuting the indices of both x_i and θ_i. Let e_1,...,e_n be the elementary symmetric polynomials in x_1,...,x_n, and let d e_j = ∑_{i=1}^n (∂e_j/∂x_i) θ_i. Define the superspace coinvariant ring SR_n = Ω_n / (e_1,...,e_n,d e_1,...,d e_n). Then its bigraded Frobenius characteristic is grFrob(SR_n;q,z) = ∑_{k=1}^n C_{n,k}(x;q) z^{n−k}, where q records x-degree, z records θ-degree, and C_{n,k}(x;q) is the symmetric function in Equation (2.5). Consequently, the ordinary bigraded Hilbert series is obtained from this last formula by replacing each s_{λ(T)} by f^{λ(T)}, the number of standard Young tableaux of shape λ(T). Lemma 2.2 identifies dim ε_μ·V with e_μ^⊥Frob(V); since the e_μ form a basis and the Hall inner product is nondegenerate, equality of all these parabolic antisymmetrized Hilbert series forces equality of graded Frobenius characteristics. Grouping terms with |J|=n−k gives the recurrence for [k]!_q Stir_q(n,k): when inserting the element n into an ordered set partition, either it creates a new block, giving the Stir_q(n−1,k−1) term, or it is inserted into one of k existing ordered positions with q-weight [k]_q, giving the [k]_q Stir_q(n−1,k) term; ordering the blocks accounts for [k]!_q. Equivalently, the same Hilbert series is obtained by taking dimensions in Theorem 5.2.
  • [arXiv:2404.17919 Theorem 1.2 (Rhoades–Wilson transfer principle)] Let K be a field of characteristic 0, let S=K[x_1,...,x_n], and let Ω=S⊗∧{θ_1,...,θ_n}, with S_n acting by simultaneous permutation of the x_i and θ_i. Let a=(S^{S_n}_+) be the ideal of S generated by symmetric polynomials with vanishing constant term, let I⊂Ω be the differential closure of a, equivalently the ideal generated by S_n-invariants in Ω with vanishing constant term, and let SR=Ω/I. For J⊆[n], let θ_J be the product of the θ_j with j∈J in increasing order, and define f_J=∏_{j∈J} x_j · ∏_{j∈J} ∏_{i=j+1}^n (x_j-x_i) ∈ S. For an ideal b⊂S and f∈S, write b:f={g∈S: gf∈b}. Suppose that for each J⊆[n], B(J)⊂S is a set of homogeneous polynomials whose images form a K-basis of S/(a:f_J). Then the disjoint union ⋃_{J⊆[n]} B(J)·θ_J has images forming a K-basis of SR. For a fixed J, examine the associated-graded quotient whose leading exterior term is h θ_J. Rhoades–Wilson compute the ideal of leading coefficients h which become zero at this stage: it is exactly a:f_J, i.e. h θ_J is killed modulo lower fermionic-support terms precisely when h f_J lies in the ordinary coinvariant ideal a. The factor f_J is the triangular/Vandermonde-type factor that appears when isolating the θ_J-coefficient of elements in the differential closure of a. Let A be the subarrangement of the augmented braid arrangement consisting of all coordinate hyperplanes H_{x_1},...,H_{x_n} together with all hyperplanes H_{x_j-x_i} for j∉J and j<i≤n. This A is a southwest arrangement, hence free. Its complementary product in the augmented braid arrangement is f~_J, so Proposition 5.2 identifies S/(a:f~_J) with the Solomon–Terao algebra ST(A,i), where i sends every partial derivative ∂_k to 1. The inequalities a_k<st(J)_k imply b_k<h_k, so these images are distinct members of the monomial basis of S/(a:f~_J). Let [r]_q = 1 + q + ... + q^{r-1}, [k]!_q = [k]_q [k-1]_q ... [1]_q, and let the q-Stirling numbers be defined by Stir_q(1,1)=1, Stir_q(1,k)=0 for k>1, and Stir_q(n,k)=Stir_q(n-1,k-1)+[k]_q Stir_q(n-1,k). Let I(Y) be the vanishing ideal and T(Y) the ideal generated by top homogeneous parts of elements of I(Y). Then Q[x_n]/T(Y) has dimension |Y|=|OP_{n,k}|. Conversely every pair (π,S) with S⊂Asc(π), |S|=n-k, gives an element of OP_{n,k}. For a permutation π, let maj(π)=∑_{i: π_i>π_{i+1}} i and asc(π)=|Asc(π)|. For σ=(π,S), let π^c be the value-complement permutation π^c_i=n-π_i+1 and define maj(σ)=maj(π^c)-∑_{i∈S} |Asc(π)∩{i,i+1,...,n-1}|. Define comaj(σ)=(n-k)(k-1)+binom(k,2)-maj(σ). Then ∑_{σ∈OP_{n,k}} q^{comaj(σ)} = ∑_{π∈S_n} q^{maj(π)} [asc(π) choose n-k]_q, where the q-binomial coefficient is interpreted as 0 if n-k>asc(π).  (from: literature_1609.07575)

A star set S={a_{j_1}<...<a_{j_h}} is equivalent to a partition λ inside an h by r-h rectangle by setting λ_t = r-h-j_t+t for t=1,...,h. These λ_t are weakly decreasing because j_{t+1}>j_t, nonnegative because j_t≤r-h+t, and at most r-h; the construction is reversible. Its size is |λ|=∑_{t=1}^h (r-j_t+1)-binom(h+1,2), since r-j_t+1 is the number of ascents weakly to the right of a_{j_t}. Now maj(π^c)=∑_{a∈Asc(π)} a and maj(π)+maj(π^c)=binom(n,2). For a subset J ⊆ [n], define its J-staircase st(J) = (st(J)_1,...,st(J)_n) by st(J)_i = |{a ∈ [i] : a ∉ J}|; equivalently st(J)_1 is 0 if 1 ∈ J and 1 if 1 ∉ J, and st(J)_{i+1}=st(J)_i if i+1 ∈ J while st(J)_{i+1}=st(J)_i+1 if i+1 ∉ J. Let [r]_q = 1+q+...+q^{r-1} for r ≥ 1 and [0]_q=0, and let [k]!_q = [k]_q [k-1]_q ... [1]_q. Define q-Stirling numbers Stir_q(n,k) by Stir_q(0,0)=1, Stir_q(0,k)=0 for k≠0, and Stir_q(n,k) = [k]_q Stir_q(n-1,k) + Stir_q(n-1,k-1). Then the polynomial identity holds: Σ_{J⊆[n]} z^{|J|} Π_{i=1}^n [st(J)_i]_q = Σ_{k=1}^n z^{n-k} [k]!_q Stir_q(n,k). Equivalently, the coefficient of z^r on the left is the q-degree generating function of all pairs consisting of a subset J⊆[n] of size r and a monomial x_1^{a_1}...x_n^{a_n} satisfying 0 ≤ a_i < st(J)_i for every i.  (from: literature_2301.09763)
    Proof sketch: Let G(n,k) be the coefficient of z^{n-k} on the left, i.e. the sum over subsets J⊆[n] with exactly k elements outside J. The formula st(J)_i=|[i]\J| makes the recurrence transparent. Split such J according to whether n∈J. If n∈J, then J'=J\{n} has k elements outside it in [n-1], the first n-1 staircase entries are those of J', and the final entry is k; this contributes [k]_q G(n-1,k). If n∉J, then J⊆[n-1] has k-1 elements outside it in [n-1], and adjoining the nth entry again multiplies by [k]_q; this contributes [k]_q G(n-1,k-1). Thus G(n,k)=[k]_q G(n-1,k)+[k]_q G(n-1,k-1), with G(1,1)=1 and the usual boundary values. The quantity H(n,k):=[k]!_q Stir_q(n,k) satisfies the same recurrence, since multiplying Stir_q(n,k)=[k]_q Stir_q(n-1,k)+Stir_q(n-1,k-1) by [k]!_q gives H(n,k)=[k]_q H(n-1,k)+[k]_q H(n-1,k-1). Hence G(n,k)=H(n,k). The monomial interpretation is immediate from Π_i [st(J)_i]_q = Σ_{0≤a_i<st(J)_i} q^{a_1+...+a_n}.

    Proof sketch: The proof has matching upper and lower bounds. For the upper bound, for every J⊆[n] the paper constructs elements q_{J,i}∈SI_n whose fermionic degree is |J| and whose Gale-leading θ_J coefficient is p_{J,i}. The polynomials p_{J,i} are the regular sequence of Theorem 4.12, with degrees st(J)_i. Choose homogeneous representatives B_n(J) for a basis of C[x_n]/(p_{J,1},...,p_{J,n}); their degree generating function is Π_i [st(J)_i]_q. A triangular straightening argument in Gale order says that any f θ_J can be reduced modulo SI_n to a linear combination of B_n(J) θ_J plus terms with strictly larger fermionic monomial in Gale order. Descending induction over Gale order shows that the union over J of B_n(J) θ_J spans SR_n, giving Hilb(SR_n;q,z) ≤ Σ_J z^{|J|}Π_i[st(J)_i]_q. For the lower bound, use differential operators D_J built from minors of the complete-homogeneous matrix H. Their key triangular property is that D_J(δ_n), where δ_n is the Vandermonde, is superharmonic and has θ_J coefficient ±f_J⊙δ_n, with only θ_K terms for K≤Gale J. The annihilator of f_J⊙δ_n in C[x_n] is (I_n:f_J). By Theorem 4.12, choose B_n(J) mapping to a basis of C[x_n]/(I_n:f_J), again with degree generating function Π_i[st(J)_i]_q. The final expression with q-Stirling numbers is exactly Lemma 2.1.

In particular, when j = 0 this specializes to the ordinary bosonic Schur expansion Hilb(R_n^(k,0); q_1,...,q_k) = ∑_{ℓ(λ)≤min(k,n)} c_λ s_λ(q_1,...,q_k).  (from: literature_2505.14885)

The condition λ∈P(k,j,n) is exactly the condition under which the corresponding simple gl(k|j)-character s_λ(q/u) can appear. If λ is a hook partition λ = (a,1^b) with a ≥ 1 and b ≥ 0, then s_λ(q/u) = q^a u^b + q^(a−1) u^(b+1). If λ is neither empty nor of the form (a,1^b), equivalently if λ_2 ≥ 2, then s_λ(q/u) = 0.  (from: literature_2505.14885)
    Proof sketch: Start from the defining expansion s_λ(q/u) = ∑_{ν⊆λ} s_ν(q) s_{λ′/ν′}(u). Since q is a single variable, s_ν(q) is nonzero only when ν is a one-row partition ν=(r), in which case it equals q^r. Since u is a single variable, the skew Schur function s_{λ′/ν′}(u) is nonzero only when the skew shape λ′/ν′ is a horizontal strip, in which case it is the corresponding power of u. For λ=(a,1^b), so λ′=(b+1,1^(a−1)), the only row partitions ν=(r) for which λ′/ν′ is a horizontal strip are r=a and r=a−1. These give the two terms q^a u^b and q^(a−1)u^(b+1), respectively; this also covers a=1, where the r=0 contribution is allowed. The empty partition gives 1. If λ_2≥2, then λ′ has at least two cells in its second column, and removing ν′=(1^r) from the first column cannot make λ′/ν′ a horizontal strip, so every summand vanishes. Useful consequence for hook coefficients: combining this lemma with Proposition 5.1 at k=j=1 gives Hilb(R_n^(1,1);q;u)=c_∅+∑_{a≥1,b≥0} c_(a,1^b)(q^a u^b+q^(a−1)u^(b+1)). Thus, if h_{r,s}=[q^r u^s]Hilb(R_n^(1,1);q;u), then for a≥1 and b≥0, c_(a,1^b)=∑_{i=0}^b (−1)^i h_{a+i,b−i}; equivalently h_{a,b}=c_(a,1^b)+c_(a+1,1^(b−1)) with the second term omitted when b=0.
  • [arXiv:2505.14885 Equation (50), quoted theorem of Rhoades--Wilson] For the coinvariant ring R_n^(1,1) with one set of n commuting variables and one set of n anticommuting variables, the bigraded Hilbert series is Hilb(R_n^(1,1); q; u) = ∑_{d=0}^n [d]_q! Stir_q(n,d) u^(n−d). Here [d]_q = 1+q+⋯+q^(d−1), [d]_q! = [d]_q [d−1]_q ⋯ [1]_q with [0]_q! = 1, and the q-Stirling numbers are defined by Stir_q(n,d) = [d]_q Stir_q(n−1,d) + Stir_q(n−1,d−1), with initial conditions Stir_q(0,d)=δ_{d,0}.  (from: literature_2505.14885)
    Proof sketch: Lentfer records this formula as a theorem proved by Rhoades--Wilson, originally conjectured by Sagan--Swanson; the present paper does not reprove it, but uses it as an input in Section 5. In the paper, substituting this formula into the cancellation identity of Proposition 5.2 yields the Sagan--Swanson identity ∑_{d=0}^n [d]_q! Stir_q(n,d)(−q)^(n−d)=1. For extracting hook Schur coefficients, combine this quoted Hilbert formula with Lemma 4.4 and Proposition 5.1. If h_{r,s}=[q^r u^s]Hilb(R_n^(1,1);q;u), then the formula gives h_{r,s}=[q^r]([n−s]_q! Stir_q(n,n−s)) for 0≤s≤n. Therefore the hook coefficient in the universal Hilbert expansion satisfies c_(a,1^b)=∑_{i=0}^b (−1)^i [q^(a+i)]([n−b+i]_q! Stir_q(n,n−b+i)) for a≥1,b≥0, with c_∅=1. This is the GL(1|1) extraction mechanism: the two monomials in the character of a hook simple module make the hook multiplicity an alternating finite difference of the q-Stirling Hilbert coefficients.

## Failed / Ruled-out Approaches
  • Use rectangle b by (des(T)-b) directly for C_b: It gives Fields' H_b summand rather than the hook coefficient C_b and double-counts the q^{-1}C_{b-1} contribution; for mu=(1,1,1), b=1 it gives C_1=q+q^2 instead of q.
  • Use a b by des(T)-b rectangle for hook coefficients.: That gives the Fields H_b summand before subtracting the q^{-1}C_{b-1} contribution; the correct hook coefficient uses [des(T)-1 choose b]_q and hence a b by des(T)-1-b rectangle.
  • Derive the result from the scalar Hilbert expansion alone: The scalar expansion does not by itself define the refined coefficients c_{lambda,mu} or justify the GL(1|1) superspace specialization; the stronger Frobenius/superspace universality theorem is needed.
  • Use the rectangle b by (des(T)-b): That gives Fields' H_b summand [des(T) choose b]_q rather than the hook coefficient C_b and overcounts, as shown by vertical-shape checks for n=3 and n=4.
  • Use a b by des(T)-b rectangle for the hook coefficient.: That rectangle gives the superspace fermionic-degree coefficient H_b before subtracting the q^{-1}C_{b-1} contribution; the hook coefficient requires [des(T)-1 choose b]_q.
  • Derive the hook formula from the original bosonic scalar Hilbert expansion alone, without superspace or GL(1|1) input.: The scalar expansion alone does not isolate hook Schur coefficients; one needs the superspace specialization or an equivalent triangular extraction mechanism.
  • Derive the hook coefficients from the scalar m-variable Hilbert expansion alone: The scalar expansion alone does not justify that the same c_lambda(n) appear in the GL(1|1) superspace Hilbert expansion; that universality/specialization statement is an external assumption.
  • Derive the theorem from the scalar Hilbert Schur expansion alone: The scalar expansion alone does not justify the (1|1) superspace specialization or the refined Fields Frobenius formula; those must be cited as external theorems.

## Referee verdict: **no flaw found**

The proposed solution really answers the original problem, under the stated ordinary Hilbert-series normalization. The key source-dependent points check out: Lentfer’s bosonic–fermionic Hilbert expansion gives the same universal Hilbert coefficients after setting \((k,j)=(m,0)\), and his Hilbert variables count dimensions positively, not supertraces. His convention for super Schur functions is the positive one, and in \((1,1)\) variables Lemma 4.4 gives exactly
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,\qquad
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1},
\]
with non-\((1,1)\)-hooks vanishing. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

The use of superspace is also correct: Murai–Rhoades–Wilson’s \(SR_n=\Omega_n/SI_n\) is exactly the \((1,1)\) bosonic–fermionic coinvariant ring, with \(q\) tracking bosonic degree and \(z\) tracking fermionic degree; their Fields Frobenius formula and tableau expansion of \(C_{n,k}\) give the asserted \(H_b(q)\) after pairing with \(h_1^n\) and applying row-insertion RSK. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

The triangular relation
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q)
\]
is correct. The \(q\)-Pascal subtraction is also correct: for a permutation with \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge1\), the contribution of \(C_b+q^{-1}C_{b-1}\) becomes
\[
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right)
=
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q .
\]
The identity permutation is exactly the missing \(\delta_{b,0}\) term.

## Final theorem

Let
\[
\operatorname{Des}(w)=\{i:w_i>w_{i+1}\},\qquad
d(w)=|\operatorname{Des}(w)|,\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in\operatorname{Des}(w)} i .
\]
Then
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]
For a nonempty hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), with \(a\ge1\), \(b\ge0\),
\[
\boxed{
\sum_{a\ge1} c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q .
}
\]
Equivalently,
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]

Here \(\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b)\) means that \(\rho\) is a partition whose Ferrers diagram fits in a rectangle of height \(b\) and width \(d(w)-1-b\). The Gaussian binomial is interpreted as zero outside its natural range.

Edge cases also check:
- \(b=0\): only the empty \(\rho\) occurs, and the identity permutation is excluded, giving \(c_{(a)}(n)\) as the number of nonidentity permutations with major index \(a\).
- \(b\ge n-1\): no permutation has \(d(w)\ge b+1\), so \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\).
- \(n=1\): only \(c_\varnothing(1)=1\), all nonempty hook coefficients vanish.

No corrected formula or counterexample is needed.

# Original Problem
Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1,
\]
and for every hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), \(a\ge1\), \(b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
w\in S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\right\}.
\]

\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a

q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

# Prior solution (for your reference only — do not include or quote it in your output)
## Original Problem

Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

## Problem Solved

Fix \(n\ge 1\). Work over \(\mathbb C\). For \(m\ge 1\), let
\[
P_n^{(m)}=\mathbb C[x_i^{(r)}:1\le i\le n,\ 1\le r\le m],
\]
with \(S_n\) acting diagonally by permuting the index \(i\). Let \(I_n^{(m)}\) be the ideal generated by all \(S_n\)-invariant polynomials in \(P_n^{(m)}\) with zero constant term, and define the diagonal coinvariant algebra
\[
R_n^{(m)}=P_n^{(m)}/I_n^{(m)}.
\]
Give \(R_n^{(m)}\) the \(\mathbb Z_{\ge0}^m\)-grading in which \(x_i^{(r)}\) has degree \(1\) in the \(r\)-th component and degree \(0\) in the others. The universal Hilbert coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) are the coefficients in the stable Schur expansion
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots,q_m)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots,q_m),
\]
where \(s_\lambda\) is the Schur polynomial and \(s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots,q_m)=0\) if \(\ell(\lambda)>m\).

A hook partition is written
\[
\lambda=(a,1^b)
\]
with \(a\ge 1\) and \(b\ge 0\). For a permutation \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\in S_n\), define
\[
{\rm Des}(w)=\{i\in\{1,\ldots,n-1\}: w_i>w_{i+1}\},
\]
\[
{\rm des}(w)=|{\rm Des}(w)|,\qquad
{\rm maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in{\rm Des}(w)} i.
\]
For integers \(h,r\ge0\), a partition \(\rho\subseteq h\times r\) means a weakly decreasing sequence
\[
\rho=(\rho_1,\ldots,\rho_h)
\]
with \(r\ge \rho_1\ge\cdots\ge\rho_h\ge0\), and \(|\rho|=\rho_1+\cdots+\rho_h\). If \(h=0\), only the empty partition is allowed.

The theorem proved here is:
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1,
\]
and for every hook \((a,1^b)\) with \(a\ge1\), \(b\ge0\),
\[
\boxed{
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\\
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
}
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ {\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

Here the Gaussian binomial coefficient is
\[
\begin{bmatrix} r\\ s\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{cases}
\displaystyle\prod_{i=1}^{s}\frac{1-q^{r-s+i}}{1-q^i},&0\le s\le r,\\[1em]
0,&\text{otherwise},
\end{cases}
\]
and, for \(0\le s\le r\),
\[
\begin{bmatrix}r\\ s\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq s\times(r-s)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]

In particular, when \(b=0\),
\[
c_{(a)}(n)=\#\{w\in S_n:{\rm des}(w)\ge1,\ {\rm maj}(w)=a\},
\]
and if \(b\ge n-1\), then
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0.
\]
This is exactly the requested hook-coefficient interpretation, not a relaxation.

## Proof

First note that the degree-zero component of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is spanned by the image of \(1\). Since every nonempty Schur polynomial is homogeneous of positive total degree and \(s_{\varnothing}=1\), the constant term of the Hilbert series gives
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1.
\]

We now use three standard facts, stated in the precise forms needed.

### Known input 1: the \(GL(1|1)\) super Hilbert specialization

Let
\[
SR_n=\mathbb C[x_1,\ldots,x_n]\otimes \Lambda(\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_n)\big/\langle \text{positive-degree }S_n\text{-invariants}\rangle
\]
be the superspace coinvariant algebra with one even set \(x_1,\ldots,x_n\) and one odd set \(\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_n\), where \(S_n\) permutes the indices simultaneously. Let \(q\) record \(x\)-degree and \(u\) record \(\theta\)-degree, using the ordinary unsigned Hilbert series
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)=\sum_{i,j\ge0}\dim(SR_n)_{i,j}q^iu^j.
\]
The \(GL(1|1)\) specialization theorem says that the same universal coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) satisfy
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)=\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u),
\]
where \(s_\lambda(q/u)\) is the one-even-one-odd super Schur character.

### Known input 2: one-even-one-odd super Schur characters

In one even variable \(q\) and one odd variable \(u\),
\[
s_{\varnothing}(q/u)=1.
\]
For a hook \((a,1^b)\), \(a\ge1\), \(b\ge0\),
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
All nonempty non-hook Schur characters vanish under this \(GL(1|1)\) specialization.

### Known input 3: ordinary superspace coinvariant Hilbert product formula

The ordinary bigraded Hilbert series of \(SR_n\) is
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-\binom{{\rm des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{{\rm des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\]

### Step 1: compute the coefficient of \(u^b\) from the product formula

Let
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)=[u^b]\,{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u).
\]
Fix \(w\in S_n\), and write
\[
d={\rm des}(w),\qquad M={\rm maj}(w).
\]
The coefficient of \(u^b\) in \(\prod_{i=1}^d(q^i+u)\) is obtained by choosing \(b\) factors to contribute \(u\), equivalently choosing \(d-b\) factors to contribute powers of \(q\). Thus
\[
[u^b]\prod_{i=1}^{d}(q^i+u)
=
q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Therefore
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}}
q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Since
\[
\binom{d+1}{2}-\binom{d-b+1}{2}
=
bd-\binom b2,
\]
we get
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

### Step 2: compare with the \(GL(1|1)\) Schur expansion

For \(b\ge0\), define
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
and set
\[
C_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0.
\]
Using the \(GL(1|1)\) specialization and the hook character formula,
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
1+
\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\left(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\right).
\]
Taking the coefficient of \(u^b\) gives the triangular relation
\[
\boxed{
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\delta_{b,0}
+
C_b^{(n)}(q)
+
q^{-1}C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
}
\]

### Step 3: solve the triangular recurrence

We claim that
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ {\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

For \(b=0\), the triangular relation gives
\[
C_0^{(n)}(q)=H_0^{(n)}(q)-1.
\]
But
\[
H_0^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{w\in S_n}q^{{\rm maj}(w)}.
\]
The unique permutation with no descents is \(12\cdots n\), and it contributes \(1\). Hence
\[
C_0^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ {\rm des}(w)\ge1}}q^{{\rm maj}(w)},
\]
which is the desired formula when \(b=0\).

Now let \(b\ge1\). From the triangular relation,
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=H_b^{(n)}(q)-q^{-1}C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
\]
Using the formula for \(H_b^{(n)}(q)\) and the induction hypothesis for \(C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q)\), the contribution of a fixed \(w\), with \(d={\rm des}(w)\) and \(M={\rm maj}(w)\), is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
-
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
this equals
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Thus
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Since
\[
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q=0
\]
unless \({\rm des}(w)-1\ge b\), this is equivalently
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ {\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

### Step 4: interpret the Gaussian binomial coefficient

If \(d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1\), then
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Therefore
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Since
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
the coefficient of \(q^a\) is exactly the number of pairs \((w,\rho)\) satisfying
\[
w\in S_n,\qquad d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,
\]
\[
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

Finally, the exponent is always positive in the nonzero case. Indeed, if \(d\ge b+1\), then the \(d\) descent positions have sum at least \(1+\cdots+d\), so
\[
{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}
\ge1.
\]
Thus the formula produces only powers \(q^a\) with \(a\ge1\), as required for hooks \((a,1^b)\).

When \(b=0\), the rectangle \(0\times(d-1)\) contains only the empty partition, so the formula reduces to
\[
c_{(a)}(n)=\#\{w\in S_n:{\rm des}(w)\ge1,\ {\rm maj}(w)=a\}.
\]
When \(b\ge n-1\), every permutation has \({\rm des}(w)\le n-1\), so the condition \({\rm des}(w)\ge b+1\) is impossible. Hence
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0
\]
for \(b\ge n-1\). This completes the proof.

\[

\]

\[
\sum_{w\in S_n}q^{{\rm maj}(w)}

Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

a one-variable term \(s_\nu(q)\) is nonzero only when \(\nu=(r)\). Also, \(s_{\lambda'/(1^r)}(u)\) is nonzero only when \(\lambda'/(1^r)\) is a horizontal strip. This happens precisely when \(\lambda\) is a hook. For \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), the only surviving choices are \(r=a\) and \(r=a-1\), giving
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\tag{2}
\]
Non-hook Schur functions vanish under this specialization, and \(s_{\varnothing}(q/u)=1\).

On the other hand, the Rhoades–Wilson Hilbert formula for \(SR_n\), together with the Remmel–Wilson/Carlitz permutation identity, gives
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-\binom{{\rm des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{{\rm des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\tag{3}
\]

Let
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)=[u^b]\,{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u).
\]
For fixed \(w\), write \(d={\rm des}(w)\) and \(M={\rm maj}(w)\). Then
\[
[u^b]\prod_{i=1}^d(q^i+u)
=
q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
with the convention that the expression is \(0\) when \(b>d\). Hence (3) gives
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}+\binom{d-b+1}{2}}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Since
\[
-\binom{d+1}{2}+\binom{d-b+1}{2}
=
-bd+\binom b2,
\]
we obtain
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\tag{4}
\]

Now define
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\qquad
C_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0.
\]
Using (1) and (2),
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
1+
\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\bigl(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\bigr).
\]
Taking the coefficient of \(u^b\) gives
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\delta_{b,0}+C_b^{(n)}(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
\tag{5}
\]

We solve this recurrence. For \(b=0\),
\[
C_0^{(n)}(q)=H_0^{(n)}(q)-1.
\]
By (4),
\[
H_0^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{w\in S_n}q^{{\rm maj}(w)}.
\]
The unique permutation with no descents is \(12\cdots n\), contributing \(1\). Thus
\[
C_0^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\{\rm des}(w)\ge1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)},
\]
which is the desired formula for \(b=0\).

Now assume \(b\ge1\). From (5),
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=H_b^{(n)}(q)-q^{-1}C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
\]
Using (4) and the induction hypothesis for \(C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q)\), the contribution of a fixed permutation \(w\), with \(d={\rm des}(w)\) and \(M={\rm maj}(w)\), is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
-
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
this simplifies to
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Therefore
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\{\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\tag{6}
\]

Finally, for \(d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1\),
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Substituting this into (6) gives
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Since
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
the coefficient of \(q^a\) is exactly the number of pairs \((w,\rho)\) satisfying
\[
w\in S_n,\qquad d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,
\]
\[
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

The exponent is always positive in the nonzero case: if \(d\ge b+1\), then the descent positions have sum at least \(1+\cdots+d\), so
\[
{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}
\ge1.
\]
Thus the formula indeed counts coefficients \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\) with \(a\ge1\). This proves the claimed hook formula.

1. **Patch for identifying \(c_\lambda(n)\).**  
   Let
   \[
   d_\lambda(n):=\sum_{\mu\vdash n}a_{\lambda,\mu}^{(n)}f^\mu.
   \]
   For \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), so diagonal supersymmetry gives
   \[
   \mathrm{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,0)};\mathbf q)
   =
   \sum_{\ell(\lambda)\le \min(k,n)}d_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q).
   \]
   The original bosonic expansion in \(k\) variables is
   \[
   \mathrm{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,0)};\mathbf q)
   =
   \sum_{\ell(\lambda)\le k}c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q),
   \]
   since \(s_\lambda(q_1,\dots,q_k)=0\) when \(\ell(\lambda)>k\). Schur polynomials with \(\ell(\lambda)\le k\) are linearly independent, hence
   \[
   c_\lambda(n)=d_\lambda(n)
   \quad\text{for }\ell(\lambda)\le \min(k,n),
   \]
   and \(c_\lambda(n)=0\) for \(n<\ell(\lambda)\le k\). Varying \(k\) proves the identification for all \(\lambda\), including all hooks.

2. **Patch for deriving equation (3).**  
   Rhoades–Wilson gives
   \[
   \mathrm{Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
   =
   \sum_{k=1}^n u^{n-k}[k]!_q\,\mathrm{Stir}_q(n,k).
   \]
   Put \(r=n-k\). The coefficient of \(u^r\) is
   \[
   [n-r]!_q\,\mathrm{Stir}_q(n,n-r).
   \]
   Remmel–Wilson’s identity gives
   \[
   \sum_{w\in S_n}q^{\mathrm{maj}(w)}
   \prod_{j=1}^{\mathrm{des}(w)}\left(1+\frac{u}{q^j}\right)
   =
   \sum_{r=0}^{n-1}u^r[n-r]!_q\,\mathrm{Stir}_q(n,n-r).
   \]
   Therefore
   \[
   \mathrm{Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
   =
   \sum_{w\in S_n}
   q^{\mathrm{maj}(w)}
   \prod_{j=1}^{\mathrm{des}(w)}\left(1+\frac{u}{q^j}\right).
   \]
   Since
   \[
   q^{\mathrm{maj}(w)}
   \prod_{j=1}^{d}\left(1+\frac{u}{q^j}\right)
   =
   q^{\mathrm{maj}(w)-\binom{d+1}{2}}
   \prod_{j=1}^{d}(q^j+u),
   \]
   where \(d=\mathrm{des}(w)\), this is exactly equation (3).

# Original Problem
Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

=
\#\{(w,\rho): w\in S_n,\ d=\mathrm{des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\
a=\mathrm{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|\}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ \mathrm{des}(w)\ge b+1}}

# Prior solution (for your reference only — do not include or quote it in your output)
**Theorem.** Fix \(n\ge 1\). For \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\in S_n\), let
\[
{\rm Des}(w)=\{i:1\le i<n,\ w_i>w_{i+1}\},\qquad
{\rm des}(w)=|{\rm Des}(w)|,\qquad
{\rm maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in{\rm Des}(w)}i .
\]
Then
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1,
\]
and for every hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), \(a\ge 1\), \(b\ge 0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\\
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ {\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q .
\]

Here
\[
\begin{bmatrix}r\\s\end{bmatrix}_q
=\sum_{\rho\subseteq s\times(r-s)}q^{|\rho|}
\]
for \(0\le s\le r\), and is \(0\) otherwise.

---

The degree-zero component of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is spanned by \(1\). Since every nonempty Schur polynomial has positive total degree and \(s_{\varnothing}=1\), the constant term of the Hilbert series gives
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1.
\]

Now let \(SR_n\) be the superspace coinvariant algebra with one bosonic and one fermionic set:
\[
SR_n
=
\mathbb C[x_1,\dots,x_n]\otimes \Lambda(\theta_1,\dots,\theta_n)
\Big/
\langle \text{positive-degree }S_n\text{-invariants}\rangle .
\]
Let \(q\) record \(x\)-degree and \(u\) record \(\theta\)-degree.

A standard diagonal supersymmetry theorem says that for the coinvariant algebra with \(k\) bosonic and \(j\) fermionic sets,
\[
R_n^{(k,j)},
\]
there are multiplicities \(a_{\lambda,\mu}^{(n)}\), independent of \(k,j\), such that its graded Frobenius characteristic has the form
\[
{\rm Frob}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q,\mathbf u;\mathbf z)
=
\sum_{\lambda,\mu}a_{\lambda,\mu}^{(n)}
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)s_\mu(\mathbf z).
\]
Applying the dimension map \(s_\mu(\mathbf z)\mapsto f^\mu=\dim S^\mu\) gives
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q,\mathbf u)
=

For \(j=0\), this is the original bosonic Schur expansion, so by uniqueness

a one-variable term \(s_\nu(q)\) is nonzero only when \(\nu=(r)\). Also, \(s_{\lambda'/(1^r)}(u)\) is nonzero only when \(\lambda'/(1^r)\) is a horizontal strip. This happens precisely when \(\lambda\) is a hook. For \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), the only surviving choices are \(r=a\) and \(r=a-1\), giving

Non-hook Schur functions vanish under this specialization, and \(s_{\varnothing}(q/u)=1\).

On the other hand, the Rhoades–Wilson Hilbert formula for \(SR_n\), together with the Remmel–Wilson/Carlitz permutation identity, gives

=

q^{{\rm maj}(w)-\binom{{\rm des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{{\rm des}(w)}(q^i+u).

Now define

\[

\sum_{\lambda\in P(k,j,n)}d_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u).

\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,0)};\mathbf q)
=
\sum_{\ell(\lambda)\le \min(k,n)}d_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q).
\]
On the other hand, the original bosonic Schur expansion in \(k\) variables is
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,0)};\mathbf q)
=
\sum_{\ell(\lambda)\le k}c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q),
\]
since \(s_\lambda(q_1,\dots,q_k)=0\) when \(\ell(\lambda)>k\). Schur polynomials of length at most \(k\) are linearly independent, so \(c_\lambda(n)=d_\lambda(n)\) for \(\ell(\lambda)\le \min(k,n)\), and \(c_\lambda(n)=0\) for \(n<\ell(\lambda)\le k\). Now specialize to \(SR_n=R_n^{(1,1)}\). The super Schur functions satisfy
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u). \tag{1}

\[

\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}}
\prod_{j=1}^{d}(q^j+u). \tag{7}
\]

For fixed \(w\) with \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\),
\[
[u^b]\prod_{j=1}^{d}(q^j+u)
=
q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}{d\brack b}_q
\]
when \(b\le d\), and is \(0\) otherwise. Hence (7) gives
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b}}
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}+\binom{d-b+1}{2}}
{d\brack b}_q.
\]
Since
\[
-\binom{d+1}{2}+\binom{d-b+1}{2}
=
-bd+\binom b2,
\]
we have
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
{\operatorname{des}(w)\brack b}_q. \tag{8}
\]

We solve (4) by induction on \(b\). For \(b=0\),
\[
C_0^{(n)}(q)=H_0^{(n)}(q)-1
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)}-1.
\]
The unique permutation with no descents is \(12\cdots n\), with major index \(0\), so
\[
C_0^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ \operatorname{des}(w)\ge1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)}.
\]
This is the desired formula for \(b=0\).

Now assume \(b\ge1\). From (4),
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=H_b^{(n)}(q)-q^{-1}C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
\]
Using (8) and the induction hypothesis, the contribution of a fixed permutation \(w\), with \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\) and \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\), is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d\brack b}_q
-
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity
\[
{d\brack b}_q
=
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q,
\]
this becomes
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d-1\brack b}_q.
\]
Therefore
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ \operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
{\operatorname{des}(w)-1\brack b}_q. \tag{9}
\]

Finally,
\[
{\operatorname{des}(w)-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(\operatorname{des}(w)-b-1)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Substituting this into (9) gives
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Since
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
the coefficient of \(q^a\) is exactly the number of pairs \((w,\rho)\) satisfying
\[
d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\qquad
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

The exponent is always positive in the nonzero case: if \(d\ge b+1\), then
\[
\operatorname{maj}(w)\ge 1+2+\cdots+d=\binom{d+1}{2},
\]
so
\[
\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}
\ge1.
\]
Thus the coefficient extraction indeed gives the stated formula for \(a\ge1\). ∎

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is

## Verdict

I find **no indexing or combinatorial error** in the claimed hook formula. The theorem is correct **provided** the proof explicitly cites the two substantial external inputs:

1. **Diagonal supersymmetry / universal super-Schur expansion**: the same universal coefficients occur after passing from \(m\) bosonic variable sets to the \((1|1)\) boson–fermion specialization. Lentfer’s diagonal supersymmetry theorem gives this universal super-Schur expansion. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.14885))  
2. **The Fields \(SR_n\) Frobenius formula**, now proved by Murai–Rhoades–Wilson: it gives the bigraded \(S_n\)-isomorphism type of the superspace coinvariant ring \(SR_n\), proving the Fields conjectures. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.24027))  

Without these inputs, the proof is not self-contained. In particular, older Hilbert-series-only results were insufficient: Rhoades–Wilson proved the bigraded Hilbert series but stated at that time that the \(S_n\)-module structure remained open. ([cambridge.org](https://www.cambridge.org/core/product/D7F1C91BE28068FC384AFE85B6AED8B8))

---

## Checks of the requested points

### 1. \(GL(1|1)\) character formula

Using the positive supersymmetric Schur convention
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)\,s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u),
\]
one has
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
Indeed, in one even variable only row shapes \(\nu=(r)\) survive, and in one odd variable the skew shape \(\lambda'/\nu'\) must be a horizontal strip. For \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), only \(\nu=(a)\) and \(\nu=(a-1)\) contribute. Non-hook shapes vanish. This is correct under the ordinary unsigned Hilbert convention; a signed supertrace convention would change signs.

### 2. Triangular relation

Refined by \(S_n\)-type, define
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b),\mu}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1,\mu}=0.
\]
Comparing coefficients of \(u^b s_\mu\) in the \((1|1)\) super-Schur expansion gives
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\delta_{b,0}\delta_{\mu,(n)}
+
C_{b,\mu}(q)
+
q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q).
\]
After forgetting \(S_n\)-structure, this becomes
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
\]
So the triangular relation is correct; the \(\delta\)-term is exactly the empty partition / degree-zero trivial representation.

### 3. Fields formula and \(q\)-Pascal step

The Fields formula gives
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Solving
\[
C_{b,\mu}=H_{b,\mu}-q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}
\]
uses
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
For a tableau with \(d=\operatorname{des}(T)\), the \(q^{-1}C_{b-1}\) contribution is exactly
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
so subtraction leaves
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Thus
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ \operatorname{des}(T)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

### 4. \(b=0,\ d=0\) exception

For \(b=0\),
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)=H_{0,\mu}(q)-\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
\]
The only standard tableau with no descents is the row tableau of shape \((n)\), so subtracting \(\delta_{\mu,(n)}\) removes exactly that case. Hence the formula correctly becomes
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ \operatorname{des}(T)\ge1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)}.
\]
No off-by-one error occurs.

### 5. Gaussian binomial to rectangles

Since
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|},
\]
the coefficient of \(q^a\) counts \((T,\rho)\) with
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(T)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

### 6. RSK convention

With ordinary row-insertion RSK,
\[
w\longleftrightarrow (P(w),Q(w)),
\]
and the standard descent convention “\(i\) is a descent if \(i+1\) lies in a lower row than \(i\),” one has
\[
\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w),\qquad
\operatorname{Des}(P(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w^{-1}).
\]
Thus the tableau \(T\) carrying the descent and major-index statistics must be identified with \(Q(w)\), not \(P(w)\). The claimed permutation formula uses the correct convention.

---

## Final validated statement

For \(n\ge1\), \(a\ge1\), and \(b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Also
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1.
\]
Consequently \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\) for \(b\ge n-1\). No smallest counterexample exists under the cited external theorems.
