## Theorem-ready Hilbert-level proof

Write

\[
d(w)=\operatorname{des}(w),\qquad M(w)=\operatorname{maj}(w),
\]

and define

\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1} c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0.
\]

Let

\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]

From the assumed \(GL(1|1)\) hook specialization,

\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
1+\sum_{a\ge1,b\ge0} c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
\left(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\right),
\]

so comparing coefficients of \(u^b\) gives the triangular relation

\[
\boxed{
H_b^{(n)}(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b^{(n)}(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
}
\tag{1}
\]

Now use the Fields/Rhoades-Wilson product formula

\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{M(w)-\binom{d(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{d(w)}(q^i+u).
\]

For fixed \(w\), the coefficient of \(u^b\) is

\[
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}}
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq [d]\\ |J|=b}}
q^{\sum_{i\notin J} i}
=
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq [d]\\ |J|=b}}
q^{M-\sum_{j\in J}j}.
\]

Using

\[
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq[d]\\ |J|=b}}q^{-\sum_{j\in J}j}
=
q^{-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix} d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]

we get

\[
\boxed{
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d(w)\ge b}}
q^{M(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix} d(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
}
\tag{2}
\]

Now define

\[
D_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{M(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix} d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

I claim \(C_b^{(n)}(q)=D_b^{(n)}(q)\). Indeed, for fixed \(w\), the contribution of \(D_b+q^{-1}D_{b-1}\) is

\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right),
\]

because

\[
q^{-1}q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}
=
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}q^{d-b}.
\]

By the \(q\)-Pascal identity,

\[
\begin{bmatrix} d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]

this equals the \(w\)-summand in \(H_b\). The only exceptional case is \(b=0,d=0\), i.e. the identity permutation, whose contribution is exactly the \(\delta_{b,0}\) term in (1). Therefore \(D_b\) satisfies the same triangular recurrence as \(C_b\), so

\[
\boxed{
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{M(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix} d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
}
\tag{3}
\]

Since

\[
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)} q^{|\rho|},
\]

we obtain the desired coefficient interpretation:

\[
\boxed{
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\quad d(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b),\\
a=M(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
}
\]

Also \(c_{\varnothing}(n)=1\). The exponent is always positive: if \(d\ge b+1\), then \(M\ge 1+\cdots+d\), so

\[
M-bd+\binom b2\ge \binom{d-b+1}{2}\ge 1.
\]

## Check: \(n=3\)

The descent-major data are:

- \(d=0,M=0\): one permutation;
- \(d=1,M=1\): two permutations;
- \(d=1,M=2\): two permutations;
- \(d=2,M=3\): one permutation.

Thus

\[
H_0=1+2q+2q^2+q^3,
\]

\[
H_1=2+3q+q^2,
\]

\[
H_2=1.
\]

The recurrence gives

\[
C_0=H_0-1=2q+2q^2+q^3,
\]

\[
C_1=H_1-q^{-1}C_0=q,
\]

\[
C_2=H_2-q^{-1}C_1=0.
\]

This matches the rectangle formula: for \(b=1\), only \(321\) has \(d\ge2\), and its contribution is

\[
q^{3-1\cdot2}=q.
\]

For \(n=4\), the same formula gives

\[
C_0=3q+5q^2+6q^3+5q^4+3q^5+q^6,
\]

\[
C_1=3q+5q^2+4q^3+q^4,
\]

\[
C_2=q,
\]

again confirming the exponent \(M-bd+\binom b2\) and the off-by-one rectangle \(b\times(d-1-b)\).

## Sufficiency and hidden assumptions

This is a fully Hilbert-level proof: no refined Frobenius coefficients \(c_{\lambda,\mu}\) are needed.

However, it is sufficient for the original hook-coefficient problem only after accepting the hidden universality input that the \(GL(1|1)\) superspace Hilbert expansion uses the same universal coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) as the ordinary multigraded Hilbert expansion. One also needs the positive, unsigned fermionic Hilbert convention, so that hooks specialize as

\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]

With those assumptions, the proof above directly recovers the original hook coefficients.

Source: final_solutions.jsonl — verified+refined text, the artifact that actually survived the verify+refine loop.
**Theorem.** Fix \(n\ge 1\). For \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\in S_n\), let
\[
\operatorname{Des}(w)=\{i:1\le i<n,\ w_i>w_{i+1}\},\quad
\operatorname{des}(w)=|\operatorname{Des}(w)|,\quad
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in\operatorname{Des}(w)}i .
\]
Then
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1,
\]
and for every hook \((a,1^b)\), \(a\ge1\), \(b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\Bigl\{(w,\rho):\, w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\Bigr\}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ \operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
{\operatorname{des}(w)-1\brack b}_q ,
\]
where
\[
{r\brack s}_q=\sum_{\rho\subseteq s\times(r-s)}q^{|\rho|}
\]
for \(0\le s\le r\), and is \(0\) otherwise.

**Proof.** The degree-zero component of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is spanned by \(1\). Since \(s_{\varnothing}=1\) and every nonempty Schur polynomial has positive total degree, the constant term of the Hilbert series gives
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1.
\]

Let \(R_n^{(k,j)}\) denote the bosonic-fermionic diagonal coinvariant ring with \(k\) commuting variable sets and \(j\) anticommuting variable sets. Thus \(R_n^{(k,0)}=R_n^{(k)}\), and \(R_n^{(1,1)}\) is the superspace coinvariant ring \(SR_n\). We use Lentfer’s diagonal supersymmetry theorem: there are nonnegative integers \(A_{\lambda,\mu}^{(n)}\), independent of \(k,j\), such that
\[
\operatorname{Frob}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q,\mathbf u;\mathbf z)
=
\sum_{\lambda\in P(k,j,n)}\sum_{\mu\vdash n}
A_{\lambda,\mu}^{(n)}s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)s_\mu(\mathbf z),
\]
where \(P(k,j,n)=\{\lambda:\ell(\lambda)\le n,\ \lambda_{k+1}\le j\}\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

Apply the dimension map \(s_\mu(\mathbf z)\mapsto f^\mu=\dim S^\mu\), and define
\[
d_\lambda(n)=\sum_{\mu\vdash n}A_{\lambda,\mu}^{(n)}f^\mu
\quad(\ell(\lambda)\le n),
\]
with \(d_\lambda(n)=0\) for \(\ell(\lambda)>n\). Then
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q,\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\lambda\in P(k,j,n)}d_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u).
\]
For \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), and \(P(k,0,n)=\{\lambda:\ell(\lambda)\le \min(k,n)\}\). Hence
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,0)};\mathbf q)
=
\sum_{\ell(\lambda)\le \min(k,n)}d_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q).
\]
On the other hand, the original bosonic Schur expansion in \(k\) variables is
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,0)};\mathbf q)
=
\sum_{\ell(\lambda)\le k}c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q),
\]
since \(s_\lambda(q_1,\dots,q_k)=0\) when \(\ell(\lambda)>k\). Schur polynomials of length at most \(k\) are linearly independent, so \(c_\lambda(n)=d_\lambda(n)\) for \(\ell(\lambda)\le \min(k,n)\), and \(c_\lambda(n)=0\) for \(n<\ell(\lambda)\le k\). Varying \(k\) gives \(c_\lambda(n)=d_\lambda(n)\) for every \(\lambda\).

Now specialize to \(SR_n=R_n^{(1,1)}\). The super Schur functions satisfy
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u). \tag{1}
\]
In one variable, \(s_\nu(q)\neq0\) only when \(\nu=(r)\), in which case \(s_\nu(q)=q^r\); similarly, \(s_{\alpha/\beta}(u)\neq0\) only when \(\alpha/\beta\) is a horizontal strip. From (1), for a hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\),
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}. \tag{2}
\]
Indeed, the only possible rows \(\nu=(r)\) giving a horizontal strip are \(r=a\) and \(r=a-1\). Also \(s_{\varnothing}(q/u)=1\). Therefore
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
1+\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\bigl(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\bigr). \tag{3}
\]

Define
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(SR_n;q,u).
\]
Taking the coefficient of \(u^b\) in (3) gives
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b^{(n)}(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q). \tag{4}
\]

We now compute \(H_b^{(n)}(q)\) independently. Rhoades and Wilson proved that
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{k=1}^n u^{n-k}[k]!_q\,\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k),
\]
where \(\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k)\) denotes the Carlitz \(q\)-Stirling number. ([cambridge.org](https://www.cambridge.org/core/product/D7F1C91BE28068FC384AFE85B6AED8B8)) Putting \(r=n-k\), this is
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{r=0}^{n-1}u^r[n-r]!_q\,\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,n-r). \tag{5}
\]
Remmel and Wilson’s identity gives
\[
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)}
\prod_{j=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}
\left(1+\frac{u}{q^j}\right)
=
\sum_{r=0}^{n-1}u^r[n-r]!_q\,\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,n-r). \tag{6}
\]
([sciencedirect.com](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0097316515000497)) Combining (5) and (6), and writing \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\), \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\), we obtain
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}}
\prod_{j=1}^{d}(q^j+u). \tag{7}
\]

For fixed \(w\) with \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\),
\[
[u^b]\prod_{j=1}^{d}(q^j+u)
=
q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}{d\brack b}_q
\]
when \(b\le d\), and is \(0\) otherwise. Hence (7) gives
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b}}
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}+\binom{d-b+1}{2}}
{d\brack b}_q.
\]
Since
\[
-\binom{d+1}{2}+\binom{d-b+1}{2}
=
-bd+\binom b2,
\]
we have
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
{\operatorname{des}(w)\brack b}_q. \tag{8}
\]

We solve (4) by induction on \(b\). For \(b=0\),
\[
C_0^{(n)}(q)=H_0^{(n)}(q)-1
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)}-1.
\]
The unique permutation with no descents is \(12\cdots n\), with major index \(0\), so
\[
C_0^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ \operatorname{des}(w)\ge1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)}.
\]
This is the desired formula for \(b=0\).

Now assume \(b\ge1\). From (4),
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=H_b^{(n)}(q)-q^{-1}C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
\]
Using (8) and the induction hypothesis, the contribution of a fixed permutation \(w\), with \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\) and \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\), is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d\brack b}_q
-
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity
\[
{d\brack b}_q
=
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q,
\]
this becomes
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d-1\brack b}_q.
\]
Therefore
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ \operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
{\operatorname{des}(w)-1\brack b}_q. \tag{9}
\]

Finally,
\[
{\operatorname{des}(w)-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(\operatorname{des}(w)-b-1)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Substituting this into (9) gives
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Since
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
the coefficient of \(q^a\) is exactly the number of pairs \((w,\rho)\) satisfying
\[
d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\qquad
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

The exponent is always positive in the nonzero case: if \(d\ge b+1\), then
\[
\operatorname{maj}(w)\ge 1+2+\cdots+d=\binom{d+1}{2},
\]
so
\[
\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}
\ge1.
\]
Thus the coefficient extraction indeed gives the stated formula for \(a\ge1\). ∎

response:
## Original Problem

Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

## Problem Solved

Fix \(n\ge 1\). Work over a characteristic-zero field, say \(\mathbb Q\). For \(m\ge1\), let
\[
P_n^{(m)}=\mathbb Q[x_i^{(r)}:1\le i\le n,\ 1\le r\le m],
\]
with \(S_n\) acting diagonally by \(\sigma\cdot x_i^{(r)}=x_{\sigma(i)}^{(r)}\). Let \(I_n^{(m)}\) be the ideal generated by all positive-degree \(S_n\)-invariant polynomials, and let
\[
R_n^{(m)}=P_n^{(m)}/I_n^{(m)}
\]
be the diagonal coinvariant algebra, multigraded by the total degree in each of the \(m\) variable sets. Let \(c_\lambda(n)\) be the universal Schur coefficients defined by
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\dots,q_m)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\dots,q_m),
\]
for all \(m\), with \(s_\lambda\) the Schur polynomial.

For a permutation \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\in S_n\), define
\[
\operatorname{Des}(w)=\{i\in\{1,\dots,n-1\}:w_i>w_{i+1}\},
\]
\[
\operatorname{des}(w)=|\operatorname{Des}(w)|,\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in\operatorname{Des}(w)}i.
\]
For integers \(b,r\ge0\), a partition \(\rho\) lies in a \(b\times r\) rectangle, written \(\rho\subseteq b\times r\), if \(\rho\) has at most \(b\) parts and every part is at most \(r\); write \(|\rho|\) for the sum of its parts.

The theorem proved is:

\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1,
\]
and for every nonempty hook partition \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), with \(a\ge1\) and \(b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q,
\]
where
\[
{r\brack s}_q=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq s\times(r-s)}q^{|\rho|}
\]
for \(0\le s\le r\), and \({r\brack s}_q=0\) otherwise.

In particular, since every \(w\in S_n\) has \(\operatorname{des}(w)\le n-1\), one gets
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\qquad\text{whenever }b\ge n-1.
\]

This is exactly the requested hook-coefficient interpretation, not a relaxation.

## Proof

First, the degree-zero component of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is spanned by \(1\). Thus the constant term of \(\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\dots,q_m)\) is \(1\). Since \(s_{\varnothing}=1\) and every nonempty Schur polynomial has positive total degree, the constant term in the Schur expansion is \(c_{\varnothing}(n)\). Hence
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1.
\]

We now use three standard inputs about the one-boson/one-fermion super coinvariant algebra. \]

# Solution
## Original Problem

Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

## Problem Solved

Fix \(n\ge 1\). Work over a characteristic-zero field, say \(\mathbb Q\). For \(m\ge1\), let
\[
P_n^{(m)}=\mathbb Q[x_i^{(r)}:1\le i\le n,\ 1\le r\le m],
\]
with \(S_n\) acting diagonally by \(\sigma\cdot x_i^{(r)}=x_{\sigma(i)}^{(r)}\). Let \(I_n^{(m)}\) be the ideal generated by all positive-degree \(S_n\)-invariant polynomials, and let
\[
R_n^{(m)}=P_n^{(m)}/I_n^{(m)}
\]
be the diagonal coinvariant algebra, multigraded by the total degree in each of the \(m\) variable sets. Let \(c_\lambda(n)\) be the universal Schur coefficients defined by
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\dots,q_m)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\dots,q_m),
\]
for all \(m\), with \(s_\lambda\) the Schur polynomial.

For a permutation \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\in S_n\), define
\[
\operatorname{Des}(w)=\{i\in\{1,\dots,n-1\}:w_i>w_{i+1}\},
\]
\[
\operatorname{des}(w)=|\operatorname{Des}(w)|,\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in\operatorname{Des}(w)}i.
\]
For integers \(b,r\ge0\), a partition \(\rho\) lies in a \(b\times r\) rectangle, written \(\rho\subseteq b\times r\), if \(\rho\) has at most \(b\) parts and every part is at most \(r\); write \(|\rho|\) for the sum of its parts.

The theorem proved is:

\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1,
\]
and for every nonempty hook partition \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), with \(a\ge1\) and \(b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q,
\]
where
\[
{r\brack s}_q=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq s\times(r-s)}q^{|\rho|}
\]
for \(0\le s\le r\), and \({r\brack s}_q=0\) otherwise.

In particular, since every \(w\in S_n\) has \(\operatorname{des}(w)\le n-1\), one gets
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\qquad\text{whenever }b\ge n-1.
\]

This is exactly the requested hook-coefficient interpretation, not a relaxation.

## Proof

First, the degree-zero component of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is spanned by \(1\). Thus the constant term of \(\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\dots,q_m)\) is \(1\). Since \(s_{\varnothing}=1\) and every nonempty Schur polynomial has positive total degree, the constant term in the Schur expansion is \(c_{\varnothing}(n)\). Hence
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1.
\]

We now use three standard inputs about the one-boson/one-fermion super coinvariant algebra. Let \(R_n^{(1,1)}\) denote the corresponding diagonal coinvariant algebra with one commuting variable set and one anticommuting variable set; let \(q\) record commuting degree and \(u\) record anticommuting degree.

**Known input 1: supersymmetric universality.** The same universal coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) appear in the one-boson/one-fermion Hilbert series:
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u),
\]
where \(s_\lambda(q/u)\) is the positive, unsigned \(GL(1|1)\) super-Schur specialization.

**Known input 2: \(GL(1|1)\) hook specialization.** In this convention,
\[
s_{\varnothing}(q/u)=1,
\]
and for hooks \((a,1^b)\), \(a\ge1\), \(b\ge0\),
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
All nonempty non-hook partitions specialize to \(0\).

Therefore
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
1+
\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\left(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\right).
\tag{1}
\]

\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\qquad
C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]
Extracting the coefficient of \(u^b\) in (1) gives the triangular relation
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q),
\tag{2}
\]
where \(\delta_{b,0}\) is \(1\) if \(b=0\) and \(0\) otherwise.

**Known input 3: Fields/Rhoades-Wilson product formula.** One has
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}

\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\tag{3}
\]

\[
[u^b]\prod_{i=1}^{d}(q^i+u)
=
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq\{1,\dots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}
q^{\sum_{i\notin J}i}.
\]
Since \(\sum_{i=1}^d i=\binom{d+1}{2}\),
\[
[u^b]\prod_{i=1}^{d}(q^i+u)
=
q^{\binom{d+1}{2}}
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq\{1,\dots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}
q^{-\sum_{j\in J}j}.
\]
The standard Gaussian-binomial identity gives
\[
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq\{1,\dots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}
q^{-\sum_{j\in J}j}
=
q^{-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q.
\]
Therefore
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b}}
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q.
\tag{4}
\]

Now define, for \(b\ge0\),
\[
D_b(q)=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}

\]
and set \(D_{-1}(q)=0\).

We claim that \(D_b(q)\) satisfies the same triangular recurrence as \(C_b(q)\):
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q).

For \(b=0\), formula (4) gives
\[

\]
The only permutation with no descents is the identity \(12\cdots n\), and it has major index \(0\). Hence
\[

=

\]

Now let \(b\ge1\). Fix \(w\in S_n\), and write \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\), \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\). The contribution of \(w\) to \(D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)\) is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{-1}q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}{d-1\brack b-1}_q.
\]
Since
\[
q^{-1}q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}
=
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}q^{d-b},
\]
this contribution equals
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity,
\[
{d\brack b}_q
=
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q,
\]
this becomes
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q,
\]
which is exactly the \(w\)-summand in (4). Thus (5) holds for all \(b\ge0\).

Comparing (2) and (5), we get
\[
C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q)
=
D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)
\]

\[
C_b(q)=D_b(q)
\]

\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q.
\tag{6}
\]

Finally, by the combinatorial interpretation of the Gaussian binomial coefficient,
\[
{d-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Substituting this into (6) gives
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}

\]
Therefore the coefficient of \(q^a\) is exactly the number of pairs \((w,\rho)\) such that
\[
w\in S_n,\qquad d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\qquad

\]

\[

\]

\[

\]

\[

\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}
\ge1.
\]

\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0
\qquad\text{for all }a\ge1\text{ whenever }b\ge n-1.
\]
This completes the proof.

<PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>
Insert the following clarifications.

1. **Supersymmetric universality.** Cite the diagonal-supersymmetry Hilbert-series theorem: for \(S_n\), there are coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\), independent of \((k,j)\), such that
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=\sum_{\lambda\in P(k,j,n)}c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u).
\]
When \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), so these are the same coefficients as in the original bosonic Schur expansion. When \(k=j=1\), this gives the super Hilbert series used in the proof.

2. **Hook specialization.** From
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u),
\]
with one \(q\)-variable and one \(u\)-variable, only \(\nu=(a)\) and \(\nu=(a-1)\) contribute for \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\). Hence
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
The empty partition gives \(1\), and non-hooks give \(0\).

3. **Product formula from Rhoades–Wilson.** Rhoades–Wilson gives
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=\sum_{k=1}^n [k]_q!\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k)u^{n-k}.
\]
To obtain the product formula used in the proof, insert the lemma
\[
\sum_{k=1}^n [k]_q!\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k)u^{n-k}
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\]
A standard proof is by comparing coefficients of \(u^b\). Let
\[
A_{n,d}=\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\\operatorname{des}(w)=d}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{d+1}{2}},
\qquad
P_{d,b}=q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}{d\brack b}_q .
\]
The coefficient of \(u^b\) on the product side is
\[
K_{n,b}=\sum_d A_{n,d}P_{d,b}.
\]
Carlitz’s \(q\)-Eulerian recurrence gives
\[
A_{n,d}=[d+1]_qA_{n-1,d}+[n-d]_qA_{n-1,d-1}.
\]
Using \(q\)-Pascal,
\[
P_{e+1,b}=q^{e+1-b}(P_{e,b}+P_{e,b-1})
\]
and
\[
[e+1]_qP_{e,b}=[e+1-b]_q(P_{e,b}+P_{e,b-1}).
\]
Therefore
\[
K_{n,b}=[n-b]_q(K_{n-1,b}+K_{n-1,b-1}).
\]
The coefficient of \(u^b\) in the Rhoades–Wilson \(q\)-Stirling side satisfies the same recurrence, since
\[
[k]_q!\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k)
=[k]_q\bigl([k]_q!\operatorname{Stir}_q(n-1,k)
+[k-1]_q!\operatorname{Stir}_q(n-1,k-1)\bigr),
\]
with \(k=n-b\). The initial conditions agree, so the product formula follows.

# Original Problem
Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=

# Prior solution (for your reference only — do not include or quote it in your output)
## Original Problem

Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

## Problem Solved

Fix \(n\ge 1\). Work over a characteristic-zero field, say \(\mathbb Q\). For \(m\ge1\), let
\[
P_n^{(m)}=\mathbb Q[x_i^{(r)}:1\le i\le n,\ 1\le r\le m],
\]
with \(S_n\) acting diagonally by \(\sigma\cdot x_i^{(r)}=x_{\sigma(i)}^{(r)}\). Let \(I_n^{(m)}\) be the ideal generated by all positive-degree \(S_n\)-invariant polynomials, and let
\[
R_n^{(m)}=P_n^{(m)}/I_n^{(m)}
\]
be the diagonal coinvariant algebra, multigraded by the total degree in each of the \(m\) variable sets. Let \(c_\lambda(n)\) be the universal Schur coefficients defined by
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\dots,q_m)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\dots,q_m),
\]
for all \(m\), with \(s_\lambda\) the Schur polynomial.

For a permutation \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\in S_n\), define
\[
\operatorname{Des}(w)=\{i\in\{1,\dots,n-1\}:w_i>w_{i+1}\},
\]
\[
\operatorname{des}(w)=|\operatorname{Des}(w)|,\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in\operatorname{Des}(w)}i.
\]
For integers \(b,r\ge0\), a partition \(\rho\) lies in a \(b\times r\) rectangle, written \(\rho\subseteq b\times r\), if \(\rho\) has at most \(b\) parts and every part is at most \(r\); write \(|\rho|\) for the sum of its parts.

The theorem proved is:

\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1,
\]
and for every nonempty hook partition \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), with \(a\ge1\) and \(b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q,
\]
where
\[
{r\brack s}_q=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq s\times(r-s)}q^{|\rho|}
\]
for \(0\le s\le r\), and \({r\brack s}_q=0\) otherwise.

In particular, since every \(w\in S_n\) has \(\operatorname{des}(w)\le n-1\), one gets
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\qquad\text{whenever }b\ge n-1.
\]

This is exactly the requested hook-coefficient interpretation, not a relaxation.

## Proof

First, the degree-zero component of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is spanned by \(1\). Thus the constant term of \(\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\dots,q_m)\) is \(1\). Since \(s_{\varnothing}=1\) and every nonempty Schur polynomial has positive total degree, the constant term in the Schur expansion is \(c_{\varnothing}(n)\). Hence
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1.
\]

We now use three standard inputs about the one-boson/one-fermion super coinvariant algebra. Let \(R_n^{(1,1)}\) denote the corresponding diagonal coinvariant algebra with one commuting variable set and one anticommuting variable set; let \(q\) record commuting degree and \(u\) record anticommuting degree.

**Known input 1: supersymmetric universality.** The same universal coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) appear in the one-boson/one-fermion Hilbert series:
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u),
\]
where \(s_\lambda(q/u)\) is the positive, unsigned \(GL(1|1)\) super-Schur specialization.

**Known input 2: \(GL(1|1)\) hook specialization.** In this convention,
\[
s_{\varnothing}(q/u)=1,
\]
and for hooks \((a,1^b)\), \(a\ge1\), \(b\ge0\),
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
All nonempty non-hook partitions specialize to \(0\).

Therefore
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
1+
\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\left(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\right).
\tag{1}
\]

For \(b\ge0\), define
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\qquad
C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]
Extracting the coefficient of \(u^b\) in (1) gives the triangular relation
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q),
\tag{2}
\]
where \(\delta_{b,0}\) is \(1\) if \(b=0\) and \(0\) otherwise.

**Known input 3: Fields/Rhoades-Wilson product formula.** One has
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\tag{3}
\]

Let \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\) and \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\). For fixed \(w\),
\[
[u^b]\prod_{i=1}^{d}(q^i+u)
=
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq\{1,\dots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}
q^{\sum_{i\notin J}i}.
\]
Since \(\sum_{i=1}^d i=\binom{d+1}{2}\),
\[
[u^b]\prod_{i=1}^{d}(q^i+u)
=
q^{\binom{d+1}{2}}
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq\{1,\dots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}
q^{-\sum_{j\in J}j}.
\]
The standard Gaussian-binomial identity gives
\[
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq\{1,\dots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}
q^{-\sum_{j\in J}j}
=
q^{-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q.
\]
Therefore
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b}}
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q.
\tag{4}
\]

Now define, for \(b\ge0\),
\[
D_b(q)=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q,
\]
and set \(D_{-1}(q)=0\).

We claim that \(D_b(q)\) satisfies the same triangular recurrence as \(C_b(q)\):
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q).
\tag{5}
\]

For \(b=0\), formula (4) gives
\[
H_0(q)=\sum_{w\in S_n}q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)}.
\]
The only permutation with no descents is the identity \(12\cdots n\), and it has major index \(0\). Hence
\[
H_0(q)=1+\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ \operatorname{des}(w)\ge1}}q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)}
=
1+D_0(q),
\]
which is (5) for \(b=0\).

Now let \(b\ge1\). Fix \(w\in S_n\), and write \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\), \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\). The contribution of \(w\) to \(D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)\) is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{-1}q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}{d-1\brack b-1}_q.
\]
Since
\[
q^{-1}q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}
=
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}q^{d-b},
\]
this contribution equals
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity,
\[
{d\brack b}_q
=
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q,
\]
this becomes
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q,
\]
which is exactly the \(w\)-summand in (4). Thus (5) holds for all \(b\ge0\).

Comparing (2) and (5), we get
\[
C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q)
=
D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)
\]
for every \(b\ge0\), with \(C_{-1}=D_{-1}=0\). For \(b=0\), this gives \(C_0(q)=D_0(q)\). Inductively, if \(C_{b-1}(q)=D_{b-1}(q)\), then the displayed equality gives \(C_b(q)=D_b(q)\). Therefore
\[
C_b(q)=D_b(q)
\]
for every \(b\ge0\). Hence
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q.
\tag{6}
\]

Finally, by the combinatorial interpretation of the Gaussian binomial coefficient,
\[
{d-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Substituting this into (6) gives
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Therefore the coefficient of \(q^a\) is exactly the number of pairs \((w,\rho)\) such that
\[
w\in S_n,\qquad d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\qquad
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

The exponent is always positive in the nonzero case. Indeed, if \(d\ge b+1\), then the sum of the \(d\) descent positions is at least \(1+2+\cdots+d\), so
\[
\operatorname{maj}(w)\ge \binom{d+1}{2}.
\]
Thus
\[
\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}
\ge1.
\]
So the coefficient extraction is genuinely for \(a\ge1\).

Finally, since \(\operatorname{des}(w)\le n-1\) for every \(w\in S_n\), if \(b\ge n-1\) then the condition \(\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1\) is impossible. Hence
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0
\qquad\text{for all }a\ge1\text{ whenever }b\ge n-1.
\]
This completes the proof.

s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
The empty partition gives \(1\), and non-hooks give \(0\).

3. **Product formula from Rhoades–Wilson.** Rhoades–Wilson gives
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=\sum_{k=1}^n [k]_q!\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k)u^{n-k}.
\]
To obtain the product formula used in the proof, insert the lemma
\[
\sum_{k=1}^n [k]_q!\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k)u^{n-k}
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\]

\[
A_{n,d}=\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\\operatorname{des}(w)=d}}

P_{d,b}=q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}{d\brack b}_q .

P_{e+1,b}=q^{e+1-b}(P_{e,b}+P_{e,b-1})

**Claim.** Fix \(n\ge 1\). Let \(c_\lambda(n)\) be the universal Schur coefficients in the Hilbert series of the diagonal coinvariant algebra with \(m\) bosonic sets of \(n\) variables. Then
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1,
\]
and for every nonempty hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), \(a\ge 1,\ b\ge 0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]

Here
\[
\operatorname{Des}(w)=\{i:w_i>w_{i+1}\},\qquad
\operatorname{des}(w)=|\operatorname{Des}(w)|,\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in\operatorname{Des}(w)}i,
\]
and \(\rho\subseteq b\times r\) means that \(\rho\) has at most \(b\) parts, each at most \(r\).

We use the diagonal-supersymmetry Hilbert-series theorem: for the bosonic/fermionic diagonal coinvariant algebra \(R_n^{(k,j)}\), there are coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\), independent of \((k,j)\), such that
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u).
\]
When \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), so these are exactly the pure-bosonic coefficients from the original Schur expansion. When \(k=j=1\), this gives
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u).
\]
This is the Hilbert-series form of Lentfer’s diagonal supersymmetry theorem. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.14885))

First, the degree-zero component of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is spanned by \(1\), so the constant term of its Hilbert series is \(1\). Since \(s_\varnothing=1\) and every nonempty Schur polynomial has positive total degree,
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]

For one bosonic and one fermionic variable, the positive super-Schur specialization satisfies
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq \lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u).
\]
In one variable, \(s_\nu(q)\neq 0\) only when \(\nu=(r)\), and \(s_{\alpha/\beta}(u)\neq 0\) only when \(\alpha/\beta\) is a horizontal strip. Hence non-hooks vanish. For a hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\),
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
1+\sum_{a\ge 1,\ b\ge 0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\bigl(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\bigr).
\]

Define
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]
Extracting the coefficient of \(u^b\) gives
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
\tag{1}
\]

Next we compute \(H_b(q)\). Rhoades and Wilson proved that
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{k=1}^n [k]_q!\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k)u^{n-k},
\]
where \(\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k)\) satisfies
\[
\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k)
=
[k]_q\operatorname{Stir}_q(n-1,k)
+
\operatorname{Stir}_q(n-1,k-1).
\]
([cambridge.org](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/forum-of-mathematics-pi/article/hilbert-series-of-the-superspace-coinvariant-ring/D7F1C91BE28068FC384AFE85B6AED8B8))

This \(q\)-Stirling formula implies the product form
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\tag{2}
\]
Indeed, let
\[
A_{n,d}
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ \operatorname{des}(w)=d}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{d+1}{2}}.
\]
Carlitz’s insertion argument gives
\[
A_{n,d}=[d+1]_qA_{n-1,d}+[n-d]_qA_{n-1,d-1}.
\tag{3}
\]
To see this, insert \(n\) into a permutation \(v\in S_{n-1}\). Inserting after a descent or at the final gap preserves the number of descents and contributes weights \(1,q,\dots,q^{\operatorname{des}(v)}\). Inserting before the first entry or after an ascent increases the number of descents by \(1\) and contributes weights \(1,q,\dots,q^{n-\operatorname{des}(v)-2}\). This gives exactly (3).

Now let
\[
P_{d,b}=[u^b]\prod_{i=1}^d(q^i+u)
=
q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}{d\brack b}_q,
\]
with the convention that \({d\brack b}_q=0\) if \(b<0\) or \(b>d\). The \(q\)-Pascal identities give
\[
P_{e+1,b}=q^{e+1-b}\bigl(P_{e,b}+P_{e,b-1}\bigr),
\]
and
\[
[e+1]_qP_{e,b}
=
[e+1-b]_q\bigl(P_{e,b}+P_{e,b-1}\bigr).
\]
Thus the coefficient \(K_{n,b}\) of \(u^b\) on the right side of (2) satisfies
\[
K_{n,b}=[n-b]_q\bigl(K_{n-1,b}+K_{n-1,b-1}\bigr).
\]
The coefficient of \(u^b\) in the Rhoades-Wilson \(q\)-Stirling formula satisfies the same recurrence, with the same initial condition \(K_{1,0}=1\). Hence (2) follows.

Using (2), for \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\) and \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\),
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d\ge b}}
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q.
\tag{4}
\]

Define
\[
D_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q,
\qquad D_{-1}(q)=0.
\]
We claim that
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q).
\tag{5}
\]
For \(b=0\), (4) gives
\[
H_0(q)=\sum_{w\in S_n}q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)}
=
1+\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ \operatorname{des}(w)\ge 1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)}
=
1+D_0(q),
\]
since the unique descent-free permutation is \(12\cdots n\).

For \(b\ge 1\), fix \(w\in S_n\) with \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\) and \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\). Its contribution to \(D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)\) is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{-1}q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}{d-1\brack b-1}_q.
\]
Since
\[
q^{-1}q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}
=
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}q^{d-b},
\]
this equals
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right)
=
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q,
\]
by \(q\)-Pascal. This is exactly the \(w\)-summand in (4). Hence (5) holds.

Comparing (1) and (5),
\[
C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q)
=
D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)
\]
for every \(b\ge 0\). Since \(C_{-1}=D_{-1}=0\), induction on \(b\) gives
\[
C_b(q)=D_b(q).
\]
Thus
\[
\sum_{a\ge 1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q.
\tag{6}
\]

Finally, the Gaussian binomial coefficient has the partition interpretation
\[
{d-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Substituting this into (6) gives
\[
\sum_{a\ge 1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Therefore \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\) is precisely the number of pairs \((w,\rho)\) satisfying
\[
w\in S_n,\qquad d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\qquad
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

The exponent is always positive when \(d\ge b+1\), because
\[
\operatorname{maj}(w)\ge 1+2+\cdots+d=\binom{d+1}{2},
\]
so
\[
\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}
\ge 1.
\]
This completes the proof.

# Original Problem
Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\ 
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|\}.
\]

Patch for Carlitz recurrence (3). Let \(v\in S_{n-1}\) have descents \(i_1<\cdots<i_e\), major index \(M\), and normalized weight \(q^{M-\binom{e+1}{2}}\).

Insert \(n\) into a gap.

1. If \(n\) is inserted after a descent \(i_j\), then the descent at \(i_j\) is replaced by one at \(i_j+1\), and descents to its right shift by \(1\). The number of descents remains \(e\), and the major-index increase is \(e-j+1\). Insertion at the final gap has increase \(0\). Thus descent-preserving insertions contribute \(1+q+\cdots+q^e=[e+1]_q\).

2. If \(n\) is inserted before the first entry or after an ascent, the number of descents increases to \(e+1\). Before the first entry, the major-index increase is \(e+1\), exactly canceled by the normalization change
\[

\]
After an ascent at position \(i\), if \(r=\#\{h\in\mathrm{Des}(v):h\le i\}\), then the major-index increase is \(i+1+(e-r)\), so the normalized exponent increase is
\[
i+1+e-r-(e+1)=i-r,
\]
the number of ascents among positions \(\le i\). As the ascents are read left to right, these shifts are \(1,\ldots,n-e-2\), together with \(0\) from the initial gap. Hence descent-increasing insertions contribute \([n-e-1]_q\).

Setting \(e=d\) in case 1 and \(e=d-1\) in case 2 gives
\[
A_{n,d}=[d+1]_qA_{n-1,d}+[n-d]_qA_{n-1,d-1}.
\]

Patch for the product-form recurrence. Put
\[
K_{n,b}=\sum_{d}A_{n,d}P_{d,b},\qquad
P_{d,b}=[u^b]\prod_{i=1}^d(q^i+u),
\]
with \(A_{n,d}=P_{d,b}=0\) outside their natural ranges. Using the recurrence for \(A_{n,d}\),
\[
K_{n,b}
=\sum_e A_{n-1,e}\bigl([e+1]_qP_{e,b}+[n-e-1]_qP_{e+1,b}\bigr).
\]
The identities
\[
P_{e+1,b}=q^{e+1-b}(P_{e,b}+P_{e,b-1}),
\]
and
\[
[e+1]_qP_{e,b}=[e+1-b]_q(P_{e,b}+P_{e,b-1})
\]
give
\[
[e+1]_qP_{e,b}+[n-e-1]_qP_{e+1,b}
=[n-b]_q(P_{e,b}+P_{e,b-1}),
\]
since \([e+1-b]_q+q^{e+1-b}[n-e-1]_q=[n-b]_q\). Therefore

\[

and the \(q\)-Stirling recursion gives the same recurrence, with \(L_{1,0}=K_{1,0}=1\) and zero boundary values. Hence the product formula (2) follows.

prompt:
You previously submitted a solution which you will find below. Unfortunately, a referee found some issues with your solution that you will need to address carefully and rigorously.
Use the prior solution and the critique below as inputs to your reasoning, but the artifact you produce must be a brand-new, self-contained solution that stands on its own.

# Original Problem
Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

# Claim You Are Establishing
After inserting the routine recurrence details above and using the verified Lentfer and Rhoades–Wilson results as black boxes, the proof establishes that for every \(n\ge1\), \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\), and for every nonempty hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), \(a\ge1\), \(b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\{(w,\rho): w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\ 
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|\}.
\]
It does not attempt to describe non-hook coefficients, which is consistent with the original problem.

Your refined solution must rigorously establish the **Claim You Are Establishing** above. The Claim may equal the Original Problem verbatim (in which case prove the original) or be a deliberately weakened / partial version (in which case prove that weaker claim — do NOT silently aim at the Original Problem). State the exact claim being proved up front and then prove it.

# Prior solution (for your reference only — do not include or quote it in your output)
**Claim.** Fix \(n\ge 1\). Let \(c_\lambda(n)\) be the universal Schur coefficients in the Hilbert series of the diagonal coinvariant algebra with \(m\) bosonic sets of \(n\) variables. Then
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1,
\]
and for every nonempty hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), \(a\ge 1,\ b\ge 0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]

Here
\[
\operatorname{Des}(w)=\{i:w_i>w_{i+1}\},\qquad
\operatorname{des}(w)=|\operatorname{Des}(w)|,\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in\operatorname{Des}(w)}i,
\]
and \(\rho\subseteq b\times r\) means that \(\rho\) has at most \(b\) parts, each at most \(r\).

We use the diagonal-supersymmetry Hilbert-series theorem: for the bosonic/fermionic diagonal coinvariant algebra \(R_n^{(k,j)}\), there are coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\), independent of \((k,j)\), such that
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u).
\]
When \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), so these are exactly the pure-bosonic coefficients from the original Schur expansion. When \(k=j=1\), this gives
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u).
\]
This is the Hilbert-series form of Lentfer’s diagonal supersymmetry theorem. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.14885))

First, the degree-zero component of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is spanned by \(1\), so the constant term of its Hilbert series is \(1\). Since \(s_\varnothing=1\) and every nonempty Schur polynomial has positive total degree,
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]

For one bosonic and one fermionic variable, the positive super-Schur specialization satisfies
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq \lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u).
\]
In one variable, \(s_\nu(q)\neq 0\) only when \(\nu=(r)\), and \(s_{\alpha/\beta}(u)\neq 0\) only when \(\alpha/\beta\) is a horizontal strip. Hence non-hooks vanish. For a hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\),
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
1+\sum_{a\ge 1,\ b\ge 0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\bigl(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\bigr).
\]

Define
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]
Extracting the coefficient of \(u^b\) gives
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
\tag{1}
\]

Next we compute \(H_b(q)\). Rhoades and Wilson proved that
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{k=1}^n [k]_q!\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k)u^{n-k},
\]
where \(\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k)\) satisfies
\[
\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k)
=
[k]_q\operatorname{Stir}_q(n-1,k)
+
\operatorname{Stir}_q(n-1,k-1).
\]
([cambridge.org](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/forum-of-mathematics-pi/article/hilbert-series-of-the-superspace-coinvariant-ring/D7F1C91BE28068FC384AFE85B6AED8B8))

This \(q\)-Stirling formula implies the product form
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\tag{2}
\]
Indeed, let
\[
A_{n,d}
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ \operatorname{des}(w)=d}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{d+1}{2}}.
\]
Carlitz’s insertion argument gives
\[
A_{n,d}=[d+1]_qA_{n-1,d}+[n-d]_qA_{n-1,d-1}.
\tag{3}
\]
To see this, insert \(n\) into a permutation \(v\in S_{n-1}\). Inserting after a descent or at the final gap preserves the number of descents and contributes weights \(1,q,\dots,q^{\operatorname{des}(v)}\). Inserting before the first entry or after an ascent increases the number of descents by \(1\) and contributes weights \(1,q,\dots,q^{n-\operatorname{des}(v)-2}\). This gives exactly (3).

Now let
\[
P_{d,b}=[u^b]\prod_{i=1}^d(q^i+u)
=
q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}{d\brack b}_q,
\]
with the convention that \({d\brack b}_q=0\) if \(b<0\) or \(b>d\). The \(q\)-Pascal identities give
\[
P_{e+1,b}=q^{e+1-b}\bigl(P_{e,b}+P_{e,b-1}\bigr),
\]
and
\[
[e+1]_qP_{e,b}
=
[e+1-b]_q\bigl(P_{e,b}+P_{e,b-1}\bigr).
\]
Thus the coefficient \(K_{n,b}\) of \(u^b\) on the right side of (2) satisfies
\[
K_{n,b}=[n-b]_q\bigl(K_{n-1,b}+K_{n-1,b-1}\bigr).
\]
The coefficient of \(u^b\) in the Rhoades-Wilson \(q\)-Stirling formula satisfies the same recurrence, with the same initial condition \(K_{1,0}=1\). Hence (2) follows.

Using (2), for \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\) and \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\),
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d\ge b}}
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q.
\tag{4}
\]

Define
\[
D_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q,
\qquad D_{-1}(q)=0.
\]
We claim that
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q).
\tag{5}
\]
For \(b=0\), (4) gives
\[
H_0(q)=\sum_{w\in S_n}q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)}
=
1+\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ \operatorname{des}(w)\ge 1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)}
=
1+D_0(q),
\]
since the unique descent-free permutation is \(12\cdots n\).

For \(b\ge 1\), fix \(w\in S_n\) with \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\) and \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\). Its contribution to \(D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)\) is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{-1}q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}{d-1\brack b-1}_q.
\]
Since
\[
q^{-1}q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}
=
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}q^{d-b},
\]
this equals
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right)
=
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q,
\]
by \(q\)-Pascal. This is exactly the \(w\)-summand in (4). Hence (5) holds.

Comparing (1) and (5),
\[
C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q)
=
D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)
\]
for every \(b\ge 0\). Since \(C_{-1}=D_{-1}=0\), induction on \(b\) gives
\[
C_b(q)=D_b(q).
\]
Thus
\[
\sum_{a\ge 1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q.
\tag{6}
\]

Finally, the Gaussian binomial coefficient has the partition interpretation
\[
{d-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Substituting this into (6) gives
\[
\sum_{a\ge 1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Therefore \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\) is precisely the number of pairs \((w,\rho)\) satisfying
\[
w\in S_n,\qquad d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\qquad
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

The exponent is always positive when \(d\ge b+1\), because
\[
\operatorname{maj}(w)\ge 1+2+\cdots+d=\binom{d+1}{2},
\]
so
\[
\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}
\ge 1.
\]
This completes the proof.

**Claim.** For every \(n\ge 1\),
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1.
\]
Moreover, for every nonempty hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), with \(a\ge1\) and \(b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\Bigl\{(w,\rho):\ w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\Bigr\}.
\]
Here
\[
\operatorname{Des}(w)=\{i:\ w_i>w_{i+1}\},\qquad
\operatorname{des}(w)=|\operatorname{Des}(w)|,\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in \operatorname{Des}(w)}i,
\]
and \(\rho\subseteq b\times r\) means that \(\rho\) is a partition with at most \(b\) parts, each at most \(r\).

**Proof.** The degree-zero component of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is spanned by \(1\), so the constant term of its Hilbert series is \(1\). Since \(s_{\varnothing}=1\) and every nonempty Schur polynomial is homogeneous of positive degree, we immediately get
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1.
\]

We now determine the hook coefficients. We use two standard facts about the superspace coinvariant algebra \(R_n^{(k,j)}\). First, its Hilbert series has the universal expansion
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\lambda}c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u),
\]
with the same coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) as in the purely bosonic case. In particular, for \(k=j=1\),
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{\lambda}c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u).
\]

For one bosonic variable \(q\) and one fermionic variable \(u\),
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\mu\subseteq\lambda}s_\mu(q)s_{\lambda'/\mu'}(u).
\]
In one variable, \(s_\mu(q)\neq0\) only when \(\mu\) is a row, and \(s_{\alpha/\beta}(u)\neq0\) only when \(\alpha/\beta\) is a horizontal strip. Thus \(s_\lambda(q/u)=0\) unless \(\lambda\) is a hook. If \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), then the only contributing rows are \((a)\) and \((a-1)\), giving
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]

Define
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and let
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]
Extracting the coefficient of \(u^b\) from the superspace Schur expansion gives
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
\tag{1}
\]

We next compute \(H_b(q)\). Let
\[
[r]_q=1+q+\cdots+q^{r-1},\qquad [r]_q!=\prod_{i=1}^r [i]_q.
\]
The Rhoades–Wilson Hilbert-series formula says
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{k=1}^n [k]_q!\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k)u^{n-k},
\]
where
\[
\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k)
=
[k]_q\operatorname{Stir}_q(n-1,k)
+
\operatorname{Stir}_q(n-1,k-1),
\]
with the usual zero boundary conditions.

We rewrite this formula in permutation form. For \(0\le d\le n-1\), set
\[
A_{n,d}
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ \operatorname{des}(w)=d}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{d+1}{2}}.
\]
We claim that
\[
A_{n,d}=[d+1]_qA_{n-1,d}+[n-d]_qA_{n-1,d-1}.
\tag{2}
\]

To prove this, insert \(n\) into a permutation \(v\in S_{n-1}\). Suppose
\[
\operatorname{Des}(v)=\{i_1<\cdots<i_e\},\qquad \operatorname{maj}(v)=M.
\]
If \(n\) is inserted after the descent \(i_j\), then the descent at \(i_j\) is replaced by one at \(i_j+1\), and descents to its right shift by \(1\). The number of descents remains \(e\), and the major-index increase is \(e-j+1\). Inserting \(n\) at the final gap also preserves the number of descents and gives major-index increase \(0\). \[

\]

\[

because a new descent appears at \(i+1\), and the \(e-r\) descents to the right shift by \(1\). The number of descents increases to \(e+1\), so the normalized exponent shift is
\[
i+1+e-r-(e+1)=i-r.
\]

\[

\[
P_{d,b}=[u^b]\prod_{i=1}^d(q^i+u).
\]

\[

=
q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}{d\brack b}_q,
\]

\[

\tag{3}
\]
and
\[

=

\tag{4}
\]

\[

\]

\[

q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\]

\[
\begin{aligned}
K_{n,b}
&=
\sum_e A_{n-1,e}
\Bigl([e+1]_qP_{e,b}+[n-e-1]_qP_{e+1,b}\Bigr) \\
&=
\sum_e A_{n-1,e}
\Bigl([e+1-b]_q+q^{e+1-b}[n-e-1]_q\Bigr)
\bigl(P_{e,b}+P_{e,b-1}\bigr) \\
&=
[n-b]_q\sum_e A_{n-1,e}\bigl(P_{e,b}+P_{e,b-1}\bigr) \\
&=
[n-b]_q\bigl(K_{n-1,b}+K_{n-1,b-1}\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\tag{5}
\]

\[
L_{n,b}=[u^b]\sum_{k=1}^n [k]_q!\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k)u^{n-k},
\]
then
\[
L_{n,b}=[n-b]_q!\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,n-b).
\]

\[

=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}(q^i+u).

\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d\ge b}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}

\]

Now define
\[
D_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q,

\[

\]

Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right).
\]
By \(q\)-Pascal,
\[
{d\brack b}_q
=
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q,
\]
so the contribution is exactly the \(w\)-summand in (7). Thus (8) holds.

Comparing (1) and (8), we have
\[
C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q)
=
D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)
\]
for every \(b\ge0\). Since \(C_{-1}=D_{-1}=0\), induction on \(b\) gives
\[
C_b(q)=D_b(q)
\]
for all \(b\ge0\). Therefore
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q.
\]

Finally, the Gaussian binomial coefficient has the partition interpretation
\[
{d-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Hence
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Taking the coefficient of \(q^a\) gives precisely
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\Bigl\{(w,\rho):\ w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\Bigr\}.
\]

The exponent is always positive when \(d\ge b+1\), since
\[
\operatorname{maj}(w)\ge 1+2+\cdots+d=\binom{d+1}{2},
\]
and therefore
\[
\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}
\ge1.
\]
Thus the formula indeed describes the coefficients of the nonempty hooks \((a,1^b)\).

## Theorem-ready Hilbert-level proof

Write

\[
d(w)=\operatorname{des}(w),\qquad M(w)=\operatorname{maj}(w),
\]

and define

\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1} c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0.
\]

Let

\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]

From the assumed \(GL(1|1)\) hook specialization,

\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
1+\sum_{a\ge1,b\ge0} c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
\left(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\right),
\]

so comparing coefficients of \(u^b\) gives the triangular relation

\[
\boxed{
H_b^{(n)}(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b^{(n)}(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
}
\tag{1}
\]

Now use the Fields/Rhoades-Wilson product formula

\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{M(w)-\binom{d(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{d(w)}(q^i+u).
\]

For fixed \(w\), the coefficient of \(u^b\) is

\[
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}}
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq [d]\\ |J|=b}}
q^{\sum_{i\notin J} i}
=
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq [d]\\ |J|=b}}
q^{M-\sum_{j\in J}j}.
\]

Using

\[
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq[d]\\ |J|=b}}q^{-\sum_{j\in J}j}
=
q^{-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix} d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]

we get

\[
\boxed{
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d(w)\ge b}}
q^{M(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix} d(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
}
\tag{2}
\]

Now define

\[
D_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{M(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix} d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

I claim \(C_b^{(n)}(q)=D_b^{(n)}(q)\). Indeed, for fixed \(w\), the contribution of \(D_b+q^{-1}D_{b-1}\) is

\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right),
\]

because

\[
q^{-1}q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}
=
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}q^{d-b}.
\]

By the \(q\)-Pascal identity,

\[
\begin{bmatrix} d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]

this equals the \(w\)-summand in \(H_b\). The only exceptional case is \(b=0,d=0\), i.e. the identity permutation, whose contribution is exactly the \(\delta_{b,0}\) term in (1). Therefore \(D_b\) satisfies the same triangular recurrence as \(C_b\), so

\[
\boxed{
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{M(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix} d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
}
\tag{3}
\]

Since

\[
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)} q^{|\rho|},
\]

we obtain the desired coefficient interpretation:

\[
\boxed{
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\quad d(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b),\\
a=M(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
}
\]

Also \(c_{\varnothing}(n)=1\). The exponent is always positive: if \(d\ge b+1\), then \(M\ge 1+\cdots+d\), so

\[
M-bd+\binom b2\ge \binom{d-b+1}{2}\ge 1.
\]

## Check: \(n=3\)

The descent-major data are:

- \(d=0,M=0\): one permutation;
- \(d=1,M=1\): two permutations;
- \(d=1,M=2\): two permutations;
- \(d=2,M=3\): one permutation.

Thus

\[
H_0=1+2q+2q^2+q^3,
\]

\[
H_1=2+3q+q^2,
\]

\[
H_2=1.
\]

The recurrence gives

\[
C_0=H_0-1=2q+2q^2+q^3,
\]

\[
C_1=H_1-q^{-1}C_0=q,
\]

\[
C_2=H_2-q^{-1}C_1=0.
\]

This matches the rectangle formula: for \(b=1\), only \(321\) has \(d\ge2\), and its contribution is

\[
q^{3-1\cdot2}=q.
\]

For \(n=4\), the same formula gives

\[
C_0=3q+5q^2+6q^3+5q^4+3q^5+q^6,
\]

\[
C_1=3q+5q^2+4q^3+q^4,
\]

\[
C_2=q,
\]

again confirming the exponent \(M-bd+\binom b2\) and the off-by-one rectangle \(b\times(d-1-b)\).

## Sufficiency and hidden assumptions

This is a fully Hilbert-level proof: no refined Frobenius coefficients \(c_{\lambda,\mu}\) are needed.

However, it is sufficient for the original hook-coefficient problem only after accepting the hidden universality input that the \(GL(1|1)\) superspace Hilbert expansion uses the same universal coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) as the ordinary multigraded Hilbert expansion. One also needs the positive, unsigned fermionic Hilbert convention, so that hooks specialize as

\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]

With those assumptions, the proof above directly recovers the original hook coefficients.

## Audit verdict

I found **no normalization discrepancy** in the hook-coefficient proof, provided the final writeup explicitly uses the **ordinary unsigned Hilbert-series convention** for fermions and either proves or cites the permutation product form. The only source-accuracy caveat is that the product formula is best presented as a **derived equivalent form** of the Fields/Rhoades–Wilson/Murai–Rhoades–Wilson formulas, not as a formula visibly stated in exactly that form.

---

## Theorem-ready external inputs

### 1. Universal super-Schur Hilbert expansion

Use Lentfer’s bosonic–fermionic coinvariant ring
\[
R_{n}^{(k,j)}
=
\mathbb C[x^{(1)},\dots,x^{(k)},\theta^{(1)},\dots,\theta^{(j)}]\Big/
\left\langle
\mathbb C[x^{(1)},\dots,x^{(k)},\theta^{(1)},\dots,\theta^{(j)}]^{\mathfrak S_n}_{+}
\right\rangle,
\]
where the \(x\)’s commute, the \(\theta\)’s anticommute, and \(\mathfrak S_n\) acts diagonally by permuting indices. Lentfer denotes the symmetric-group case by \(R_n^{(k,j)}\), so the ordinary \(m\)-set diagonal coinvariant algebra is \(R_n^{(m,0)}\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

The exact universality input is:

\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\lambda\in P(k,j,n)}
c_\lambda(n)\,s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u),
\qquad
P(k,j,n)=\{\lambda:\ell(\lambda)\le n,\ \lambda_{k+1}\le j\},
\]
with **nonnegative integers \(c_\lambda(n)\) independent of \((k,j)\)**. For \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), so these are exactly the ordinary Schur-Hilbert coefficients in the user’s \(m\)-boson expansion. For \(k=j=1\), the same \(c_\lambda(n)\) occur in the one-boson/one-fermion Hilbert series. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

**Caveat.** In the \((1,1)\) specialization, only \(\lambda_2\le1\) can contribute, i.e. \(\lambda=\varnothing\) or a hook \((a,1^b)\). Thus this specialization is sufficient for hook coefficients but does not determine nonhook coefficients.

---

### 2. Super-Schur normalization and signs

Use Lentfer’s positive super-Schur convention:
\[
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}
s_\nu(\mathbf q)\,s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(\mathbf u).
\]
This is also written plethystically as \(s_\lambda[\mathbf q-\epsilon\mathbf u]\), and **it is not the signed supertrace convention**. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

For one even variable \(q\) and one odd Hilbert variable \(u\),
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
\]
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\qquad(a\ge1,\ b\ge0),
\]
and
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=0\quad\text{if }\lambda\text{ is not contained in a }(1,1)\text{-hook}.
\]
This is exactly Lentfer’s Lemma 4.4. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

So, with
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u),
\]
the required triangular relation is
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
\]

**Sign caveat.** If someone uses a signed supertrace variable, then \(u\) is replaced by \(-u\), and the hook specialization would acquire alternating signs. That convention would break the proof. For example, at \(n=2\), the unsigned Hilbert series is \(1+q+u\), whereas a signed product would give \(1+q-u\).

---

### 3. Matching \(R_n^{(1,1)}\) with the superspace coinvariant ring \(SR_n\)

Murai–Rhoades–Wilson define
\[
\Omega_n=\mathbb F[x_1,\dots,x_n]\otimes \wedge\{\theta_1,\dots,\theta_n\},
\]
with diagonal action
\[
w\cdot x_i=x_{w(i)},\qquad w\cdot\theta_i=\theta_{w(i)}.
\]
They define
\[
SI_n=\big((\Omega_n)^{\mathfrak S_n}_{+}\big)
\]
and
\[
SR_n=\Omega_n/SI_n.
\]
They also record Solomon’s generating-set normalization
\[
SI_n=(e_1,\dots,e_n,de_1,\dots,de_n).
\]
Thus \(SR_n\) is exactly Lentfer’s \(R_n^{(1,1)}\), after identifying \(x_i=x_i^{(1)}\) and \(\theta_i=\theta_i^{(1)}\). Their variable \(z\) for fermionic degree should be renamed \(u\) in the hook proof. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

---

### 4. Superspace Hilbert/product formula

A source-clean input is Murai–Rhoades–Wilson’s Fields Frobenius formula:
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,z)
=
\sum_{k=1}^n C_{n,k}(\mathbf x;q)\,z^{n-k},
\]
where \(q\) tracks bosonic degree and \(z\) tracks fermionic degree. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

Their tableau expansion gives
\[
C_{n,k}(\mathbf x;q)
=
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(n)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)+\binom{n-k}{2}-(n-k)\operatorname{des}(T)}
\begin{bmatrix}
\operatorname{des}(T)\\ n-k
\end{bmatrix}_q
s_{\lambda(T)}(\mathbf x).
\]
Setting \(b=n-k\), pairing the Frobenius characteristic with \(h_{(1^n)}=h_1^n\) to forget the \(\mathfrak S_n\)-structure, and applying ordinary row-insertion RSK with \(\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w)\), gives
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}
\operatorname{des}(w)\\ b
\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
The Hilbert-series-from-Frobenius pairing convention is also explicitly stated by Lentfer. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

Equivalently,
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\]
Indeed, the coefficient of \(u^b\) in the \(w\)-summand is
\[
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{d+1}{2}}
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq[d]\\ |J|=b}}
q^{\sum_{i\notin J}i}
=
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
where \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\).

**Source caveat.** The sources state the \(q\)-Stirling Hilbert formula and the Frobenius formula; the above permutation product form should be presented as a short derivation, not as a verbatim quoted theorem unless a writeup independently cites a source where it is printed in that form. Rhoades–Wilson proved the Hilbert series, while Murai–Rhoades–Wilson later proved the full Fields Frobenius structure. ([cambridge.org](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/forum-of-mathematics-pi/article/hilbert-series-of-the-superspace-coinvariant-ring/D7F1C91BE28068FC384AFE85B6AED8B8))

---

## Final normalization checklist

- Use \(u\) as an **ordinary positive fermionic Hilbert variable**, not a supertrace variable.
- Identify \(R_n^{(1,1)}=SR_n\).
- Rename Murai–Rhoades–Wilson’s fermionic variable \(z\) to \(u\).
- Use \([r]_q=1+q+\cdots+q^{r-1}\) and the standard Gaussian binomial.
- In RSK, the statistic-bearing tableau is the **recording tableau** \(Q(w)\); with row insertion, \(\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w)\). If a different RSK convention is used, the inverse permutation may appear.

Under these normalizations, the hook formula in t5/t6 is source-consistent and has no smallest counterexample.

## Verdict

I find **no indexing or combinatorial error** in the claimed hook formula. The theorem is correct **provided** the proof explicitly cites the two substantial external inputs:

1. **Diagonal supersymmetry / universal super-Schur expansion**: the same universal coefficients occur after passing from \(m\) bosonic variable sets to the \((1|1)\) boson–fermion specialization. Lentfer’s diagonal supersymmetry theorem gives this universal super-Schur expansion. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.14885))  
2. **The Fields \(SR_n\) Frobenius formula**, now proved by Murai–Rhoades–Wilson: it gives the bigraded \(S_n\)-isomorphism type of the superspace coinvariant ring \(SR_n\), proving the Fields conjectures. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.24027))  

Without these inputs, the proof is not self-contained. In particular, older Hilbert-series-only results were insufficient: Rhoades–Wilson proved the bigraded Hilbert series but stated at that time that the \(S_n\)-module structure remained open. ([cambridge.org](https://www.cambridge.org/core/product/D7F1C91BE28068FC384AFE85B6AED8B8))

---

## Checks of the requested points

### 1. \(GL(1|1)\) character formula

Using the positive supersymmetric Schur convention
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)\,s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u),
\]
one has
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
Indeed, in one even variable only row shapes \(\nu=(r)\) survive, and in one odd variable the skew shape \(\lambda'/\nu'\) must be a horizontal strip. For \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), only \(\nu=(a)\) and \(\nu=(a-1)\) contribute. Non-hook shapes vanish. This is correct under the ordinary unsigned Hilbert convention; a signed supertrace convention would change signs.

### 2. Triangular relation

Refined by \(S_n\)-type, define
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b),\mu}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1,\mu}=0.
\]
Comparing coefficients of \(u^b s_\mu\) in the \((1|1)\) super-Schur expansion gives
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\delta_{b,0}\delta_{\mu,(n)}
+
C_{b,\mu}(q)
+
q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q).
\]
After forgetting \(S_n\)-structure, this becomes
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
\]
So the triangular relation is correct; the \(\delta\)-term is exactly the empty partition / degree-zero trivial representation.

### 3. Fields formula and \(q\)-Pascal step

The Fields formula gives
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Solving
\[
C_{b,\mu}=H_{b,\mu}-q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}
\]
uses
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
For a tableau with \(d=\operatorname{des}(T)\), the \(q^{-1}C_{b-1}\) contribution is exactly
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
so subtraction leaves
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Thus
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ \operatorname{des}(T)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

### 4. \(b=0,\ d=0\) exception

For \(b=0\),
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)=H_{0,\mu}(q)-\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
\]
The only standard tableau with no descents is the row tableau of shape \((n)\), so subtracting \(\delta_{\mu,(n)}\) removes exactly that case. Hence the formula correctly becomes
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ \operatorname{des}(T)\ge1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)}.
\]
No off-by-one error occurs.

### 5. Gaussian binomial to rectangles

Since
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|},
\]
the coefficient of \(q^a\) counts \((T,\rho)\) with
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(T)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

### 6. RSK convention

With ordinary row-insertion RSK,
\[
w\longleftrightarrow (P(w),Q(w)),
\]
and the standard descent convention “\(i\) is a descent if \(i+1\) lies in a lower row than \(i\),” one has
\[
\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w),\qquad
\operatorname{Des}(P(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w^{-1}).
\]
Thus the tableau \(T\) carrying the descent and major-index statistics must be identified with \(Q(w)\), not \(P(w)\). The claimed permutation formula uses the correct convention.

---

## Final validated statement

For \(n\ge1\), \(a\ge1\), and \(b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Also
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1.
\]
Consequently \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\) for \(b\ge n-1\). No smallest counterexample exists under the cited external theorems.
