## Task writeup_r3_90423d — writeup (full problem): For every n≥1 and every nonempty hook partition λ=(a,1^b) with a≥1 and b≥0, the universal Hilbert-series coefficient c_λ(n) equals the number of pairs (w,rho) such that w is a permutation of n, des(w)≥b+1, rho is contained in a b by des(w)-1-b rectangle, and a=maj(w)-b des(w)+binom(b,2)+|rho|; also c_∅(n)=1.
[verified ✓ | is_relaxation: False | problem_solved (verifier-canonical, self-contained): Accepting the verified black-box results, the proof establishes the full stated hook interpretation: for every \(n\ge1\), \(c_{\varnothing}(n)=1\), and for every hook \((a,1^b)\), \(a\ge1\), \(b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\{(w,\rho):w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|\}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ \operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
{\operatorname{des}(w)-1\brack b}_q .
\]]
Source: final_solutions.jsonl — verified+refined text, the artifact that actually survived the verify+refine loop.
**Theorem.** Fix \(n\ge 1\). For \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\in S_n\), let
\[
\operatorname{Des}(w)=\{i:1\le i<n,\ w_i>w_{i+1}\},\quad
\operatorname{des}(w)=|\operatorname{Des}(w)|,\quad
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in\operatorname{Des}(w)}i .
\]
Then
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1,
\]
and for every hook \((a,1^b)\), \(a\ge1\), \(b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\Bigl\{(w,\rho):\, w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\Bigr\}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ \operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
{\operatorname{des}(w)-1\brack b}_q ,
\]
where
\[
{r\brack s}_q=\sum_{\rho\subseteq s\times(r-s)}q^{|\rho|}
\]
for \(0\le s\le r\), and is \(0\) otherwise.

**Proof.** The degree-zero component of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is spanned by \(1\). Since \(s_{\varnothing}=1\) and every nonempty Schur polynomial has positive total degree, the constant term of the Hilbert series gives
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1.
\]

Let \(R_n^{(k,j)}\) denote the bosonic-fermionic diagonal coinvariant ring with \(k\) commuting variable sets and \(j\) anticommuting variable sets. Thus \(R_n^{(k,0)}=R_n^{(k)}\), and \(R_n^{(1,1)}\) is the superspace coinvariant ring \(SR_n\). We use Lentfer’s diagonal supersymmetry theorem: there are nonnegative integers \(A_{\lambda,\mu}^{(n)}\), independent of \(k,j\), such that
\[
\operatorname{Frob}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q,\mathbf u;\mathbf z)
=
\sum_{\lambda\in P(k,j,n)}\sum_{\mu\vdash n}
A_{\lambda,\mu}^{(n)}s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)s_\mu(\mathbf z),
\]
where \(P(k,j,n)=\{\lambda:\ell(\lambda)\le n,\ \lambda_{k+1}\le j\}\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

Apply the dimension map \(s_\mu(\mathbf z)\mapsto f^\mu=\dim S^\mu\), and define
\[
d_\lambda(n)=\sum_{\mu\vdash n}A_{\lambda,\mu}^{(n)}f^\mu
\quad(\ell(\lambda)\le n),
\]
with \(d_\lambda(n)=0\) for \(\ell(\lambda)>n\). Then
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q,\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\lambda\in P(k,j,n)}d_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u).
\]
For \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), and \(P(k,0,n)=\{\lambda:\ell(\lambda)\le \min(k,n)\}\). Hence
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,0)};\mathbf q)
=
\sum_{\ell(\lambda)\le \min(k,n)}d_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q).
\]
On the other hand, the original bosonic Schur expansion in \(k\) variables is
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,0)};\mathbf q)
=
\sum_{\ell(\lambda)\le k}c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q),
\]
since \(s_\lambda(q_1,\dots,q_k)=0\) when \(\ell(\lambda)>k\). Schur polynomials of length at most \(k\) are linearly independent, so \(c_\lambda(n)=d_\lambda(n)\) for \(\ell(\lambda)\le \min(k,n)\), and \(c_\lambda(n)=0\) for \(n<\ell(\lambda)\le k\). Varying \(k\) gives \(c_\lambda(n)=d_\lambda(n)\) for every \(\lambda\).

Now specialize to \(SR_n=R_n^{(1,1)}\). The super Schur functions satisfy
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u). \tag{1}
\]
In one variable, \(s_\nu(q)\neq0\) only when \(\nu=(r)\), in which case \(s_\nu(q)=q^r\); similarly, \(s_{\alpha/\beta}(u)\neq0\) only when \(\alpha/\beta\) is a horizontal strip. From (1), for a hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\),
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}. \tag{2}
\]
Indeed, the only possible rows \(\nu=(r)\) giving a horizontal strip are \(r=a\) and \(r=a-1\). Also \(s_{\varnothing}(q/u)=1\). Therefore
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
1+\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\bigl(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\bigr). \tag{3}
\]

Define
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(SR_n;q,u).
\]
Taking the coefficient of \(u^b\) in (3) gives
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b^{(n)}(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q). \tag{4}
\]

We now compute \(H_b^{(n)}(q)\) independently. Rhoades and Wilson proved that
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{k=1}^n u^{n-k}[k]!_q\,\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k),
\]
where \(\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k)\) denotes the Carlitz \(q\)-Stirling number. ([cambridge.org](https://www.cambridge.org/core/product/D7F1C91BE28068FC384AFE85B6AED8B8)) Putting \(r=n-k\), this is
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{r=0}^{n-1}u^r[n-r]!_q\,\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,n-r). \tag{5}
\]
Remmel and Wilson’s identity gives
\[
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)}
\prod_{j=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}
\left(1+\frac{u}{q^j}\right)
=
\sum_{r=0}^{n-1}u^r[n-r]!_q\,\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,n-r). \tag{6}
\]
([sciencedirect.com](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0097316515000497)) Combining (5) and (6), and writing \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\), \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\), we obtain
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}}
\prod_{j=1}^{d}(q^j+u). \tag{7}
\]

For fixed \(w\) with \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\),
\[
[u^b]\prod_{j=1}^{d}(q^j+u)
=
q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}{d\brack b}_q
\]
when \(b\le d\), and is \(0\) otherwise. Hence (7) gives
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b}}
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}+\binom{d-b+1}{2}}
{d\brack b}_q.
\]
Since
\[
-\binom{d+1}{2}+\binom{d-b+1}{2}
=
-bd+\binom b2,
\]
we have
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
{\operatorname{des}(w)\brack b}_q. \tag{8}
\]

We solve (4) by induction on \(b\). For \(b=0\),
\[
C_0^{(n)}(q)=H_0^{(n)}(q)-1
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)}-1.
\]
The unique permutation with no descents is \(12\cdots n\), with major index \(0\), so
\[
C_0^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ \operatorname{des}(w)\ge1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)}.
\]
This is the desired formula for \(b=0\).

Now assume \(b\ge1\). From (4),
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=H_b^{(n)}(q)-q^{-1}C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
\]
Using (8) and the induction hypothesis, the contribution of a fixed permutation \(w\), with \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\) and \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\), is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d\brack b}_q
-
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity
\[
{d\brack b}_q
=
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q,
\]
this becomes
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d-1\brack b}_q.
\]
Therefore
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ \operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
{\operatorname{des}(w)-1\brack b}_q. \tag{9}
\]

Finally,
\[
{\operatorname{des}(w)-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(\operatorname{des}(w)-b-1)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Substituting this into (9) gives
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Since
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
the coefficient of \(q^a\) is exactly the number of pairs \((w,\rho)\) satisfying
\[
d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\qquad
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

The exponent is always positive in the nonzero case: if \(d\ge b+1\), then
\[
\operatorname{maj}(w)\ge 1+2+\cdots+d=\binom{d+1}{2},
\]
so
\[
\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}
\ge1.
\]
Thus the coefficient extraction indeed gives the stated formula for \(a\ge1\). ∎

response:
## Original Problem

Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

## Problem Solved

For every integer \(n\ge1\), let \(c_\lambda(n)\) be the universal Schur-Hilbert coefficients defined by
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\dots,q_m)=\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\dots,q_m)
\]
for the diagonal coinvariant algebra \(R_n^{(m)}\) with \(m\) sets of \(n\) commuting variables. For \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\in S_n\), define
\[
{\rm Des}(w)=\{i:1\le i<n,\ w_i>w_{i+1}\},\quad
{\rm des}(w)=|{\rm Des}(w)|,\quad
{\rm maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in{\rm Des}(w)}i.
\]
For a partition \(\rho\), write \(|\rho|\) for the sum of its parts, and say that \(\rho\) is contained in a \(b\) by \(r\) rectangle if \(\rho\) has at most \(b\) parts and largest part at most \(r\).

Then
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1,
\]
and for every nonempty hook partition \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), with \(a\ge1\) and \(b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\Bigl\{(w,\rho):\, w\in S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\Bigr\}.
\]
Equivalently, if
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
then
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q,
\]
where \({r\brack s}_q\) is the Gaussian binomial coefficient, with invalid values taken to be \(0\). In particular, \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\) for all \(a\ge1\) whenever \(b\ge n-1\). This is exactly the requested hook-shape interpretation, not a relaxation.

## Proof

Fix \(n\ge1\). We use two known inputs.

First, by diagonal supersymmetry, the same universal coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) occurring in the ordinary bosonic Schur-Hilbert expansion also occur in the ordinary unsigned Hilbert series of the one-boson/one-fermion coinvariant ring \(R_n^{(1,1)}\), expanded in positive super-Schur functions:
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u).
\]
Here \(q\) records bosonic degree and \(u\) records fermionic degree as a positive Hilbert variable; \(u\) is not a signed supertrace variable. Lentfer’s diagonal supersymmetry theorem is precisely the universality statement that these super-Schur coefficients are independent of the numbers of bosonic and fermionic variable sets. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.14885))

In one even variable \(q\) and one odd Hilbert variable \(u\), the positive super-Schur functions satisfy
\[
s_{\varnothing}(q/u)=1,
\]
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\qquad(a\ge1,\ b\ge0),
\]
and \(s_\lambda(q/u)=0\) if \(\lambda\) is not a hook. Hence
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
c_{\varnothing}(n)+
\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\bigl(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\bigr).
\]
The constant term of the Hilbert series is \(1\), so
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1.
\]

Define
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\,{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]
Comparing coefficients of \(u^b\) gives the triangular relation
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q). \tag{1}
\]

Second, we use the Fields/Rhoades-Wilson superspace Frobenius formula in tableau form, as proved via the Fields Conjectures. Murai, Rhoades, and Wilson prove the bigraded \(S_n\)-module structure of the superspace coinvariant ring \(SR_n=R_n^{(1,1)}\), and Theorem 5.2 gives the required Frobenius characteristic formula. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.24027)) In the form needed here, for \(b\ge0\),
\[
[u^b]\,{\rm grFrob}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n}
\left(
\sum_{T\in{\rm SYT}(\mu)}
q^{{\rm maj}(T)-b\,{\rm des}(T)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(T)\brack b}_q
\right)s_\mu.
\]
Here \(T\) is a standard Young tableau, with descent set defined so that \(i\) is a descent of \(T\) when \(i+1\) lies in a lower row than \(i\).

To forget the \(S_n\)-module structure, pair the Frobenius characteristic with \(h_1^n\). Since
\[
h_1^n=\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu s_\mu,
\]
where \(f^\mu=\#\,{\rm SYT}(\mu)\), this sends \(s_\mu\) to \(f^\mu\). Therefore
\[
H_b(q)=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu
\sum_{T\in{\rm SYT}(\mu)}
q^{{\rm maj}(T)-b\,{\rm des}(T)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(T)\brack b}_q.
\]
By row-insertion RSK, permutations \(w\in S_n\) correspond to pairs \((U,T)\) of standard tableaux of the same shape, where the recording tableau \(T=Q(w)\) satisfies
\[
{\rm Des}(T)={\rm Des}(w).
\]
Thus the preceding tableau sum becomes
\[
H_b(q)=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(w)\brack b}_q. \tag{2}
\]

Now define
\[
D_b(q)=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q.
\]
We show that \(C_b(q)=D_b(q)\) for every \(b\ge0\).

For \(b=0\), equation (2) gives
\[
H_0(q)=\sum_{w\in S_n}q^{{\rm maj}(w)}.
\]
The unique descent-free permutation is \(12\cdots n\), with major index \(0\). Hence
\[
D_0(q)=\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ {\rm des}(w)\ge1}}q^{{\rm maj}(w)}
=H_0(q)-1.
\]
This matches (1), so \(C_0(q)=D_0(q)\).

For \(b\ge1\), compare \(D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)\) with \(H_b(q)\). Fix \(w\in S_n\), and write \(d={\rm des}(w)\), \(M={\rm maj}(w)\). The contribution of this \(w\) to \(D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)\), using the convention that invalid Gaussian binomials vanish, is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d-1\brack b}_q+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right).
\]
By the Gaussian Pascal identity
\[
{d\brack b}_q
=
{d-1\brack b}_q+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q,
\]
this equals
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q,
\]
which is exactly the \(w\)-summand in (2). Therefore
\[
D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)=H_b(q)\qquad(b\ge1).
\]
Together with \(D_0(q)=H_0(q)-1\), the polynomials \(D_b(q)\) satisfy the same triangular recurrence (1) as the \(C_b(q)\). Hence, by induction on \(b\),
\[
C_b(q)=D_b(q)
\]
for all \(b\ge0\). That is,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q. \tag{3}
\]

Finally,
\[
{d-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Substituting this into (3) gives
\[
C_b(q)=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Extracting the coefficient of \(q^a\) gives exactly the claimed count of pairs \((w,\rho)\).

The edge cases follow immediately. For \(b=0\), the rectangle has height \(0\), so only \(\rho=\varnothing\) occurs; hence \(c_{(a)}(n)\) counts non-identity permutations with \({\rm maj}(w)=a\). If \(b\ge n-1\), then the condition \({\rm des}(w)\ge b+1\) is impossible because \({\rm des}(w)\le n-1\), so all such hook coefficients vanish. In particular, when \(n=1\), all nonempty hook coefficients are \(0\), while \(c_{\varnothing}(1)=1\). ∎

Lentfer’s diagonal supersymmetry theorem is precisely the universality statement that these super-Schur coefficients are independent of the numbers of bosonic and fermionic variable sets. In one even variable \(q\) and one odd Hilbert variable \(u\), the positive super-Schur functions satisfy
\[
s_{\varnothing}(q/u)=1,
\]
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\qquad(a\ge1,\ b\ge0),
\]
and \(s_\lambda(q/u)=0\) if \(\lambda\) is not a hook. Hence
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
c_{\varnothing}(n)+
\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\bigl(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\bigr).
\]
The constant term of the Hilbert series is \(1\), so
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1.
\]

Define
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\,{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]
Comparing coefficients of \(u^b\) gives the triangular relation
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q). \tag{1}
\]

Second, we use the Fields/Rhoades-Wilson superspace Frobenius formula in tableau form, as proved via the Fields Conjectures. Murai, Rhoades, and Wilson prove the bigraded \(S_n\)-module structure of the superspace coinvariant ring \(SR_n=R_n^{(1,1)}\), and Theorem 5.2 gives the required Frobenius characteristic formula. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.24027)) In the form needed here, for \(b\ge0\),
\[
[u^b]\,{\rm grFrob}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n}
\left(
\sum_{T\in{\rm SYT}(\mu)}
q^{{\rm maj}(T)-b\,{\rm des}(T)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(T)\brack b}_q
\right)s_\mu.
\]
Here \(T\) is a standard Young tableau, with descent set defined so that \(i\) is a descent of \(T\) when \(i+1\) lies in a lower row than \(i\).

To forget the \(S_n\)-module structure, pair the Frobenius characteristic with \(h_1^n\). Since
\[
h_1^n=\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu s_\mu,
\]
where \(f^\mu=\#\,{\rm SYT}(\mu)\), this sends \(s_\mu\) to \(f^\mu\). Therefore
\[
H_b(q)=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu
\sum_{T\in{\rm SYT}(\mu)}
q^{{\rm maj}(T)-b\,{\rm des}(T)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(T)\brack b}_q.
\]
By row-insertion RSK, permutations \(w\in S_n\) correspond to pairs \((U,T)\) of standard tableaux of the same shape, where the recording tableau \(T=Q(w)\) satisfies
\[
{\rm Des}(T)={\rm Des}(w).
\]
Thus the preceding tableau sum becomes
\[
H_b(q)=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(w)\brack b}_q. \tag{2}
\]

Now define
\[
D_b(q)=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q.
\]
We show that \(C_b(q)=D_b(q)\) for every \(b\ge0\).

For \(b=0\), equation (2) gives
\[
H_0(q)=\sum_{w\in S_n}q^{{\rm maj}(w)}.
\]
The unique descent-free permutation is \(12\cdots n\), with major index \(0\). Hence
\[
D_0(q)=\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ {\rm des}(w)\ge1}}q^{{\rm maj}(w)}
=H_0(q)-1.
\]
This matches (1), so \(C_0(q)=D_0(q)\).

For \(b\ge1\), compare \(D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)\) with \(H_b(q)\). Fix \(w\in S_n\), and write \(d={\rm des}(w)\), \(M={\rm maj}(w)\). The contribution of this \(w\) to \(D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)\), using the convention that invalid Gaussian binomials vanish, is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d-1\brack b}_q+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right).
\]
By the Gaussian Pascal identity
\[
{d\brack b}_q
=
{d-1\brack b}_q+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q,
\]
this equals
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q,
\]
which is exactly the \(w\)-summand in (2). Therefore
\[
D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)=H_b(q)\qquad(b\ge1).
\]
Together with \(D_0(q)=H_0(q)-1\), the polynomials \(D_b(q)\) satisfy the same triangular recurrence (1) as the \(C_b(q)\). Hence, by induction on \(b\),
\[
C_b(q)=D_b(q)
\]
for all \(b\ge0\). That is,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q. \tag{3}
\]

Finally,
\[
{d-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Substituting this into (3) gives
\[
C_b(q)=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Extracting the coefficient of \(q^a\) gives exactly the claimed count of pairs \((w,\rho)\).

The edge cases follow immediately. For \(b=0\), the rectangle has height \(0\), so only \(\rho=\varnothing\) occurs; hence \(c_{(a)}(n)\) counts non-identity permutations with \({\rm maj}(w)=a\). If \(b\ge n-1\), then the condition \({\rm des}(w)\ge b+1\) is impossible because \({\rm des}(w)\le n-1\), so all such hook coefficients vanish. In particular, when \(n=1\), all nonempty hook coefficients are \(0\), while \(c_{\varnothing}(1)=1\). ∎

## Original Problem

Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

## Problem Solved

For every integer \(n\ge1\), let \(c_\lambda(n)\) be the universal Schur-Hilbert coefficients defined by
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\dots,q_m)=\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\dots,q_m)
\]
for the diagonal coinvariant algebra \(R_n^{(m)}\) with \(m\) sets of \(n\) commuting variables. For \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\in S_n\), define
\[
{\rm Des}(w)=\{i:1\le i<n,\ w_i>w_{i+1}\},\quad
{\rm des}(w)=|{\rm Des}(w)|,\quad
{\rm maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in{\rm Des}(w)}i.
\]
For a partition \(\rho\), write \(|\rho|\) for the sum of its parts, and say that \(\rho\) is contained in a \(b\) by \(r\) rectangle if \(\rho\) has at most \(b\) parts and largest part at most \(r\).

Then
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1,
\]
and for every nonempty hook partition \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), with \(a\ge1\) and \(b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\Bigl\{(w,\rho):\, w\in S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\Bigr\}.
\]
Equivalently, if
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
then
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q,
\]
where \({r\brack s}_q\) is the Gaussian binomial coefficient, with invalid values taken to be \(0\). In particular, \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\) for all \(a\ge1\) whenever \(b\ge n-1\). This is exactly the requested hook-shape interpretation, not a relaxation.

\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u).
\]

Lentfer’s diagonal supersymmetry theorem is precisely the universality statement that these super-Schur coefficients are independent of the numbers of bosonic and fermionic variable sets. In one even variable \(q\) and one odd Hilbert variable \(u\), the positive super-Schur functions satisfy
\[
s_{\varnothing}(q/u)=1,
\]
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\qquad(a\ge1,\ b\ge0),
\]

\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
c_{\varnothing}(n)+
\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\bigl(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\bigr).

The constant term of the Hilbert series is \(1\), so
\[

\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0,

\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\,{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]

\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q). \tag{1}
\]

([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.24027)) In the form needed here, for \(b\ge0\),
\[
[u^b]\,{\rm grFrob}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n}
\left(
\sum_{T\in{\rm SYT}(\mu)}
q^{{\rm maj}(T)-b\,{\rm des}(T)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(T)\brack b}_q
\right)s_\mu.

To forget the \(S_n\)-module structure, pair the Frobenius characteristic with \(h_1^n\). Since
\[
h_1^n=\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu s_\mu,
\]

\[
H_b(q)=

q^{{\rm maj}(T)-b\,{\rm des}(T)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(T)\brack b}_q.

By row-insertion RSK, permutations \(w\in S_n\) correspond to pairs \((U,T)\) of standard tableaux of the same shape, where the recording tableau \(T=Q(w)\) satisfies
\[
{\rm Des}(T)={\rm Des}(w).

Thus the preceding tableau sum becomes
\[
H_b(q)=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}

Now define
\[
D_b(q)=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}

\[

\[

\[
\operatorname{Des}(w)=\{i:1\le i<n,\ w_i>w_{i+1}\},\qquad
\operatorname{des}(w)=|\operatorname{Des}(w)|,\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in\operatorname{Des}(w)} i .
\]

\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1,
\]

\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\Bigl\{(w,\rho):\, w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\Bigr\}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}

\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u),
\]
where \(q\) records bosonic degree, \(u\) records fermionic degree, and \(s_\lambda(q/u)\) is the positive super-Schur function. This is the Hilbert-series specialization of the universal super-Schur character expansion. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.14885))

Second, the superspace Frobenius formula gives, for \(b\ge0\),
\[
[u^b]\operatorname{grFrob}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n}
\left(
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\,\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
{\operatorname{des}(T)\brack b}_q
\right)s_\mu .
\]

We first record the needed one-variable super-Schur specialization.

### Lemma

In one even Hilbert variable \(q\) and one odd Hilbert variable \(u\),
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
\]
and, for \(a\ge1,\ b\ge0\),
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]

\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=0.
\]

### Proof of the lemma

\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\mu\subseteq\lambda}s_\mu(q)\,s_{\lambda'/\mu'}(u).
\]
In one variable, \(s_\mu(q)\neq0\) only when \(\mu=(r)\), in which case \(s_\mu(q)=q^r\). Also, \(s_{\alpha/\beta}(u)\neq0\) only when \(\alpha/\beta\) is a horizontal strip, i.e. has at most one box in each column; in that case it equals \(u^{|\alpha/\beta|}\).

If \(\lambda\) is not a hook, then \(\lambda_2\ge2\), so \(\lambda'\) has at least two boxes in its second column. Removing \(\mu'=(1^r)\) removes boxes only from the first column, so \(\lambda'/\mu'\) still has two boxes in the second column. Thus no term contributes.

If \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), then \(\lambda'=(b+1,1^{a-1})\). The only choices of \(r\) for which \(\lambda'/(1^r)\) is a horizontal strip are \(r=a\) and \(r=a-1\). These give respectively
\[
q^a u^b,\qquad q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]

\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
c_\varnothing(n)
+
\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\bigl(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\bigr).

The \((0,0)\)-bidegree of \(R_n^{(1,1)}\) is one-dimensional, so
\[

\]

\[

\[

\[

You previously submitted a proof which a careful referee has ACCEPTED as essentially correct. The referee did note a few minor *expository* issues — things like notation, organization, missing citations for textbook facts — but emphasized these do NOT affect the rigor of the proof. Your task is a light polish to address those expository issues only.

# Original Problem
Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

# Claim Established (do NOT change this)
For every \(n\ge 1\), the proof establishes the hook-case combinatorial interpretation of the universal Hilbert-series coefficients:
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1,
\]
and for every nonempty hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=
\#\{(w,\rho): w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|\}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q .
\]

# Current Accepted Proof
## Claim

Fix \(n\ge 1\). For \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\in S_n\), let
\[
\operatorname{Des}(w)=\{i:1\le i<n,\ w_i>w_{i+1}\},\qquad
\operatorname{des}(w)=|\operatorname{Des}(w)|,\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in\operatorname{Des}(w)} i .
\]
Let \({r\brack s}_q\) denote the Gaussian binomial coefficient, with value \(0\) if \(s<0\), \(r<0\), or \(s>r\). Then the universal Schur-Hilbert coefficients for \(R_n^{(m)}\) satisfy
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1,
\]
and, for every nonempty hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), \(a\ge1,\ b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\Bigl\{(w,\rho):\, w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\Bigr\}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q .
\]

## Proof

We use two standard inputs. First, diagonal supersymmetry says that the same universal coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) occur in the one-boson/one-fermion coinvariant algebra \(R_n^{(1,1)}\):
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u),
\]
where \(q\) records bosonic degree, \(u\) records fermionic degree, and \(s_\lambda(q/u)\) is the positive super-Schur function. This is the Hilbert-series specialization of the universal super-Schur character expansion. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.14885))

Second, the superspace Frobenius formula gives, for \(b\ge0\),
\[
[u^b]\operatorname{grFrob}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n}
\left(
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\,\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
{\operatorname{des}(T)\brack b}_q
\right)s_\mu .
\]
This is Theorem 5.2 together with the Schur expansion in equation (2.5) of the Fields-conjecture formula. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.24027))

We first record the needed one-variable super-Schur specialization.

### Lemma

In one even Hilbert variable \(q\) and one odd Hilbert variable \(u\),
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
\]
and, for \(a\ge1,\ b\ge0\),
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
If \(\lambda\) is not a hook, then
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=0.
\]

### Proof of the lemma

Use the positive hook-Schur formula
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\mu\subseteq\lambda}s_\mu(q)\,s_{\lambda'/\mu'}(u).
\]
In one variable, \(s_\mu(q)\neq0\) only when \(\mu=(r)\), in which case \(s_\mu(q)=q^r\). Also, \(s_{\alpha/\beta}(u)\neq0\) only when \(\alpha/\beta\) is a horizontal strip, i.e. has at most one box in each column; in that case it equals \(u^{|\alpha/\beta|}\).

If \(\lambda\) is not a hook, then \(\lambda_2\ge2\), so \(\lambda'\) has at least two boxes in its second column. Removing \(\mu'=(1^r)\) removes boxes only from the first column, so \(\lambda'/\mu'\) still has two boxes in the second column. Thus no term contributes.

If \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), then \(\lambda'=(b+1,1^{a-1})\). The only choices of \(r\) for which \(\lambda'/(1^r)\) is a horizontal strip are \(r=a\) and \(r=a-1\). These give respectively
\[
q^a u^b,\qquad q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
This proves the lemma. \(\square\)

Now expand the superspace Hilbert series using the lemma:
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
c_\varnothing(n)
+
\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\bigl(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\bigr).
\]
The \((0,0)\)-bidegree of \(R_n^{(1,1)}\) is one-dimensional, so
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]

For \(b\ge0\), define
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\qquad
C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]
Comparing coefficients of \(u^b\) gives the triangular relation
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q). \tag{1}
\]

Next compute \(H_b(q)\). Pairing the Frobenius characteristic with \(h_1^n\) forgets the \(S_n\)-module structure, since
\[
h_1^n=\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu s_\mu,
\]
where \(f^\mu=\#\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\). Hence
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\,\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
{\operatorname{des}(T)\brack b}_q .
\]
By RSK, permutations \(w\in S_n\) correspond to pairs \((P,Q)\) of standard tableaux of the same shape, and \(\operatorname{Des}(Q)=\operatorname{Des}(w)\). Therefore
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
{\operatorname{des}(w)\brack b}_q . \tag{2}
\]

Now set
\[
D_b(q)=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q .
\]
We show \(C_b(q)=D_b(q)\) for every \(b\ge0\).

For \(b=0\), equation (2) gives
\[
H_0(q)=\sum_{w\in S_n}q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)}.
\]
The unique descent-free permutation is \(12\cdots n\), with major index \(0\). Thus
\[
D_0(q)=H_0(q)-1.
\]
By (1), \(C_0(q)=H_0(q)-1\), so \(C_0(q)=D_0(q)\).

Now let \(b\ge1\). Fix \(w\in S_n\), and write
\[
d=\operatorname{des}(w),\qquad M=\operatorname{maj}(w).
\]
The contribution of \(w\) to \(D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)\) is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right).
\]
By the Gaussian Pascal identity,
\[
{d\brack b}_q
=
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q,
\]
this equals
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q,
\]
which is exactly the contribution of \(w\) to \(H_b(q)\) in (2). Therefore
\[
D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)=H_b(q).
\]
Together with (1) and the already proved equality \(C_0=D_0\), induction on \(b\) gives
\[
C_b(q)=D_b(q)
\]
for all \(b\ge0\). Hence
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q .
\]

Finally, use the standard rectangle interpretation
\[
{d-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Substituting gives
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Extracting the coefficient of \(q^a\) yields exactly
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\Bigl\{(w,\rho):\, w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\Bigr\}.
\]
This proves the stated hook formula.

Now set
\[
D_b(q)=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q .
\]
We show \(C_b(q)=D_b(q)\) for every \(b\ge0\).

For \(b=0\), equation (2) gives
\[
H_0(q)=\sum_{w\in S_n}q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)}.
\]
The unique descent-free permutation is \(12\cdots n\), with major index \(0\). Thus
\[
D_0(q)=H_0(q)-1.
\]
By (1), \(C_0(q)=H_0(q)-1\), so \(C_0(q)=D_0(q)\).

Now let \(b\ge1\). Fix \(w\in S_n\), and write
\[
d=\operatorname{des}(w),\qquad M=\operatorname{maj}(w).
\]
Using the zero convention for Gaussian binomial coefficients, the contribution of \(w\) to \(D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)\) is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right).
\]
By the Gaussian Pascal identity,
\[
{d\brack b}_q
=
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q,
\]
this equals
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q,
\]
which is exactly the contribution of \(w\) to \(H_b(q)\) in (2). Therefore
\[
D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)=H_b(q).
\]
Together with (1) and the already proved equality \(C_0=D_0\), induction on \(b\) gives
\[
C_b(q)=D_b(q)
\]
for all \(b\ge0\). Hence
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q .
\]

Finally, use the standard rectangle interpretation of the Gaussian binomial coefficient,
\[
{d-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Substituting gives
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Extracting the coefficient of \(q^a\) yields
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\Bigl\{(w,\rho):\, w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\Bigr\}.
\]
This proves the stated hook formula.

statement: For every n≥1 and every nonempty hook partition λ=(a,1^b) with a≥1 and b≥0, the universal Hilbert-series coefficient c_λ(n) equals the number of pairs (w,rho) such that w is a permutation in S_n, des(w)≥b+1, rho is a partition contained in a b by des(w)-1-b rectangle, and a=maj(w)-b des(w)+binom(b,2)+|rho|; additionally c_empty(n)=1.

## Theorem-ready Hilbert-level proof

Write

\[
d(w)=\operatorname{des}(w),\qquad M(w)=\operatorname{maj}(w),
\]

and define

\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1} c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0.
\]

Let

\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]

From the assumed \(GL(1|1)\) hook specialization,

\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
1+\sum_{a\ge1,b\ge0} c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
\left(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\right),
\]

so comparing coefficients of \(u^b\) gives the triangular relation

\[
\boxed{
H_b^{(n)}(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b^{(n)}(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
}
\tag{1}
\]

Now use the Fields/Rhoades-Wilson product formula

\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{M(w)-\binom{d(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{d(w)}(q^i+u).
\]

For fixed \(w\), the coefficient of \(u^b\) is

\[
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}}
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq [d]\\ |J|=b}}
q^{\sum_{i\notin J} i}
=
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq [d]\\ |J|=b}}
q^{M-\sum_{j\in J}j}.
\]

Using

\[
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq[d]\\ |J|=b}}q^{-\sum_{j\in J}j}
=
q^{-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix} d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]

we get

\[
\boxed{
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d(w)\ge b}}
q^{M(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix} d(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
}
\tag{2}
\]

Now define

\[
D_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{M(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix} d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

I claim \(C_b^{(n)}(q)=D_b^{(n)}(q)\). Indeed, for fixed \(w\), the contribution of \(D_b+q^{-1}D_{b-1}\) is

\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right),
\]

because

\[
q^{-1}q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}
=
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}q^{d-b}.
\]

By the \(q\)-Pascal identity,

\[
\begin{bmatrix} d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]

this equals the \(w\)-summand in \(H_b\). The only exceptional case is \(b=0,d=0\), i.e. the identity permutation, whose contribution is exactly the \(\delta_{b,0}\) term in (1). Therefore \(D_b\) satisfies the same triangular recurrence as \(C_b\), so

\[
\boxed{
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{M(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix} d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
}
\tag{3}
\]

Since

\[
\begin{bmatrix} d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)} q^{|\rho|},
\]

we obtain the desired coefficient interpretation:

\[
\boxed{
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\quad d(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b),\\
a=M(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
}
\]

Also \(c_{\varnothing}(n)=1\). The exponent is always positive: if \(d\ge b+1\), then \(M\ge 1+\cdots+d\), so

\[
M-bd+\binom b2\ge \binom{d-b+1}{2}\ge 1.
\]

## Check: \(n=3\)

The descent-major data are:

- \(d=0,M=0\): one permutation;
- \(d=1,M=1\): two permutations;
- \(d=1,M=2\): two permutations;
- \(d=2,M=3\): one permutation.

Thus

\[
H_0=1+2q+2q^2+q^3,
\]

\[
H_1=2+3q+q^2,
\]

\[
H_2=1.
\]

The recurrence gives

\[
C_0=H_0-1=2q+2q^2+q^3,
\]

\[
C_1=H_1-q^{-1}C_0=q,
\]

\[
C_2=H_2-q^{-1}C_1=0.
\]

This matches the rectangle formula: for \(b=1\), only \(321\) has \(d\ge2\), and its contribution is

\[
q^{3-1\cdot2}=q.
\]

For \(n=4\), the same formula gives

\[
C_0=3q+5q^2+6q^3+5q^4+3q^5+q^6,
\]

\[
C_1=3q+5q^2+4q^3+q^4,
\]

\[
C_2=q,
\]

again confirming the exponent \(M-bd+\binom b2\) and the off-by-one rectangle \(b\times(d-1-b)\).

## Sufficiency and hidden assumptions

This is a fully Hilbert-level proof: no refined Frobenius coefficients \(c_{\lambda,\mu}\) are needed.

However, it is sufficient for the original hook-coefficient problem only after accepting the hidden universality input that the \(GL(1|1)\) superspace Hilbert expansion uses the same universal coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) as the ordinary multigraded Hilbert expansion. One also needs the positive, unsigned fermionic Hilbert convention, so that hooks specialize as

\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]

With those assumptions, the proof above directly recovers the original hook coefficients.

## Audit verdict

I found **no normalization discrepancy** in the hook-coefficient proof, provided the final writeup explicitly uses the **ordinary unsigned Hilbert-series convention** for fermions and either proves or cites the permutation product form. The only source-accuracy caveat is that the product formula is best presented as a **derived equivalent form** of the Fields/Rhoades–Wilson/Murai–Rhoades–Wilson formulas, not as a formula visibly stated in exactly that form.

---

## Theorem-ready external inputs

### 1. Universal super-Schur Hilbert expansion

Use Lentfer’s bosonic–fermionic coinvariant ring
\[
R_{n}^{(k,j)}
=
\mathbb C[x^{(1)},\dots,x^{(k)},\theta^{(1)},\dots,\theta^{(j)}]\Big/
\left\langle
\mathbb C[x^{(1)},\dots,x^{(k)},\theta^{(1)},\dots,\theta^{(j)}]^{\mathfrak S_n}_{+}
\right\rangle,
\]
where the \(x\)’s commute, the \(\theta\)’s anticommute, and \(\mathfrak S_n\) acts diagonally by permuting indices. Lentfer denotes the symmetric-group case by \(R_n^{(k,j)}\), so the ordinary \(m\)-set diagonal coinvariant algebra is \(R_n^{(m,0)}\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

The exact universality input is:

\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\lambda\in P(k,j,n)}
c_\lambda(n)\,s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u),
\qquad
P(k,j,n)=\{\lambda:\ell(\lambda)\le n,\ \lambda_{k+1}\le j\},
\]
with **nonnegative integers \(c_\lambda(n)\) independent of \((k,j)\)**. For \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), so these are exactly the ordinary Schur-Hilbert coefficients in the user’s \(m\)-boson expansion. For \(k=j=1\), the same \(c_\lambda(n)\) occur in the one-boson/one-fermion Hilbert series. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

**Caveat.** In the \((1,1)\) specialization, only \(\lambda_2\le1\) can contribute, i.e. \(\lambda=\varnothing\) or a hook \((a,1^b)\). Thus this specialization is sufficient for hook coefficients but does not determine nonhook coefficients.

---

### 2. Super-Schur normalization and signs

Use Lentfer’s positive super-Schur convention:
\[
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}
s_\nu(\mathbf q)\,s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(\mathbf u).
\]
This is also written plethystically as \(s_\lambda[\mathbf q-\epsilon\mathbf u]\), and **it is not the signed supertrace convention**. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

For one even variable \(q\) and one odd Hilbert variable \(u\),
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
\]
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\qquad(a\ge1,\ b\ge0),
\]
and
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=0\quad\text{if }\lambda\text{ is not contained in a }(1,1)\text{-hook}.
\]
This is exactly Lentfer’s Lemma 4.4. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

So, with
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u),
\]
the required triangular relation is
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
\]

**Sign caveat.** If someone uses a signed supertrace variable, then \(u\) is replaced by \(-u\), and the hook specialization would acquire alternating signs. That convention would break the proof. For example, at \(n=2\), the unsigned Hilbert series is \(1+q+u\), whereas a signed product would give \(1+q-u\).

---

### 3. Matching \(R_n^{(1,1)}\) with the superspace coinvariant ring \(SR_n\)

Murai–Rhoades–Wilson define
\[
\Omega_n=\mathbb F[x_1,\dots,x_n]\otimes \wedge\{\theta_1,\dots,\theta_n\},
\]
with diagonal action
\[
w\cdot x_i=x_{w(i)},\qquad w\cdot\theta_i=\theta_{w(i)}.
\]
They define
\[
SI_n=\big((\Omega_n)^{\mathfrak S_n}_{+}\big)
\]
and
\[
SR_n=\Omega_n/SI_n.
\]
They also record Solomon’s generating-set normalization
\[
SI_n=(e_1,\dots,e_n,de_1,\dots,de_n).
\]
Thus \(SR_n\) is exactly Lentfer’s \(R_n^{(1,1)}\), after identifying \(x_i=x_i^{(1)}\) and \(\theta_i=\theta_i^{(1)}\). Their variable \(z\) for fermionic degree should be renamed \(u\) in the hook proof. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

---

### 4. Superspace Hilbert/product formula

A source-clean input is Murai–Rhoades–Wilson’s Fields Frobenius formula:
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,z)
=
\sum_{k=1}^n C_{n,k}(\mathbf x;q)\,z^{n-k},
\]
where \(q\) tracks bosonic degree and \(z\) tracks fermionic degree. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

Their tableau expansion gives
\[
C_{n,k}(\mathbf x;q)
=
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(n)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)+\binom{n-k}{2}-(n-k)\operatorname{des}(T)}
\begin{bmatrix}
\operatorname{des}(T)\\ n-k
\end{bmatrix}_q
s_{\lambda(T)}(\mathbf x).
\]
Setting \(b=n-k\), pairing the Frobenius characteristic with \(h_{(1^n)}=h_1^n\) to forget the \(\mathfrak S_n\)-structure, and applying ordinary row-insertion RSK with \(\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w)\), gives
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}
\operatorname{des}(w)\\ b
\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
The Hilbert-series-from-Frobenius pairing convention is also explicitly stated by Lentfer. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

Equivalently,
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\]
Indeed, the coefficient of \(u^b\) in the \(w\)-summand is
\[
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{d+1}{2}}
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq[d]\\ |J|=b}}
q^{\sum_{i\notin J}i}
=
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
where \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\).

**Source caveat.** The sources state the \(q\)-Stirling Hilbert formula and the Frobenius formula; the above permutation product form should be presented as a short derivation, not as a verbatim quoted theorem unless a writeup independently cites a source where it is printed in that form. Rhoades–Wilson proved the Hilbert series, while Murai–Rhoades–Wilson later proved the full Fields Frobenius structure. ([cambridge.org](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/forum-of-mathematics-pi/article/hilbert-series-of-the-superspace-coinvariant-ring/D7F1C91BE28068FC384AFE85B6AED8B8))

---

## Final normalization checklist

- Use \(u\) as an **ordinary positive fermionic Hilbert variable**, not a supertrace variable.
- Identify \(R_n^{(1,1)}=SR_n\).
- Rename Murai–Rhoades–Wilson’s fermionic variable \(z\) to \(u\).
- Use \([r]_q=1+q+\cdots+q^{r-1}\) and the standard Gaussian binomial.
- In RSK, the statistic-bearing tableau is the **recording tableau** \(Q(w)\); with row insertion, \(\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w)\). If a different RSK convention is used, the inverse permutation may appear.

Under these normalizations, the hook formula in t5/t6 is source-consistent and has no smallest counterexample.

## Audit verdict

I find **no broken step and no counterexample**. The proposed hook formula is correct, provided the final write-up states the external inputs and conventions precisely.

### Key checks

1. **Universality really gives the original bosonic \(c_\lambda(n)\).**  
   Yes. Lentfer’s diagonal supersymmetry theorem gives universal Frobenius coefficients \(c_{\lambda\mu}\), and his Hilbert-series proposition defines
   \[
   c_\lambda=\sum_{\mu\vdash n}c_{\lambda\mu}\langle s_\mu,h_{(1^n)}\rangle
   \]
   independent of \((k,j)\). For \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), so these are exactly the original bosonic Schur-Hilbert coefficients. For \((k,j)=(1,1)\), the same \(c_\lambda\) occur. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

2. **Triangular relation.**  
   Correct with the **ordinary unsigned** Hilbert variable \(u\). Lentfer’s one-even/one-odd specialization is
   \[
   s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1},\qquad s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
   \]
   with no alternating sign. Hence
   \[
   H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
   \]
   The \(\delta_{b,0}\) is exactly the empty partition contribution. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

3. **\(q\)-Pascal step.**  
   Correct. If \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\), then the subtraction uses
   \[
   q^{-1}q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}
   =
   q^{M-bd+\binom b2}q^{d-b},
   \]
   and
   \[
   \begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
   =
   \begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
   +q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q.
   \]
   This gives exactly \(\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q\), with no shift error.

4. **Fields + RSK.**  
   Correct, but the write-up should explicitly say **row-insertion RSK and recording tableau \(Q(w)\)**. Murai–Rhoades–Wilson’s \(C_{n,k}\) tableau formula has exponent
   \[
   \operatorname{maj}(T)+\binom{n-k}{2}-(n-k)\operatorname{des}(T)
   \]
   and Gaussian factor \(\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)\\ n-k\end{bmatrix}_q\); their descent convention is \(i\) appears in a higher row than \(i+1\). Pairing with \(h_{(1^n)}\) and using RSK with \(T=Q(w)\) gives \(\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w)\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

5. **Edge cases.**  
   All check out:
   - \(n=1\): only the identity has \(d=0\), so all nonempty hook coefficients vanish and \(c_\varnothing(1)=1\).
   - \(b=0\): rectangle height \(0\), so only \(\rho=\varnothing\); \(c_{(a)}(n)\) counts permutations with at least one descent and \(\operatorname{maj}(w)=a\).
   - \(b\ge n-1\): condition \(\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1\) is impossible, so coefficient is \(0\).
   - Vertical hooks \((1,1^b)\) are included; for \(b=n-2\) only the reverse permutation contributes, giving \(c_{(1,1^{n-2})}(n)=1\), while \(b=n-1\) gives \(0\).

---

## Theorem-ready proof skeleton

Let
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]

By the \(GL(1|1)\) specialization,
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
1+\sum_{a\ge1,b\ge0}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
\left(q^au^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\right),
\]
so
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
\]

From Fields’ Frobenius formula, pairing with \(h_{(1^n)}\), and row-insertion RSK using \(Q(w)\),
\[
H_b(q)=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

Now set
\[
D_b(q)=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
For each fixed \(w\), \(D_b+q^{-1}D_{b-1}\) contributes
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right)
=
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
The only missing case is \(b=0,d=0\), exactly supplied by \(\delta_{b,0}\). Thus \(D_b=C_b\).

Finally,
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|},
\]
so
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Also \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\) from the degree-zero component.

response:
## Original Problem

Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

## Problem Solved

Fix an integer \(n\ge 1\). Let \(R_n^{(m)}\) be the diagonal coinvariant algebra with \(m\) sets of \(n\) commuting variables, and let \(c_\lambda(n)\) be the universal Schur-Hilbert coefficients defined by
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots,q_m)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots,q_m),
\]
independent of \(m\).

For a permutation \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\in S_n\), define
\[
{\rm Des}(w)=\{i\in\{1,\ldots,n-1\}:w_i>w_{i+1}\},
\]
\[
{\rm des}(w)=|{\rm Des}(w)|,\qquad
{\rm maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in{\rm Des}(w)}i.
\]
A hook partition is written
\[
\lambda=(a,1^b)=(a,\underbrace{1,\ldots,1}_{b\text{ times}})
\]
with \(a\ge 1\) and \(b\ge 0\). The empty partition is denoted \(\varnothing\).

For integers \(r,s\), use the Gaussian binomial convention
\[
\begin{bmatrix}r\\s\end{bmatrix}_q=0
\quad\text{unless }0\le s\le r,
\]
and, for \(0\le s\le r\),
\[
\begin{bmatrix}r\\s\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\prod_{i=1}^{s}\frac{1-q^{r-s+i}}{1-q^i}
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq s\times(r-s)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Here \(\rho\subseteq s\times t\) means that \(\rho=(\rho_1,\ldots,\rho_s)\) is a partition with
\[
t\ge \rho_1\ge\cdots\ge \rho_s\ge 0,
\]
and \(|\rho|=\rho_1+\cdots+\rho_s\). For \(s=0\), there is one empty partition of size \(0\).

The result proved is:

\[
\boxed{c_\varnothing(n)=1.}
\]

For every hook \((a,1^b)\) with \(a\ge 1\) and \(b\ge 0\),
\[
\boxed{
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\\[2mm]
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\\[2mm]
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
}
\]
This is exactly the requested hook-coefficient interpretation; the statement also records the degree-zero coefficient \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\).

## Proof

We use three standard Hilbert-series inputs.

1. **Super-Schur universality.** The same Hilbert coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) occur in the one-bosonic, one-fermionic superspace specialization:
   \[
   {\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
   =
   \sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u),
   \]
   where \(q\) records the ordinary bosonic degree and \(u\) is an ordinary unsigned fermionic Hilbert variable.

2. **One-even, one-odd super-Schur specialization.** In one even variable \(q\) and one odd Hilbert variable \(u\),
   \[
   s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
   \]
   and for \(a\ge 1,\ b\ge 0\),
   \[
   s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)
   =
   q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
   \]
   All nonempty non-hook shapes vanish in this specialization.

3. **Superspace Hilbert formula.** The Fields/Rhoades-Wilson superspace Hilbert formula, equivalently after RSK, gives
   \[
   {\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
   =
   \sum_{w\in S_n}
   q^{{\rm maj}(w)-\binom{{\rm des}(w)+1}{2}}
   \prod_{i=1}^{{\rm des}(w)}(q^i+u).
   \]

First, the degree-zero component of the coinvariant algebra is one-dimensional, and only \(s_\varnothing\) contributes to total degree zero. Hence
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]

Now define, for \(b\ge 0\),
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
and set \(C_{-1}(q)=0\). Also define
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\,{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]

By the super-Schur specialization,
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
1+\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
\left(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\right).
\]
Taking the coefficient of \(u^b\) gives the triangular relation
\[
\boxed{
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
}
\tag{1}
\]

Next compute \(H_b(q)\) from the product formula. Fix \(w\in S_n\), and write
\[
d={\rm des}(w),\qquad M={\rm maj}(w).
\]
The coefficient of \(u^b\) in the \(w\)-summand is
\[
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}}
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq\{1,\ldots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}
q^{\sum_{i\notin J}i}.
\]
Since
\[
\sum_{i=1}^d i=\binom{d+1}{2},
\]
this equals
\[
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq\{1,\ldots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}
q^{M-\sum_{j\in J}j}.
\]
Using the standard subset form of the Gaussian binomial,
\[
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq\{1,\ldots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}
q^{-\sum_{j\in J}j}
=
q^{-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
we obtain
\[
\boxed{
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)\\b\end{bmatrix}_q.
}
\tag{2}
\]

Now set
\[
D_b(q)=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
and set \(D_{-1}(q)=0\). We show that \(D_b(q)\) satisfies the same triangular recurrence as \(C_b(q)\).

Fix \(w\in S_n\), and again write \(d={\rm des}(w)\), \(M={\rm maj}(w)\). If \(d\ge 1\), then the contribution of \(w\) to
\[
D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)
\]
is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
this is exactly
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
the \(w\)-summand in formula \((2)\).

The only missing case is \(b=0,d=0\). There is exactly one permutation with \(d=0\), namely the identity permutation, and its contribution to \(H_0(q)\) is \(1\). This is exactly supplied by the term \(\delta_{b,0}\). Hence
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q).
\tag{3}
\]
Comparing \((1)\) and \((3)\), and using \(C_{-1}=D_{-1}=0\), gives by induction on \(b\) that
\[
C_b(q)=D_b(q)
\]
for all \(b\ge 0\).

Therefore
\[
C_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Using
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|},
\]
we get
\[
C_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Since
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
the coefficient of \(q^a\) is precisely the number of pairs \((w,\rho)\) satisfying
\[
w\in S_n,\qquad d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,
\]
\[
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),
\]
and
\[
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]
This proves the claimed combinatorial interpretation.

Finally, the edge cases agree with the formula. The empty coefficient is \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\). When \(b=0\), the rectangle has height \(0\), so \(\rho=\varnothing\), and \(c_{(a)}(n)\) counts permutations with at least one descent and \({\rm maj}(w)=a\). If \(b\ge n-1\), then \({\rm des}(w)\le n-1\), so the condition \({\rm des}(w)\ge b+1\) is impossible and the coefficient vanishes. For \(n=1\), the only permutation has no descents, so all nonempty hook coefficients vanish and only \(c_\varnothing(1)=1\) remains.

# Claim Being Verified
Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

# Solution
## Original Problem

Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

## Problem Solved

Fix an integer \(n\ge 1\). Let \(R_n^{(m)}\) be the diagonal coinvariant algebra with \(m\) sets of \(n\) commuting variables, and let \(c_\lambda(n)\) be the universal Schur-Hilbert coefficients defined by
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots,q_m)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots,q_m),
\]
independent of \(m\).

For a permutation \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\in S_n\), define
\[
{\rm Des}(w)=\{i\in\{1,\ldots,n-1\}:w_i>w_{i+1}\},
\]
\[
{\rm des}(w)=|{\rm Des}(w)|,\qquad
{\rm maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in{\rm Des}(w)}i.
\]
A hook partition is written
\[
\lambda=(a,1^b)=(a,\underbrace{1,\ldots,1}_{b\text{ times}})
\]
with \(a\ge 1\) and \(b\ge 0\). The empty partition is denoted \(\varnothing\).

For integers \(r,s\), use the Gaussian binomial convention
\[
\begin{bmatrix}r\\s\end{bmatrix}_q=0
\quad\text{unless }0\le s\le r,
\]
and, for \(0\le s\le r\),
\[
\begin{bmatrix}r\\s\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\prod_{i=1}^{s}\frac{1-q^{r-s+i}}{1-q^i}
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq s\times(r-s)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Here \(\rho\subseteq s\times t\) means that \(\rho=(\rho_1,\ldots,\rho_s)\) is a partition with
\[
t\ge \rho_1\ge\cdots\ge \rho_s\ge 0,
\]
and \(|\rho|=\rho_1+\cdots+\rho_s\). For \(s=0\), there is one empty partition of size \(0\).

The result proved is:

\[
\boxed{c_\varnothing(n)=1.}
\]

For every hook \((a,1^b)\) with \(a\ge 1\) and \(b\ge 0\),
\[
\boxed{
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\\[2mm]
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\\[2mm]
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
}
\]
This is exactly the requested hook-coefficient interpretation; the statement also records the degree-zero coefficient \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\).

## Proof

We use three standard Hilbert-series inputs.

1. **Super-Schur universality.** The same Hilbert coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) occur in the one-bosonic, one-fermionic superspace specialization:
   \[
   {\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
   =
   \sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u),
   \]
   where \(q\) records the ordinary bosonic degree and \(u\) is an ordinary unsigned fermionic Hilbert variable.

2. **One-even, one-odd super-Schur specialization.** In one even variable \(q\) and one odd Hilbert variable \(u\),
   \[
   s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
   \]
   and for \(a\ge 1,\ b\ge 0\),
   \[
   s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)
   =
   q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
   \]
   All nonempty non-hook shapes vanish in this specialization.

3. **Superspace Hilbert formula.** The Fields/Rhoades-Wilson superspace Hilbert formula, equivalently after RSK, gives
   \[
   {\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
   =
   \sum_{w\in S_n}
   q^{{\rm maj}(w)-\binom{{\rm des}(w)+1}{2}}
   \prod_{i=1}^{{\rm des}(w)}(q^i+u).
   \]

First, the degree-zero component of the coinvariant algebra is one-dimensional, and only \(s_\varnothing\) contributes to total degree zero. Hence
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]

Now define, for \(b\ge 0\),
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
and set \(C_{-1}(q)=0\). Also define
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\,{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]

By the super-Schur specialization,
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
1+\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
\left(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\right).
\]
Taking the coefficient of \(u^b\) gives the triangular relation
\[
\boxed{
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
}
\tag{1}
\]

Next compute \(H_b(q)\) from the product formula. Fix \(w\in S_n\), and write
\[
d={\rm des}(w),\qquad M={\rm maj}(w).
\]
The coefficient of \(u^b\) in the \(w\)-summand is
\[
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}}
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq\{1,\ldots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}
q^{\sum_{i\notin J}i}.
\]
Since
\[
\sum_{i=1}^d i=\binom{d+1}{2},
\]
this equals
\[
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq\{1,\ldots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}
q^{M-\sum_{j\in J}j}.
\]
Using the standard subset form of the Gaussian binomial,
\[
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq\{1,\ldots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}
q^{-\sum_{j\in J}j}
=
q^{-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
we obtain
\[
\boxed{
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)\\b\end{bmatrix}_q.
}
\tag{2}
\]

Now set
\[
D_b(q)=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
and set \(D_{-1}(q)=0\). We show that \(D_b(q)\) satisfies the same triangular recurrence as \(C_b(q)\).

Fix \(w\in S_n\), and again write \(d={\rm des}(w)\), \(M={\rm maj}(w)\). If \(d\ge 1\), then the contribution of \(w\) to
\[
D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)
\]
is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
this is exactly
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
the \(w\)-summand in formula \((2)\).

The only missing case is \(b=0,d=0\). There is exactly one permutation with \(d=0\), namely the identity permutation, and its contribution to \(H_0(q)\) is \(1\). This is exactly supplied by the term \(\delta_{b,0}\). Hence
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q).
\tag{3}
\]
Comparing \((1)\) and \((3)\), and using \(C_{-1}=D_{-1}=0\), gives by induction on \(b\) that
\[
C_b(q)=D_b(q)
\]
for all \(b\ge 0\).

Therefore
\[
C_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Using
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|},
\]
we get
\[
C_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Since
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
the coefficient of \(q^a\) is precisely the number of pairs \((w,\rho)\) satisfying
\[
w\in S_n,\qquad d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,
\]
\[
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),
\]
and
\[
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]
This proves the claimed combinatorial interpretation.

Finally, the edge cases agree with the formula. The empty coefficient is \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\). When \(b=0\), the rectangle has height \(0\), so \(\rho=\varnothing\), and \(c_{(a)}(n)\) counts permutations with at least one descent and \({\rm maj}(w)=a\). If \(b\ge n-1\), then \({\rm des}(w)\le n-1\), so the condition \({\rm des}(w)\ge b+1\) is impossible and the coefficient vanishes. For \(n=1\), the only permutation has no descents, so all nonempty hook coefficients vanish and only \(c_\varnothing(1)=1\) remains.

   \[
   E_{n,d}(q)=\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\{\rm des}(w)=d}}q^{{\rm maj}(w)}.
   \]
   The standard insertion-of-\(n\) argument gives the \(q\)-Eulerian recurrence
   \[
   E_{n,d}=[d+1]_qE_{n-1,d}+q^d[n-d]_qE_{n-1,d-1}.
   \]
   The coefficient of \(u^b\) in the product side is

   A_{n,b}:=\sum_d q^{-bd+\binom b2}\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_qE_{n,d}(q).

   \]

   A_{n,b}=[n-b]_q(A_{n-1,b-1}+A_{n-1,b}).
   \]
   The coefficient
   \[
   G_{n,b}:=[n-b]!_q{\rm Stir}_q(n,n-b)
   \]
   in the Rhoades–Wilson \(q\)-Stirling formula satisfies the same recurrence by the defining recurrence for \({\rm Stir}_q(n,k)\), with the same initial values. Hence \(A_{n,b}=G_{n,b}\) for all \(b\).

3. **Patch for the super-Schur specialization.** Add the derivation:

   \[

   \]

=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
w\in S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\ 
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|

# Prior solution (for your reference only — do not include or quote it in your output)
## Original Problem

Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

## Problem Solved

Fix an integer \(n\ge 1\). Let \(R_n^{(m)}\) be the diagonal coinvariant algebra with \(m\) sets of \(n\) commuting variables, and let \(c_\lambda(n)\) be the universal Schur-Hilbert coefficients defined by
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots,q_m)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots,q_m),
\]
independent of \(m\).

For a permutation \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\in S_n\), define
\[
{\rm Des}(w)=\{i\in\{1,\ldots,n-1\}:w_i>w_{i+1}\},
\]
\[
{\rm des}(w)=|{\rm Des}(w)|,\qquad
{\rm maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in{\rm Des}(w)}i.
\]
A hook partition is written
\[
\lambda=(a,1^b)=(a,\underbrace{1,\ldots,1}_{b\text{ times}})
\]
with \(a\ge 1\) and \(b\ge 0\). The empty partition is denoted \(\varnothing\).

For integers \(r,s\), use the Gaussian binomial convention
\[
\begin{bmatrix}r\\s\end{bmatrix}_q=0
\quad\text{unless }0\le s\le r,
\]
and, for \(0\le s\le r\),
\[
\begin{bmatrix}r\\s\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\prod_{i=1}^{s}\frac{1-q^{r-s+i}}{1-q^i}
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq s\times(r-s)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Here \(\rho\subseteq s\times t\) means that \(\rho=(\rho_1,\ldots,\rho_s)\) is a partition with
\[
t\ge \rho_1\ge\cdots\ge \rho_s\ge 0,
\]
and \(|\rho|=\rho_1+\cdots+\rho_s\). For \(s=0\), there is one empty partition of size \(0\).

The result proved is:

\[
\boxed{c_\varnothing(n)=1.}
\]

For every hook \((a,1^b)\) with \(a\ge 1\) and \(b\ge 0\),
\[
\boxed{
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\\[2mm]
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\\[2mm]
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
}
\]
This is exactly the requested hook-coefficient interpretation; the statement also records the degree-zero coefficient \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\).

## Proof

We use three standard Hilbert-series inputs.

1. **Super-Schur universality.** The same Hilbert coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) occur in the one-bosonic, one-fermionic superspace specialization:
   \[
   {\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
   =
   \sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u),
   \]
   where \(q\) records the ordinary bosonic degree and \(u\) is an ordinary unsigned fermionic Hilbert variable.

2. **One-even, one-odd super-Schur specialization.** In one even variable \(q\) and one odd Hilbert variable \(u\),
   \[
   s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
   \]
   and for \(a\ge 1,\ b\ge 0\),
   \[
   s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)
   =
   q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
   \]
   All nonempty non-hook shapes vanish in this specialization.

3. **Superspace Hilbert formula.** The Fields/Rhoades-Wilson superspace Hilbert formula, equivalently after RSK, gives
   \[
   {\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
   =
   \sum_{w\in S_n}
   q^{{\rm maj}(w)-\binom{{\rm des}(w)+1}{2}}
   \prod_{i=1}^{{\rm des}(w)}(q^i+u).
   \]

First, the degree-zero component of the coinvariant algebra is one-dimensional, and only \(s_\varnothing\) contributes to total degree zero. Hence
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]

Now define, for \(b\ge 0\),
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
and set \(C_{-1}(q)=0\). Also define
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\,{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]

By the super-Schur specialization,
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
1+\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
\left(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\right).
\]
Taking the coefficient of \(u^b\) gives the triangular relation
\[
\boxed{
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
}
\tag{1}
\]

Next compute \(H_b(q)\) from the product formula. Fix \(w\in S_n\), and write
\[
d={\rm des}(w),\qquad M={\rm maj}(w).
\]
The coefficient of \(u^b\) in the \(w\)-summand is
\[
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}}
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq\{1,\ldots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}
q^{\sum_{i\notin J}i}.
\]
Since
\[
\sum_{i=1}^d i=\binom{d+1}{2},
\]
this equals
\[
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq\{1,\ldots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}
q^{M-\sum_{j\in J}j}.
\]
Using the standard subset form of the Gaussian binomial,
\[
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq\{1,\ldots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}
q^{-\sum_{j\in J}j}
=
q^{-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
we obtain
\[
\boxed{
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)\\b\end{bmatrix}_q.
}
\tag{2}
\]

Now set
\[
D_b(q)=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
and set \(D_{-1}(q)=0\). We show that \(D_b(q)\) satisfies the same triangular recurrence as \(C_b(q)\).

Fix \(w\in S_n\), and again write \(d={\rm des}(w)\), \(M={\rm maj}(w)\). If \(d\ge 1\), then the contribution of \(w\) to
\[
D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)
\]
is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
this is exactly
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
the \(w\)-summand in formula \((2)\).

The only missing case is \(b=0,d=0\). There is exactly one permutation with \(d=0\), namely the identity permutation, and its contribution to \(H_0(q)\) is \(1\). This is exactly supplied by the term \(\delta_{b,0}\). Hence
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q).
\tag{3}
\]
Comparing \((1)\) and \((3)\), and using \(C_{-1}=D_{-1}=0\), gives by induction on \(b\) that
\[
C_b(q)=D_b(q)
\]
for all \(b\ge 0\).

Therefore
\[
C_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Using
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|},
\]
we get
\[
C_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Since
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
the coefficient of \(q^a\) is precisely the number of pairs \((w,\rho)\) satisfying
\[
w\in S_n,\qquad d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,
\]
\[
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),
\]
and
\[
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]
This proves the claimed combinatorial interpretation.

Finally, the edge cases agree with the formula. The empty coefficient is \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\). When \(b=0\), the rectangle has height \(0\), so \(\rho=\varnothing\), and \(c_{(a)}(n)\) counts permutations with at least one descent and \({\rm maj}(w)=a\). If \(b\ge n-1\), then \({\rm des}(w)\le n-1\), so the condition \({\rm des}(w)\ge b+1\) is impossible and the coefficient vanishes. For \(n=1\), the only permutation has no descents, so all nonempty hook coefficients vanish and only \(c_\varnothing(1)=1\) remains.
