**Claim.** Fix \(n\ge 1\). Let \(R_n^{(m)}\) be the diagonal coinvariant algebra of \(\mathfrak S_n\) in \(m\) commuting sets of \(n\) variables, and let
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\dots,q_m)=\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\dots,q_m)
\]
be its universal Schur-Hilbert expansion. Then
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]
For every hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), with \(a\ge 1\) and \(b\ge 0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in \mathfrak S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\\[1mm]
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\\[1mm]
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Here \({\rm des}(w)\) is the number of descents of \(w\), \({\rm maj}(w)\) is the sum of its descent positions, and \(\rho\subseteq b\times t\) means a partition with at most \(b\) parts, each at most \(t\).

We use the diagonal supersymmetry theorem for bosonic–fermionic coinvariant rings: for fixed \(n\), the Hilbert series of the \((k,j)\)-bosonic–fermionic diagonal coinvariant ring has an expansion
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q,\mathbf u)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u),
\]
with the same Hilbert-level coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) for all \(k,j\). If the theorem is stated at the \(\mathfrak S_n\)-character level, evaluating the character at the identity gives this Hilbert-series form. Taking \(j=0\) gives the purely bosonic expansion above, and taking \(k=j=1\) gives the superspace specialization used below. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.14885))

Let
\[
SR_n
=
\mathbb C[x_1,\dots,x_n]\otimes \bigwedge(\theta_1,\dots,\theta_n)/SI_n
\]
be the superspace coinvariant ring, where \(SI_n\) is generated by \(\mathfrak S_n\)-invariants with zero constant term. Rhoades–Wilson prove its bigraded Hilbert series in \(q\)-Stirling form; equivalently, by the Carlitz \(q\)-Eulerian/\(q\)-Stirling identity,
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in\mathfrak S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-\binom{{\rm des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{{\rm des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\tag{1}
\]
The quotient definition and the \(q\)-Stirling Hilbert formula are those of Rhoades–Wilson. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2301.09763))

Now compute the one-even, one-odd super-Schur specialization. With the unsigned Hilbert convention for odd variables,
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq \lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u).
\]
Since \(s_\nu(q)\neq 0\) in one variable only when \(\nu\) is a row, and \(s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u)\neq 0\) in one variable only when \(\lambda'/\nu'\) is a horizontal strip, every nonempty non-hook shape vanishes. For a hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), the only surviving rows are \(\nu=(a)\) and \(\nu=(a-1)\), giving
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\tag{2}
\]

The degree-zero component of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is the constants, so \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\). Define
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\,{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u).
\]
Using (2) in the superspace expansion,
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
1+\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
\left(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\right).
\]
Therefore, for every \(b\ge 0\),
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
\tag{3}
\]

Next compute \(H_b(q)\) from (1). Fix \(w\in\mathfrak S_n\), and write
\[
d={\rm des}(w),\qquad M={\rm maj}(w).
\]
The coefficient of \(u^b\) in the \(w\)-summand of (1) is
\[
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}}
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq \{1,\dots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}
q^{\sum_{i\notin J} i}
=
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq \{1,\dots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}
q^{M-\sum_{j\in J}j}.
\]
Using
\[
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq \{1,\dots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}q^{-\sum_{j\in J}j}
=
q^{-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q,
\]
we obtain
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{w\in\mathfrak S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(w)\brack b}_q.
\tag{4}
\]

Now define
\[
D_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q,
\qquad D_{-1}(q)=0.
\]
For a fixed \(w\) with \(d={\rm des}(w)\ge 1\), its contribution to
\[
D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)
\]
is
\[
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity,
\[
{d\brack b}_q={d-1\brack b}_q+q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q,
\]
this equals the \(w\)-summand in (4). The unique permutation with \(d=0\) is the identity, and its contribution to \(H_b(q)\) is exactly \(\delta_{b,0}\). Hence
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q).
\tag{5}
\]
Comparing (3) and (5), and using \(C_{-1}=D_{-1}=0\), induction on \(b\) gives
\[
C_b(q)=D_b(q)
\qquad\text{for all }b\ge 0.
\]

Finally,
\[
{d-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Therefore
\[
C_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Since
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
extracting the coefficient of \(q^a\) gives exactly
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
w\in \mathfrak S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\ 
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\right\}.
\]
This proves the stated hook interpretation.

\right\}.
\]

# Solution
**Claim.** Fix \(n\ge 1\). Let \(R_n^{(m)}\) be the diagonal coinvariant algebra of \(\mathfrak S_n\) in \(m\) commuting sets of \(n\) variables, and let
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\dots,q_m)=\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\dots,q_m)
\]
be its universal Schur-Hilbert expansion. Then
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]
For every hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), with \(a\ge 1\) and \(b\ge 0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in \mathfrak S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\\[1mm]
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\\[1mm]
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Here \({\rm des}(w)\) is the number of descents of \(w\), \({\rm maj}(w)\) is the sum of its descent positions, and \(\rho\subseteq b\times t\) means a partition with at most \(b\) parts, each at most \(t\).

We use the diagonal supersymmetry theorem for bosonic–fermionic coinvariant rings: for fixed \(n\), the Hilbert series of the \((k,j)\)-bosonic–fermionic diagonal coinvariant ring has an expansion
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q,\mathbf u)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u),
\]
with the same Hilbert-level coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) for all \(k,j\). If the theorem is stated at the \(\mathfrak S_n\)-character level, evaluating the character at the identity gives this Hilbert-series form. Taking \(j=0\) gives the purely bosonic expansion above, and taking \(k=j=1\) gives the superspace specialization used below. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.14885))

Let
\[
SR_n
=
\mathbb C[x_1,\dots,x_n]\otimes \bigwedge(\theta_1,\dots,\theta_n)/SI_n
\]
be the superspace coinvariant ring, where \(SI_n\) is generated by \(\mathfrak S_n\)-invariants with zero constant term. Rhoades–Wilson prove its bigraded Hilbert series in \(q\)-Stirling form; equivalently, by the Carlitz \(q\)-Eulerian/\(q\)-Stirling identity,
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in\mathfrak S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-\binom{{\rm des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{{\rm des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\tag{1}
\]
The quotient definition and the \(q\)-Stirling Hilbert formula are those of Rhoades–Wilson. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2301.09763))

Now compute the one-even, one-odd super-Schur specialization. With the unsigned Hilbert convention for odd variables,
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq \lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u).
\]
Since \(s_\nu(q)\neq 0\) in one variable only when \(\nu\) is a row, and \(s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u)\neq 0\) in one variable only when \(\lambda'/\nu'\) is a horizontal strip, every nonempty non-hook shape vanishes. For a hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), the only surviving rows are \(\nu=(a)\) and \(\nu=(a-1)\), giving
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\tag{2}
\]

The degree-zero component of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is the constants, so \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\). Define
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\,{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u).
\]
Using (2) in the superspace expansion,
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
1+\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
\left(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\right).
\]
Therefore, for every \(b\ge 0\),
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
\tag{3}
\]

Next compute \(H_b(q)\) from (1). Fix \(w\in\mathfrak S_n\), and write
\[
d={\rm des}(w),\qquad M={\rm maj}(w).
\]
The coefficient of \(u^b\) in the \(w\)-summand of (1) is
\[
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}}
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq \{1,\dots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}
q^{\sum_{i\notin J} i}
=
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq \{1,\dots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}
q^{M-\sum_{j\in J}j}.
\]
Using
\[
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq \{1,\dots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}q^{-\sum_{j\in J}j}
=
q^{-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q,
\]
we obtain
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{w\in\mathfrak S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(w)\brack b}_q.
\tag{4}
\]

Now define
\[
D_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q,
\qquad D_{-1}(q)=0.
\]
For a fixed \(w\) with \(d={\rm des}(w)\ge 1\), its contribution to
\[
D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)
\]
is
\[
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity,
\[
{d\brack b}_q={d-1\brack b}_q+q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q,
\]
this equals the \(w\)-summand in (4). The unique permutation with \(d=0\) is the identity, and its contribution to \(H_b(q)\) is exactly \(\delta_{b,0}\). Hence
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q).
\tag{5}
\]
Comparing (3) and (5), and using \(C_{-1}=D_{-1}=0\), induction on \(b\) gives
\[
C_b(q)=D_b(q)
\qquad\text{for all }b\ge 0.
\]

Finally,
\[
{d-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Therefore
\[
C_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Since
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
extracting the coefficient of \(q^a\) gives exactly
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
w\in \mathfrak S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\ 
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\right\}.
\]
This proves the stated hook interpretation.

1. **Patch for the Carlitz normalization used in (1).** Insert the following lemma after the Rhoades–Wilson \(q\)-Stirling formula.

Let \({\rm Stir}_q(n,k)\) be defined by
\[

\qquad {\rm Stir}_q(0,k)=\delta_{0,k}.

\[

\[

\[
q^{M-be+\binom b2}{{e}\brack b}_q [e+1]_q.
\]
The remaining \(n-1-e\) slots increase the descent number to \(e+1\); their major-index increments are \(e+1,e+2,\dots,n-1\). Their total contribution is
\[
q^{M-be+\binom b2}\,
q^{e+1-b}[n-e-1]_q{{e+1}\brack b}_q.
\]
Thus the total contribution of \(v\) to \(E_{n,b}\) is
\[
q^{M-be+\binom b2}
\left(
[e+1]_q{{e}\brack b}_q
+
q^{e+1-b}[n-e-1]_q{{e+1}\brack b}_q
\right).
\]
Using
\[
[e+1]_q{{e}\brack b}_q=[e+1-b]_q{{e+1}\brack b}_q
\]
and \([r]_q+q^r[s]_q=[r+s]_q\), the bracket equals
\[
[n-b]_q{{e+1}\brack b}_q.
\]

\[
{{e+1}\brack b}_q={{e}\brack b}_q+q^{e+1-b}{{e}\brack b-1}_q,
\]

E_{n,b}=[n-b]_q(E_{n-1,b}+E_{n-1,b-1}).
\]

Therefore the Rhoades–Wilson formula
\[
\sum_{k=0}^n u^{n-k}[k]_q!\,{\rm Stir}_q(n,k)
\]

\[
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-\binom{{\rm des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{{\rm des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\]
This proves formula (1).

Thus the asserted formula remains valid in the out-of-support hook cases.

# Solution
## Original Problem

Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

## Problem Solved

For every integer \(n\ge 1\), let \(c_\lambda(n)\) denote the universal Schur coefficient in the multigraded Hilbert-series expansion of \(R_n^{(m)}\). If \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\) is a hook partition, where \(a\ge 1\) and \(b\ge 0\), then
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Here \(S_n\) is the symmetric group, a descent of \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\) is an index \(i\in\{1,\dots,n-1\}\) with \(w_i>w_{i+1}\),
\[
\operatorname{des}(w)=|\{i:w_i>w_{i+1}\}|,\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{w_i>w_{i+1}} i.
\]
A partition \(\rho\subseteq b\times r\) means that \(\rho\) has at most \(b\) parts and every part is at most \(r\); the empty partition is allowed, and \(|\rho|\) denotes the sum of its parts. For a standard Young tableau \(T\), a descent is an index \(i\) such that \(i+1\) appears in a strictly lower row than \(i\), and \(\operatorname{des}(T)\), \(\operatorname{maj}(T)\) are defined analogously.

The empty coefficient is
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]
Consequently,
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\qquad\text{whenever }b\ge n-1.
\]

Gaussian binomial coefficients are used with the convention
\[
\biggl[{r\atop s}\biggr]_q
=
\begin{cases}
\displaystyle\prod_{i=1}^{s}\frac{1-q^{r-s+i}}{1-q^i},&0\le s\le r,\\[1em]
0,&r<0,\ s<0,\text{ or }s>r.
\end{cases}
\]
Equivalently, for \(0\le s\le r\),
\[
\biggl[{r\atop s}\biggr]_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq s\times(r-s)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]

This proves the original hook-coefficient problem as stated; it is not a relaxation.

## Proof

Fix \(n\ge 1\). For a partition \(\mu\vdash n\), let \(\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\) be the set of standard Young tableaux of shape \(\mu\), and let \(f^\mu=|\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)|\).

We use the following standard inputs.

1. There are refined universal Frobenius coefficients \(c_{\lambda,\mu}\) such that, after passing to the one-bosonic-one-fermionic specialization,
   \[
   \operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)
   =
   \sum_{\lambda,\,\mu\vdash n}
   c_{\lambda,\mu}\,s_\lambda(q/u)\,s_\mu.
   \]
   The original Hilbert coefficient is obtained by forgetting the \(S_n\)-module structure:
   \[
   c_\lambda(n)=\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu c_{\lambda,\mu}.
   \]
   Also, the degree-zero piece is the constant polynomial carrying the trivial representation, so
   \[
   c_{\varnothing,\mu}=\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
   \]

2. In one bosonic variable \(q\) and one fermionic variable \(u\),
   \[
   s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
   \]
   and for hooks,
   \[
   s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
   \]
   All nonempty non-hook Schur functions vanish under this specialization.

3. Fields’ formula gives
   \[
   \operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)
   =
   \sum_T
   q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(T)+1}{2}}
   \prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(T)}(q^i+u)\,
   s_{\operatorname{shape}(T)},
   \]
   where the sum is over all standard Young tableaux \(T\) of size \(n\).

### 1. Extracting \(u^b s_\mu\) from Fields’ formula

Let \(H_{b,\mu}(q)\) be the coefficient of \(u^b s_\mu\) in \(\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)\).

Fix \(T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\), and write
\[
d=\operatorname{des}(T),\qquad M=\operatorname{maj}(T).
\]
The coefficient of \(u^b\) in
\[
\prod_{i=1}^{d}(q^i+u)
\]
is obtained by choosing \(b\) factors contributing \(u\), or equivalently \(d-b\) factors contributing \(q^i\). Thus, for \(0\le b\le d\),
\[
[u^b]\prod_{i=1}^{d}(q^i+u)
=
q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}
\biggl[{d\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]
Multiplying by Fields’ prefactor gives
\[
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}}
q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}
\biggl[{d\atop b}\biggr]_q
=
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\biggl[{d\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]
Therefore
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
\biggl[{\operatorname{des}(T)\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]

### 2. The triangular relation for hook coefficients

For \(b\ge 0\), define
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1} c_{(a,1^b),\mu}q^a,
\qquad
C_{-1,\mu}(q)=0.
\]
Compare the coefficient of \(u^b s_\mu\) in
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{\lambda,\mu}
c_{\lambda,\mu}s_\lambda(q/u)s_\mu.
\]
The empty partition contributes only when \(b=0\), giving
\[
\delta_{b,0}\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
\]
A hook \((a,1^b)\) contributes \(q^a u^b\), giving \(C_{b,\mu}(q)\). A hook \((a,1^{b-1})\) contributes \(q^{a-1}u^b\), giving \(q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q)\). Hence
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\delta_{b,0}\delta_{\mu,(n)}
+
C_{b,\mu}(q)
+
q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q).
\]

### 3. Solving the recurrence

We claim that
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ \operatorname{des}(T)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
\biggl[{\operatorname{des}(T)-1\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]

For \(b=0\), the triangular relation gives
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)=H_{0,\mu}(q)-\delta_{\mu,(n)}
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)}
-\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
\]
A standard tableau has no descents iff it is the unique row tableau of shape \((n)\). Therefore
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ \operatorname{des}(T)\ge 1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)},
\]
which is the claimed formula for \(b=0\), since
\[
\biggl[{\operatorname{des}(T)-1\atop 0}\biggr]_q=1
\]
when \(\operatorname{des}(T)\ge 1\).

Now assume \(b\ge 1\) and the formula is known for \(b-1\). From the triangular relation,
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)=H_{b,\mu}(q)-q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q).
\]
For a fixed tableau \(T\), with \(d=\operatorname{des}(T)\) and \(M=\operatorname{maj}(T)\), the contribution from \(q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q)\) is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
q^{d-b}
\biggl[{d-1\atop b-1}\biggr]_q.
\]
Thus
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_T
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\biggl[{d\atop b}\biggr]_q
-
q^{d-b}
\biggl[{d-1\atop b-1}\biggr]_q
\right).
\]
Using the \(q\)-Pascal identity
\[
\biggl[{d\atop b}\biggr]_q
=
\biggl[{d-1\atop b}\biggr]_q
+
q^{d-b}
\biggl[{d-1\atop b-1}\biggr]_q,
\]
we get
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_T
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\biggl[{d-1\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]
By the Gaussian convention, this summand is zero unless \(d-1\ge b\), i.e. unless \(d\ge b+1\). Hence
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ d\ge b+1}}
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\biggl[{d-1\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]

### 4. Translating the Gaussian binomial

For \(d\ge b+1\),
\[
\biggl[{d-1\atop b}\biggr]_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Therefore the coefficient of \(q^a\) in \(C_{b,\mu}(q)\) counts pairs \((T,\rho)\) such that
\[
T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu),\qquad d=\operatorname{des}(T)\ge b+1,
\]
\[
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(T)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

The exponent is always positive in this situation. Indeed, if \(d\ge b+1\), then the \(d\) descent positions have sum at least \(1+2+\cdots+d\), so
\[
\operatorname{maj}(T)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}
\ge 1.
\]
Thus the formula produces only hook arms \(a\ge 1\), as required.

### 5. Passing from tableaux to permutations by RSK

Now use
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu c_{(a,1^b),\mu}.
\]
Multiplying by \(f^\mu\) means choosing an additional standard tableau \(U\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\). Hence \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\) counts quadruples
\[
(\mu,T,U,\rho)
\]
such that
\[
\mu\vdash n,\qquad T,U\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu),
\]
\[
d=\operatorname{des}(T)\ge b+1,\qquad
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(T)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

By the Robinson-Schensted-Knuth correspondence, permutations \(w\in S_n\) are in bijection with pairs \((P(w),Q(w))\) of standard Young tableaux of the same shape. Moreover,
\[
\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w),
\]
and therefore
\[
\operatorname{des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{des}(w),\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(Q(w))=\operatorname{maj}(w).
\]
Identify \(U=P(w)\) and \(T=Q(w)\). Then the above quadruples are in bijection with pairs \((w,\rho)\) satisfying
\[
w\in S_n,\qquad d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,
\]
\[
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]
This proves the claimed permutation interpretation.

### 6. Edge cases

For the empty partition,
\[
c_\varnothing(n)
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu c_{\varnothing,\mu}
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu \delta_{\mu,(n)}
=
f^{(n)}
=
1.
\]

When \(b=0\), the rectangle is \(0\times(d-1)\), so \(\rho\) must be empty. The formula becomes
\[
c_{(a)}(n)
=
\#\{w\in S_n:\operatorname{des}(w)\ge 1,\ \operatorname{maj}(w)=a\}.
\]
Since \(a\ge 1\), the condition \(\operatorname{maj}(w)=a\) already forces at least one descent.

Finally, every permutation in \(S_n\) has at most \(n-1\) descents. If \(b\ge n-1\), then the condition
\[
\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1
\]
is impossible. Hence
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0
\qquad\text{for all }b\ge n-1.
\]

# Original Problem
Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

With the two routine patches above, the proof establishes the claimed hook formula for the universal Hilbert-Schur coefficients of the diagonal coinvariant algebra of \(S_n\): \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\), and for every hook \((a,1^b)\), \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\) is counted by the stated pairs \((w,\rho)\). **Claim.** Fix \(n\ge 1\). Let \(R_n^{(m)}\) be the diagonal coinvariant algebra of \(\mathfrak S_n\) in \(m\) commuting sets of \(n\) variables, and let
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\dots,q_m)=\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\dots,q_m)
\]
be its universal Schur-Hilbert expansion. Then
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]
For every hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), with \(a\ge 1\) and \(b\ge 0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in \mathfrak S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\\[1mm]
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\\[1mm]
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Here \({\rm des}(w)\) is the number of descents of \(w\), \({\rm maj}(w)\) is the sum of its descent positions, and \(\rho\subseteq b\times t\) means a partition with at most \(b\) parts, each at most \(t\).

We use the diagonal supersymmetry theorem for bosonic–fermionic coinvariant rings: for fixed \(n\), the Hilbert series of the \((k,j)\)-bosonic–fermionic diagonal coinvariant ring has an expansion
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q,\mathbf u)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u),
\]
with the same Hilbert-level coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) for all \(k,j\). If the theorem is stated at the \(\mathfrak S_n\)-character level, evaluating the character at the identity gives this Hilbert-series form. Taking \(j=0\) gives the purely bosonic expansion above, and taking \(k=j=1\) gives the superspace specialization used below. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.14885))

Let
\[
SR_n
=
\mathbb C[x_1,\dots,x_n]\otimes \bigwedge(\theta_1,\dots,\theta_n)/SI_n
\]
be the superspace coinvariant ring, where \(SI_n\) is generated by \(\mathfrak S_n\)-invariants with zero constant term. Rhoades–Wilson prove its bigraded Hilbert series in \(q\)-Stirling form; equivalently, by the Carlitz \(q\)-Eulerian/\(q\)-Stirling identity,
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in\mathfrak S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-\binom{{\rm des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{{\rm des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\tag{1}
\]
The quotient definition and the \(q\)-Stirling Hilbert formula are those of Rhoades–Wilson. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2301.09763))

Now compute the one-even, one-odd super-Schur specialization. With the unsigned Hilbert convention for odd variables,
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq \lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u).
\]
Since \(s_\nu(q)\neq 0\) in one variable only when \(\nu\) is a row, and \(s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u)\neq 0\) in one variable only when \(\lambda'/\nu'\) is a horizontal strip, every nonempty non-hook shape vanishes. For a hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), the only surviving rows are \(\nu=(a)\) and \(\nu=(a-1)\), giving
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\tag{2}
\]

The degree-zero component of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is the constants, so \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\). Define
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0,

\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\,{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u).

Using (2) in the superspace expansion,
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
1+\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
\left(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\right).
\]
Therefore, for every \(b\ge 0\),
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
\tag{3}

\[
d={\rm des}(w),\qquad M={\rm maj}(w).
\]
The coefficient of \(u^b\) in the \(w\)-summand of (1) is
\[
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}}
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq \{1,\dots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}
q^{\sum_{i\notin J} i}
=
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq \{1,\dots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}
q^{M-\sum_{j\in J}j}.
\]
Using
\[
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq \{1,\dots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}q^{-\sum_{j\in J}j}
=
q^{-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q,

\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{w\in\mathfrak S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(w)\brack b}_q.
\tag{4}
\]

Now define
\[
D_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q,
\qquad D_{-1}(q)=0.
\]
For a fixed \(w\) with \(d={\rm des}(w)\ge 1\), its contribution to
\[
D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)
\]
is
\[
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right).

By the \(q\)-Pascal identity,

{d\brack b}_q={d-1\brack b}_q+q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q,

this equals the \(w\)-summand in (4). The unique permutation with \(d=0\) is the identity, and its contribution to \(H_b(q)\) is exactly \(\delta_{b,0}\). Hence
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q).
\tag{5}
\]
Comparing (3) and (5), and using \(C_{-1}=D_{-1}=0\), induction on \(b\) gives
\[
C_b(q)=D_b(q)
\qquad\text{for all }b\ge 0.
\]

Finally,
\[
{d-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Therefore
\[
C_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Since
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
extracting the coefficient of \(q^a\) gives exactly
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
w\in \mathfrak S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\ 
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\right\}.
\]
This proves the stated hook interpretation.

PATCHES REQUIRED FOR CORRECTNESS:
1. **Patch for the Carlitz normalization used in (1).** Insert the following lemma after the Rhoades–Wilson \(q\)-Stirling formula.

Let \({\rm Stir}_q(n,k)\) be defined by
\[
{\rm Stir}_q(n,k)={\rm Stir}_q(n-1,k-1)+[k]_q{\rm Stir}_q(n-1,k),
\qquad {\rm Stir}_q(0,k)=\delta_{0,k}.
\]
For \(0\le b\le n\), set
\[
O_{n,b}=[n-b]_q!\,{\rm Stir}_q(n,n-b)
\]
and
\[
E_{n,b}=\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(w)\brack b}_q .
\]
Then \(O_{n,b}=E_{n,b}\).

Proof: From the \(q\)-Stirling recurrence,
\[
O_{n,b}=[n-b]_q\bigl(O_{n-1,b}+O_{n-1,b-1}\bigr),
\]
with the usual convention that out-of-range terms are zero.

Now prove the same recurrence for \(E_{n,b}\) by inserting \(n\) into a permutation \(v\in S_{n-1}\). Write \(e={\rm des}(v)\) and \(M={\rm maj}(v)\). The \(e+1\) slots after a descent of \(v\), together with the terminal slot, keep the descent number \(e\); their major-index increments are \(0,1,\dots,e\). Their total contribution is
\[
q^{M-be+\binom b2}{{e}\brack b}_q [e+1]_q.
\]
The remaining \(n-1-e\) slots increase the descent number to \(e+1\); their major-index increments are \(e+1,e+2,\dots,n-1\). Their total contribution is
\[
q^{M-be+\binom b2}\,
q^{e+1-b}[n-e-1]_q{{e+1}\brack b}_q.
\]
Thus the total contribution of \(v\) to \(E_{n,b}\) is
\[
q^{M-be+\binom b2}
\left(
[e+1]_q{{e}\brack b}_q
+
q^{e+1-b}[n-e-1]_q{{e+1}\brack b}_q
\right).
\]
Using
\[
[e+1]_q{{e}\brack b}_q=[e+1-b]_q{{e+1}\brack b}_q
\]
and \([r]_q+q^r[s]_q=[r+s]_q\), the bracket equals
\[
[n-b]_q{{e+1}\brack b}_q.
\]
Then \(q\)-Pascal gives
\[
{{e+1}\brack b}_q={{e}\brack b}_q+q^{e+1-b}{{e}\brack b-1}_q,
\]
so the contribution equals \([n-b]_q\) times the contribution of \(v\) to \(E_{n-1,b}+E_{n-1,b-1}\). Hence
\[
E_{n,b}=[n-b]_q(E_{n-1,b}+E_{n-1,b-1}).
\]
The initial conditions agree, so \(E_{n,b}=O_{n,b}\).

Therefore the Rhoades–Wilson formula
\[
\sum_{k=0}^n u^{n-k}[k]_q!\,{\rm Stir}_q(n,k)
\]
has \(u^b\)-coefficient \(E_{n,b}\), which is exactly the \(u^b\)-coefficient of
\[
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-\binom{{\rm des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{{\rm des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\]
This proves formula (1).

2. **Patch for hooks of length \(>n\).** Add: By diagonal supersymmetry / Howe-duality support, only partitions \(\lambda\) with \(\ell(\lambda)\le n\) occur in the universal Hilbert expansion, so \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\) for \(b\ge n\). On the claimed combinatorial side, \({\rm des}(w)\le n-1\), so the condition \({\rm des}(w)\ge b+1\) is impossible for \(b\ge n-1\) when appropriate. Thus the asserted formula remains valid in the out-of-support hook cases.

**Claim.** For fixed \(n\ge 1\), let
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\dots,q_m)=\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\dots,q_m)
\]
be the universal Schur expansion for the diagonal coinvariant algebra of \(\mathfrak S_n\). Then
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]
For every hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), with \(a\ge 1\), \(b\ge 0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in \mathfrak S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\\
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Here \(\rho\subseteq b\times t\) means that \(\rho\) is a partition with at most \(b\) parts, each at most \(t\).

We use two standard inputs. First, diagonal supersymmetry gives, for the bosonic–fermionic diagonal coinvariant ring with \(k\) commuting and \(j\) anticommuting sets,
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q,\mathbf u)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u),
\]
with the same coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\). We use the unsigned Hilbert convention for odd variables, so
\[
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(\mathbf q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(\mathbf u).
\]
Also, only partitions \(\lambda\) with \(\ell(\lambda)\le n\) occur.

Second, for the one-boson, one-fermion superspace coinvariant ring \(SR_n=R_n^{(1,1)}\), the Rhoades–Wilson Hilbert series is
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{k=0}^n u^{n-k}[k]_q!\,{\rm Stir}_q(n,k),
\]
where \([r]_q=1+q+\cdots+q^{r-1}\), and
\[
{\rm Stir}_q(N,K)
=
{\rm Stir}_q(N-1,K-1)+[K]_q{\rm Stir}_q(N-1,K),
\qquad
{\rm Stir}_q(0,K)=\delta_{0,K}.
\]

We first record the required Carlitz normalization. For \(0\le b\le N\), set
\[
O_{N,b}=[N-b]_q!\,{\rm Stir}_q(N,N-b),
\]
and
\[
E_{N,b}
=
\sum_{w\in \mathfrak S_N}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(w)\brack b}_q.
\]
Then \(O_{N,b}=E_{N,b}\).

Indeed, the \(q\)-Stirling recurrence gives
\[
O_{N,b}=[N-b]_q\bigl(O_{N-1,b}+O_{N-1,b-1}\bigr).
\]
The same recurrence holds for \(E_{N,b}\). Insert \(N\) into a permutation \(v\in\mathfrak S_{N-1}\). Write \(e={\rm des}(v)\), \(M={\rm maj}(v)\). The \(e+1\) slots after descents of \(v\), together with the terminal slot, preserve the descent number; their major-index increments are \(0,1,\dots,e\). Their total contribution is
\[
q^{M-be+\binom b2}{e\brack b}_q[e+1]_q.
\]
The remaining \(N-1-e\) slots increase the descent number to \(e+1\); their major-index increments are \(e+1,e+2,\dots,N-1\). Their total contribution is
\[
q^{M-be+\binom b2}
q^{e+1-b}[N-e-1]_q{e+1\brack b}_q.
\]
Thus the total contribution of all insertions of \(N\) into \(v\) is
\[
q^{M-be+\binom b2}
\left(
[e+1]_q{e\brack b}_q
+
q^{e+1-b}[N-e-1]_q{e+1\brack b}_q
\right).
\]
Using
\[
[e+1]_q{e\brack b}_q=[e+1-b]_q{e+1\brack b}_q
\]
and \([r]_q+q^r[s]_q=[r+s]_q\), the bracket becomes
\[
[N-b]_q{e+1\brack b}_q.
\]
Then \(q\)-Pascal gives
\[
{e+1\brack b}_q={e\brack b}_q+q^{e+1-b}{e\brack b-1}_q,
\]
so the contribution is \([N-b]_q\) times the contribution of \(v\) to \(E_{N-1,b}+E_{N-1,b-1}\). The initial conditions agree, hence \(O_{N,b}=E_{N,b}\).

Therefore, if
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\,{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u),
\]
then
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{w\in\mathfrak S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(w)\brack b}_q.
\tag{1}
\]

Now compute the same coefficient using supersymmetry. In one even variable \(q\) and one odd variable \(u\),
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)
=
\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u).
\]
Since \(s_\nu(q)\neq 0\) only when \(\nu\) is a row, and \(s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u)\neq 0\) only when \(\lambda'/\nu'\) is a horizontal strip, the only nonempty \(\lambda\) that survive are hooks. For \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\),
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\tag{2}
\]

Define
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0.
\]
The empty partition contributes \(1\), so by (2),
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
1+\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\bigl(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\bigr).
\]
Hence
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
\tag{3}
\]

Next define
\[
D_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q,
\qquad D_{-1}(q)=0.
\]
For a fixed nonidentity \(w\) with \(d={\rm des}(w)\ge1\), its contribution to
\[
D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)
\]
is
\[
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right).
\]
By \(q\)-Pascal,
\[
{d\brack b}_q
=
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q,
\]
so this equals the \(w\)-summand in (1). The unique permutation with \(d=0\) is the identity, whose contribution to (1) is exactly \(\delta_{b,0}\). Therefore
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q).
\tag{4}
\]

Comparing (3) and (4), and using \(C_{-1}=D_{-1}=0\), induction on \(b\) gives
\[
C_b(q)=D_b(q)
\qquad\text{for all } b\ge0.
\tag{5}
\]

Finally,
\[
{d-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Thus (5) becomes
\[
C_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Since
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
extracting the coefficient of \(q^a\) gives the claimed counting formula.

The constant term of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is the one-dimensional space of constants, and no nonempty Schur polynomial has degree \(0\), so \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\). Also, if \(b\ge n\), then \(\ell(a,1^b)>n\), so \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\); on the combinatorial side, \({\rm des}(w)\le n-1\), so the required condition \({\rm des}(w)\ge b+1\) is impossible. Thus the formula holds in all hook cases.

# Prior solution (for your reference only — do not include or quote it in your output)
## Original Problem

Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

## Problem Solved

For every integer \(n\ge 1\), let \(c_\lambda(n)\) denote the universal Schur coefficient in the multigraded Hilbert-series expansion of \(R_n^{(m)}\). If \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\) is a hook partition, where \(a\ge 1\) and \(b\ge 0\), then
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Here \(S_n\) is the symmetric group, a descent of \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\) is an index \(i\in\{1,\dots,n-1\}\) with \(w_i>w_{i+1}\),
\[
\operatorname{des}(w)=|\{i:w_i>w_{i+1}\}|,\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{w_i>w_{i+1}} i.
\]
A partition \(\rho\subseteq b\times r\) means that \(\rho\) has at most \(b\) parts and every part is at most \(r\); the empty partition is allowed, and \(|\rho|\) denotes the sum of its parts. For a standard Young tableau \(T\), a descent is an index \(i\) such that \(i+1\) appears in a strictly lower row than \(i\), and \(\operatorname{des}(T)\), \(\operatorname{maj}(T)\) are defined analogously.

The empty coefficient is
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]
Consequently,
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\qquad\text{whenever }b\ge n-1.
\]

Gaussian binomial coefficients are used with the convention
\[
\biggl[{r\atop s}\biggr]_q
=
\begin{cases}
\displaystyle\prod_{i=1}^{s}\frac{1-q^{r-s+i}}{1-q^i},&0\le s\le r,\\[1em]
0,&r<0,\ s<0,\text{ or }s>r.
\end{cases}
\]
Equivalently, for \(0\le s\le r\),
\[
\biggl[{r\atop s}\biggr]_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq s\times(r-s)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]

This proves the original hook-coefficient problem as stated; it is not a relaxation.

## Proof

Fix \(n\ge 1\). For a partition \(\mu\vdash n\), let \(\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\) be the set of standard Young tableaux of shape \(\mu\), and let \(f^\mu=|\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)|\).

We use the following standard inputs.

1. There are refined universal Frobenius coefficients \(c_{\lambda,\mu}\) such that, after passing to the one-bosonic-one-fermionic specialization,
   \[
   \operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)
   =
   \sum_{\lambda,\,\mu\vdash n}
   c_{\lambda,\mu}\,s_\lambda(q/u)\,s_\mu.
   \]
   The original Hilbert coefficient is obtained by forgetting the \(S_n\)-module structure:
   \[
   c_\lambda(n)=\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu c_{\lambda,\mu}.
   \]
   Also, the degree-zero piece is the constant polynomial carrying the trivial representation, so
   \[
   c_{\varnothing,\mu}=\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
   \]

2. In one bosonic variable \(q\) and one fermionic variable \(u\),
   \[
   s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
   \]
   and for hooks,
   \[
   s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
   \]
   All nonempty non-hook Schur functions vanish under this specialization.

3. Fields’ formula gives
   \[
   \operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)
   =
   \sum_T
   q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(T)+1}{2}}
   \prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(T)}(q^i+u)\,
   s_{\operatorname{shape}(T)},
   \]
   where the sum is over all standard Young tableaux \(T\) of size \(n\).

### 1. Extracting \(u^b s_\mu\) from Fields’ formula

Let \(H_{b,\mu}(q)\) be the coefficient of \(u^b s_\mu\) in \(\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)\).

Fix \(T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\), and write
\[
d=\operatorname{des}(T),\qquad M=\operatorname{maj}(T).
\]
The coefficient of \(u^b\) in
\[
\prod_{i=1}^{d}(q^i+u)
\]
is obtained by choosing \(b\) factors contributing \(u\), or equivalently \(d-b\) factors contributing \(q^i\). Thus, for \(0\le b\le d\),
\[
[u^b]\prod_{i=1}^{d}(q^i+u)
=
q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}
\biggl[{d\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]
Multiplying by Fields’ prefactor gives
\[
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}}
q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}
\biggl[{d\atop b}\biggr]_q
=
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\biggl[{d\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]
Therefore
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
\biggl[{\operatorname{des}(T)\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]

### 2. The triangular relation for hook coefficients

For \(b\ge 0\), define
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1} c_{(a,1^b),\mu}q^a,
\qquad
C_{-1,\mu}(q)=0.
\]
Compare the coefficient of \(u^b s_\mu\) in
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{\lambda,\mu}
c_{\lambda,\mu}s_\lambda(q/u)s_\mu.
\]
The empty partition contributes only when \(b=0\), giving
\[
\delta_{b,0}\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
\]
A hook \((a,1^b)\) contributes \(q^a u^b\), giving \(C_{b,\mu}(q)\). A hook \((a,1^{b-1})\) contributes \(q^{a-1}u^b\), giving \(q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q)\). Hence
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\delta_{b,0}\delta_{\mu,(n)}
+
C_{b,\mu}(q)
+
q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q).
\]

### 3. Solving the recurrence

We claim that
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ \operatorname{des}(T)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
\biggl[{\operatorname{des}(T)-1\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]

For \(b=0\), the triangular relation gives
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)=H_{0,\mu}(q)-\delta_{\mu,(n)}
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)}
-\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
\]
A standard tableau has no descents iff it is the unique row tableau of shape \((n)\). Therefore
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ \operatorname{des}(T)\ge 1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)},
\]
which is the claimed formula for \(b=0\), since
\[
\biggl[{\operatorname{des}(T)-1\atop 0}\biggr]_q=1
\]
when \(\operatorname{des}(T)\ge 1\).

Now assume \(b\ge 1\) and the formula is known for \(b-1\). From the triangular relation,
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)=H_{b,\mu}(q)-q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q).
\]
For a fixed tableau \(T\), with \(d=\operatorname{des}(T)\) and \(M=\operatorname{maj}(T)\), the contribution from \(q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q)\) is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
q^{d-b}
\biggl[{d-1\atop b-1}\biggr]_q.
\]
Thus
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_T
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\biggl[{d\atop b}\biggr]_q
-
q^{d-b}
\biggl[{d-1\atop b-1}\biggr]_q
\right).
\]
Using the \(q\)-Pascal identity
\[
\biggl[{d\atop b}\biggr]_q
=
\biggl[{d-1\atop b}\biggr]_q
+
q^{d-b}
\biggl[{d-1\atop b-1}\biggr]_q,
\]
we get
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_T
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\biggl[{d-1\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]
By the Gaussian convention, this summand is zero unless \(d-1\ge b\), i.e. unless \(d\ge b+1\). Hence
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ d\ge b+1}}
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\biggl[{d-1\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]

### 4. Translating the Gaussian binomial

For \(d\ge b+1\),
\[
\biggl[{d-1\atop b}\biggr]_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Therefore the coefficient of \(q^a\) in \(C_{b,\mu}(q)\) counts pairs \((T,\rho)\) such that
\[
T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu),\qquad d=\operatorname{des}(T)\ge b+1,
\]
\[
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(T)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

The exponent is always positive in this situation. Indeed, if \(d\ge b+1\), then the \(d\) descent positions have sum at least \(1+2+\cdots+d\), so
\[
\operatorname{maj}(T)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}
\ge 1.
\]
Thus the formula produces only hook arms \(a\ge 1\), as required.

### 5. Passing from tableaux to permutations by RSK

Now use
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu c_{(a,1^b),\mu}.
\]
Multiplying by \(f^\mu\) means choosing an additional standard tableau \(U\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\). Hence \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\) counts quadruples
\[
(\mu,T,U,\rho)
\]
such that
\[
\mu\vdash n,\qquad T,U\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu),
\]
\[
d=\operatorname{des}(T)\ge b+1,\qquad
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(T)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

By the Robinson-Schensted-Knuth correspondence, permutations \(w\in S_n\) are in bijection with pairs \((P(w),Q(w))\) of standard Young tableaux of the same shape. Moreover,
\[
\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w),
\]
and therefore
\[
\operatorname{des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{des}(w),\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(Q(w))=\operatorname{maj}(w).
\]
Identify \(U=P(w)\) and \(T=Q(w)\). Then the above quadruples are in bijection with pairs \((w,\rho)\) satisfying
\[
w\in S_n,\qquad d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,
\]
\[
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]
This proves the claimed permutation interpretation.

### 6. Edge cases

For the empty partition,
\[
c_\varnothing(n)
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu c_{\varnothing,\mu}
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu \delta_{\mu,(n)}
=
f^{(n)}
=
1.
\]

When \(b=0\), the rectangle is \(0\times(d-1)\), so \(\rho\) must be empty. The formula becomes
\[
c_{(a)}(n)
=
\#\{w\in S_n:\operatorname{des}(w)\ge 1,\ \operatorname{maj}(w)=a\}.
\]
Since \(a\ge 1\), the condition \(\operatorname{maj}(w)=a\) already forces at least one descent.

Finally, every permutation in \(S_n\) has at most \(n-1\) descents. If \(b\ge n-1\), then the condition
\[
\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1
\]
is impossible. Hence
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0
\qquad\text{for all }b\ge n-1.
\]

**Claim.** Fix \(n\ge 1\). For every hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\) with \(a\ge 1\), \(b\ge 0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\Bigl\{(w,\rho): w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\Bigr\}.
\]
Here \(\rho\subseteq b\times r\) means that \(\rho\) is a partition with at most \(b\) parts, each at most \(r\). Also
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1,
\qquad
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\quad\text{if }b\ge n-1.
\]

We prove this using the refined Frobenius coefficients from diagonal supersymmetry, the one-bosonic-one-fermionic super Schur specialization, and the Murai–Rhoades–Wilson theorem on the superspace coinvariant ring.

Let \(R_n^{(k,j)}\) denote the diagonal coinvariant ring with \(k\) bosonic and \(j\) fermionic sets. Thus \(R_n^{(m)}=R_n^{(m,0)}\), and \(SR_n:=R_n^{(1,1)}\). Diagonal supersymmetry gives nonnegative integers \(c_{\lambda,\mu}\), independent of \((k,j)\), such that
\[
\operatorname{Frob}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\lambda\in P(k,j,n)}\sum_{\mu\vdash n}
c_{\lambda,\mu}\,s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)\,s_\mu,
\]
where \(P(k,j,n)=\{\lambda:\ell(\lambda)\le n,\ \lambda_{k+1}\le j\}\). For the purely bosonic ring, \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), and forgetting the \(S_n\)-module structure gives
\[
c_\lambda(n)=\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu c_{\lambda,\mu},
\qquad f^\mu:=|\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)|.
\]
The degree-zero piece is the one-dimensional trivial representation, so
\[
c_{\varnothing,\mu}=\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
\]
These are the standard diagonal-supersymmetry inputs. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

For one bosonic variable \(q\) and one fermionic variable \(u\),
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,\qquad
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1},
\]
and every non-hook shape specializes to \(0\). This follows from the super Schur formula
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u).
\]
([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

Finally, the Murai–Rhoades–Wilson theorem gives
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{r\ge0}u^r
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(n)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-r\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom r2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)\\ r\end{bmatrix}_q
s_{\operatorname{shape}(T)}.
\]
This is their \(C_{n,k}\)-form with \(r=n-k\). Equivalently, it matches the product form
\[
\sum_T q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(T)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(T)}(q^i+u)\,s_{\operatorname{shape}(T)},
\]
because
\[
[u^r]\prod_{i=1}^{d}(q^i+u)
=
q^{\binom{d-r+1}{2}}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ r\end{bmatrix}_q
\]
and
\[
-\binom{d+1}{2}+\binom{d-r+1}{2}
=
-rd+\binom r2.
\]
([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

We use the convention
\[
\begin{bmatrix}r\\s\end{bmatrix}_q=0
\quad\text{unless }0\le s\le r,
\]
and, for \(0\le s\le r\),
\[
\begin{bmatrix}r\\s\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq s\times(r-s)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]

For fixed \(\mu\vdash n\), let
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q):=[u^b s_\mu]\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u).
\]
From Murai–Rhoades–Wilson,
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

Define
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q):=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b),\mu}q^a,
\qquad
C_{-1,\mu}(q):=0.
\]
Using the one-variable super Schur specialization,
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\delta_{b,0}\delta_{\mu,(n)}
+
C_{b,\mu}(q)
+
q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q).
\]

We now solve this recurrence. For \(b=0\),
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)}
-\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
\]
The unique standard tableau with no descents is the row tableau of shape \((n)\). Hence
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ \operatorname{des}(T)\ge1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)}.
\]

Assume \(b\ge1\). For \(T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\), write
\[
d=\operatorname{des}(T),\qquad M=\operatorname{maj}(T).
\]
Using the recurrence and the induction hypothesis,
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_T
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
-
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity,
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
so
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ d\ge b+1}}
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Therefore,
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}q^{|\rho|},
\]
and the coefficient of \(q^a\) in \(C_{b,\mu}(q)\) counts pairs \((T,\rho)\) such that
\[
T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu),\quad d=\operatorname{des}(T)\ge b+1,\quad
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(T)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

The exponent is always positive under \(d\ge b+1\), since the sum of \(d\) descent positions is at least \(1+\cdots+d\), so
\[
\operatorname{maj}(T)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}
\ge1.
\]

Now pass from refined Frobenius coefficients to Hilbert coefficients:
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=\sum_{\mu\vdash n}f^\mu c_{(a,1^b),\mu}.
\]
Multiplying by \(f^\mu\) means choosing a second standard tableau \(U\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\). Hence \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\) counts quadruples \((\mu,T,U,\rho)\) with \(T,U\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\) satisfying the conditions above.

By the Robinson–Schensted correspondence, permutations \(w\in S_n\) are in bijection with pairs \((P(w),Q(w))\) of standard Young tableaux of the same shape, and
\[
\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w).
\]
Taking \(T=Q(w)\) and \(U=P(w)\), the quadruples are therefore in bijection with pairs \((w,\rho)\) such that
\[
w\in S_n,\quad d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\quad
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]
This proves the stated hook formula.

For the empty partition,
\[
c_\varnothing(n)
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu c_{\varnothing,\mu}
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu\delta_{\mu,(n)}
=
f^{(n)}
=
1.
\]

Finally, every permutation in \(S_n\) has at most \(n-1\) descents. If \(b\ge n-1\), then \(d\ge b+1\ge n\) is impossible. Hence the counting set is empty, and
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0
\qquad(b\ge n-1).
\]

prompt:
I need you to check the following solution very carefully and let me know if you find any gaps, classifying them as either major or minor.
You should also carefully check that all bibliographic references are valid.

# Original Problem
Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

# Claim Being Verified
With the two routine patches above, the proof establishes the claimed hook formula for the universal Hilbert-Schur coefficients of the diagonal coinvariant algebra of \(S_n\): \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\), and for every hook \((a,1^b)\), \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\) is counted by the stated pairs \((w,\rho)\). The proof relies on the verified diagonal supersymmetry theorem and the verified Rhoades–Wilson \(q\)-Stirling Hilbert series theorem, plus the patched Carlitz coefficient identity.

# Solution
**Claim.** For fixed \(n\ge 1\), let
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\dots,q_m)=\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\dots,q_m)
\]
be the universal Schur expansion for the diagonal coinvariant algebra of \(\mathfrak S_n\). Then
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]
For every hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), with \(a\ge 1\), \(b\ge 0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in \mathfrak S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\\
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Here \(\rho\subseteq b\times t\) means that \(\rho\) is a partition with at most \(b\) parts, each at most \(t\).

We use two standard inputs. First, diagonal supersymmetry gives, for the bosonic–fermionic diagonal coinvariant ring with \(k\) commuting and \(j\) anticommuting sets,
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q,\mathbf u)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u),
\]
with the same coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\). We use the unsigned Hilbert convention for odd variables, so
\[
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(\mathbf q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(\mathbf u).
\]
Also, only partitions \(\lambda\) with \(\ell(\lambda)\le n\) occur.

Second, for the one-boson, one-fermion superspace coinvariant ring \(SR_n=R_n^{(1,1)}\), the Rhoades–Wilson Hilbert series is
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{k=0}^n u^{n-k}[k]_q!\,{\rm Stir}_q(n,k),
\]
where \([r]_q=1+q+\cdots+q^{r-1}\), and
\[
{\rm Stir}_q(N,K)
=
{\rm Stir}_q(N-1,K-1)+[K]_q{\rm Stir}_q(N-1,K),
\qquad
{\rm Stir}_q(0,K)=\delta_{0,K}.
\]

We first record the required Carlitz normalization. For \(0\le b\le N\), set
\[
O_{N,b}=[N-b]_q!\,{\rm Stir}_q(N,N-b),
\]
and
\[
E_{N,b}
=
\sum_{w\in \mathfrak S_N}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(w)\brack b}_q.
\]
Then \(O_{N,b}=E_{N,b}\).

Indeed, the \(q\)-Stirling recurrence gives
\[
O_{N,b}=[N-b]_q\bigl(O_{N-1,b}+O_{N-1,b-1}\bigr).
\]
The same recurrence holds for \(E_{N,b}\). Insert \(N\) into a permutation \(v\in\mathfrak S_{N-1}\). Write \(e={\rm des}(v)\), \(M={\rm maj}(v)\). The \(e+1\) slots after descents of \(v\), together with the terminal slot, preserve the descent number; their major-index increments are \(0,1,\dots,e\). Their total contribution is
\[
q^{M-be+\binom b2}{e\brack b}_q[e+1]_q.
\]
The remaining \(N-1-e\) slots increase the descent number to \(e+1\); their major-index increments are \(e+1,e+2,\dots,N-1\). Their total contribution is
\[
q^{M-be+\binom b2}
q^{e+1-b}[N-e-1]_q{e+1\brack b}_q.
\]
Thus the total contribution of all insertions of \(N\) into \(v\) is
\[
q^{M-be+\binom b2}
\left(
[e+1]_q{e\brack b}_q
+
q^{e+1-b}[N-e-1]_q{e+1\brack b}_q
\right).
\]
Using
\[
[e+1]_q{e\brack b}_q=[e+1-b]_q{e+1\brack b}_q
\]
and \([r]_q+q^r[s]_q=[r+s]_q\), the bracket becomes
\[
[N-b]_q{e+1\brack b}_q.
\]
Then \(q\)-Pascal gives
\[
{e+1\brack b}_q={e\brack b}_q+q^{e+1-b}{e\brack b-1}_q,
\]
so the contribution is \([N-b]_q\) times the contribution of \(v\) to \(E_{N-1,b}+E_{N-1,b-1}\). The initial conditions agree, hence \(O_{N,b}=E_{N,b}\).

Therefore, if
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\,{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u),
\]
then
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{w\in\mathfrak S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(w)\brack b}_q.
\tag{1}
\]

Now compute the same coefficient using supersymmetry. In one even variable \(q\) and one odd variable \(u\),
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)
=
\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u).
\]
Since \(s_\nu(q)\neq 0\) only when \(\nu\) is a row, and \(s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u)\neq 0\) only when \(\lambda'/\nu'\) is a horizontal strip, the only nonempty \(\lambda\) that survive are hooks. For \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\),
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\tag{2}
\]

Define
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0.
\]
The empty partition contributes \(1\), so by (2),
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
1+\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\bigl(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\bigr).
\]
Hence
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
\tag{3}
\]

Next define
\[
D_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q,
\qquad D_{-1}(q)=0.
\]
For a fixed nonidentity \(w\) with \(d={\rm des}(w)\ge1\), its contribution to
\[
D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)
\]
is
\[
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right).
\]
By \(q\)-Pascal,
\[
{d\brack b}_q
=
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q,
\]
so this equals the \(w\)-summand in (1). The unique permutation with \(d=0\) is the identity, whose contribution to (1) is exactly \(\delta_{b,0}\). Therefore
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q).
\tag{4}
\]

Comparing (3) and (4), and using \(C_{-1}=D_{-1}=0\), induction on \(b\) gives
\[
C_b(q)=D_b(q)
\qquad\text{for all } b\ge0.
\tag{5}
\]

Finally,
\[
{d-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Thus (5) becomes
\[
C_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Since
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
extracting the coefficient of \(q^a\) gives the claimed counting formula.

The constant term of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is the one-dimensional space of constants, and no nonempty Schur polynomial has degree \(0\), so \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\). Also, if \(b\ge n\), then \(\ell(a,1^b)>n\), so \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\); on the combinatorial side, \({\rm des}(w)\le n-1\), so the required condition \({\rm des}(w)\ge b+1\) is impossible. Thus the formula holds in all hook cases.

prompt:
I need you to check the following solution very carefully and let me know if you find any gaps, classifying them as either major or minor.
You should also carefully check that all bibliographic references are valid.

# Original Problem
Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

# Claim Being Verified
After adding the cited black-box inputs, the proof establishes the stated hook formula: for \(a\ge1\), \(b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\{(w,\rho): w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\ 
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|\}.
\]
It also establishes \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\) and \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\) for \(b\ge n-1\).

# Solution
**Claim.** Fix \(n\ge 1\). For every hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\) with \(a\ge 1\), \(b\ge 0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\Bigl\{(w,\rho): w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\Bigr\}.
\]
Here \(\rho\subseteq b\times r\) means that \(\rho\) is a partition with at most \(b\) parts, each at most \(r\). Also
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1,
\qquad
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\quad\text{if }b\ge n-1.
\]

We prove this using the refined Frobenius coefficients from diagonal supersymmetry, the one-bosonic-one-fermionic super Schur specialization, and the Murai–Rhoades–Wilson theorem on the superspace coinvariant ring.

Let \(R_n^{(k,j)}\) denote the diagonal coinvariant ring with \(k\) bosonic and \(j\) fermionic sets. Thus \(R_n^{(m)}=R_n^{(m,0)}\), and \(SR_n:=R_n^{(1,1)}\). Diagonal supersymmetry gives nonnegative integers \(c_{\lambda,\mu}\), independent of \((k,j)\), such that
\[
\operatorname{Frob}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\lambda\in P(k,j,n)}\sum_{\mu\vdash n}
c_{\lambda,\mu}\,s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)\,s_\mu,
\]
where \(P(k,j,n)=\{\lambda:\ell(\lambda)\le n,\ \lambda_{k+1}\le j\}\). For the purely bosonic ring, \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), and forgetting the \(S_n\)-module structure gives
\[
c_\lambda(n)=\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu c_{\lambda,\mu},
\qquad f^\mu:=|\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)|.
\]
The degree-zero piece is the one-dimensional trivial representation, so
\[
c_{\varnothing,\mu}=\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
\]
These are the standard diagonal-supersymmetry inputs. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

For one bosonic variable \(q\) and one fermionic variable \(u\),
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,\qquad
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1},
\]
and every non-hook shape specializes to \(0\). This follows from the super Schur formula
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u).
\]
([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

Finally, the Murai–Rhoades–Wilson theorem gives
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{r\ge0}u^r
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(n)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-r\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom r2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)\\ r\end{bmatrix}_q
s_{\operatorname{shape}(T)}.
\]
This is their \(C_{n,k}\)-form with \(r=n-k\). Equivalently, it matches the product form
\[
\sum_T q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(T)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(T)}(q^i+u)\,s_{\operatorname{shape}(T)},
\]
because
\[
[u^r]\prod_{i=1}^{d}(q^i+u)
=
q^{\binom{d-r+1}{2}}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ r\end{bmatrix}_q
\]
and
\[
-\binom{d+1}{2}+\binom{d-r+1}{2}
=
-rd+\binom r2.
\]
([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

We use the convention
\[
\begin{bmatrix}r\\s\end{bmatrix}_q=0
\quad\text{unless }0\le s\le r,
\]
and, for \(0\le s\le r\),
\[
\begin{bmatrix}r\\s\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq s\times(r-s)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]

For fixed \(\mu\vdash n\), let
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q):=[u^b s_\mu]\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u).
\]
From Murai–Rhoades–Wilson,
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

Define
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q):=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b),\mu}q^a,
\qquad
C_{-1,\mu}(q):=0.
\]
Using the one-variable super Schur specialization,
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\delta_{b,0}\delta_{\mu,(n)}
+
C_{b,\mu}(q)
+
q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q).
\]

We now solve this recurrence. For \(b=0\),
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)}
-\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
\]
The unique standard tableau with no descents is the row tableau of shape \((n)\). Hence
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ \operatorname{des}(T)\ge1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)}.
\]

Assume \(b\ge1\). For \(T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\), write
\[
d=\operatorname{des}(T),\qquad M=\operatorname{maj}(T).
\]
Using the recurrence and the induction hypothesis,
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_T
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
-
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity,
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
so
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ d\ge b+1}}
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Therefore,
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}q^{|\rho|},
\]
and the coefficient of \(q^a\) in \(C_{b,\mu}(q)\) counts pairs \((T,\rho)\) such that
\[
T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu),\quad d=\operatorname{des}(T)\ge b+1,\quad
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(T)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

The exponent is always positive under \(d\ge b+1\), since the sum of \(d\) descent positions is at least \(1+\cdots+d\), so
\[
\operatorname{maj}(T)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}
\ge1.
\]

Now pass from refined Frobenius coefficients to Hilbert coefficients:
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=\sum_{\mu\vdash n}f^\mu c_{(a,1^b),\mu}.
\]
Multiplying by \(f^\mu\) means choosing a second standard tableau \(U\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\). Hence \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\) counts quadruples \((\mu,T,U,\rho)\) with \(T,U\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\) satisfying the conditions above.

By the Robinson–Schensted correspondence, permutations \(w\in S_n\) are in bijection with pairs \((P(w),Q(w))\) of standard Young tableaux of the same shape, and
\[
\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w).
\]
Taking \(T=Q(w)\) and \(U=P(w)\), the quadruples are therefore in bijection with pairs \((w,\rho)\) such that
\[
w\in S_n,\quad d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\quad
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]
This proves the stated hook formula.

For the empty partition,
\[
c_\varnothing(n)
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu c_{\varnothing,\mu}
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu\delta_{\mu,(n)}
=
f^{(n)}
=
1.
\]

Finally, every permutation in \(S_n\) has at most \(n-1\) descents. If \(b\ge n-1\), then \(d\ge b+1\ge n\) is impossible. Hence the counting set is empty, and
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0
\qquad(b\ge n-1).
\]

prompt:
You previously submitted a proof which a careful referee has ACCEPTED as essentially correct. The referee did note a few minor *expository* issues — things like notation, organization, missing citations for textbook facts — but emphasized these do NOT affect the rigor of the proof. Your task is a light polish to address those expository issues only.

# Original Problem
Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

# Claim Established (do NOT change this)
For every \(n\ge1\), the proof establishes the hook coefficients in the universal Hilbert–Schur expansion of the diagonal coinvariant algebra \(R_n^{(m)}\). Specifically,
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1,
\]
and for every hook \((a,1^b)\), \(a\ge1\), \(b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
w\in S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\right\}.
\]
The argument also correctly gives zero in the impossible cases, e.g. when the descent condition cannot be met.

# Current Accepted Proof
**Claim.** For fixed \(n\ge 1\), let
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\dots,q_m)=\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\dots,q_m)
\]
be the universal Schur expansion for the diagonal coinvariant algebra of \(\mathfrak S_n\). Then
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]
For every hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), with \(a\ge 1\), \(b\ge 0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in \mathfrak S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\\
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Here \(\rho\subseteq b\times t\) means that \(\rho\) is a partition with at most \(b\) parts, each at most \(t\).

We use two standard inputs. First, diagonal supersymmetry gives, for the bosonic–fermionic diagonal coinvariant ring with \(k\) commuting and \(j\) anticommuting sets,
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q,\mathbf u)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u),
\]
with the same coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\). We use the unsigned Hilbert convention for odd variables, so
\[
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(\mathbf q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(\mathbf u).
\]
Also, only partitions \(\lambda\) with \(\ell(\lambda)\le n\) occur.

Second, for the one-boson, one-fermion superspace coinvariant ring \(SR_n=R_n^{(1,1)}\), the Rhoades–Wilson Hilbert series is
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{k=0}^n u^{n-k}[k]_q!\,{\rm Stir}_q(n,k),
\]
where \([r]_q=1+q+\cdots+q^{r-1}\), and
\[
{\rm Stir}_q(N,K)
=
{\rm Stir}_q(N-1,K-1)+[K]_q{\rm Stir}_q(N-1,K),
\qquad
{\rm Stir}_q(0,K)=\delta_{0,K}.
\]

We first record the required Carlitz normalization. For \(0\le b\le N\), set
\[
O_{N,b}=[N-b]_q!\,{\rm Stir}_q(N,N-b),
\]
and
\[
E_{N,b}
=
\sum_{w\in \mathfrak S_N}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(w)\brack b}_q.
\]
Then \(O_{N,b}=E_{N,b}\).

Indeed, the \(q\)-Stirling recurrence gives
\[
O_{N,b}=[N-b]_q\bigl(O_{N-1,b}+O_{N-1,b-1}\bigr).
\]
The same recurrence holds for \(E_{N,b}\). Insert \(N\) into a permutation \(v\in\mathfrak S_{N-1}\). Write \(e={\rm des}(v)\), \(M={\rm maj}(v)\). The \(e+1\) slots after descents of \(v\), together with the terminal slot, preserve the descent number; their major-index increments are \(0,1,\dots,e\). Their total contribution is
\[
q^{M-be+\binom b2}{e\brack b}_q[e+1]_q.
\]
The remaining \(N-1-e\) slots increase the descent number to \(e+1\); their major-index increments are \(e+1,e+2,\dots,N-1\). Their total contribution is
\[
q^{M-be+\binom b2}
q^{e+1-b}[N-e-1]_q{e+1\brack b}_q.
\]
Thus the total contribution of all insertions of \(N\) into \(v\) is
\[
q^{M-be+\binom b2}
\left(
[e+1]_q{e\brack b}_q
+
q^{e+1-b}[N-e-1]_q{e+1\brack b}_q
\right).
\]
Using
\[
[e+1]_q{e\brack b}_q=[e+1-b]_q{e+1\brack b}_q
\]
and \([r]_q+q^r[s]_q=[r+s]_q\), the bracket becomes
\[
[N-b]_q{e+1\brack b}_q.
\]
Then \(q\)-Pascal gives
\[
{e+1\brack b}_q={e\brack b}_q+q^{e+1-b}{e\brack b-1}_q,
\]
so the contribution is \([N-b]_q\) times the contribution of \(v\) to \(E_{N-1,b}+E_{N-1,b-1}\). The initial conditions agree, hence \(O_{N,b}=E_{N,b}\).

Therefore, if
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\,{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u),
\]
then
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{w\in\mathfrak S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(w)\brack b}_q.
\tag{1}
\]

Now compute the same coefficient using supersymmetry. In one even variable \(q\) and one odd variable \(u\),
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)
=
\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u).
\]
Since \(s_\nu(q)\neq 0\) only when \(\nu\) is a row, and \(s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u)\neq 0\) only when \(\lambda'/\nu'\) is a horizontal strip, the only nonempty \(\lambda\) that survive are hooks. For \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\),
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\tag{2}
\]

Define
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0.
\]
The empty partition contributes \(1\), so by (2),
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
1+\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\bigl(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\bigr).
\]
Hence
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
\tag{3}
\]

Next define
\[
D_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q,
\qquad D_{-1}(q)=0.
\]
For a fixed nonidentity \(w\) with \(d={\rm des}(w)\ge1\), its contribution to
\[
D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)
\]
is
\[
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right).
\]
By \(q\)-Pascal,
\[
{d\brack b}_q
=
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q,
\]
so this equals the \(w\)-summand in (1). The unique permutation with \(d=0\) is the identity, whose contribution to (1) is exactly \(\delta_{b,0}\). Therefore
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q).
\tag{4}
\]

Comparing (3) and (4), and using \(C_{-1}=D_{-1}=0\), induction on \(b\) gives
\[
C_b(q)=D_b(q)
\qquad\text{for all } b\ge0.
\tag{5}
\]

Finally,
\[
{d-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Thus (5) becomes
\[
C_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Since
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
extracting the coefficient of \(q^a\) gives the claimed counting formula.

The constant term of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is the one-dimensional space of constants, and no nonempty Schur polynomial has degree \(0\), so \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\). Also, if \(b\ge n\), then \(\ell(a,1^b)>n\), so \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\); on the combinatorial side, \({\rm des}(w)\le n-1\), so the required condition \({\rm des}(w)\ge b+1\) is impossible. Thus the formula holds in all hook cases.

# Expository Issues to Address (EXPOSITION ONLY items only)
- EXPOSITION ONLY — The proof should give explicit theorem citations for the diagonal supersymmetry theorem and the Rhoades–Wilson Hilbert series theorem. The citation audit verifies both inputs and their hypotheses; this is not a mathematical gap.

**Claim.** Fix \(n\ge 1\). For every hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\) with \(a\ge 1\), \(b\ge 0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\Bigl\{(w,\rho): w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|

We prove this using the refined Frobenius coefficients from diagonal supersymmetry, the one-bosonic-one-fermionic super Schur specialization, and the Murai–Rhoades–Wilson theorem on the superspace coinvariant ring.

\[
\operatorname{Frob}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\lambda\in P(k,j,n)}\sum_{\mu\vdash n}

\]
where \(P(k,j,n)=\{\lambda:\ell(\lambda)\le n,\ \lambda_{k+1}\le j\}\). For the purely bosonic ring, \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), and forgetting the \(S_n\)-module structure gives
\[
c_\lambda(n)=\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu c_{\lambda,\mu},
\qquad f^\mu:=|\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)|.
\]
The degree-zero piece is the one-dimensional trivial representation, so
\[
c_{\varnothing,\mu}=\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
\]
These are the standard diagonal-supersymmetry inputs. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

For one bosonic variable \(q\) and one fermionic variable \(u\),
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,\qquad
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1},
\]
and every non-hook shape specializes to \(0\). This follows from the super Schur formula
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u).
\]
([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

Finally, the Murai–Rhoades–Wilson theorem gives
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{r\ge0}u^r
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(n)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-r\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom r2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)\\ r\end{bmatrix}_q
s_{\operatorname{shape}(T)}.
\]
This is their \(C_{n,k}\)-form with \(r=n-k\). Equivalently, it matches the product form
\[
\sum_T q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(T)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(T)}(q^i+u)\,s_{\operatorname{shape}(T)},
\]
because
\[
[u^r]\prod_{i=1}^{d}(q^i+u)
=
q^{\binom{d-r+1}{2}}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ r\end{bmatrix}_q
\]
and
\[
-\binom{d+1}{2}+\binom{d-r+1}{2}
=
-rd+\binom r2.
\]
([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

We use the convention
\[
\begin{bmatrix}r\\s\end{bmatrix}_q=0
\quad\text{unless }0\le s\le r,
\]
and, for \(0\le s\le r\),
\[
\begin{bmatrix}r\\s\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq s\times(r-s)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]

For fixed \(\mu\vdash n\), let
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q):=[u^b s_\mu]\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u).
\]
From Murai–Rhoades–Wilson,
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

Define
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q):=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b),\mu}q^a,
\qquad
C_{-1,\mu}(q):=0.
\]
Using the one-variable super Schur specialization,
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\delta_{b,0}\delta_{\mu,(n)}
+
C_{b,\mu}(q)
+
q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q).
\]

We now solve this recurrence. For \(b=0\),
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)}
-\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
\]
The unique standard tableau with no descents is the row tableau of shape \((n)\). Hence
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ \operatorname{des}(T)\ge1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)}.
\]

Assume \(b\ge1\). For \(T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\), write
\[
d=\operatorname{des}(T),\qquad M=\operatorname{maj}(T).
\]
Using the recurrence and the induction hypothesis,
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_T
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
-
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity,
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
so
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ d\ge b+1}}
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Therefore,
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}q^{|\rho|},
\]
and the coefficient of \(q^a\) in \(C_{b,\mu}(q)\) counts pairs \((T,\rho)\) such that
\[
T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu),\quad d=\operatorname{des}(T)\ge b+1,\quad
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(T)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

The exponent is always positive under \(d\ge b+1\), since the sum of \(d\) descent positions is at least \(1+\cdots+d\), so
\[
\operatorname{maj}(T)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}
\ge1.
\]

Now pass from refined Frobenius coefficients to Hilbert coefficients:
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=\sum_{\mu\vdash n}f^\mu c_{(a,1^b),\mu}.
\]
Multiplying by \(f^\mu\) means choosing a second standard tableau \(U\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\). Hence \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\) counts quadruples \((\mu,T,U,\rho)\) with \(T,U\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\) satisfying the conditions above.

By the Robinson–Schensted correspondence, permutations \(w\in S_n\) are in bijection with pairs \((P(w),Q(w))\) of standard Young tableaux of the same shape, and
\[
\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w).
\]
Taking \(T=Q(w)\) and \(U=P(w)\), the quadruples are therefore in bijection with pairs \((w,\rho)\) such that
\[
w\in S_n,\quad d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\quad
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]
This proves the stated hook formula.

For the empty partition,
\[
c_\varnothing(n)
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu c_{\varnothing,\mu}
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu\delta_{\mu,(n)}
=
f^{(n)}
=
1.
\]

Finally, every permutation in \(S_n\) has at most \(n-1\) descents. If \(b\ge n-1\), then \(d\ge b+1\ge n\) is impossible. Hence the counting set is empty, and
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0
\qquad(b\ge n-1).
\]
