where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

## Problem Solved

**Theorem.** Fix \(n\ge 1\). Let
\[
R_n^{(m)}
=
\mathbb C[x_i^{(r)}:1\le i\le n,\ 1\le r\le m]\Big/
\left\langle
\mathbb C[x_i^{(r)}]^{\mathfrak S_n}_{+}
\right\rangle
\]
be the diagonal coinvariant algebra with \(m\) bosonic sets of \(n\) variables, where \(\mathfrak S_n\) acts by \(w\cdot x_i^{(r)}=x_{w(i)}^{(r)}\), and where the Hilbert series is multigraded by the \(m\) variable sets:
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots,q_m)
=
\sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m\ge 0}
\dim (R_n^{(m)})_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}
q_1^{i_1}\cdots q_m^{i_m}.
\]
Suppose the universal Schur expansion is
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots,q_m)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots,q_m),
\]
with coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) independent of \(m\).

For a permutation \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\in \mathfrak S_n\), define
\[
\operatorname{Des}(w)=\{i\in\{1,\ldots,n-1\}: w_i>w_{i+1}\},
\]
\[
d(w)=\operatorname{des}(w)=|\operatorname{Des}(w)|,
\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in\operatorname{Des}(w)} i.
\]
For integers \(r,b\), define the Gaussian binomial coefficient by
\[
\begin{bmatrix}r\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\prod_{i=1}^b \frac{1-q^{r-b+i}}{1-q^i}
\quad\text{if }0\le b\le r,
\]
and set it equal to \(0\) otherwise. A partition \(\rho\) fits inside a \(b\) by \(r\) rectangle, written \(\rho\subseteq b\times r\), if \(\rho\) has at most \(b\) parts and every part is at most \(r\); \(|\rho|\) denotes its number of boxes.

Then
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]
For every nonempty hook partition \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), with \(a\ge 1\) and \(b\ge 0\),
\[
\boxed{
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in \mathfrak S_n,\ d(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
}
\]
Equivalently, if
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1} c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
then
\[
\boxed{
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
}
\]
This proves the original hook-coefficient problem exactly; it is not a relaxation.

## Proof

### 1. Supersymmetric universality input

We use the standard bosonic-fermionic extension of the diagonal coinvariant algebra. Let \(R_n^{(k,j)}\) denote the quotient with \(k\) commuting, or bosonic, variable sets and \(j\) anticommuting, or fermionic, variable sets, modulo the ideal generated by positive-degree diagonal \(\mathfrak S_n\)-invariants. Its Hilbert series is graded by bosonic variables \(\mathbf q=(q_1,\ldots,q_k)\) and fermionic variables \(\mathbf u=(u_1,\ldots,u_j)\).

The needed universality theorem says that
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u),
\]
where \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)\) is the positive super Schur function and the coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) are the same universal coefficients as in the purely bosonic case. Setting \(j=0\) gives
\[
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q),
\]
so one recovers the original expansion for \(R_n^{(m)}=R_n^{(m,0)}\).

The fermionic variables here are ordinary positive Hilbert variables: they count dimensions of fermionic-degree pieces. They are not signed supertrace variables.

Since the constant term of every coinvariant Hilbert series is \(1\), and every nonempty Schur or super Schur function has positive total degree, the coefficient of \(s_\varnothing\) is
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]

### 2. The \(GL(1|1)\) specialization and the triangular relation

Now specialize to one bosonic variable \(q\) and one fermionic variable \(u\). The positive super Schur specialization is
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
\]
and, for \(a\ge 1\), \(b\ge 0\),
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
All nonempty non-hooks vanish in this one-boson, one-fermion specialization.

Therefore
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q;u)
=
1+
\sum_{a\ge 1,\ b\ge 0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
\left(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\right).
\]
Define
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q;u),
\]
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\qquad
C_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0.
\]
Comparing coefficients of \(u^b\) gives the triangular relation
\[
\boxed{
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\delta_{b,0}+C_b^{(n)}(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
}
\tag{1}
\]

### 3. The superspace Hilbert formula

Let \(SR_n=R_n^{(1,1)}\) be the superspace coinvariant ring with one bosonic variable set and one fermionic variable set.

For a standard Young tableau \(T\), define
\[
\operatorname{Des}(T)=\{i: i+1\text{ lies in a lower row than }i\},
\]
\[
d(T)=|\operatorname{Des}(T)|,
\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(T)=\sum_{i\in\operatorname{Des}(T)}i.
\]
The Fields/Murai-Rhoades-Wilson superspace Frobenius formula may be written as
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-\binom{d(T)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{d(T)}(q^i+u)\,
s_{\operatorname{shape}(T)}.
\tag{2}
\]

Taking the ordinary Hilbert series replaces \(s_{\operatorname{shape}(T)}\) by the number of standard Young tableaux of the same shape. Thus
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}_n}
f^{\operatorname{shape}(T)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-\binom{d(T)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{d(T)}(q^i+u),
\]
where \(f^\mu\) is the number of standard Young tableaux of shape \(\mu\).

By row-insertion RSK, permutations \(w\in \mathfrak S_n\) are in bijection with pairs \((P(w),Q(w))\) of standard Young tableaux of the same shape, and the recording tableau satisfies
\[
\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w).
\]
Thus the Hilbert series becomes
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q;u)
=
\sum_{w\in\mathfrak S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{d(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{d(w)}(q^i+u).
\tag{3}
\]

Extract the coefficient of \(u^b\). For fixed \(w\), write \(d=d(w)\) and \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\). Then
\[
[u^b]\prod_{i=1}^d(q^i+u)
=
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq [d]\\ |J|=b}}
q^{\sum_{i\notin J} i}.
\]
Therefore
\[
[u^b]\left(
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^d(q^i+u)
\right)
=
q^M
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq [d]\\ |J|=b}}
q^{-\sum_{j\in J}j}.
\]
Using
\[
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq [d]\\ |J|=b}}
q^{-\sum_{j\in J}j}
=
q^{-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
we get
\[
\boxed{
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{w\in\mathfrak S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
}
\tag{4}
\]

### 4. Solving the triangular recurrence

Define
\[
D_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
and set \(D_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0\).

We prove that \(D_b^{(n)}(q)\) satisfies the same recurrence as \(C_b^{(n)}(q)\). The identity permutation has \(d(w)=0\) and \(\operatorname{maj}(w)=0\). Its contribution to \(H_b^{(n)}(q)\) is exactly \(\delta_{b,0}\), and it contributes to neither \(D_b^{(n)}(q)\) nor \(q^{-1}D_{b-1}^{(n)}(q)\).

Now fix a nonidentity permutation \(w\), so \(d=d(w)\ge 1\), and let \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\). Its contribution to \(D_b^{(n)}(q)\) is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Its contribution to \(q^{-1}D_{b-1}^{(n)}(q)\) is
\[
q^{-1}
q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Since
\[
q^{-1}
q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}
=
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}q^{d-b},
\]
the combined contribution is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity,
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
this equals
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
which is exactly the \(w\)-summand in \(H_b^{(n)}(q)\) from (4).

This also handles \(b=0\): then \(D_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0\), and every nonidentity permutation contributes \(q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)}\) to \(D_0^{(n)}(q)\), while the identity permutation is accounted for by the \(\delta_{0,0}\) term.

Thus
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\delta_{b,0}+D_b^{(n)}(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
\tag{5}
\]
Comparing (5) with the triangular relation (1), and using the initial conditions
\[
C_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=D_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0,
\]
we conclude recursively that
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=D_b^{(n)}(q)
\]
for every \(b\ge 0\). Hence
\[
\boxed{
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
}
\tag{6}
\]

### 5. Extracting the coefficient interpretation

The Gaussian binomial coefficient has the partition interpretation
\[
\begin{bmatrix}r\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(r-b)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
In (6), \(r=d(w)-1\). Therefore
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
This is why the rectangle has \(b\) rows and \(d(w)-1-b\) columns.

Substituting this into (6) gives
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Taking the coefficient of \(q^a\) yields
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in \mathfrak S_n,\ d(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]

Finally, the edge cases agree with the formula:

- If \(b=0\), the rectangle has height \(0\), so only \(\rho=\varnothing\) occurs. Hence
  \[
  c_{(a)}(n)
  =
  \#\{w\in\mathfrak S_n:d(w)\ge 1,\ \operatorname{maj}(w)=a\}.
  \]
  The identity permutation is excluded here and is exactly the source of \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\).

- If \(b\ge n-1\), then \(d(w)\le n-1\) for every \(w\in\mathfrak S_n\), so the condition \(d(w)\ge b+1\) is impossible. Thus
  \[
  c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0.
  \]

- If \(n=1\), the only permutation is the identity, so
  \[
  c_\varnothing(1)=1
  \]
  and all nonempty hook coefficients vanish.

This completes the combinatorial interpretation of the hook coefficients.

\]

### 3. The superspace Hilbert formula

Let \(SR_n=R_n^{(1,1)}\) be the superspace coinvariant ring with one bosonic variable set and one fermionic variable set.

For a standard Young tableau \(T\), define
\[
\operatorname{Des}(T)=\{i: i+1\text{ lies in a lower row than }i\},
\]
\[
d(T)=|\operatorname{Des}(T)|,
\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(T)=\sum_{i\in\operatorname{Des}(T)}i.
\]
The Fields/Murai-Rhoades-Wilson superspace Frobenius formula may be written as
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-\binom{d(T)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{d(T)}(q^i+u)\,
s_{\operatorname{shape}(T)}.
\tag{2}
\]

Taking the ordinary Hilbert series replaces \(s_{\operatorname{shape}(T)}\) by the number of standard Young tableaux of the same shape. Thus
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}_n}
f^{\operatorname{shape}(T)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-\binom{d(T)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{d(T)}(q^i+u),
\]
where \(f^\mu\) is the number of standard Young tableaux of shape \(\mu\).

By row-insertion RSK, permutations \(w\in \mathfrak S_n\) are in bijection with pairs \((P(w),Q(w))\) of standard Young tableaux of the same shape, and the recording tableau satisfies
\[
\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w).
\]
Thus the Hilbert series becomes
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q;u)
=
\sum_{w\in\mathfrak S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{d(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{d(w)}(q^i+u).
\tag{3}
\]

Extract the coefficient of \(u^b\). For fixed \(w\), write \(d=d(w)\) and \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\). Then
\[
[u^b]\prod_{i=1}^d(q^i+u)
=
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq [d]\\ |J|=b}}
q^{\sum_{i\notin J} i}.
\]
Therefore
\[
[u^b]\left(
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^d(q^i+u)
\right)
=
q^M
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq [d]\\ |J|=b}}
q^{-\sum_{j\in J}j}.
\]
Using
\[
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq [d]\\ |J|=b}}
q^{-\sum_{j\in J}j}
=
q^{-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
we get
\[
\boxed{
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{w\in\mathfrak S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
}
\tag{4}
\]

### 4. Solving the triangular recurrence

Define
\[
D_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
and set \(D_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0\).

We prove that \(D_b^{(n)}(q)\) satisfies the same recurrence as \(C_b^{(n)}(q)\). The identity permutation has \(d(w)=0\) and \(\operatorname{maj}(w)=0\). Its contribution to \(H_b^{(n)}(q)\) is exactly \(\delta_{b,0}\), and it contributes to neither \(D_b^{(n)}(q)\) nor \(q^{-1}D_{b-1}^{(n)}(q)\).

Now fix a nonidentity permutation \(w\), so \(d=d(w)\ge 1\), and let \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\). Its contribution to \(D_b^{(n)}(q)\) is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Its contribution to \(q^{-1}D_{b-1}^{(n)}(q)\) is
\[
q^{-1}
q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Since
\[
q^{-1}
q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}
=
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}q^{d-b},
\]
the combined contribution is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity,
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
this equals
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
which is exactly the \(w\)-summand in \(H_b^{(n)}(q)\) from (4).

This also handles \(b=0\): then \(D_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0\), and every nonidentity permutation contributes \(q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)}\) to \(D_0^{(n)}(q)\), while the identity permutation is accounted for by the \(\delta_{0,0}\) term.

Thus
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\delta_{b,0}+D_b^{(n)}(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
\tag{5}
\]
Comparing (5) with the triangular relation (1), and using the initial conditions
\[
C_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=D_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0,
\]
we conclude recursively that
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=D_b^{(n)}(q)
\]
for every \(b\ge 0\). Hence
\[
\boxed{
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
}
\tag{6}
\]

### 5. Extracting the coefficient interpretation

The Gaussian binomial coefficient has the partition interpretation
\[
\begin{bmatrix}r\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(r-b)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
In (6), \(r=d(w)-1\). Therefore
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
This is why the rectangle has \(b\) rows and \(d(w)-1-b\) columns.

Substituting this into (6) gives
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Taking the coefficient of \(q^a\) yields
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in \mathfrak S_n,\ d(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]

Finally, the edge cases agree with the formula:

- If \(b=0\), the rectangle has height \(0\), so only \(\rho=\varnothing\) occurs. Hence
  \[
  c_{(a)}(n)
  =
  \#\{w\in\mathfrak S_n:d(w)\ge 1,\ \operatorname{maj}(w)=a\}.
  \]
  The identity permutation is excluded here and is exactly the source of \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\).

- If \(b\ge n-1\), then \(d(w)\le n-1\) for every \(w\in\mathfrak S_n\), so the condition \(d(w)\ge b+1\) is impossible. Thus
  \[
  c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0.
  \]

- If \(n=1\), the only permutation is the identity, so
  \[
  c_\varnothing(1)=1
  \]
  and all nonempty hook coefficients vanish.

This completes the combinatorial interpretation of the hook coefficients.

## Original Problem

Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

## Problem Solved

**Theorem.** Fix \(n\ge 1\). Let
\[
R_n^{(m)}
=
\mathbb C[x_i^{(r)}:1\le i\le n,\ 1\le r\le m]\Big/
\left\langle
\mathbb C[x_i^{(r)}]^{\mathfrak S_n}_{+}
\right\rangle
\]
be the diagonal coinvariant algebra with \(m\) bosonic sets of \(n\) variables, where \(\mathfrak S_n\) acts by \(w\cdot x_i^{(r)}=x_{w(i)}^{(r)}\), and where the Hilbert series is multigraded by the \(m\) variable sets:
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots,q_m)
=
\sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m\ge 0}
\dim (R_n^{(m)})_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}
q_1^{i_1}\cdots q_m^{i_m}.
\]
Suppose the universal Schur expansion is
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots,q_m)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots,q_m),
\]
with coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) independent of \(m\).

For a permutation \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\in \mathfrak S_n\), define
\[
\operatorname{Des}(w)=\{i\in\{1,\ldots,n-1\}: w_i>w_{i+1}\},
\]
\[
d(w)=\operatorname{des}(w)=|\operatorname{Des}(w)|,
\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in\operatorname{Des}(w)} i.
\]
For integers \(r,b\), define the Gaussian binomial coefficient by
\[
\begin{bmatrix}r\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\prod_{i=1}^b \frac{1-q^{r-b+i}}{1-q^i}
\quad\text{if }0\le b\le r,
\]
and set it equal to \(0\) otherwise. A partition \(\rho\) fits inside a \(b\) by \(r\) rectangle, written \(\rho\subseteq b\times r\), if \(\rho\) has at most \(b\) parts and every part is at most \(r\); \(|\rho|\) denotes its number of boxes.

Then
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]
For every nonempty hook partition \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), with \(a\ge 1\) and \(b\ge 0\),
\[
\boxed{
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in \mathfrak S_n,\ d(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
}
\]
Equivalently, if
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1} c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
then
\[
\boxed{
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
}
\]
This proves the original hook-coefficient problem exactly; it is not a relaxation.

## Proof

### 1. Supersymmetric universality input

We use the standard bosonic-fermionic extension of the diagonal coinvariant algebra. Let \(R_n^{(k,j)}\) denote the quotient with \(k\) commuting, or bosonic, variable sets and \(j\) anticommuting, or fermionic, variable sets, modulo the ideal generated by positive-degree diagonal \(\mathfrak S_n\)-invariants. Its Hilbert series is graded by bosonic variables \(\mathbf q=(q_1,\ldots,q_k)\) and fermionic variables \(\mathbf u=(u_1,\ldots,u_j)\).

The needed universality theorem says that
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u),
\]
where \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)\) is the positive super Schur function and the coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) are the same universal coefficients as in the purely bosonic case. Setting \(j=0\) gives
\[
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q),
\]
so one recovers the original expansion for \(R_n^{(m)}=R_n^{(m,0)}\).

The fermionic variables here are ordinary positive Hilbert variables: they count dimensions of fermionic-degree pieces. They are not signed supertrace variables.

Since the constant term of every coinvariant Hilbert series is \(1\), and every nonempty Schur or super Schur function has positive total degree, the coefficient of \(s_\varnothing\) is
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]

### 2. The \(GL(1|1)\) specialization and the triangular relation

Now specialize to one bosonic variable \(q\) and one fermionic variable \(u\). The positive super Schur specialization is
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
\]
and, for \(a\ge 1\), \(b\ge 0\),
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
All nonempty non-hooks vanish in this one-boson, one-fermion specialization.

Therefore
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q;u)
=
1+
\sum_{a\ge 1,\ b\ge 0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
\left(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\right).
\]
Define
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q;u),
\]
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\qquad
C_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0.
\]
Comparing coefficients of \(u^b\) gives the triangular relation
\[
\boxed{
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\delta_{b,0}+C_b^{(n)}(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
}
\tag{1}
\]

### 3. The superspace Hilbert formula

Let \(SR_n=R_n^{(1,1)}\) be the superspace coinvariant ring with one bosonic variable set and one fermionic variable set.

For a standard Young tableau \(T\), define
\[
\operatorname{Des}(T)=\{i: i+1\text{ lies in a lower row than }i\},
\]
\[
d(T)=|\operatorname{Des}(T)|,
\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(T)=\sum_{i\in\operatorname{Des}(T)}i.
\]
The Fields/Murai-Rhoades-Wilson superspace Frobenius formula may be written as
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-\binom{d(T)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{d(T)}(q^i+u)\,
s_{\operatorname{shape}(T)}.
\tag{2}
\]

Taking the ordinary Hilbert series replaces \(s_{\operatorname{shape}(T)}\) by the number of standard Young tableaux of the same shape. Thus
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}_n}
f^{\operatorname{shape}(T)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-\binom{d(T)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{d(T)}(q^i+u),
\]
where \(f^\mu\) is the number of standard Young tableaux of shape \(\mu\).

By row-insertion RSK, permutations \(w\in \mathfrak S_n\) are in bijection with pairs \((P(w),Q(w))\) of standard Young tableaux of the same shape, and the recording tableau satisfies
\[
\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w).
\]
Thus the Hilbert series becomes
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q;u)
=
\sum_{w\in\mathfrak S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{d(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{d(w)}(q^i+u).
\tag{3}
\]

Extract the coefficient of \(u^b\). For fixed \(w\), write \(d=d(w)\) and \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\). Then
\[
[u^b]\prod_{i=1}^d(q^i+u)
=
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq [d]\\ |J|=b}}
q^{\sum_{i\notin J} i}.
\]
Therefore
\[
[u^b]\left(
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^d(q^i+u)
\right)
=
q^M
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq [d]\\ |J|=b}}
q^{-\sum_{j\in J}j}.
\]
Using
\[
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq [d]\\ |J|=b}}
q^{-\sum_{j\in J}j}
=
q^{-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
we get
\[
\boxed{
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{w\in\mathfrak S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
}
\tag{4}
\]

### 4. Solving the triangular recurrence

Define
\[
D_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
and set \(D_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0\).

We prove that \(D_b^{(n)}(q)\) satisfies the same recurrence as \(C_b^{(n)}(q)\). The identity permutation has \(d(w)=0\) and \(\operatorname{maj}(w)=0\). Its contribution to \(H_b^{(n)}(q)\) is exactly \(\delta_{b,0}\), and it contributes to neither \(D_b^{(n)}(q)\) nor \(q^{-1}D_{b-1}^{(n)}(q)\).

Now fix a nonidentity permutation \(w\), so \(d=d(w)\ge 1\), and let \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\). Its contribution to \(D_b^{(n)}(q)\) is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Its contribution to \(q^{-1}D_{b-1}^{(n)}(q)\) is
\[
q^{-1}
q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Since
\[
q^{-1}
q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}
=
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}q^{d-b},
\]
the combined contribution is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity,
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
this equals
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
which is exactly the \(w\)-summand in \(H_b^{(n)}(q)\) from (4).

This also handles \(b=0\): then \(D_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0\), and every nonidentity permutation contributes \(q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)}\) to \(D_0^{(n)}(q)\), while the identity permutation is accounted for by the \(\delta_{0,0}\) term.

Thus
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\delta_{b,0}+D_b^{(n)}(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
\tag{5}
\]
Comparing (5) with the triangular relation (1), and using the initial conditions
\[
C_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=D_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0,
\]
we conclude recursively that
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=D_b^{(n)}(q)
\]
for every \(b\ge 0\). Hence
\[
\boxed{
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
}
\tag{6}
\]

### 5. Extracting the coefficient interpretation

The Gaussian binomial coefficient has the partition interpretation
\[
\begin{bmatrix}r\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(r-b)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
In (6), \(r=d(w)-1\). Therefore
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
This is why the rectangle has \(b\) rows and \(d(w)-1-b\) columns.

Substituting this into (6) gives
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Taking the coefficient of \(q^a\) yields
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in \mathfrak S_n,\ d(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]

Finally, the edge cases agree with the formula:

- If \(b=0\), the rectangle has height \(0\), so only \(\rho=\varnothing\) occurs. Hence
  \[
  c_{(a)}(n)
  =
  \#\{w\in\mathfrak S_n:d(w)\ge 1,\ \operatorname{maj}(w)=a\}.
  \]
  The identity permutation is excluded here and is exactly the source of \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\).

- If \(b\ge n-1\), then \(d(w)\le n-1\) for every \(w\in\mathfrak S_n\), so the condition \(d(w)\ge b+1\) is impossible. Thus
  \[
  c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0.
  \]

- If \(n=1\), the only permutation is the identity, so
  \[
  c_\varnothing(1)=1
  \]
  and all nonempty hook coefficients vanish.

This completes the combinatorial interpretation of the hook coefficients.

PATCHES REQUIRED FOR CORRECTNESS:
1. **Universality/Hilbert conversion.** Insert: Lentfer’s theorem gives
\[
\operatorname{Frob}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=\sum_{\lambda,\mu} c_{\lambda,\mu}\,s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)\,s_\mu(\mathbf z),
\]
with \(c_{\lambda,\mu}\) independent of \((k,j)\). Applying the dimension map \(s_\mu(\mathbf z)\mapsto f^\mu=\dim V^\mu\) gives
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=\sum_\lambda \left(\sum_{\mu\vdash n} c_{\lambda,\mu}f^\mu\right)s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u).
\]
For \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), so the Hilbert coefficients in the original purely bosonic problem are exactly
\[
c_\lambda(n)=\sum_{\mu\vdash n}c_{\lambda,\mu}f^\mu.
\]

2. **\(GL(1|1)\) specialization.** Using
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u),
\]
one-variable Schur functions force \(\nu=(r)\), and \(s_{\lambda'/(1^r)}(u)\) is nonzero iff the skew shape \(\lambda'/(1^r)\) is a horizontal strip. If \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), only \(r=a\) and \(r=a-1\) work, giving respectively \(q^a u^b\) and \(q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\). If \(\lambda\) is not a hook, no such \(r\) works, so \(s_\lambda(q/u)=0\).

3. **Product form of the MRW formula.** From MRW,
\[
[u^r]\,\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_T q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)+\binom r2-rd(T)}
\begin{bmatrix}d(T)\\ r\end{bmatrix}_q s_{\operatorname{shape}(T)}.
\]
For \(d=d(T)\),
\[
[u^r]\prod_{i=1}^d(q^i+u)
=
q^{\binom{d+1}{2}-rd+\binom r2}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ r\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Therefore
\[
\sum_T q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-\binom{d(T)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{d(T)}(q^i+u)s_{\operatorname{shape}(T)}
\]
has exactly the same \(u^r\)-coefficients, proving the product formula used in the proof.

### Proof

s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)\,

\]

\[
s_\mu(\mathbf z)\longmapsto f^\mu=\#\operatorname{SYT}(\mu),
\]
turns graded Frobenius series into ordinary Hilbert series. Hence
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u),
\]
where

c_\lambda(n)=\sum_{\mu\vdash n}a_{\lambda,\mu}(n)f^\mu.
\]
For \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), so these are exactly the coefficients in the original purely bosonic Hilbert series.

The constant term of \(\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(m)})\) is \(1\), and every nonempty Schur polynomial has positive degree. Therefore
\[

\]

Now specialize to one bosonic variable \(q\) and one fermionic variable \(u\). The positive super Schur function satisfies
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u).
\]
Since \(s_\nu(q)\neq 0\) only when \(\nu=(r)\), and a one-variable skew Schur function \(s_{\lambda'/(1^r)}(u)\) is nonzero exactly when \(\lambda'/(1^r)\) is a horizontal strip, only hooks survive. More precisely,
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
\]
and for \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), \(a\ge 1\),
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]

\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q;u),

C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0.

\begin{bmatrix}d(T)\\ r\end{bmatrix}_q

\]

\[

\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q;u)
=

\prod_{i=1}^{d(w)}(q^i+u).
\]

\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{w\in\mathfrak S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}

\]

\[
D_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}

# Original Problem
Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

$$

\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b),\\[2pt]

where

\begin{bmatrix}r\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q

### Proof

\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u;\mathbf z)
=
\sum_{\lambda,\mu} a_{\lambda,\mu}(n)\,
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)\,
s_\mu(\mathbf z),
\]
where \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)\) is the positive super Schur function, \(\mu\vdash n\), and the coefficients \(a_{\lambda,\mu}(n)\) are independent of \(k,j\).

\[
s_\mu(\mathbf z)\longmapsto f^\mu=\#\operatorname{SYT}(\mu),
\]

\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u),
\]

\[
c_\lambda(n)=\sum_{\mu\vdash n}a_{\lambda,\mu}(n)f^\mu.
\]

The constant term of \(\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(m)})\) is \(1\), and every nonempty Schur polynomial has positive degree. Therefore
\[

\[

\[

\[

\[

\[

\tag{1}

\[

\[

\begin{bmatrix}d(T)\\ r\end{bmatrix}_q

\[

\[

\[

prompt:
I need you to check the following solution very carefully and let me know if you find any gaps, classifying them as either major or minor.
You should also carefully check that all bibliographic references are valid.

# Original Problem
Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

# Claim Being Verified
As written, the internal algebraic extraction proves the claimed hook formula conditional on the stated supersymmetric universality and superspace Hilbert-series inputs. After adding the patches above, it establishes the full requested interpretation: \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\), and for every hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\) with \(a\ge1\), \(b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\{(w,\rho): w\in S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|\}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}{d-1\brack b}_q.
\]

# Solution
**Theorem.** Fix \(n\ge 1\). For \(w\in S_n\), let
\[
{\rm Des}(w)=\{i:1\le i<n,\ w_i>w_{i+1}\},\qquad
{\rm des}(w)=|{\rm Des}(w)|,\qquad
{\rm maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in{\rm Des}(w)}i.
\]
For \(0\le b\le r\), use
\[
{r\brack b}_q=\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(r-b)}q^{|\rho|},
\]
where \(\rho\subseteq b\times(r-b)\) means that \(\rho\) has at most \(b\) parts, each at most \(r-b\). Also set \({r\brack b}_q=0\) if \(b<0\) or \(b>r\).

Then the universal Schur-Hilbert coefficient of the empty partition is
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1,
\]
and for every hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\) with \(a\ge 1\), \(b\ge 0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\Bigl\{(w,\rho):\,
w\in S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\Bigr\}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}{d-1\brack b}_q .
\]

**Proof.** Let \(R_n^{(k,j)}\) denote the diagonal coinvariant algebra with \(k\) commuting and \(j\) anticommuting sets of \(n\) variables. Diagonal supersymmetry gives universal Frobenius coefficients \(\gamma_{\lambda,\mu}(n)\), independent of \(k,j\), such that
\[
{\rm grFrob}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q,\mathbf u;\mathbf z)
=
\sum_{\lambda}\sum_{\mu\vdash n}
\gamma_{\lambda,\mu}(n)\,
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)\,s_\mu(\mathbf z).
\]
Taking ordinary Hilbert series means taking dimensions of the \(S_n\)-modules, so \(s_\mu(\mathbf z)\) is replaced by \(f^\mu=\dim S^\mu\). Hence
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q,\mathbf u)
=
\sum_\lambda
\left(\sum_{\mu\vdash n}\gamma_{\lambda,\mu}(n)f^\mu\right)
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u).
\]
For \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), so the coefficient in parentheses is exactly the bosonic coefficient \(c_\lambda(n)\). Therefore the same \(c_\lambda(n)\) occurs for the one-even, one-odd specialization \(SR_n:=R_n^{(1,1)}\):
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)=\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u).
\tag{1}
\]

We next evaluate \(s_\lambda(q/u)\). By the hook-Schur identity,
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u).
\]
In one variable, \(s_\nu(q)\neq 0\) only when \(\nu=(r)\), in which case \(s_\nu(q)=q^r\). Also \(s_{\alpha/\beta}(u)\neq0\) only when \(\alpha/\beta\) is a horizontal strip, in which case it equals \(u^{|\alpha/\beta|}\).

Thus
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1.
\]
If \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), then \(\lambda'=(b+1,1^{a-1})\), and the only contributing \(\nu=(r)\) are \(r=a\) and \(r=a-1\). They give
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\tag{2}
\]
If \(\lambda\) is nonempty and not a hook, then \(\lambda_2\ge2\), so for every \(r\), the skew shape \(\lambda'/(1^r)\) has at least two cells in some column; hence it is not a horizontal strip. Therefore \(s_\lambda(q/u)=0\) for nonhook \(\lambda\).

The degree-zero component of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is spanned by \(1\), and every nonempty Schur polynomial has positive total degree. Hence
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]

For \(b\ge0\), define
\[
A_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad A_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\,{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u).
\]
Using (1) and (2),
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+A_b(q)+q^{-1}A_{b-1}(q).
\tag{3}
\]

Now we compute \(H_b(q)\) independently. The superspace Frobenius formula of Murai–Rhoades–Wilson gives
\[
{\rm grFrob}(SR_n;q,u;\mathbf z)=\sum_{k=1}^n C_{n,k}(\mathbf z;q)u^{n-k}.
\]
The Wilson/Haglund–Rhoades–Shimozono Schur expansion says that for \(b=n-k\),
\[
C_{n,n-b}(\mathbf z;q)
=
\sum_{\lambda\vdash n}\sum_{T\in{\rm SYT}(\lambda)}
q^{{\rm maj}(T)-b\,{\rm des}(T)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(T)\brack b}_q\,s_\lambda(\mathbf z).
\]
Taking dimensions replaces \(s_\lambda(\mathbf z)\) by \(f^\lambda=\# {\rm SYT}(\lambda)\). Under row-insertion RSK, a permutation \(w\in S_n\) corresponds to a pair \((P,Q)\) of standard tableaux of the same shape, with
\[
{\rm Des}(w)={\rm Des}(Q).
\]
Therefore
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(w)\brack b}_q.
\tag{4}
\]

For \(b\ge0\), set
\[
B_b(q)=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}{d-1\brack b}_q,
\qquad B_{-1}(q)=0.
\]
We claim that
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+B_b(q)+q^{-1}B_{b-1}(q).
\tag{5}
\]
Fix \(w\in S_n\), and write \(d={\rm des}(w)\), \(M={\rm maj}(w)\). If \(d\ge1\), then the contribution of \(w\) to \(B_b(q)+q^{-1}B_{b-1}(q)\) is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d-1\brack b}_q+q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity
\[
{d\brack b}_q={d-1\brack b}_q+q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q,
\]
this equals
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q,
\]
which is exactly the \(w\)-summand in (4). The only permutation with \(d=0\) is the identity, and it contributes \(1\) to \(H_0(q)\) and \(0\) to \(H_b(q)\) for \(b>0\); this accounts for the extra \(\delta_{b,0}\). Thus (5) holds.

Comparing (3) and (5), and using \(A_{-1}=B_{-1}=0\), induction on \(b\) gives
\[
A_b(q)=B_b(q)\qquad(b\ge0).
\]
Hence
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}{d-1\brack b}_q.
\]

Finally,
\[
{d-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Substituting this into the last formula gives
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Taking the coefficient of \(q^a\) gives the stated counting formula.

The exponent is always positive in the nonzero case: if \(d\ge b+1\), then
\[
{\rm maj}(w)\ge 1+2+\cdots+d=\binom{d+1}{2},
\]
so
\[
{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}\ge1.
\]
Thus the coefficient extraction indeed concerns \(a\ge1\). ∎

You previously submitted a proof which a careful referee has ACCEPTED as essentially correct. The referee did note a few minor *expository* issues — things like notation, organization, missing citations for textbook facts — but emphasized these do NOT affect the rigor of the proof. Your task is a light polish to address those expository issues only.

# Original Problem
Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

# Claim Established (do NOT change this)
Over characteristic \(0\), the proof establishes the claimed hook interpretation: \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\), and for every hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), \(a\ge1\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=
\#\{(w,\rho): d(w)\ge b+1,\ \rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b),\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\tbinom b2+|\rho|\}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q .
\]

# Current Accepted Proof
**Claim.** Fix \(n\ge 1\). For \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\in\mathfrak S_n\), let
\[
\operatorname{Des}(w)=\{i: w_i>w_{i+1}\},\qquad d(w)=|\operatorname{Des}(w)|,\qquad 
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in\operatorname{Des}(w)}i.
\]
Then the universal Hilbert-Schur coefficients of \(R_n^{(m)}\) satisfy
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1,
\]
and, for every hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\) with \(a\ge1\), \(b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
d(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b),\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\right\}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
where
\[
\begin{bmatrix}r\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(r-b)}q^{|\rho|}
\]
for \(0\le b\le r\), and is \(0\) otherwise.

**Proof.** Work over a characteristic-zero field. We use the bosonic-fermionic diagonal coinvariant ring \(R_n^{(k,j)}\), with \(k\) commuting sets and \(j\) anticommuting sets of \(n\) variables, with diagonal \(\mathfrak S_n\)-action. The diagonal supersymmetry theorem says that, for finite groups in characteristic \(0\), the graded Frobenius series has a universal positive-super-Schur expansion
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u;\mathbf z)
=
\sum_{\lambda,\mu} a_{\lambda,\mu}(n)\,
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)\,
s_\mu(\mathbf z),
\]
with coefficients \(a_{\lambda,\mu}(n)\) independent of \((k,j)\). Since \(\mathfrak S_n\) is finite, this theorem applies here. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.14885))

Applying the dimension map on Frobenius characteristics,
\[
s_\mu(\mathbf z)\longmapsto f^\mu=\dim S^\mu,
\]
turns graded Frobenius series into Hilbert series. Thus
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\lambda} c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u),
\qquad
c_\lambda(n)=\sum_{\mu\vdash n}a_{\lambda,\mu}(n)f^\mu.
\]
For \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), so these are exactly the universal coefficients in the original purely bosonic Hilbert-Schur expansion.

The degree-zero component of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is the constants, hence has dimension \(1\), while every nonempty Schur polynomial has positive total degree. Therefore
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]

Now specialize to one bosonic grading variable \(q\) and one fermionic grading variable \(u\). The positive super Schur function satisfies
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq \lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u).
\]
Since \(s_\nu(q)\neq0\) only when \(\nu\) is a single row, and a one-variable skew Schur function \(s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u)\) is nonzero exactly when \(\lambda'/\nu'\) is a horizontal strip, only hooks survive. More precisely,
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
\]
and for \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), \(a\ge1\),
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
Indeed, for a hook \((a,1^b)\), the only surviving choices are \(\nu=(a)\) and \(\nu=(a-1)\).

Define
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q;u),
\]
and
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0.
\]
The preceding specialization gives
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b^{(n)}(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
\tag{1}
\]

We next compute \(H_b^{(n)}(q)\) directly. Let \(SR_n=R_n^{(1,1)}\). The proved Fields Conjecture for the superspace coinvariant ring gives
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u;\mathbf z)
=
\sum_{k=1}^n u^{\,n-k}C_{n,k}(\mathbf z;q),
\]
where
\[
C_{n,k}(\mathbf z;q)
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)+\binom{n-k}{2}-(n-k)d(T)}
\begin{bmatrix}d(T)\\ n-k\end{bmatrix}_q
s_{\operatorname{shape}(T)}(\mathbf z).
\]
Here \(\operatorname{Des}(T)=\{i:i+1\text{ lies in a lower row than }i\}\), \(d(T)=|\operatorname{Des}(T)|\), and \(\operatorname{maj}(T)=\sum_{i\in\operatorname{Des}(T)}i\). The theorem applies because \(SR_n\) is exactly the rank-\(n\) superspace coinvariant ring with diagonal \(\mathfrak S_n\)-action. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.24027))

Setting \(r=n-k\), we get
\[
[u^r]\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u;\mathbf z)
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)+\binom r2-rd(T)}
\begin{bmatrix}d(T)\\ r\end{bmatrix}_q
s_{\operatorname{shape}(T)}(\mathbf z).
\]
Applying the dimension map gives
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}_n}
f^{\operatorname{shape}(T)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)+\binom b2-bd(T)}
\begin{bmatrix}d(T)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Under RSK, \(w\leftrightarrow(P(w),Q(w))\), one has
\[
\operatorname{Des}(w)=\operatorname{Des}(Q(w)),
\]
and for each fixed recording tableau \(Q\) of shape \(\mu\), there are \(f^\mu\) possible insertion tableaux \(P\). Therefore
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{w\in\mathfrak S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\tag{2}
\]

Now define
\[
D_b^{(n)}(q)=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\qquad D_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0.
\]
We claim that
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)=\delta_{b,0}+D_b^{(n)}(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
\tag{3}
\]
The identity permutation has \(d=0\), \(\operatorname{maj}=0\), and contributes only \(\delta_{b,0}\) to (2). For any nonidentity \(w\), write \(d=d(w)\), \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\). Its contribution to \(D_b^{(n)}(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}^{(n)}(q)\) is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity,
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
this equals the \(w\)-summand in (2). Hence (3) holds.

Comparing (1) and (3), and using \(C_{-1}^{(n)}=D_{-1}^{(n)}=0\), we get by induction on \(b\) that
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=D_b^{(n)}(q)
\]
for every \(b\ge0\). Thus
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

Finally,
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in\mathfrak S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Taking the coefficient of \(q^a\) gives
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
d(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b),\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\right\}.
\]
This proves the claimed hook-coefficient interpretation.

# Prior solution (for your reference only — do not include or quote it in your output)
**Theorem.** Fix \(n\ge 1\). For \(w\in S_n\), let
\[
{\rm Des}(w)=\{i:1\le i<n,\ w_i>w_{i+1}\},\qquad
{\rm des}(w)=|{\rm Des}(w)|,\qquad
{\rm maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in{\rm Des}(w)}i.
\]
For \(0\le b\le r\), use
\[
{r\brack b}_q=\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(r-b)}q^{|\rho|},
\]
where \(\rho\subseteq b\times(r-b)\) means that \(\rho\) has at most \(b\) parts, each at most \(r-b\). Also set \({r\brack b}_q=0\) if \(b<0\) or \(b>r\).

Then the universal Schur-Hilbert coefficient of the empty partition is
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1,
\]
and for every hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\) with \(a\ge 1\), \(b\ge 0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\Bigl\{(w,\rho):\,
w\in S_n,\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\Bigr\}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}{d-1\brack b}_q .
\]

**Proof.** Let \(R_n^{(k,j)}\) denote the diagonal coinvariant algebra with \(k\) commuting and \(j\) anticommuting sets of \(n\) variables. Diagonal supersymmetry gives universal Frobenius coefficients \(\gamma_{\lambda,\mu}(n)\), independent of \(k,j\), such that
\[
{\rm grFrob}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q,\mathbf u;\mathbf z)
=
\sum_{\lambda}\sum_{\mu\vdash n}
\gamma_{\lambda,\mu}(n)\,
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)\,s_\mu(\mathbf z).
\]
Taking ordinary Hilbert series means taking dimensions of the \(S_n\)-modules, so \(s_\mu(\mathbf z)\) is replaced by \(f^\mu=\dim S^\mu\). Hence
\[
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q,\mathbf u)
=
\sum_\lambda
\left(\sum_{\mu\vdash n}\gamma_{\lambda,\mu}(n)f^\mu\right)
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u).
\]
For \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), so the coefficient in parentheses is exactly the bosonic coefficient \(c_\lambda(n)\). Therefore the same \(c_\lambda(n)\) occurs for the one-even, one-odd specialization \(SR_n:=R_n^{(1,1)}\):
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)=\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u).
\tag{1}
\]

We next evaluate \(s_\lambda(q/u)\). By the hook-Schur identity,
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u).
\]
In one variable, \(s_\nu(q)\neq 0\) only when \(\nu=(r)\), in which case \(s_\nu(q)=q^r\). Also \(s_{\alpha/\beta}(u)\neq0\) only when \(\alpha/\beta\) is a horizontal strip, in which case it equals \(u^{|\alpha/\beta|}\).

Thus
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1.
\]
If \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), then \(\lambda'=(b+1,1^{a-1})\), and the only contributing \(\nu=(r)\) are \(r=a\) and \(r=a-1\). They give
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\tag{2}
\]
If \(\lambda\) is nonempty and not a hook, then \(\lambda_2\ge2\), so for every \(r\), the skew shape \(\lambda'/(1^r)\) has at least two cells in some column; hence it is not a horizontal strip. Therefore \(s_\lambda(q/u)=0\) for nonhook \(\lambda\).

The degree-zero component of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is spanned by \(1\), and every nonempty Schur polynomial has positive total degree. Hence
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]

For \(b\ge0\), define
\[
A_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad A_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\,{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u).
\]
Using (1) and (2),
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+A_b(q)+q^{-1}A_{b-1}(q).
\tag{3}
\]

Now we compute \(H_b(q)\) independently. The superspace Frobenius formula of Murai–Rhoades–Wilson gives
\[
{\rm grFrob}(SR_n;q,u;\mathbf z)=\sum_{k=1}^n C_{n,k}(\mathbf z;q)u^{n-k}.
\]
The Wilson/Haglund–Rhoades–Shimozono Schur expansion says that for \(b=n-k\),
\[
C_{n,n-b}(\mathbf z;q)
=
\sum_{\lambda\vdash n}\sum_{T\in{\rm SYT}(\lambda)}
q^{{\rm maj}(T)-b\,{\rm des}(T)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(T)\brack b}_q\,s_\lambda(\mathbf z).
\]
Taking dimensions replaces \(s_\lambda(\mathbf z)\) by \(f^\lambda=\# {\rm SYT}(\lambda)\). Under row-insertion RSK, a permutation \(w\in S_n\) corresponds to a pair \((P,Q)\) of standard tableaux of the same shape, with
\[
{\rm Des}(w)={\rm Des}(Q).
\]
Therefore
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(w)\brack b}_q.
\tag{4}
\]

For \(b\ge0\), set
\[
B_b(q)=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}{d-1\brack b}_q,
\qquad B_{-1}(q)=0.
\]
We claim that
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+B_b(q)+q^{-1}B_{b-1}(q).
\tag{5}
\]
Fix \(w\in S_n\), and write \(d={\rm des}(w)\), \(M={\rm maj}(w)\). If \(d\ge1\), then the contribution of \(w\) to \(B_b(q)+q^{-1}B_{b-1}(q)\) is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d-1\brack b}_q+q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity
\[
{d\brack b}_q={d-1\brack b}_q+q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q,
\]
this equals
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q,
\]
which is exactly the \(w\)-summand in (4). The only permutation with \(d=0\) is the identity, and it contributes \(1\) to \(H_0(q)\) and \(0\) to \(H_b(q)\) for \(b>0\); this accounts for the extra \(\delta_{b,0}\). Thus (5) holds.

Comparing (3) and (5), and using \(A_{-1}=B_{-1}=0\), induction on \(b\) gives
\[
A_b(q)=B_b(q)\qquad(b\ge0).
\]
Hence
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}{d-1\brack b}_q.
\]

Finally,
\[
{d-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Substituting this into the last formula gives
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Taking the coefficient of \(q^a\) gives the stated counting formula.

The exponent is always positive in the nonzero case: if \(d\ge b+1\), then
\[
{\rm maj}(w)\ge 1+2+\cdots+d=\binom{d+1}{2},
\]
so
\[
{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}\ge1.
\]
Thus the coefficient extraction indeed concerns \(a\ge1\). ∎

s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u).

# Original Problem
Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is

Therefore the same coefficients occur in the one-even, one-odd specialization:
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)=\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u).
\tag{1}
\]

The super Schur convention used here is
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u).
\tag{2}
\]
In one variable, \(s_\nu(q)\neq0\) only for \(\nu=(r)\), and \(s_{\alpha/\beta}(u)\neq0\) only when \(\alpha/\beta\) is a horizontal strip, in which case it equals \(u^{|\alpha/\beta|}\). This is the convention stated in Lentfer’s Equation (12). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

For a hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), \(\lambda'=(b+1,1^{a-1})\). The only \(r\) for which \(\lambda'/(1^r)\) is a horizontal strip are \(r=a\) and \(r=a-1\). Hence
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\tag{3}
\]
Also \(s_\varnothing(q/u)=1\). If \(\lambda\neq\varnothing\) is not a hook, then \(\lambda_2\ge2\), so after removing any \((1^r)\) from \(\lambda'\), column \(2\) still contains at least two cells; hence no horizontal strip occurs and
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=0.
\]

The degree-zero piece of the bosonic coinvariant algebra is spanned by \(1\), while every nonempty Schur polynomial has positive total degree. Thus
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]

For \(b\ge0\), define
\[
A_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad A_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\,{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u).
\]
Using (1) and (3),
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+A_b(q)+q^{-1}A_{b-1}(q).
\tag{4}
\]

We now compute \(H_b(q)\) independently. Murai–Rhoades–Wilson prove, over characteristic \(0\), that
\[
{\rm grFrob}(SR_n;q,u;\mathbf z)
=
\sum_{k=1}^n C_{n,k}(\mathbf z;q)u^{n-k},
\tag{5}
\]
where \(q\) tracks bosonic degree and \(u\) tracks fermionic degree. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027)) Their Equation (2.5) gives the Schur expansion
\[
C_{n,k}(\mathbf z;q)
=
\sum_{T\in{\rm SYT}(n)}
q^{{\rm maj}(T)+\binom{n-k}{2}-(n-k){\rm des}(T)}
{{\rm des}(T)\brack n-k}_q
s_{\lambda(T)}(\mathbf z).
\tag{6}
\]
([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

Taking dimensions in (6), and putting \(b=n-k\), gives for \(0\le b\le n-1\)
\[
H_b(q)=
\sum_{T\in{\rm SYT}(n)}
f^{\lambda(T)}
q^{{\rm maj}(T)-b{\rm des}(T)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(T)\brack b}_q .
\]
Under row-insertion RSK, a permutation \(w\in S_n\) corresponds to a pair \((P,Q)\) of standard tableaux of the same shape, and with the usual convention \(i\in{\rm Des}(Q)\) iff \(i+1\) lies in a lower row than \(i\), one has
\[
{\rm Des}(w)={\rm Des}(Q).
\]
Thus
\[
H_b(q)=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
{{\rm des}(w)\brack b}_q .
\tag{7}
\]
For \(b\ge n\), (5) has no \(u^b\)-term, so \(H_b(q)=0\); the right side of (7) also vanishes because \({\rm des}(w)\le n-1<b\). Hence (7) holds for every \(b\ge0\).

Now set
\[
B_b(q)=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}{d-1\brack b}_q,
\qquad B_{-1}(q)=0.
\]
We claim that
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+B_b(q)+q^{-1}B_{b-1}(q).
\tag{8}
\]
Fix \(w\in S_n\), and write \(d={\rm des}(w)\), \(M={\rm maj}(w)\). If \(d\ge1\), the contribution of \(w\) to \(B_b+q^{-1}B_{b-1}\) is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d-1\brack b}_q+q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity,
\[
{d\brack b}_q={d-1\brack b}_q+q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q,
\]
this equals
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q,
\]
which is exactly the \(w\)-summand in (7). The only permutation with \(d=0\) is the identity; it contributes \(1\) to \(H_0(q)\) and \(0\) to \(H_b(q)\) for \(b>0\). This accounts for \(\delta_{b,0}\), proving (8).

Comparing (4) and (8) gives
\[
A_b(q)+q^{-1}A_{b-1}(q)=B_b(q)+q^{-1}B_{b-1}(q).
\]
Since \(A_{-1}=B_{-1}=0\), induction on \(b\) yields \(A_b(q)=B_b(q)\) for all \(b\ge0\). Therefore
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}{d-1\brack b}_q .
\]

Finally,
\[
{d-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Substituting this gives
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Taking the coefficient of \(q^a\) gives the stated counting formula.

The exponent is always positive in the nonzero case: if \(d\ge b+1\), then
\[
{\rm maj}(w)\ge 1+2+\cdots+d=\binom{d+1}{2},
\]
so
\[
{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}\ge1.
\]
Thus the coefficient extraction indeed concerns \(a\ge1\). ∎

prompt:
You are revising a candidate proof of a mathematical problem.

# Original Problem
Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

# Candidate Proof (on the right track, may have gaps or unnecessary jargon)
## Original Problem

Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

## Problem Solved

For every integer \(n\ge 1\), let \(c_\lambda(n)\) denote the universal Schur coefficient in the multigraded Hilbert-series expansion of \(R_n^{(m)}\). If \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\) is a hook partition, where \(a\ge 1\) and \(b\ge 0\), then
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Here \(S_n\) is the symmetric group, a descent of \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\) is an index \(i\in\{1,\dots,n-1\}\) with \(w_i>w_{i+1}\),
\[
\operatorname{des}(w)=|\{i:w_i>w_{i+1}\}|,\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{w_i>w_{i+1}} i.
\]
A partition \(\rho\subseteq b\times r\) means that \(\rho\) has at most \(b\) parts and every part is at most \(r\); the empty partition is allowed, and \(|\rho|\) denotes the sum of its parts. For a standard Young tableau \(T\), a descent is an index \(i\) such that \(i+1\) appears in a strictly lower row than \(i\), and \(\operatorname{des}(T)\), \(\operatorname{maj}(T)\) are defined analogously.

The empty coefficient is
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]
Consequently,
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\qquad\text{whenever }b\ge n-1.
\]

Gaussian binomial coefficients are used with the convention
\[
\biggl[{r\atop s}\biggr]_q
=
\begin{cases}
\displaystyle\prod_{i=1}^{s}\frac{1-q^{r-s+i}}{1-q^i},&0\le s\le r,\\[1em]
0,&r<0,\ s<0,\text{ or }s>r.
\end{cases}
\]
Equivalently, for \(0\le s\le r\),
\[
\biggl[{r\atop s}\biggr]_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq s\times(r-s)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]

This proves the original hook-coefficient problem as stated; it is not a relaxation.

## Proof

Fix \(n\ge 1\). For a partition \(\mu\vdash n\), let \(\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\) be the set of standard Young tableaux of shape \(\mu\), and let \(f^\mu=|\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)|\).

We use the following standard inputs.

1. There are refined universal Frobenius coefficients \(c_{\lambda,\mu}\) such that, after passing to the one-bosonic-one-fermionic specialization,
   \[
   \operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)
   =
   \sum_{\lambda,\,\mu\vdash n}
   c_{\lambda,\mu}\,s_\lambda(q/u)\,s_\mu.
   \]
   The original Hilbert coefficient is obtained by forgetting the \(S_n\)-module structure:
   \[
   c_\lambda(n)=\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu c_{\lambda,\mu}.
   \]
   Also, the degree-zero piece is the constant polynomial carrying the trivial representation, so
   \[
   c_{\varnothing,\mu}=\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
   \]

2. In one bosonic variable \(q\) and one fermionic variable \(u\),
   \[
   s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
   \]
   and for hooks,
   \[
   s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
   \]
   All nonempty non-hook Schur functions vanish under this specialization.

3. Fields’ formula gives
   \[
   \operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)
   =
   \sum_T
   q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(T)+1}{2}}
   \prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(T)}(q^i+u)\,
   s_{\operatorname{shape}(T)},
   \]
   where the sum is over all standard Young tableaux \(T\) of size \(n\).

### 1. Extracting \(u^b s_\mu\) from Fields’ formula

Let \(H_{b,\mu}(q)\) be the coefficient of \(u^b s_\mu\) in \(\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)\).

Fix \(T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\), and write
\[
d=\operatorname{des}(T),\qquad M=\operatorname{maj}(T).
\]
The coefficient of \(u^b\) in
\[
\prod_{i=1}^{d}(q^i+u)
\]
is obtained by choosing \(b\) factors contributing \(u\), or equivalently \(d-b\) factors contributing \(q^i\). Thus, for \(0\le b\le d\),
\[
[u^b]\prod_{i=1}^{d}(q^i+u)
=
q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}
\biggl[{d\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]
Multiplying by Fields’ prefactor gives
\[
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}}
q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}
\biggl[{d\atop b}\biggr]_q
=
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\biggl[{d\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]
Therefore
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
\biggl[{\operatorname{des}(T)\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]

### 2. The triangular relation for hook coefficients

For \(b\ge 0\), define
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)=\sum_{a\ge 1} c_{(a,1^b),\mu}q^a,
\qquad
C_{-1,\mu}(q)=0.
\]
Compare the coefficient of \(u^b s_\mu\) in
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{\lambda,\mu}
c_{\lambda,\mu}s_\lambda(q/u)s_\mu.
\]
The empty partition contributes only when \(b=0\), giving
\[
\delta_{b,0}\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
\]
A hook \((a,1^b)\) contributes \(q^a u^b\), giving \(C_{b,\mu}(q)\). A hook \((a,1^{b-1})\) contributes \(q^{a-1}u^b\), giving \(q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q)\). Hence
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\delta_{b,0}\delta_{\mu,(n)}
+
C_{b,\mu}(q)
+
q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q).
\]

### 3. Solving the recurrence

We claim that
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ \operatorname{des}(T)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
\biggl[{\operatorname{des}(T)-1\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]

For \(b=0\), the triangular relation gives
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)=H_{0,\mu}(q)-\delta_{\mu,(n)}
=
\sum_{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)}
-\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
\]
A standard tableau has no descents iff it is the unique row tableau of shape \((n)\). Therefore
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ \operatorname{des}(T)\ge 1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)},
\]
which is the claimed formula for \(b=0\), since
\[
\biggl[{\operatorname{des}(T)-1\atop 0}\biggr]_q=1
\]
when \(\operatorname{des}(T)\ge 1\).

Now assume \(b\ge 1\) and the formula is known for \(b-1\). From the triangular relation,
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)=H_{b,\mu}(q)-q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q).
\]
For a fixed tableau \(T\), with \(d=\operatorname{des}(T)\) and \(M=\operatorname{maj}(T)\), the contribution from \(q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q)\) is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
q^{d-b}
\biggl[{d-1\atop b-1}\biggr]_q.
\]
Thus
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_T
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\biggl[{d\atop b}\biggr]_q
-
q^{d-b}
\biggl[{d-1\atop b-1}\biggr]_q
\right).
\]
Using the \(q\)-Pascal identity
\[
\biggl[{d\atop b}\biggr]_q
=
\biggl[{d-1\atop b}\biggr]_q
+
q^{d-b}
\biggl[{d-1\atop b-1}\biggr]_q,
\]
we get
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_T
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\biggl[{d-1\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]
By the Gaussian convention, this summand is zero unless \(d-1\ge b\), i.e. unless \(d\ge b+1\). Hence
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ d\ge b+1}}
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\biggl[{d-1\atop b}\biggr]_q.
\]

### 4. Translating the Gaussian binomial

For \(d\ge b+1\),
\[
\biggl[{d-1\atop b}\biggr]_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Therefore the coefficient of \(q^a\) in \(C_{b,\mu}(q)\) counts pairs \((T,\rho)\) such that
\[
T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu),\qquad d=\operatorname{des}(T)\ge b+1,
\]
\[
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(T)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

The exponent is always positive in this situation. Indeed, if \(d\ge b+1\), then the \(d\) descent positions have sum at least \(1+2+\cdots+d\), so
\[
\operatorname{maj}(T)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}
\ge 1.
\]
Thus the formula produces only hook arms \(a\ge 1\), as required.

### 5. Passing from tableaux to permutations by RSK

Now use
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu c_{(a,1^b),\mu}.
\]
Multiplying by \(f^\mu\) means choosing an additional standard tableau \(U\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\). Hence \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\) counts quadruples
\[
(\mu,T,U,\rho)
\]
such that
\[
\mu\vdash n,\qquad T,U\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu),
\]
\[
d=\operatorname{des}(T)\ge b+1,\qquad
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(T)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

By the Robinson-Schensted-Knuth correspondence, permutations \(w\in S_n\) are in bijection with pairs \((P(w),Q(w))\) of standard Young tableaux of the same shape. Moreover,
\[
\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w),
\]
and therefore
\[
\operatorname{des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{des}(w),\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(Q(w))=\operatorname{maj}(w).
\]
Identify \(U=P(w)\) and \(T=Q(w)\). Then the above quadruples are in bijection with pairs \((w,\rho)\) satisfying
\[
w\in S_n,\qquad d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,
\]
\[
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]
This proves the claimed permutation interpretation.

### 6. Edge cases

For the empty partition,
\[
c_\varnothing(n)
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu c_{\varnothing,\mu}
=
\sum_{\mu\vdash n} f^\mu \delta_{\mu,(n)}
=
f^{(n)}
=
1.
\]

When \(b=0\), the rectangle is \(0\times(d-1)\), so \(\rho\) must be empty. The formula becomes
\[
c_{(a)}(n)
=
\#\{w\in S_n:\operatorname{des}(w)\ge 1,\ \operatorname{maj}(w)=a\}.
\]
Since \(a\ge 1\), the condition \(\operatorname{maj}(w)=a\) already forces at least one descent.

Finally, every permutation in \(S_n\) has at most \(n-1\) descents. If \(b\ge n-1\), then the condition
\[
\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1
\]
is impossible. Hence
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0
\qquad\text{for all }b\ge n-1.
\]

# Instructions

This approach seems to be quite promising. Can you make this approach work to solve the original problem (even if the quantitative bounds are slightly worse)? I want you to be able to get an end-to-end solution without using unnecessary jargon and/or any gaps. No need to optimize constants. The proof should be correct.
